This could occur because of ignorance, a desire for perfection, or even excitement. Sometimes over-processing occurs because an employee hasn’t had training on the most efficient way to handle a task. Other times, it occurs because an employee or process is more thorough than is worthwhile. A goal of any process should be to do just enough useful and necessary work to ensure that customer or end-user expectations are met.

One example of over-processing often occurs in healthcare administrative offices during the insurance verification process. Insurance verification occurs when a healthcare provider’s office attempts to verify that a person is covered by insurance for the services that are about to be rendered. Depending on the type of insurance coverage and the office’s policies, a staff member either checks benefits via a computer program or calls the insurance company.

In most cases, the goal of insurance verification is simply to ensure that the insured is covered by the plan for the date the services are to be rendered. Sometimes, however, an office worker delves deeper into the verification, spending up to an hour on the phone with an insurance company to receive detailed benefits or calling back to check with another representative to ensure the original information provided was correct. While specific cases exist that require in-depth insurance verification, basic doctor’s visits don’t require this level of work. A staff member who is taking up to an hour to verify insurance coverage is overproducing, and it’s probably causing productivity problems for the office as a whole.

In consumer-centric processes, over-processing occurs when you put more into a product than the value afforded by the customer. While product excellence is important, at some point the work you put into a product exceeds that which is deemed necessary or useful by the customer, and this is often tied to price point. A customer expects more out of a premium, more expensive model, for example.

An example of over-processing might be painting the bottom of an inside cabinet shelf when no one will ever see it. In a restaurant, ironing table linens to an exacting standard could be considered over- processing, especially if you remove the linens between meals for washing. In most restaurants, much of the table linen drapes over the table; owners might want linens that aren’t full of wrinkles, but they don’t need crisp and perfect seams. In the technical world, building an app with 100 features when 99 percent of people only want to use 10 main functions can be considered both over-processing and over- producing.

A value stream map, covered in Chapter 35, is a good tool for identifying any points of over-processing. Any part of the process that doesn’t provide value could be considered over-processing; when the process features a series of linked events and none provide value, it’s even more likely that over- processing is occurring.

In a true Lean process, every step of a process provides value, but it can be tricky to determine when value is not occurring. Quality is important to both the success of the process and the end customer, for example, but the customer doesn’t care, or usually even realize, that your process is imbued with quality checks throughout. They care instead that a process takes 10 minutes longer because of those quality checks—teams have to dig deep into processes, metrics, and customer voice to determine if those 10 extra minutes are providing enough added value to cover the annoyance or loss of customer because of
the added time. This knowledge is all ascertained through data collection and statistical analysis discussed in detail throughout this book.
Waiting

Muda of waiting refers to any idle time in a process, whether that idle time is for machinery or people. In other words, an employee or machine is working below capacity or is not working at all due to waiting on inputs from another part of the process. Waiting occurs when steps in the process are not properly coordinated, when processes are unreliable, when work is batched too large, during rework, and during long changeovers between staff or machines.

In a retail or fast food environment, when one cashier’s shift is over, another cashier takes over at the station. To maintain financial integrity, almost all companies switch out cash register drawers during the change between cashiers; if there is a mistake with the drawer, the company knows who was running the register at that point in time, making it easier to find trends or issues. However, when the drawer is being changed out, the register usually can’t be used. The cashier is simply waiting, sometimes along with the customer.

To reduce the muda of waiting in this cash register example, most organizations use a process that includes a series of register drawers that are prepped at the beginning of the day or shift. The drawers are designed to be interchangeable in the registers, so the old drawer can be pulled and the new drawer put in in less than 30 seconds, solving the waste of waiting.

Waste of waiting is common in construction environments. Construction of roads, bridges, buildings and other structures requires close attention to order and detail: you can’t build the roadway of a bridge until the pillars are in place and steady. This concept is seen even on a small scale in construction, causing paid employees to often stand around waiting for others to complete a piece of the job before they can finish the assigned task.

Because construction is a field of specialists and certifications, one employee often cannot do the work another can. In other words, employee tasks are not interchangeable. This causes additional muda of waiting; one group of construction workers might have to wait on the forklift operator to come and move some items. Meanwhile, the forklift operator is finishing up with a task on the other side of the construction lot. This isn’t a problem limited to construction: some offices have policies requiring an official IT staff member to handle any computer issue. Whether it’s a software glitch, a troubleshooting error, or simply the need to switch out an underperforming mouse, regular office staff must send a support ticket or make a phone call and wait for IT staff to solve the problem.

You can eliminate waste of waiting within many processes by balancing machinery, people, and production. The process will only perform as fast as the slowest link; beefing up the production of a single element does nothing for the whole, so teams must work to balance and improve the entire process.

Sometimes, scheduling is a key component in eliminating waiting. In the construction example, advanced planning and scheduling tactics could reduce the chance that one team would be waiting for a forklift operator while he or she is busy elsewhere. Understanding organizational, team, project, and process needs also helps leadership provide the right amount of resources to reduce waiting. The construction site might benefit from two forklift operators, for example. In the IT example, a company

can reduce wait times by maximizing IT staffing at high-volume times or implementing processes within the IT department to create more efficient responses to help tickets. An auxiliary IT staff member can be hired to handle less technical issues such as switching out an under-performing mouse.

zh.text: Muda是一个日语单词，翻译过来就是浪费。它描述了无用、不必要或闲置的概念。过程中必须消除“muda”的概念是丰田生产系统和精益制造的驱动概念。 Muda 是流程中的一项非增值任务 (NVA)。有些类型的 Muda 比其他类型更容易识别，这就是精益六西格玛部署价值流图等工具的原因。通过了解各个级别的流程，团队更有可能识别各种形式的 Muda。 丰田公司总工程师大野泰井（Taiicho Ohno）表示，有七种资源经常被滥用和管理不善：生产过剩、校正、库存、移动、运输、过度加工和等待。
生产过剩
生产过剩是最容易发现的浪费形式之一，因为它往往会导致我们通常认为的浪费。生产过剩意味着产品、零件或服务生产得太快、在错误的时间或者生产的数量过多。要理解生产过剩的概念，请考虑一家提供汉堡包和炸薯条午餐的基本快餐店。餐厅不提供早餐，并于上午 11:00 向午餐人群开放。
如果厨师在上午 11:00 点起烤架，那么他们可能会推迟一天的开始，因为可能会立即下几个订单。然而，如果厨师们开始制作汉堡肉饼
上午 10:30，他们的肉饼可能会在食用前放置一段时间，如果肉饼被扔掉，就会导致顾客不满意或浪费。从上午 10:30 开始每 10 分钟制作 10 个肉饼就属于生产过剩——肉饼制作得太早了。
如果餐厅老板做了一些研究，并且知道周二上午 11:00 到 11:15 之间的平均订单数是 10 个汉堡包，该怎么办？他们可能会指示厨师在上午 10:50 开始制作肉饼，每 10 分钟制作 5 个肉饼。目标是使肉饼制作与客户订单保持一致，从而减少等待时间，但客户仍然能够享用新鲜的肉饼。
到了中午，业主们知道订单往往会很快到来，因此他们要求厨师每 10 分钟制作 15 个肉饼。然而，到下午 2:00，订单量通常会降至每小时 10 个汉堡。如果厨师仍然每 10 分钟制作 15 个，那么该过程就会出现生产过剩。
在某些时候，餐厅的客流量可能需要定制烧烤——厨师只按照订单制作汉堡肉饼，以避免浪费任何材料。通过了解餐厅的客流量趋势，业主能够估计需求并创建流程，减少厨房中产生的废物量，同时仍满足顾客的质量要求。
生产过剩通常与流程的有形结果相关，尽管这些结果不一定是最终的或所述流程的“最终产品”。考虑一家打印名片、文具、邀请函和其他文件的企业。也许这家企业提供打印和邮寄服务；为了完成这项服务，该公司使用机器驱动的流程来打印、折叠和填充邮件。打印机每小时能够打印 1,000 页，但折页机每小时只能折叠 800 页。

即使客户想要打印 1,000 页并邮寄，如果将第一台机器设置为以最大速度运行，打印机也会产能过剩。该过程将需要超过一小时，因为它取决于最慢的机器。由于生产过剩不会导致有形浪费——打印的页面最终将被折叠并邮寄——公司的流程负责人必须考虑其他因素，以决定暂时的生产过剩是否对流程有害。在折叠过程之前堆叠额外的纸张是否会产生额外的错误风险？以最大容量运行印刷机是否会不必要地对其造成不必要的压力或磨损？如果任一问题的答案是肯定的，那么就存在应从流程中消除的浪费。
报告、数字资产和流程准备方面也可能存在生产过剩。几乎所有在商业环境中工作的人都熟悉报告要求，就像几乎所有创建报告的人都知道信息经常未被阅读的不幸事实一样。创建无人阅读的报告，或者在概览就足够的情况下创建非常详细的报告，就是生产过剩。
准备流程中未使用的设备也属于生产过剩。手术中心通常在轮班开始前准备好机器、设备托盘和手术室。目标是为任何患者手术创建高效的流程；如果在手术过程中出现问题，工作人员还必须能够快速使用设备。一天只安排 10 台手术，准备 20 托盘设备，如果多余的托盘没有用处，可能会被视为生产过剩。
在某些情况下，例如上面的医学示例，流程可能需要轻微的生产过剩。 如果安排 10 台手术，工作人员可能会准备 13 或 14 个设备托盘。这样，如果紧急手术到达，或者现有托盘出现问题，就可以使用备用设备。通过了解流程和要求中的细微差别，六西格玛团队可以更好地识别生产过剩与监管或问题解决政策可能要求的生产过剩。
消除生产过剩的关键是规划。在上面的示例中，您会注意到，当流程操作员了解流程中固有的需求时，每个解决方案都会出现。当业主知道可能有多少订单时，他们就能够制定减少浪费的计划。当印刷商了解流程中每台机器的功能时，他或她就可以规划最高效的印刷运行。
修正
这种形式的浪费也称为返工浪费，经常困扰热衷于传统质量计划的组织。为了消除最终产品中的缺陷，组织会进行过程中的质量检查，将有缺陷的工作送回进行纠正。虽然在某些情况下可能需要返工，特别是当材料特别有价值并且工作值得节省而不是报废时，但在过程中仍然存在浪费，应予以识别和分析。
当返工发生时，它会增加整体流程时间并使用额外的劳动力和材料来生产较少数量的产品或产出。让我们看一个大型汽车保险公司呼叫中心的示例。该中心的一些电话来自在事故中车辆受损的人。请考虑以下处理此类调用的过程：
来电者报告汽车损坏。
代表以计算机形式记录信息。
代表根据呼叫者提供的信息做出决定： o 如果被视为紧急情况，则索赔将被转至立即处理 o 如果是单车事故或天灾事故，索赔将转至 A 组 o 如果是多车事故，则索赔将转至 B 组
现在，想象一下 B 团队的一名员工的工作队列中收到了一份索赔。输入计算机的信息不完整，因此该员工无法确定该索赔是否与多车事故有关。 员工可能会将索赔发送回原始队列，这意味着呼叫中心员工必须给被保险人回电以收集更多信息。然后，根据新信息，索赔将再次被路由，并再次在工作队列中等待。
修正错误的最大问题之一是，经常会出现需要进行处理的情况。也许B队没有能力处理单车事故，所以如果要完成索赔，就必须改变工作路线。但是为什么不让下线团队成员处理任何索赔，或者创建一个流程将索赔重新路由到正确的团队而不将工作发送回原始队列呢？
纠正或返工可以发生在任何类型的过程中。制造过程剔除有缺陷的零件和产品；有时材料会被重新加工以获得更好的结果，有时它们会被报废——这也是一种浪费。呼叫中心和数字工作队列因返工而闻名，因为这很容易
以数字格式来回发送作品。在某些情况下，返工的发生并不是因为纠正，而仅仅是因为部门或工人的职责重叠。
为了消除返工或纠正，组织必须使用双重方法。首先，必须解决返工的根本原因——导致错误的原因。是否需要进一步的员工培训？是否可以改变流程以使其更不易出错？在某些情况下，可以部署后面单元中讨论的过程控制原则，包括称为“防错”的策略，以使在过程中产生缺陷比不产生缺陷更困难。当缺陷被避免时，返工也就被避免了。

rows: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
zh.text: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。

rows: 术语说明 Poka-Yoke（防错）：通过设计让错误更难发生，或发生后能立即被发现。
zh.text: 术语说明 Poka-Yoke（防错）：通过设计让错误更难发生，或发生后能立即被发现。

除了解决错误的根本原因之外，组织还应该制定减少返工浪费的质量步骤。在有关保险公司呼叫中心的示例中，我们注意到，对于下线工作人员来说，将索赔重新路由到正确的团队比将其返回到原始团队更有效。不过，这种方法有时会引起文化问题。领导者和工作人员有一种感觉，第一团队或第一团队成员应该对错误负责。追究责任的一种方法是让那个人纠正他或她的错误。 虽然在时间和资源允许的情况下，返工错误可能是一种很好的培训方法，但它并不总是可行，也无法实现高效的日常流程。相反，通过目标设定和指标，员工可以更有效地承担责任，以实现整个组织的更大利益。
库存

库存的Muda与生产过剩的Muda类似；事实上，生产过剩会造成库存浪费。当材料或投入在流程的某个步骤之前堆积起来时，就会出现库存混乱；这种现象也称为瓶颈。还记得生产过剩的印刷例子吗？如果第一台机器设置为最高生产水平，每小时将生成 1,000 页。折页机每小时只能处理 800 页，因此每小时会创建 200 页的库存。如果两台机器以最高产量运行四个小时，该流程将生成 800 页的库存。除非折叠过程变得更快，否则这些额外的页面将被浪费，并将继续堆积。
当在制造或服务过程中需要物品之前购买或创建物品时，可能会发生库存浪费。库存还可以出现在工作队列、数字数据队列、甚至电子邮件收件箱中。如果您每天收到 300 封电子邮件，而您只定期处理其中 30 封，则说明您的通信流程存在问题。
虽然库存浪费可能发生在任何流程中，但在批量操作的流程中尤其常见。传统的精益智慧认为要避免批处理——涉及在将产品或输出推下生产线之前创建一定数量的产品或输出的过程。减少批量可以缩短交货时间——交付最终产品所需的时间。它还减少了流程每个步骤之前发生的库存量。
虽然精益思维通常会产生“批量化是不好的”心态，但您无法总是避免它，并且减少单个输出的交付时间并不总是流程的主要目标。假设一位面包师正在准备一打纸杯蛋糕。他可以批量准备订单，也可以单独准备每个纸杯蛋糕。显然，混合配料并批量烘烤纸杯蛋糕更有意义。 批量装饰它们也很有效——面包师可能会在所有纸杯蛋糕上涂上糖霜，在所有纸杯蛋糕上添加滚边，然后在所有纸杯蛋糕上放上糖果。面包师的配料工作
因为第一个纸杯蛋糕在第 12 个纸杯蛋糕完成之前不会离开面包店——它们都会一起移动，因为它们被视为一个整体。
您还可以通过了解流程并根据历史指标制定库存决策来减少库存浪费。每周处理 50,000 到 100,000 箱的运输中心如果只想拥有一周的供应量，就不会下 300,000 箱的订单。如果面包师只想做两个馅饼，他就不会为七个馅饼打奶油。
运动
运动的 Muda 与员工本身在流程中的移动方式有关。这种类型的浪费通常与制造、仓储、运输、交付或工业领域中的人力流程相关，但运动浪费甚至可能出现在计算机化的流程中。
例如，如果数据输入员工在输入信息时必须在屏幕之间来回单击，这可能是运动混乱。如果系统设计为在一个窗口中输入一个数字，然后在另一个窗口中输入第二个数字，那么窗口之间的点击就会浪费动作。移动到另一个窗口涉及鼠标或键盘操作，如果在同一窗口上输入数据，则可以消除这些操作。这似乎是一个无关紧要的细节，但想象一下当数据员工每天数百或数千次完成此过程时会发生什么。浪费绝对会增加：
如果每个数据输入员工平均每小时完成 600 个条目，并且团队中有 10 名员工，则该团队每天要完成 48,000 个条目。
如果在两个窗口之间切换只需要多花半秒，那么该团队每天仍然要花费总共 400 分钟（刚刚超过 6.5 小时）进行切换。
如果数据工作者的平均时薪为 10 美元，那么团队每周要额外花费 325 美元（每年近 17,000 美元）来支付窗口之间切换的费用。
鉴于这些数字，在同一窗口中包含这两个数据元素的编程更改可以在一年内为公司节省 17,000 美元。 当您可以将时间和金钱数字应用于废物时，同样的概念可以应用于任何形式的 Muda。

运动混乱的其他例子包括要求个人在工作之间来回移动的任务、由于工作布局不当而产生的额外运动，或者当员工离开某个区域并且由于忘记某件事而必须返回一次或多次时发生的运动。公共图书馆长期以来一直采用一种简单的工具来避免重新上架过程中的混乱：首先在购物车上订购书籍。推车允许员工携带许多书籍而无需来回移动，并且将书籍按顺序放在推车上可以让个人在书堆中移动一次。如果书籍被随意放置在推车上，则随后在书架之间的移动就会浪费动作和时间。
简化公司流程可以消除行动混乱，并且必须收集和分析数据以识别不必要的行动。在制造和类似环境中用于跟踪运动的常用工具称为意大利面条图。从工作区的基本鸟瞰图开始。包括家具、计算机站、机械、门和墙壁。观察实际过程，用图表上的线跟踪所有运动。
如果绘制正确，图表看起来就像一串意大利面条掉到了你的页面上。该过程完成后，您可以查看图表以了解运动在何处多次交叉或不必要地偏离路径。这可以帮助您找到简化流程中移动的机会 - 有时，就像移动家具或资源一样简单，以减少不必要的移动。值得注意的是，意大利面条图仅揭示了时间运动的快照；有时，在一天的不同时间或与不同的员工一起完成多个这样的图表是值得的，以揭示流程的整体情况和可能的 Muda。
运输
运输的 Muda 与移动的 Muda 类似，只不过运输涉及产出、产品或资源的移动。有时也称为运输的muda。例如，在玩偶制造工厂中，如果将玩偶眼睛和玩偶脸部粘合在一起的胶水保存在库存室中，并根据需要运送到流程中，则可能会出现运输混乱。
如果打印一份费用报告，然后交给经理批准，然后经理将其装在办公室内部的信封中，交给主管，然后主管再将其交给会计部门，那么就发生了运输混乱。尤其如此，因为正确有效地使用适当的技术可以让组织通过计算机处理昂贵的报告，而无需任何传输。
如果厨师准备好盘子并放在柜台上，厨房助理将盘子移到另一张桌子上，服务员知道要拿起它，那么就发生了传送。然后服务员把盘子端到顾客面前。
传送可以涉及物品的物理移动或数据或工作流程的数字移动。许多工作环境中都存在电子邮件字符串，通常会出现数字传输的混乱。首席执行官可能会向董事发送电子邮件，请求提供数据。主管向经理发送电子邮件，经理向主管发送电子邮件，主管向主题专家发送电子邮件。 SME 将信息传递给她的主管，然后电子邮件沿着链条返回。相同的请求和信息可能只需要传达一次，但它却被传达了多次。这会带来很多出错的机会。
有些人可能会提到指挥链问题，但就本示例而言，如果数据请求是一个重复的过程，那么指挥链有必要通知首席执行官此信息的来源。更进一步，真正的精益流程需要消除该流程中的所有浪费。如果数据要求每周一次，那么精益理想要求中小企业每周自动生成数据并将其发送给首席执行官。
物理运输通常比数字运输更容易定位和寻址。意大利面条图、流程图或价值流图可以帮助您识别可能存在运输混乱的区域。意大利面条图在物理运输情况下效果很好，流程图可以帮助您识别数字环境中的运输。

rows: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。
zh.text: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。
一旦识别出运输混乱，您可以通过更改工作的流程、布局或库存要求来消除它。如果运输废物不是由于不良的流程设计或工作区域布局造成的，那么它可能与另一种形式的 Muda 有关。 转移经常出现在涉及大量纠正的流程中，因为工作会在员工或区域之间来回转移。通过解决修正的误区，你通常也会解决传送的误区。
过度处理 当员工或流程向产品或服务投入的资源多于客户所重视的资源时，就会发生过度处理。这种情况的发生可能是因为无知、追求完美，甚至是兴奋。有时，过度处理的发生是因为员工没有接受过以最有效的方式处理任务的培训。其他时候，发生这种情况是因为员工或流程过于彻底，不值得。任何流程的目标都应该是做足够有用且必要的工作，以确保满足客户或最终用户的期望。
过度处理的一个例子经常发生在医疗保健行政办公室的保险验证过程中。当医疗保健提供者的办公室试图验证一个人是否拥有即将提供的服务的保险时，就会发生保险验证。根据保险范围的类型和办公室的政策，工作人员可以通过计算机程序检查福利或致电保险公司。

在大多数情况下，保险验证的目的只是确保被保险人在提供服务之日受到计划的保障。然而，有时，办公室工作人员会更深入地进行验证，花长达一个小时的时间与保险公司通电话以获取详细的福利，或回电与另一位代表核实以确保所提供的原始信息正确无误。虽然存在需要深入保险验证的特定情况，但基本的就诊不需要这种级别的工作。一名员工需要花费长达一个小时来核实保险范围，这已经是生产力过剩，而且可能会给整个办公室带来生产力问题。
在以消费者为中心的流程中，当您对产品的投入超过客户提供的价值时，就会发生过度加工。虽然产品卓越很重要，但在某些时候，您在产品中投入的工作超出了客户认为必要或有用的程度，而这通常与价格点相关。例如，客户对优质、更昂贵的型号有更多期望。
过度处理的一个例子可能是在没有人看到的情况下对柜内架子的底部进行涂漆。在餐馆里，将桌布熨烫到严格的标准可能会被认为是过度加工，特别是如果你在两餐之间将桌布取下来清洗的话。在大多数餐厅，大部分桌布都垂在桌子上。业主可能想要没有皱纹的亚麻布，但他们不需要清晰完美的接缝。在技​​术领域，当 99% 的人只想使用 10 个主要功能时，构建一个具有 100 个功能的应用程序可以被视为过度处理和过度生产。
第 35 章介绍的价值流图是识别过度加工点的好工具。流程中任何不提供价值的部分都可能被视为过度处理；当流程具有一系列相互关联的事件并且没有一个事件提供价值时，更有可能发生过度处理。
在真正的精益流程中，流程的每一步都提供价值，但确定何时没有价值可能很棘手。例如，质量对于流程的成功和最终客户都很重要，但客户并不关心，甚至通常不会意识到您的流程自始至终都充满了质量检查。相反，他们关心的是，由于这些质量检查，流程需要多花 10 分钟——团队必须深入研究流程、指标和客户声音，以确定这 10 分钟的额外时间是否提供了足够的附加值，以弥补因以下原因而造成的烦恼或客户流失：
增加的时间。这些知识都是通过本书详细讨论的数据收集和统计分析来确定的。
等待
等待的 Muda 是指流程中的任何空闲时间，无论该空闲时间是用于机器还是人员。换句话说，由于等待流程另一部分的输入，员工或机器正在低于容量工作或根本不工作。当流程中的步骤未正确协调、流程不可靠、工作批量太大、返工期间以及人员或机器之间的长时间转换期间，就会出现等待。
在零售或快餐环境中，当一名收银员轮班结束后，另一名收银员将接替收银员。 为了保持财务诚信，几乎所有公司都会在收银员换班时更换收银机抽屉；如果抽屉出现错误，公司就知道当时谁在运行收银机，从而更容易发现趋势或问题。然而，当更换抽屉时，收银机通常无法使用。收银员只是在等待，有时甚至和顾客一起等待。
为了减少此收银机示例中的等待混乱，大多数组织使用的流程包括在一天或轮班开始时准备好的一系列收银机抽屉。收银台抽屉设计为可互换，30秒内即可拉出旧抽屉，放入新抽屉，解决了等待的浪费。
等待的浪费在建筑环境中很常见。道路、桥梁、建筑物和其他结构的建设需要密切关注秩序和细节：在支柱就位并稳定之前，无法建造桥梁的道路。这种概念即使在小规模的建筑中也能看到，导致带薪员工经常站在那里等待其他人完成一项工作，然后才能完成分配的任务。

由于建筑是一个需要专家和认证的领域，一名员工通常无法完成另一名员工可以完成的工作。换句话说，员工的任务是不可互换的。这会导致额外的等待时间；一组建筑工人可能必须等待叉车操作员来移动一些物品。与此同时，叉车操作员正在施工场地的另一边完成一项任务。这不仅仅是建筑方面的问题：一些办公室制定了政策，要求正式 IT 工作人员处理任何计算机问题。无论是软件故障、故障排除错误，还是只是需要更换性能不佳的鼠标，普通办公室工作人员都必须发送支持票或拨打电话，等待 IT 人员解决问题。
您可以通过平衡机器、人员和生产来消除许多流程中的等待浪费。该过程的执行速度仅与最慢的链接一样快；加强单个元素的生产对整体没有任何帮助，因此团队必须努力平衡和改进整个过程。
有时，日程安排是消除等待的关键组成部分。 在施工示例中，先进的规划和调度策略可以减少团队在叉车操作员忙于其他地方时等待叉车操作员的机会。了解组织、团队、项目和流程的需求也有助于领导层提供适量的资源来减少等待。例如，建筑工地可能会受益于两名叉车操作员。在 IT 示例中，一家公司
可以通过在高峰时段最大限度地增加 IT 人员配置或在 IT 部门内实施流程来创建更有效的帮助请求响应来减少等待时间。可以聘请一名辅助 IT 人员来处理技术性较低的问题，例如更换性能不佳的鼠标。

en.text: While Toyota originally defined seven common muda, even Taiichi Ohno agreed that other types of waste exist. In some cases, what seems like other types of waste are just more defined or specific types of the seven muda discussed above. Some forms of waste don’t seem to fit neatly into one of the seven muda categories.
Talent

Talent can be wasted when a process doesn’t make the most use of the labor or staff available. If a process calls for data to be entered, and the staff member slowest on 10-key is assigned to the task, resources are being wasted. Hiring the wrong person, putting staff in the wrong position, or ignoring a staff member’s growth potential could all be instances of muda of talent. Wasted talent is more a concern for leadership and human resources than for process improvement specialists, though Six Sigma experts should be aware that the way personnel resources are handled can drastically impact the efficiency of a process.
Ideas

Muda of ideas occurs when the thoughts and ideas of people are discounted, not sought out, or misappropriated in a way that doesn’t make sense. Leadership and project teams can often overlook subject matter experts who have detailed insight into a process and, as such, could offer first-hand knowledge and ideas. The result could be the design of a process that works great in theory but falls flat on a granular level once it’s instituted.

One reason waste of ideas is such a concern for organizations is that people themselves rarely come forward with thoughts. Staff members might think they don’t have anything real to contribute, might feel like their ideas won’t be heard, or could be anxious about looking silly or ignorant.

Six Sigma Green or Black Belts in charge of projects can facilitate less muda of ideas by encouraging subject matter experts to contribute and encouraging leaders to seek all ideas before moving forward with change. Brainstorming tools, which are covered in later chapters, are valuable for this purpose because they are designed to create a safe haven for all ideas. By fostering all ideas in a safe environment, teams can foster valuable ideas and avoid waste of ideas.
Capital/Cash

Saving for a rainy day is smart, but banking cash when there are profits to gain isn’t always the right decision. Muda of capital or cash occurs when leadership decides not to invest in upgrades or improvements that would create additional cash flow. This type of waste is very similar to waiting, except the cash itself is waiting, often for a time when leadership feels safe enough to spend it.

Six Sigma helps reduce muda of cash because statistical analysis helps point leaders to decisions that involve the least risk or most gain. No business decision is 100 percent guaranteed, but Six Sigma helps leaders hedge bets by using statistical data in the decision-making process so they don’t sit on cash or capital that could be used to drive gains in efficiency, production, and profit.

zh.text: 虽然丰田最初定义了七种常见的废物，但就连大野耐一也同意存在其他类型的废物。在某些情况下，看似其他类型的废物只是上面讨论的七种废物中更明确或特定的类型。某些形式的废物似乎并不完全属于七个 Muda 类别之一。
人才
当流程没有充分利用可用的劳动力或员工时，人才可能会被浪费。如果某个流程要求输入数据，并且将 10 键操作最慢的员工分配给该任务，那么就会浪费资源。聘用错误的人、将员工放在错误的位置或忽视员工的成长潜力都可能是人才流失的例子。人才浪费更多的是领导层和人力资源而不是流程改进专家的担忧，尽管六西格玛专家应该意识到人力资源的处理方式会极大地影响流程的效率。
想法
当人们的思想和观点被低估、未被寻找或以一种没有意义的方式被盗用时，就会出现思想的 Muda。领导层和项目团队经常会忽视主题专家，他们对流程有详细的了解，因此可以提供第一手的知识和想法。结果可能是设计出一个理论上运行良好的流程，但一旦实施，在粒度层面上就会表现不佳。
组织如此关注浪费想法的原因之一是人们自己很少提出想法。 员工可能认为他们没有任何实际贡献，可能觉得他们的想法不会被听到，或者可能担心自己看起来愚蠢或无知。
六西格玛 负责项目的绿色或黑带人员可以通过鼓励主题专家贡献并鼓励领导者在推进变革之前寻求所有想法来减少想法的混乱。后面章节中介绍的头脑风暴工具对于此目的很有价值，因为它们旨在为所有想法创建一个安全港。通过在安全的环境中培育所有想法，团队可以培育有价值的想法并避免想法浪费。
资本/现金
未雨绸缪是明智之举，但当有利润可赚时存入现金并不总是正确的决定。当领导层决定不投资于能够创造额外现金流的升级或改进时，就会出现资本或现金的 Muda。这种类型的浪费与等待非常相似，只不过现金本身正在等待，通常是在领导层感到足够安全可以花掉它的时候。
六西格玛有助于减少现金浪费，因为统计分析可以帮助领导者做出风险最小或收益最大的决策。没有任何商业决策是百分百保证的，但六西格玛通过在决策过程中使用统计数据来帮助领导者对冲赌注，这样他们就不会坐拥可用于提高效率、产量和利润的现金或资本。

en.text: All muda is waste that fails to add value to a product or process as defined by the customer or end-user. All muda can also be divided into two overall types, which can help organizations prioritize waste for project and improvement purposes. Muda can be referred to as type I or type II.
Type I Muda

Type I muda are non-value-added tasks that might actually be essential or required by circumstances. Inspection of products during a process might be required if the process is known to produce defects. Organizations don’t want defects to reach customers, so they put quality controls in place. The act of the inspection, and the time and expense it adds to a process -- are all muda. However, an organization can’t remove that waste until it addresses the cause of the defects within the process.

Sometimes, auxiliary processes within an organization are Type I muda. For example, the external end customer doesn’t receive direct value from human resource processes within an organization. At the same time, if employees don’t receive pay checks or support regarding benefits, they aren’t likely to continue performing work. Those processes are then essential to the organization. Instead of removing the muda completely, teams might look for ways to make essential muda as efficient and cost-effective as possible.
Type II Muda

Type II muda are non-value-added tasks that are not essential and can be immediately removed from a process. For example, if a product is carried to and from several work stations while it is being completed, it’s likely type II muda of conveyance exists. By rearranging the workflow, teams might be able to reduce the muda by a substantial amount without making any actual changes to how the product is put together.
5S

5S is a Japanese Lean approach to organizing a workspace, so that by making a process more effective and efficient, it will become easier to identify and expunge muda. 5S relies on visual cues and a clean work area to enhance efficiencies, reduce accidents, and standardize workflows to reduce defects. The method is based on five steps:
Sort (Seiri)
Straighten (Seiton)
Shine (Seiso)
Standardize (Seiketsu)
Sustain (Shitsuke)
Phase I: Sort
During the sort phase, all items or materials in a workspace are reviewed, removing unneeded items and keeping necessary resources.

Consider the copy room in an office: over the years, supplies, tools, and machinery have piled up. When going through the room, teams might decide that the stapler and Scotch tape stay; people still need to staple pages or access tape. The old paper cutter isn’t necessary for the team, since no one ever performs paper-cutting duties. However, someone notes that the team in shipping and warehousing has to manually cut pages down sometimes, so the paper cutter is relocated to that department. The team decides to toss a bin of miscellaneous loose paper and an old fax machine because a new copy machine includes fax capability. By eliminating obstacles and unnecessary items, costs, time, and employee frustration are also removed.
The sort step lets you take inventory of an area, discover unused or wasted resources, and make room for reorganization. Sort can also be applied with computerized processes.
Phase 2: Straighten

Once excess is removed from the work area, teams must provide a streamlined and easy-to-use location for everything necessary to the workspace. During the straighten phase, every item, tool, or material is given a home. To facilitate ongoing organization, the location of resources should be labeled clearly. The idea is to create a workspace that anyone could use: if someone from another area comes to your copy room, it should only take a few seconds to locate the right size paper. Employees in a factory should be able to move from station to station, finding equipment and tools with ease. The goal is to provide the visual controls that allow for common-sense operation. Labeling a shelf for letter-sized paper, arranging sockets in size order in a drawer labeled sockets, or parking the forklift in a marked area of factory floor when not in use are all good examples. Labeling the stapler with a label maker is an example of things going beyond common sense: you don’t need to label items that most people in the workplace would recognize on site.

The straighten phase also works well in a digital environment, especially when computers or systems are used by a variety of people. In an office that has a shared workspace policy, computer desktops might be pushed out by technical resources so that the same programs are available to everyone. Not only are all programs the same, but the icons are in the same location on each desktop so users don’t have to search for programs if they move to a new workstation.
Phase 3: Shine

The third phase in the 5S methodology is targeted to keeping the workplace clean and neat. Seiso can also be translated to “sweep, sanitize, or scrub.” The goal is to shine the work space by cleaning it, maintaining equipment, and returning items to the proper place after use. In a computerized environment, the shine phase can be accomplished by naming files in a manner that makes them easy to locate, keeping folder structures intact, and deleting or archiving files that are no longer necessary.
Shine can be applied to any environment, physical or digital.
Phase 4: Standardize

The standardize phase is used to maintain the progress achieved in all previous phases. By keeping high standards of orderliness in place, the benefits of the 5S methodology can be long-term. The stress and speed of a daily workday can make it hard to keep up with the 5S standards. If everyone is committed to working together, the benefits can be ongoing.
Phase 5: Sustain

5S only works if everyone on the team or within the organization commits to the process. Employees must follow the rules that are set up for standardizing and sustaining the organization. Otherwise, the team enters a cycle of cleaning up after a period of failing to keep up with the standards of 5S.
The overall benefits the 5S method includes:
Reduced risks of accidents and safety issues
Increased compliance with regulations from organizations such as OSHA
A foundation that makes additional improvements easier to implement
Waste is easier to identify and eliminate
Production and quality are generally improved
All of these benefits translate to increased profits and customer satisfaction, which are the overall goals of the Six Sigma methodology.

zh.text: 所有 Muda 都是无法为客户或最终用户定义的产品或流程增加价值的浪费。所有 Muda 还可以分为两种总体类型，这可以帮助组织优先考虑项目和改进目的的浪费。 Muda 可称为 I 型或 II 型。
I型穆达
I 类 Muda 是非增值任务，实际上可能是必要的或环境所需要的。如果已知流程会产生缺陷，则可能需要在流程中对产品进行检查。组织不希望客户收到缺陷，因此他们实施了质量控制。检查的行为，以及它给流程增加的时间和费用——都是 Muda。然而，在解决流程中缺陷的原因之前，组织无法消除这种浪费。
有时，组织内的辅助流程是 I 型 muda。 例如，外部最终客户不会从组织内的人力资源流程中获得直接价值。与此同时，如果员工没有收到工资或福利支持，他们就不太可能继续工作。这些流程对于组织来说至关重要。团队可能会寻找方法使基本的 Muda 尽可能高效且具有成本效益，而不是完全消除 Muda。
II型穆达
II 类 Muda 是非增值任务，不是必需的，可以立即从流程中删除。例如，如果产品在完成过程中往返于多个工作站，则很可能存在 II 类运输误区。通过重新安排工作流程，团队可能能够大幅减少混乱，而无需对产品的组合方式进行任何实际更改。
5S
5S 是一种日本精益组织工作空间的方法，通过使流程更加有效和高效，可以更轻松地识别和消除 Muda。 5S 依靠视觉提示和干净的工作区域来提高效率、减少事故并标准化工作流程以减少缺陷。该方法基于五个步骤：
排序（整理）
拉直（整顿）
闪耀 (Seiso)
标准化（雪洁）
维持（Shitsuke）
第一阶段：排序
在排序阶段，将审查工作区中的所有项目或材料，删除不需要的项目并保留必要的资源。

想象一下办公室里的复印室：多年来，用品、工具和机械堆积如山。当穿过房间时，团队可能会决定留下订书机和透明胶带；人们仍然需要装订页面或访问磁带。团队不需要旧的切纸机，因为没有人执行剪纸任务。不过，有人指出，运输和仓储团队有时需要手动裁切页面，因此切纸机被转移到该部门。该团队决定扔掉一箱杂乱的纸张和一台旧传真机，因为新复印机具有传真功能。通过消除障碍和不必要的物品，成本、时间和员工的挫败感也随之消除。
排序步骤可让您清点区域、发现未使用或浪费的资源，并为重组腾出空间。排序也可以应用于计算机化流程。
第二阶段：拉直

一旦从工作区域移除多余的东西，团队必须为工作空间所需的一切提供一个精简且易于使用的位置。在拉直阶段，每个物品、工具或材料都有一个归宿。为了便于持续组织，应清楚地标记资源的位置。我们的想法是创建一个任何人都可以使用的工作空间：如果来自其他地区的人来到您的复印室，只需几秒钟即可找到合适尺寸的纸张。工厂里的员工应该能够从一个工作站移动到另一个工作站，轻松找到设备和工具。目标是提供允许常识性操作的视觉控制。在信纸尺寸的架子上贴上标签，在抽屉中按尺寸顺序排列插座并贴上插座标签，或者在不使用时将叉车停放在工厂车间的标记区域，都是很好的例子。使用标签机在订书机上贴标签是一个超越常识的例子：您不需要为工作场所中大多数人在现场都能认出的物品贴上标签。
整顿阶段在数字环境中也很有效，尤其是当计算机或系统由不同的人使用时。在具有共享工作区策略的办公室中，计算机桌面可能会被技术资源推出，以便每个人都可以使用相同的程序。不仅所有程序都相同，而且图标位于每个桌面上的相同位置，因此用户在移动到新工作站时无需搜索程序。
第三阶段：闪耀
5S 方法的第三阶段旨在保持工作场所干净整洁。 Seiso 也可以翻译为“清扫、消毒或擦洗”。目标是通过清洁工作空间、维护设备以及在使用后将物品放回正确的位置来使工作空间焕然一新。在计算机化环境中，可以通过以易于查找的方式命名文件、保持文件夹结构完整以及删除或归档不再需要的文件来完成闪耀阶段。
Shine 可以应用于任何环境，无论是物理环境还是数字环境。
第四阶段： 标准化
标准化阶段用于维护之前所有阶段取得的进展。通过保持高标准的有序性，5S 方法的好处可以是长期的。日常工作日的压力和速度可能会让人很难跟上 5S 标准。如果每个人都致力于共同努力，那么好处就会持续存在。
第五阶段：维持
仅当团队或组织内的每个人都致力于该流程时，5S 才能发挥作用。员工必须遵守为标准化和维持组织而制定的规则。否则，团队在一段时间未能达到5S标准后，就会进入清理循环。
5S 方法的总体优势包括：
降低事故和安全问题的风险
提高对 OSHA 等组织法规的遵守程度
使额外改进更容易实施的基础
废物更容易识别和消除
产量和质量普遍提高
所有这些好处都转化为利润的增加和客户满意度的提高，这是六西格玛方法论的总体目标。

en.text: Just-in-Time manufacturing, or JIT, is another Lean concept that originated with Toyota. Originally, JIT took a literal meaning. The goal of JIT manufacturing was to produce an output “just in time,” or “as needed” by the customer. The customer was the person or process that required the output; sometimes, that meant the end customers, and, other times, the customer was a different employee or process within the organization.

In a JIT processing situation, one machine might produce a part required by another machine. JIT manufacturing means that the first machine supplies only the amount of parts that the second machine can process. If the second machine can process one part per minute, the first machine is set to produce one part per minute. You’ll recognize this idea from the sections on muda of inventory and overproduction.

It’s obviously not always possible to run a process just-in-time for the end customer, but most modern companies do try to come as close as possible. Using predictive analytics, companies attempt to estimate how many of each product will sell before they produce those products. In some cases, such as with book publishing, companies run a smaller number of items first. If those items sell well or sell out, the company orders bigger and bigger runs of the product.

Some entrepreneurs have applied JIT manufacturing to DVD sales. These individuals and companies buy the rights to films that have never been released on DVD or Blu-ray before. They usually go after low- cost films that are likely to have a small cult or niche following. Because the demand is small, it isn’t feasible to print and market these DVDs in traditional fashion. However, the companies make a stable profit by selling the DVDs through online retailers such as Amazon.com and printing the DVDs to order. Modern technology lets this process occur with minimal expense and waiting.

Today, JIT mentalities are less about the literal idea of providing the product just in time; rather, it has become a more general concept of Lean manufacturing that helps organizations eliminate waste in the process.
Lean Concepts Crop Up in Many Improvement Methodologies

We’ve covered many of the high level Lean concepts, and you’ll see some of these concepts repeat throughout the rest of this book. While Six Sigma is concerned with improving processes and reducing defects, eliminating waste and increasing efficiencies goes hand-in-hand with these goals.

zh.text: 准时化制造（JIT）是起源于丰田的另一个精益概念。最初，JIT 是字面意思。 JIT 制造的目标是“及时”或“根据客户的需要”生产产品。客户是需要输出的人或过程；有时，这意味着最终客户，而有时，客户是组织内的不同员工或流程。
在 JIT 加工情况下，一台机器可能生产另一台机器所需的零件。 JIT 制造意味着第一台机器仅提供第二台机器可以加工的零件数量。如果第二台机器每分钟可以加工一个零件，则第一台机器设置为每分钟生产一个零件。您将从有关库存和生产过剩的 Muda 部分中认识到这个想法。
显然，为最终客户及时运行流程并不总是可能的，但大多数现代公司确实试图尽可能接近。使用预测分析，公司试图在生产这些产品之前估计每种产品的销量。 在某些情况下，例如图书出版，公司首先运行较少数量的项目。如果这些产品卖得好或卖光了，公司就会订购越来越多的产品。
一些企业家已将JIT制造应用于DVD销售。这些个人和公司购买以前从未以 DVD 或蓝光形式发行的电影的版权。他们通常追求低成本的电影，这些电影可能有少量的狂热或小众追随者。由于需求量很小，以传统方式印刷和销售这些 DVD 是不可行的。然而，这些公司通过亚马逊等在线零售商销售 DVD 并按订单印刷 DVD 来获得稳定的利润。现代技术让这一过程以最少的费用和等待进行。
如今，JIT 心态已不再是“及时提供产品”的字面意思；而是“准时”。相反，它已经成为精益制造的一个更普遍的概念，帮助组织消除过程中的浪费。
精益概念出现在许多改进方法中
我们已经介绍了许多高级精益概念，您将看到其中一些概念在本书的其余部分中重复出现。虽然六西格玛关注改进流程和减少缺陷，但消除浪费和提高效率与这些目标密切相关。

title.zh: 第 5 章：六西格玛基础概念

source.extraction: python-docx body-order extraction; source-TOC sectionized bilingual content

en.text: In the last chapter, we covered some of the major concepts associated with Lean. In this chapter, we’ll look at some of the major concepts of the Six Sigma methodology. These, along with the concepts introduced in Chapter 1, are some of the building blocks used in improvement projects and statistical process control.

zh.text: 在上一章中，我们介绍了与精益相关的一些主要概念。在本章中，我们将探讨六西格玛方法论的一些主要概念。这些以及第一章中介绍的概念是改进项目和统计过程控制中使用的一些构建块。

rows: 术语说明 SPC（统计过程控制）：用统计方法监控过程表现，区分普通波动和异常信号。
zh.text: 术语说明 SPC（统计过程控制）：用统计方法监控过程表现，区分普通波动和异常信号。
标准差

en.text: The driving goal of Six Sigma is to reduce defects. By reducing defects, teams can increase productivity, decrease overall costs, increase customer satisfaction, and create maximum profit. One idea inherent in the Six Sigma methodology is that variance is the root of many defects.

For example, if an oven heats to exactly 350 degrees in five minutes and stays at that temperature until it is turned off, it is less likely to burn cookies. If a cook measures each ingredient exactly, he or she is more likely to turn out cookies that consistently taste good. Add variation in the process, and consistency is lost. When consistency is lost, defects are introduced. If the oven doesn’t maintain an exact temperature all the time, the cookies might burn. If the cook puts in a cup of sugar instead of a cup and a half, the cookies might not be sweet enough. Variation makes for inconsistent quality.

It’s important to note that removing variation alone doesn’t always improve quality. What if the cook set the oven to 400 degrees all the time and only used half a cup of sugar for each batch? The process has no variation, and neither do the results. The cookies will always be bland and burnt.

Six Sigma process improvement teams usually take a two-step approach to improvements. First, they have to determine if the process is functional. In the cookie example, does the recipe work at all? Is there even a recipe? Once the team determines there is a workable recipe, they make improvements to remove the variation that causes outputs to deviate from the result intended by the recipe.

The statistical measure used by teams to understand variation in a process is known as standard deviation. Standard deviation is represented in math by the lower case Greek letter Sigma – the σ you saw in Chapter 1.

Standard deviation measures the distance between data points and the mean of all data. A large standard deviation means an overall wide spread of points; a smaller standard deviation means a closely clustered set of points.

The image above provides a graphical representation of deviation. Imagine the vertical axis is a measure of time and the horizontal axis is a measure of temperature. The center line in each image represents the mean temperature. You can see that the temperature over time varies much more in the figure on the right.
Calculating Standard Deviation for Population Data
Standard deviation is a statistical concept. The formula for standard deviation when dealing with data of the entire population is:

You would use this formula if you have all the data elements of a population and not just a random sampling of data elements. For example, if you wanted to find out what the deviation was in the size of pizzas made, you could ask staff to measure each pizza before serving it. You would have the data for the entire population of pizzas for the day, so you could use the equation above. However, if you wanted to calculate standard deviation when you have sample data, you would use the equation from the next section.

If you’re new to statistics, the equation for standard deviation looks complicated. We’ll break it down and run through some exercises on calculating standard deviation manually, but in practice, you will usually use a statistical software tool to make this calculation automatically.
For our explanation, we’ll use a data set from a teacher. She wants to find the standard deviation of scores on the latest test. The scores from her class of 15 students are:
1. Calculate the mean.
To begin the standard deviation calculation, you need to know the mean for the population. The mean is
represented mathematically by the Greek letter mu, or μ. Mean is calculated by adding all of the numbers and dividing that sum by the number of items in a data set. In this case, there are 15 items.
2. Subtract the mean and square it.
The formula calls for you to take each number in the data set, subtract the mean from it, and square the result. The first number is 67, so:
If you apply that concept to all 15 numbers, you end up with a list of results:

60.84 77.44 163.84 190.44 3. Find the mean of the results. The rest of the formula under the square root sign simply tells you to add up all the numbers you just calculated and divide by N, where N is the number of items in your data set. Or, to put it another way, you need to find the mean of the new numbers you just calculated. The sum of the numbers above is 1496.4. 1496.4 / 15 = 99.76 This new number, 99.76, is called the variance. 4. Find the square root of the variance. The standard deviation is the square root of the variance. In this case, the square root of 99.76, which is 9.987. The standard deviation for the test scores is 9.987.
Calculating Standard Deviation with Sample Data

While statistics based on total population data are always more accurate than those based on sample data, you’ll probably work from sample data more often. It just becomes too expensive or even impossible to get population data for many elements. Sometimes, the data is measuring events or states over time, which means population data doesn’t exist. For example, if you wanted to understand temperature fluctuations in a warehouse, you might record the temperature at a certain location every ten minutes. After several days, you have sufficient sample data to analyze. Other examples of sample data include:
A random sample of reasons for denied medical claims
Measurements for river height taken three times per day for a month
The formula for standard deviation based on sample data is:
𝑁

Since mu is the mean of population data, it’s been replaced in this formula with x-bar, which is the average of the data points in your sample. Sigma has been replaced with s, but the only mathematical difference is that you divide by N-1 instead of N to get the variance as a way to make up for some of the accuracy lost in using a sampling.

Using the same data from the population example above, let’s assume that the 15 grades the teacher had were a random sampling from all of her classes. The only difference in the math would come in the second to last step, where we divide by 14 instead of 15, so:
1496.4 / 14 = 106.885 The square root of 106.885 is 10.338, which would be the standard deviation for the sample.
Standard Deviation in Excel

Admittedly, if you’re calculating standard deviation by hand, it’s a lot of arithmetic. Luckily, once the statistical concepts behind the numbers are understood, statistical analysis software, such as Excel and Minitab, can be used to accurately crunch numbers. Standard deviation is automatically calculated in most statistical analysis software programs by clicking a button after you enter your data sets. The standard deviation is also calculated automatically by such software programs when you initiate other calculations that require standard deviation. We’ll look at some of these functions more in-depth in the chapters on using Excel add-ons and Minitab for statistical analysis.
In the meantime, you can quickly calculate standard deviation in Excel using the standard deviation function. To do so:
1. Enter your data set in a column
2. In a new cell, enter =STDEV()
3. Select the cells with data you want to calculate standard deviation for.
4. Hit enter
Note: The formula in Excel calculates a sample standard deviation using the N-1 math, which means you can use this formula for samples and not for populations.
Why Calculate Standard Deviation?

Standard deviation gives you an idea of how much variation actually exists in a process while taking outliers somewhat into account. In the example of the grades from above, the sample standard deviation indicates that most of the grades are going to fall within 10.33 points on either side of the average.

That tells the teacher that students have a fairly wide performance on her test. If the results were an average score of 90 with a standard deviation of 3, he or she might assume that students in class were

learning and retaining the knowledge as expected. If the average score was 64 with a standard deviation of 2, then he or she might assume students in class were not retaining the knowledge as expected or there was some issue with the test structure. Both of these situations indicate a small variance in the way students are performing, which points to the success or problem being tied to the class, the teaching, or the test.

On the other hand, if the average score was 60 with a standard deviation of 30, then some students were performing very well while others were performing poorly. This might indicate to the teacher that some students are falling behind. If he or she took samples from several classes, he or she might investigate and realize that the lowest scores were mostly from one class, which could indicate that he or she forgot to adequately cover a certain concept in that class.

Standard deviation alone serves as a pointer for where to investigate within the process for problems or solutions. Another reason to calculate it is because it is involved in many of the other statistical processes we cover in later chapters. Standard deviation becomes an important concept in both analysis and statistical process control and often serves as the starting point for further Statistical Six Sigma analysis.

rows: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
zh.text: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
六西格玛的驱动目标是减少缺陷。通过减少缺陷，团队可以提高生产力、降低总体成本、提高客户满意度并创造最大利润。六西格玛方法论中固有的一个想法是，差异是许多缺陷的根源。
例如，如果烤箱在五分钟内精确加热到 350 度，并保持在该温度直到关闭，则烧焦饼干的可能性较小。如果厨师准确地测量每种成分，他或她更有可能制作出味道始终如一的美味饼干。在过程中增加变化，就会失去一致性。当失去一致性时，就会引入缺陷。如果烤箱不能始终保持准确的温度，饼干可能会烧焦。如果厨师放了一杯糖而不是一杯半，饼干可能不够甜。变化导致质量不一致。
值得注意的是，仅消除变异并不总能提高质量。如果厨师始终将烤箱设置为 400 度，并且每批只使用半杯糖怎么办？过程没有变化，结果也没有变化。饼干总是乏味且烧焦。
六西格玛流程改进团队通常采取两步法进行改进。首先，他们必须确定该流程是否正常运行。在饼干的例子中，这个食谱真的有用吗？有食谱吗？一旦团队确定存在可行的配方，他们就会进行改进，以消除导致输出偏离配方预期结果的变化。
团队用来了解过程变化的统计度量称为标准偏差。标准差在数学中用小写希腊字母 Sigma 表示——您在第 1 章中看到的 σ。
标准差衡量数据点之间的距离和所有数据的平均值。标准差大意味着点总体分布广泛； 较小的标准差意味着一组紧密聚集的点。
上图提供了偏差的图形表示。想象一下，垂直轴是时间的度量，水平轴是温度的度量。每幅图像的中心线代表平均温度。您可以在右图中看到温度随时间的变化更大。
计算总体数据的标准差

rows: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
标准差是一个统计概念。处理总体数据时的标准差公式为：
如果您拥有总体的所有数据元素而不仅仅是数据元素的随机抽样，则可以使用此公式。例如，如果您想了解制作的披萨尺寸的偏差有多大，您可以要求工作人员在提供披萨之前测量每个披萨的尺寸。您将获得当天所有披萨的数据，因此您可以使用上面的等式。但是，如果您想在有样本数据时计算标准差，则可以使用下一节中的公式。

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
如果您是统计学新手，标准差方程看起来会很复杂。我们将对其进行分解并进行一些手动计算标准差的练习，但在实践中，您通常会使用统计软件工具来自动进行此计算。
为了进行解释，我们将使用老师提供的数据集。她想要找到最新测试分数的标准差。她班上 15 名学生的成绩是：
1. 计算平均值。
要开始计算标准差，您需要知道总体的平均值。平均值是
在数学上用希腊字母 mu 或 μ 表示。平均值是通过将所有数字相加并将总和除以数据集中的项目数来计算的。在本例中，有 15 个项目。
2. 减去平均值并平方。
该公式要求您获取数据集中的每个数字，从中减去平均值，并对结果求平方。第一个数字是 67，所以：
如果您将该概念应用于所有 15 个数字，您最终会得到一个结果列表：

60.84 77.44 163.84 190.44 3. 求结果的平均值。平方根符号下的公式的其余部分只是告诉您将刚刚计算的所有数字相加并除以 N，其中 N 是数据集中的项目数。或者，换句话说，您需要找到刚刚计算的新数字的平均值。上述数字的总和是 1496.4。 1496.4 / 15 = 99.76 这个新数字 99.76 称为方差。 4. 求方差的平方根。标准差是方差的平方根。在本例中，99.76 的平方根为 9.987。测试分数的标准差为 9.987。
使用样本数据计算标准差
虽然基于总人口数据的统计数据总是比基于样本数据的统计数据更准确，但您可能会更频繁地使用样本数据。获取许多元素的人口数据变得过于昂贵，甚至不可能。有时，数据是随着时间的推移测量事件或状态，这意味着人口数据不存在。例如，如果您想了解仓库中的温度波动，您可能每十分钟记录一次特定位置的温度。几天后，您就有了足够的样本数据可供分析。样本数据的其他示例包括：
拒绝医疗索赔原因的随机样本
一个月内每天测量三次河流高度
基于样本数据的标准差的公式为：
𝑁
由于 mu 是总体数据的平均值，因此在此公式中将其替换为 x-bar，即样本中数据点的平均值。 Sigma 已替换为 s，但唯一的数学差异是除以 N-1 而不是 N 来获得方差，以此弥补使用采样时损失的一些精度。

rows: 术语说明 X-Bar Chart（均值图）：监控样本均值随时间变化的控制图。
zh.text: 术语说明 X-Bar Chart（均值图）：监控样本均值随时间变化的控制图。
使用上面人口示例中的相同数据，我们假设老师的 15 个成绩是从她所有班级中随机抽样的。 数学上的唯一区别在于倒数第二步，我们除以 14 而不是 15，因此：
Excel 中的标准差

诚然，如果您手动计算标准差，则需要进行大量算术运算。幸运的是，一旦理解了数字背后的统计概念，就可以使用 Excel 和 Minitab 等统计分析软件来准确地处理数字。在大多数统计分析软件程序中，输入数据集后单击按钮即可自动计算标准差。当您启动需要标准差的其他计算时，此类软件程序也会自动计算标准差。我们将在有关使用 Excel 插件和 Minitab 进行统计分析的章节中更深入地介绍其中一些函数。
同时，您可以使用Excel中的标准差函数快速计算标准差。为此：
1. 在列中输入您的数据集
2. 在新单元格中，输入 =STDEV()
3. 选择包含要计算标准差的数据的单元格。
4. 按回车键
注意：Excel 中的公式使用 N-1 数学计算样本标准差，这意味着您可以将此公式用于样本而不是总体。
为什么要计算标准差？
标准偏差可以让您了解过程中实际存在多少变化，同时在一定程度上考虑异常值。在上面的成绩示例中，样本标准差表明大多数成绩将落在平均值两侧的 10.33 分之内。
这告诉老师学生在她的测试中表现相当广泛。如果结果是平均分 90 分，标准差为 3，他或她可能会认为班上的学生是
按预期学习并保留知识。如果平均分数为 64，标准差为 2，那么他或她可能会认为课堂上的学生没有按预期保留知识，或者测试结构存在一些问题。 这两种情况都表明学生的表现存在微小差异，这表明成功或问题与课程、教学或测试有关。
另一方面，如果平均分是 60，标准差是 30，那么有些学生表现很好，而另一些学生表现很差。这可能向老师表明有些学生落后了。如果他或她从多个班级中抽取样本，他或她可能会调查并意识到最低分数大部分来自一个班级，这可能表明他或她忘记充分涵盖该班级中的某个概念。
标准偏差本身可以作为在流程中调查问题或解决方案的位置的指针。计算它的另一个原因是因为它涉及我们在后面的章节中介绍的许多其他统计过程。标准差成为分析和统计过程控制中的重要概念，并且常常作为进一步统计六西格玛分析的起点。

en.text: The Pareto principle, also called the 80/20 rule, says that 20 percent of the causes lead to 80 percent of the effects. This there is also called the law of the vital few: the vital few inputs drive the majority of the outputs.

The Pareto principle was first suggested by a management consultant named Joseph Juran. Juran named the principle for Vilfredo Pareto, an economist in Italy who wrote that 20 percent of the nation’s people owned 80 percent of its land. The principle has become common in various circles. Business professionals commonly state that 80 percent of sales come from 20 percent of customers, and volunteer organizations usually operate with 20 percent of the people doing 80 percent of the work.

The principle is critical to Six Sigma not because causes and effects line up nicely via an 80/20 breakdown, but because it almost universally applies that a few inputs create more impact than all of the other inputs. Individuals seeking to reduce defects can almost always identify three to four inputs that, if improved, will create dramatic impact on the outcome. While resources, costs, and difficulty of improvements also play a role in solution selections, understanding which inputs or root causes are high on a Pareto chart let project teams determine where improvements will make the biggest impact to the bottom line.

The Pareto principle is best displayed using a Pareto chart, which is a graphical representation of data elements – usually inputs or causes – in a ranked bar chart. Unlike a regular bar chart, the bars are arranged in order of height, with the highest on the left and the lowest on the right. Statistical software used to create such charts adds formatting and other elements automatically, but you can also create a basic Pareto chart in Excel.

To illustrate the Pareto principle, we’ll look at a common situation involving defects in the medical field—specifically in the process for submitting medical claims. Payers often deny claims, and they do so for a variety of reasons. When claims are denied, provider offices have to rework, resubmit, or appeal the denials. Some denial reasons are not appealable, which means the provider’s office loses the revenue associated with the claim.

We’ll imagine a medical office that is experiencing a cash flow problem because of claim denials. The office gathers data about the denials and creates a Pareto chart so the team can see where the bulk of the denials are coming from. The data is listed below, followed by a basic Pareto chart created in Excel.

Reasons for Denying Medical Claims Reason Count Duplicate claim 18012 Timely Filing 13245 No beneficiary found 10215 Claim lacks information 4548 Service not covered 2154 Medical necessity 1423 Date of service issue 526
Reasons for claim denials
Duplicate claim Timely Filing No beneficiary
Service not
Date of service
Claim lacks information
Medical
necessity
found
covered
issue

From the Pareto chart, you can see that the top three denial reasons account for 80 percent of the denied claims. An experienced billing team could tell you three things just from looking at this data:

1. The office has muda of rework. They are sending a large percentage of claims more than one time. 2. The office has an efficiency problem. Almost a fourth of their claims are not making it to the payer prior to timely filing deadlines.
3. The office has an insurance verification problem, because a fifth of their claims are being sent with information that doesn’t match anything on the payer’s end.

Addressing duplicate claims is important because it reduces rework and could enhance the office’s relationship with insurance companies. However, the team might choose to work on the timely filing problem first because timely filing denials are final, which means the office is losing the revenue associated with all those claims. Filing claims on time is not difficult in many cases, given the fact that most payers allow months or even a year for claims to be filed, so this could be an “easy” win for the team. A Pareto chart often uncovers low-hanging fruit in this manner.
Creating a Basic Pareto Chart in Excel
If you don’t have statistical software, you can create a basic Pareto chart like the one above in Excel. Use the claims denial data or data of your own to practice making a Pareto chart.

1. Create a column for the data labels. Pareto charts work well when you have quantifiable causes for a defect or other effect. In the example, the data labels are the reason for the denial. No matter what type of data you are using, enter it in order from largest to smallest for Pareto chart purposes. 2. Create a column for count. Enter the total for each cause in that column. 3. Create a column for cumulative count. This column provides a running total. You can calculate the numbers manually or using Excel. In the data table below, you would set C3 = B3. In C4, you would enter the formula =C3+B4. You can drag that formula down and Excel will change the references for each cell so you get =C4+B5, =C5+B6…and so forth. 4. Create a column for percent. In the data table below, the formula for D3 is =B3/$C$9. Cell C9 has the total of all denials, so we want to divide each individual denial total by C9. The dollar signs in the formula let you copy it into each lower cell. The first reference will change, moving to the next line, but the dollar signs tell Excel to keep the C9 reference for each calculation. The final result is a table that looks like this:

Reason Count Cumulative Percent Duplicate claim 18012 18012 35.9% Timely Filing 13245 31257 26.4% No beneficiary found 10215 41472 20.4% Claim lacks information 4548 46020 9.1% Service not covered 2154 48174 4.3% Medical necessity 1423 49597 2.8% Date of service issue 526 50123 1.0%
5. To create the Pareto chart, highlight the information in both the Reason and Percent column and select Insert  Chart  Bar chart.
6. The bar chart will be created automatically. Select Add Chart Element  Trendline, and add either an exponential or linear trendline.
7. Select Add Chart Element  Data Labels, and select the format of data label you prefer for your chart.
Use of Pareto Charts

Pareto charts are helpful analytical tools when you need to analyze frequencies or causes of problems. They also help narrow an approach for a problem that has many causes or is too broad to address in a single improvement project. Like the claims denials example above, you can find a single cause to work on that can yield large results across the entire process.

Pareto charts are also helpful when communicating information about causes to others, especially those outside of the Six Sigma process. Although Pareto charts are a powerful analytical tool, they also represent complex data in a visual format that is familiar to most anyone. Business professionals know how to read a bar chart, and putting the chart in order only makes it easier for individuals to see the true causes behind issues. For this reason, many Six Sigma experts regularly include Pareto charts when presenting to business leaders and others, especially if the data might be considered surprising or need visual reinforcement.

Voice of the Customer Voice of the Customer, or VOC, is a foundational concept in many quality programs. The goal of quality is to make a better, more consistent product. One of the ways you know you’ve reached this goal is that your customers will be more consistently satisfied. The only way to reach this goal is to seek feedback from the customer, making VOC data critical to collect before, during, and after improvement projects.

Successful VOC programs are proactive and constant in their desire for feedback, and technology makes it possible to seek customer feedback in numerous ways. Some methods for capturing feedback include:
Surveys via telephone, mail, email, or online
Focus groups in person or online
Interviews
Beta or user testing
Feedback forms
Customer complaints
Social media or site interaction
Reviews
Forums
The VOC can be sought as a means to clarify needs and desires, clarify specific problems with a process, or as a regular part of improvement, customer service, and marketing agendas.
Building a VOC Campaign

Asking the right questions, in the right way, helps you create powerful VOC campaigns that provide useable data for Six Sigma teams. We’ll talk about two specific types of VOC campaigns in this section: general customer feedback and specific customer feedback.
General Feedback

General customer feedback is often obtained through feedback forms, customer complaint records, and passive information gathering via websites or social media. Through such methods, organizations are usually testing general waters to get a temperature reading: are customers happy overall, dissatisfied overall, and is there any direction as to the cause of customer feelings?
Pick up a feedback form in any fast food restaurant or access the online survey usually linked to on a receipt and you’ll get a good idea of the type of information sought in general VOC campaigns.

Kroger, a grocery store chain in the United States, includes a link to a survey on most of its receipts. The survey first asks for the date, time, and an entry code from the receipt. This helps the company know where and when a person shopped so they can attribute feedback to the right location and staff.
Next, the Kroger survey asks in which areas of the store a person shopped. The rest of the survey asks specific questions about each area of the store a person visited, including:
What was the overall satisfaction with the store?
What was the customer’s satisfaction with:
o Employee friendliness o Prices o Service o Cleanliness o Items being available o Weekly specials o Ease of movement o Quality of brands
o Check out times
Whether the shopper is likely to recommend the store to another person in the next 30 days.

These questions are designed to gauge general customer feelings on critical quality elements for the store. Understanding your critical to quality factors, or CTQs, is important to designing a strong VOC campaign. We’ll cover CTQs more in depth in Chapter 8.

General VOC feedback is often used as a smoke alarm. A smoke alarm is designed to alert individuals in a business, home, or other building that the possibility of a fire exists. Smoke alarms are set at a sensitive level, so they go off when smoke is present and people within the building can take action. Often, the alarm and early action saves lives and can even reduce damage associated with a potential fire.

VOC data can work the same way. If numbers change suddenly in a certain area, an organization knows to look deeper into the issue. It’s an indicator that a problem could exist; early investigation and action can help prevent problems from becoming bigger or more costly. For example, if a certain Kroger store always scored high in cleanliness, and the numbers dropped consistently across a month, then store management might need to revisit maintenance and cleaning training.

Results from general VOC feedback are also used in some organizations as an indicator of quality for certain employees. Sales and services staff are often rewarded financially and in other ways for high customer satisfaction scores. This also increases employee drive and satisfaction.
Specific Feedback

Sometimes, organizations want feedback that is specific to a problem, product, or idea. The same tools used in general feedback campaigns can be used in specific campaigns, but you can also tailor the VOC tool to the need. If you want feedback about a new app, you could use a beta test. If you want to test a product, idea, or marketing campaign, an in-person or online focus group might be best.

For specific feedback, you have to ask specific questions. This is especially true if you are seeking additional information or clarification of general feedback. For example, if Kroger did see a problem with ratings on cleanliness, it might want more information about how and where customers note uncleanliness. Without additional feedback, managers might have staff mopping the floors more when customers really felt the store was dirty because of a lack of lighting or because shelves were stocked in a sloppy manner.
Selecting the Right VOC Tools

Getting the right type of feedback—and keeping costs and timelines within budget—requires selecting the right VOC tool for your project. The table below rates each tool on relative cost and provides some brief pros and cons.
Tool
Cost Benefits Disadvantages
Feedback form Low -Gathers a lot of data from many sources
-An individual must decide to leave feedback, skewing results to people who feel strongly one way or the other
-Can be geared toward numeric data for easier analysis
-Requires a lot of labor hours
Survey via phone High -Can randomly select, which allows you to draw conclusions for the entire population
-Customers may be annoyed by unwanted phone calls
Survey via mail Medium -Can randomly select, which allows you to draw conclusions for the entire population.
-The customer must send it back for it to count. Because many people won’t do so, you have to send more surveys to get a statistical sampling.
-Lower cost alternative to phone or in-person surveys.
Social media Low -Ongoing ability to seek feedback.
-Requires an established social media following.
-Ability to ask questions on the fly.
-Relies on followers and fans, which means you are asking for feedback from people who already favor your brand in some way.
-Possibly the least expensive option for VOC.
Focus groups in person High -Lets moderators seek more in-depth answers or feedback immediately

-Limits data pool to local customers or those willing to travel. -Can’t use data to make assumptions about the general population. -Customers may be less inclined to be honest when face-to-face with surveyors
-Can’t use data to make assumptions about general population.
Focus groups online Low -Lets moderators seek more in-depth answers or feedback immediately -Doesn’t require travel
and you can access customers across the globe
-Takes time and requires experienced users or testers.
User or beta testing High -Provides feedback about a specific produce, service, or process.
The Likert Scale

When designing your own VOC tool, keep in mind how you intend to use the information gained. If you want to input data into statistical analysis software to test hypothesis or create visual charts, then you need to ask questions that yield actual data points that can be analyzed using statistics. A popular way to do this is with a Likert Scale.
Using a Likert Scale, you would frame all questions so they are answered via a 5-point ranking. The ranking can be any number of things, but most commonly is some variation of:
- Strongly agree - Agree - Neutral - Disagree - Strongly disagree

The answers are coded with numbers when data is entered into statistical software. For example, an answer of strongly agree might be coded as 10. Agree would be 7, neutral 5, disagree 3, and strongly disagree 1. By using numerical data, you can easily create charts and graphs and run more in-depth statistical analysis, which is covered in future chapters.

rows: 术语说明 Pareto（帕累托）：按影响大小排序，常用于找出“少数关键原因”。
zh.text: 术语说明 Pareto（帕累托）：按影响大小排序，常用于找出“少数关键原因”。
帕累托原则，也称为 80/20 规则，指出 20% 的原因导致 80% 的结果。这也被称为少数重要法则：少数重要的投入驱动大部分的产出。
帕累托原则最初是由一位名叫约瑟夫·朱兰（Joseph Juran）的管理顾问提出的。朱兰以意大利经济学家维尔弗雷多·帕累托 (Vilfredo Pareto) 的名字命名了这一原理，帕累托写道，该国 20% 的人拥有全国 80% 的土地。这一原则已在各个圈子中变得普遍。商业专业人士通常表示，80% 的销售额来自 20% 的客户，而志愿者组织通常由 20% 的人完成 80% 的工作。
这一原则对六西格玛至关重要，不是因为因果关系通过 80/20 细分很好地排列在一起，而是因为它几乎普遍适用，即少数投入比所有其他投入产生的影响更大。寻求减少缺陷的个人几乎总是可以识别出三到四个输入，如果加以改进，将对结果产生巨大影响。 虽然资源、成本和改进难度也在解决方案选择中发挥着作用，但了解帕累托图上哪些输入或根本原因较高，可以让项目团队确定哪些改进将对利润产生最大影响。

rows: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
zh.text: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
帕累托原理最好使用帕累托图来显示，帕累托图是数据元素（通常是输入或原因）在排名条形图中的图形表示。与常规条形图不同，条形图按高度顺序排列，最高的在左侧，最低的在右侧。用于创建此类图表的统计软件会自动添加格式和其他元素，但您也可以在 Excel 中创建基本帕累托图。
为了说明帕累托原则，我们将看一下涉及医疗领域缺陷的常见情况，特别是在提交医疗索赔的过程中。付款人经常出于各种原因拒绝索赔。当索赔被拒绝时，提供商办公室必须重新处理、重新提交或对拒绝提出上诉。有些拒绝原因不可上诉，这意味着提供商的办公室会损失与索赔相关的收入。
我们想象一下，一家医疗机构因索赔被拒而面临现金流问题。该办公室收集有关否认的数据并创建帕累托图，以便团队可以了解大部分否认的来源。下面列出了数据，后面是在 Excel 中创建的基本帕累托图。
拒绝医疗索赔的原因 原因计数 重复索赔 18012 及时提交 13245 未找到受益人 10215 索赔缺乏信息 4548 服务不承保 2154 医疗必需品 1423 服务发出日期 526
索赔被拒绝的原因
重复索赔 及时提交 无受益人
服务不行
服务日期
索赔缺乏信息
医疗
必要性
发现
覆盖
问题
从帕累托图中，您可以看到前三个拒绝原因占被拒绝索赔的 80%。经验丰富的计费团队只需查看这些数据就可以告诉您三件事：
1、办公室有返工现象。他们多次发送很大比例的索赔。 2. 办公室存在效率问题。近四分之一的索赔没有在及时提交截止日期之前发送给付款人。
3. 该办公室存在保险验证问题，因为他们发送的索赔中有五分之一的信息与付款人端的任何内容都不匹配。
解决重复索赔很重要，因为它可以减少返工，并可以增强办公室与保险公司的关系。然而，该团队可能会选择首先解决及时提交问题，因为及时提交拒绝是最终的，这意味着该办公室正在损失与所有这些索赔相关的收入。在许多情况下，按时提交索赔并不困难，因为大多数付款人允许数月甚至一年的时间提出索赔，因此这对团队来说可能是一个“轻松”的胜利。帕累托图经常以这种方式揭示容易实现的目标。
在 Excel 中创建基本帕累托图
如果您没有统计软件，您可以创建一个基本的帕累托图，如上面在 Excel 中的图表。使用索赔否认数据或您自己的数据来练习制作帕累托图。

1. 为数据标签创建一列。当缺陷或其他影响有可量化的原因时，帕累托图会发挥很好的作用。在示例中，数据标签是拒绝的原因。无论您使用什么类型的数据，都请按从大到小的顺序输入数据，以用于帕累托图。 2. 创建一个计数列。在该列中输入每个原因的总计。 3. 创建累计计数列。此列提供运行总计。您可以手动或使用 Excel 计算数字。在下面的数据表中，您可以设置 C3 = B3。在 C4 中，您将输入公式 =C3+B4。您可以将该公式向下拖动，Excel 将更改每个单元格的引用，以便您得到 =C4+B5、=C5+B6...等等。 4. 创建百分比列。在下面的数据表中，D3的公式为=B3/$C$9。单元格 C9 包含所有拒绝的总数，因此我们要将每个单独的拒绝总数除以 C9。公式中的美元符号可让您将其复制到每个较低的单元格中。第一个引用将发生变化，移至下一行，但美元符号告诉 Excel 为每次计算保留 C9 引用。最终结果是一个如下所示的表格：
5. 要创建帕累托图，请突出显示原因和百分比列中的信息，然后选择插入 → 图表 → 条形图。
6. 条形图将自动创建。选择添加图表元素 → 趋势线，然后添加指数或线性趋势线。
7. 选择添加图表元素 → 数据标签，然后选择您喜欢的图表数据标签格式。
帕累托图的使用
当您需要分析问题的频率或原因时，帕累托图是有用的分析工具。它们还有助于缩小解决具有多种原因或过于广泛而无法在单个改进项目中解决的问题的方法。就像上面的索赔拒绝示例一样，您可以找到一个可以在整个过程中产生巨大结果的单一原因来解决。
帕累托图在向他人传达有关原因的信息时也很有帮助，尤其是那些六西格玛过程之外的人。尽管帕累托图是一种强大的分析工具，但它们也以大多数人熟悉的可视化格式表示复杂的数据。商业专业人士知道如何阅读条形图，将图表按顺序排列只会让个人更容易看到问题背后的真正原因。出于这个原因，许多六西格玛专家在向企业领导者和其他人演示时经常使用帕累托图，特别是当数据可能被认为令人惊讶或需要视觉强化时。
客户之声（VOC） 客户之声（VOC）或VOC，是许多质量计划的基本概念。质量的目标是生产更好、更一致的产品。您知道自己已实现此目标的方法之一是您的客户将更加满意。实现这一目标的唯一方法是寻求客户的反馈，因此 VOC 数据对于改进项目之前、期间和之后的收集至关重要。
成功的 VOC 项目积极主动且始终如一地渴望获得反馈，而技术使得以多种方式寻求客户反馈成为可能。捕获反馈的一些方法包括：
通过电话、信件、电子邮件或在线进行调查
面对面或在线焦点小组
采访
Beta 版或用户测试
反馈表
客户投诉
社交媒体或网站互动

rows: 术语说明 Interaction（交互作用）：一个因素的效果会随另一个因素水平不同而变化。
zh.text: 术语说明 Interaction（交互作用）：一个因素的效果会随另一个因素水平不同而变化。
评论
论坛
VOC 可以作为澄清需求和愿望、澄清流程中的具体问题的一种手段，也可以作为改进、客户服务和营销议程的常规部分。
开展 VOC 活动
以正确的方式提出正确的问题，可以帮助您创建强大的 VOC 活动，为六西格玛团队提供可用的数据。我们将在本节中讨论两种特定类型的 VOC 活动：一般客户反馈和特定客户反馈。
一般反馈
一般客户反馈通常是通过反馈表、客户投诉记录以及通过网站或社交媒体收集的被动信息获得的。通过这些方法，组织通常会测试总体情况以获取温度读数：客户总体满意、总体不满意，以及客户感受的原因有什么方向吗？
在任何一家快餐店拿起一份反馈表或访问收据上通常链接的在线调查，您就会很好地了解一般 VOC 活动中寻求的信息类型。
美国杂货连锁店克罗格 (Kroger) 提供了一个针对其大部分收据进行的调查的链接。调查首先要求提供日期、时间和收据中的输入代码。这有助于公司了解人们在何时何地购物，以便他们可以将反馈归因于正确的地点和员工。
接下来，克罗格调查询问人们在商店的哪些区域购物。调查的其余部分询问有关人们访问过的商店每个区域的具体问题，包括：
对商店的总体满意度如何？
客户对什么感到满意：
o 员工友好度 o 价格 o 服务 o 清洁度 o 物品供应 o 每周特价 o 出行方便 o 品牌质量
o 退房时间
购物者是否有可能在未来 30 天内向其他人推荐该商店。
这些问题旨在评估一般顾客对商店关键质量要素的感受。了解关键质量特性（CTQ）因素或 CTQ 对于设计强大的 VOC 活动非常重要。我们将在第 8 章中更深入地介绍 CTQ。

rows: 术语说明 CTQ（关键质量特性）：把客户需求转化为可测量的质量要求。
zh.text: 术语说明 CTQ（关键质量特性）：把客户需求转化为可测量的质量要求。
一般VOC反馈常被用作烟雾报警器。烟雾报警器旨在提醒企业、家庭或其他建筑物中的人员存在发生火灾的可能性。烟雾警报器设置在敏感水平，因此当存在烟雾并且建筑物内的人员可以采取行动时，烟雾警报器就会响起。通常，警报和早期行动可以挽救生命，甚至可以减少与潜在火灾相关的损失。
VOC 数据可以以同样的方式工作。如果某个领域的数字突然发生变化，组织就会知道要更深入地研究这个问题。它表明可能存在问题；及早调查和采取行动有助于防止问题变得更大或成本更高。例如，如果某家克罗格商店在清洁度方面始终得分很高，并且该数字在一个月内持续下降，那么商店管理层可能需要重新审视维护和清洁培训。
一些组织还使用一般 VOC 反馈的结果作为某些员工的质量指标。销售和服务人员通常会因客户满意度高而获得经济和其他方式的奖励。这也提高了员工的动力和满意度。
具体反馈
有时，组织需要针对特定问题、产品或想法的反馈。一般反馈活动中使用的相同工具可以用于特定活动，但您也可以根据需要定制 VOC 工具。如果您需要有关新应用程序的反馈，可以使用 Beta 测试。如果您想测试产品、想法或营销活动，面对面或在线焦点小组可能是最好的选择。
对于具体的反馈，您必须提出具体的问题。如果您正在寻求其他信息或一般反馈的澄清，则尤其如此。例如，如果克罗格确实发现清洁度评级存在问题，它可能需要更多有关顾客如何以及在何处注意到不清洁的信息。如果没有额外的反馈，当顾客因为缺乏照明或货架上的物品杂乱而确实觉得商店很脏时，经理可能会让员工更多地擦地板。
选择正确的 VOC 工具
获得正确类型的反馈并将成本和时间表控制在预算范围内需要为您的项目选择正确的 VOC 工具。下表根据相对成本对每种工具进行了评级，并提供了一些简要的优缺点。
工具
成本 优点 缺点
反馈表低 - 从多个来源收集大量数据
- 个人必须决定留下反馈，将结果偏向于有强烈感觉的人
-可以针对数字数据以便于分析
-需要大量的工时
通过电话调查高 - 可以随机选择，这使您可以对整个人群得出结论
- 顾客可能会因骚扰电话而烦恼
通过邮件进行调查 中等 - 可以随机选择，这使您可以对整个人群得出结论。
- 客户必须将其寄回才算计数。因为很多人不会这样做，所以你必须发送更多调查来获得统计抽样。
- 电话或现场调查的成本更低。
社交媒体 低 - 持续寻求反馈的能力。
-需要已建立的社交媒体关注者。
- 能够即时提问。
-依赖追随者和粉丝，这意味着您正在向那些已经以某种方式支持您的品牌的人寻求反馈。
-可能是 VOC 最便宜的选择。
现场焦点小组高 - 让主持人立即寻求更深入的答案或反馈
-将数据池限制为本地客户或愿意出差的客户。 - 不能使用数据对一般人群做出假设。 - 客户在与测量员面对面时可能不太愿意诚实
- 不能使用数据对一般人群做出假设。
在线焦点小组 低 -让主持人立即寻求更深入的答案或反馈 -不需要出差
您可以接触到全球各地的客户
-需要时间并且需要有经验的用户或测试人员。
用户或 beta 测试 高 - 提供有关特定产品、服务或流程的反馈。
李克特量表
在设计您自己的 VOC 工具时，请记住您打算如何使用所获得的信息。如果您想将数据输入统计分析软件来检验假设或创建可视化图表，那么您需要提出一些问题来产生可以使用统计进行分析的实际数据点。一种流行的方法是使用李克特量表。
使用李克特量表，您可以构建所有问题，以便通过 5 分排名来回答它们。排名可以是任意数量的，但最常见的是以下一些变化：
- 非常同意 - 同意 - 中立 - 不同意 - 非常不同意
当数据输入统计软件时，答案会用数字编码。例如，强烈同意的答案可能编码为 10。同意为 7，中立为 5，不同意为 3，强烈不同意为 1。通过使用数值数据，您可以轻松创建图表和图形并运行更深入的统计分析，这将在以后的章节中介绍。

en.text: We introduced some ideas about Six Sigma metrics in Chapter 1 when we talked about sigma level and defects per million opportunities, or DPMO. Metrics are extremely utilized when applying Six Sigma to processes and improvements, requiring that anyone working in a Six Sigma environment be familiar with them.
Defects per Million Opportunities
Many Six Sigma metrics come with an equation, just like standard deviation. For example, the equation for DPMO is:
(number of defects in a sample/opportunities for a defect in the sample) * 1,000,000

For example, if a mail-order retailer examines quality of the order process, it might sample forms entered by customer service representatives. If each form has 10 fields, then there are 10 opportunities for an error on each form. If the retailer reviews 90 forms, then there are 10 * 90, or 900, total opportunities for errors.
During the review, the retailer finds 2 errors, or defects. To calculate DPMO, the math would be as follows:
(2/900) * 1,000,000 = 2,222 defects per million opportunities.
Defects per Unit
DPU is a measure of how many defects there are in relation to the number of units tested. DPU is concerned with total defects, and one unit could have more than one defect. The formula for DPU is:
Number of defects found / number of units in the sample
For example, if a publisher printed 1,000 books and pulled out 50 books for quality checks, it might be looking for the following defects:
- Incorrect printing - Incorrect alignment - Missing pages - A loose spine - Torn cover
Out of 50 books, the publisher discovers:
- 3 books are missing pages - 1 book is missing pages and has a torn cover - 2 books have loose spines - 1 book has incorrect printing and incorrect alignment
There are 9 total errors, as two books had two defects each.
The DPU is calculated by dividing defects by number of units sampled. In this case, 9/50 = 0.18.
DPU provides an average level of quality—it tells you how many defects on average each unit can be expected to have. In this case, that is 0.18 defects on average.
First Time Yield (FTY)
First time yield is the ratio of units produced to units attempted to produce. For example, if you put 12 cookies in the oven, but only 10 come out edible, then you haven’t produced 12 cookies.
The formula for FTY is:
Number of good units produced / number of units entering the process
In the cookie example, the FTY is 10/12, or .833.
Most products or services are created via multiple processes; you multiply the FTY for each process to calculate an overall FTY. For example, consider the following process chain:
- 100 units enter process A and 95 units exit.
- 95 units enter process B and 85 good units are achieved. - 85 units enter process C and 80 good units exit.
The FTY would be calculated as follows:
- 95/100 = 0.95 = FTY of process A - 85/95 = 0.89 = FTY of process B - 80/85 = 0.94 = FTY of process C - 0.95 * 0.89 * 0.94 = 0.79
The overall FTY of the process is 0.79.
Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

The rolled throughput yield, or RTY, provides a probability that a unit will be generated by a process with no defects. One of the main differences between RTY and basic yield or first time yield is that RTY considers whether rework was needed to generate the number of final units. This is a valuable concern, because organizations don’t always think about the rework that is inherent in a process, which means they often measure a process and deem it successful even if muda is present.

RTY is calculated in a similar manner to FTY, but it takes rework into account. If process A from the FTY example only achieved a yield of 95 because someone reworked five items to make them good, then RTY calculations add five instances of rework into the ratio. The formula is:
(Number of units entering - (scrap + rework))/number of units entering process
In the case of process A: (100 - (5+5))/100 = 90/100 = 0.9
Consider the following process chain:

- 100 units enter process A. Five are scrapped, 5 are reworked, and a total of 95 are produced. - 95 units enter process B. Ten are scrapped, 5 are reworked, and a total of 85 are produced. - 85 units enter process C. Five are scrapped, 15 are reworked, and a total of 80 are produced.
The RTY is calculated as follows:

The overall RTY for the process is 0.574, which is a much lower rate than when you look at FTY alone. RTY doesn’t provide an indication of final production or sales, but a low RTY indicates that there is waste in the process in the form of rework.

zh.text: 我们在第一章讨论西格玛水平和每百万机会缺陷数数数（DPMO）时介绍了有关六西格玛指标的一些想法。在将六西格玛应用于流程和改进时，指标被极大地利用，要求在六西格玛环境中工作的任何人都熟悉它们。

rows: 术语说明 Sigma level（西格玛水平）：衡量过程缺陷率的尺度；水平越高，单位机会中产生缺陷的概率越低。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sigma level（西格玛水平）：衡量过程缺陷率的尺度；水平越高，单位机会中产生缺陷的概率越低。
每百万次机会的缺陷数
许多六西格玛指标都带有一个方程，就像标准差一样。例如，DPMO 的方程为：
（样本中的缺陷数量/样本中出现缺陷的机会）* 1,000,000
例如，如果邮购零售商检查订单流程的质量，它可能会对客户服务代表输入的表格进行抽样。如果每个表单有 10 个字段，则每个表单上有 10 次出错机会。如果零售商检查 90 个表格，则总共有 10 * 90 或 900 个错误机会。
在审查过程中，零售商发现了 2 个错误或缺陷。要计算 DPMO，数学计算如下：
每单位缺陷数
DPU 是衡量缺陷数量与测试单元数量之间的关系的指标。 DPU 关注的是总缺陷数，一个单元可能有多个缺陷。 DPU的计算公式为：
发现的缺陷数/样本中的单位数
例如，如果出版商印刷了 1,000 本书并取出 50 本书进行质量检查，则可能会寻找以下缺陷：
- 打印不正确 - 对齐不正确 - 缺页 - 书脊松动 - 封面破损
在 50 本书中，出版商发现：
- 3 本书缺页 - 1 本书缺页且封面破损 - 2 本书书脊松动 - 1 本书印刷不正确且对齐不正确
总共有 9 个错误，因为两本书各有两个缺陷。
DPU 的计算方法是将缺陷除以抽样单位数。在本例中，9/50 = 0.18。
DPU 提供平均质量水平 — 它告诉您每个单元平均预计有多少缺陷。在本例中，平均缺陷数为 0.18。
首次收益率 (FTY)
首次产量是已生产的单位与尝试生产的单位的比率。例如，如果您将 12 块饼干放入烤箱，但只有 10 块出来可以食用，那么您还没有制作出 12 块饼干。
FTY 的公式为：
生产的好单位数/进入流程的单位数
在 cookie 示例中，FTY 为 10/12，即 0.833。
大多数产品或服务都是通过多个流程创建的；将每个流程的 FTY 相乘即可计算总体 FTY。例如，考虑以下流程链：
- 100 个单位进入流程 A，95 个单位退出。
- 95 个单元进入流程 B，并获得 85 个良好单元。 - 85 个单位进入流程 C，80 个良好单位退出。
FTY 计算如下：
该过程的总体 FTY 为 0.79。
滚动吞吐量 (RTY)
滚动产出率 (RTY) 提供了通过流程生产无缺陷单元的概率。 RTY 与基本良率或首次良率之间的主要区别之一是，RTY 考虑是否需要返工来生成最终单位的数量。 这是一个值得关注的问题，因为组织并不总是考虑流程中固有的返工，这意味着即使存在 Muda，他们也经常衡量流程并认为它是成功的。
RTY 的计算方式与 FTY 类似，但它考虑了返工。如果 FTY 示例中的流程 A 仅实现了 95 的产量，因为有人返工了 5 个项目以使它们变得良好，则 RTY 计算会将 5 个返工实例添加到比率中。公式为：
(进入的单位数量-(报废+返工))/进入处理的单位数量
考虑以下流程链：
- 100 件进入流程 A。其中 5 件报废，5 件返工，总共生产 95 件。 - 95 件进入流程 B。其中 10 件报废，5 件返工，总共生产 85 件。 - 85 件进入流程 C。其中 5 件报废，15 件返工，总共生产 80 件。
RTY 计算如下：
该流程的总体 RTY 为 0.574，这比单独查看 FTY 时要低得多。 RTY 不提供最终产量或销量的指标，但较低的 RTY 表明流程中存在返工形式的浪费。

title.zh: 第 6 章：处理问题的方法

source.extraction: python-docx body-order extraction; source-TOC sectionized bilingual content

en.text: Understanding how to approach the problem – beginning to identify the problem and defining it with a statement – is critical to creating a foundation for successful Six Sigma projects. In later chapters, we’ll cover the importance of defining a variety of project, process, and problem aspects, but in this chapter, we’re going to discuss the project in general, digging deeper via a series of why questions, and creating a general problem statement as a launching point for a project.
Problem Functions: y = f(x)
Because Six Sigma approaches things with a statistical mindset, it considers all problems as a function. Using mathematical symbols, this looks like:

The y=f(x) statement can be used in two ways. First, it is a general map for stating a problem. Y (the problem) occurs because some X (input or cause) is occurring. In reality, Y is usually occurring because of some group of causes or inputs, which means there are going to be more than one X inputs.

The idea can also be applied to specific processes and outcomes within the problem. As you get more and more granular, the y=f(x) concept becomes increasingly mathematical; in many cases, you can graph the relationship between the output (y) and the input (x).

To understand the concept of thinking of problems as a function, let’s look at a problem that might occur for a large HVAC service provider. The manager of a service team has discovered that service calls are taking much longer than expected; in fact, his five team members take 1.75 times longer on average than other service reps in the company to handle all types of calls.

To find out what might be causing the situation, the manager researches the problem by talking to the reps, talking to the customers, and going out on random calls with all five representatives. He makes the following observations:
One representative is a native to the area the team services, which means he or she knows many of the customers personally. This results in friendly chatter that lengthens the time on the job.

One representative is providing homeowners with very in-depth explanations and education about HVAC issues, sometimes over and beyond what the homeowner would ever need to know regarding his or her HVAC unit.

One representative is new to the job and takes longer to complete each task because he or she is unsure of the work, has to double-check the work, or calls another rep to ask questions about the work.
The remaining two reps perform work in times that are on par with company averages.
The manager distills this data down to two overall causes for the problem:
Too much talking (reps one and two)
Inadequate training
The problem can now be stated as a function:
The extra time is a function of too much talking and inappropriate training.

The manager also now has two root causes to address. The example is simple, but it illustrates the basic concept in defining a y=f(x) relationship for a problem and its causes. It’s not always so easy to conduct the research and analysis to find the relationship, but the relationship is always present.
Some other examples of y=f(x) relationships include:
Low customer satisfaction with hamburger taste is a function of an uncalibrated grill.
Low employee morale is a function of a poor time-off approval system.
Customer wait times are a function of technology distractions for employees.
The 5 Whys

Data analysis is one of the best ways to validate a y=f(x) assumption, but teams who are familiar with processes can often arrive at some basic relationships through a process known as the 5 Whys. This is a brainstorming tool that asks increasingly granular why questions about a problem or process, seeking to understand the root cause or actual problem. The 5 Whys can be used to define a problem or to begin seeking causes.

For example, consider the hamburger example above. Teams addressing a problem of customer satisfaction might begin doing so because feedback forms have shown a lower-than-normal satisfaction with food quality over the past week.
The team first asks: Why are customers dissatisfied with the food?

Looking at feedback tied to orders, the team notes that the customers who are rating the food poorly are mostly customers who ordered hamburgers of some type. The answer to the first question is that the customers are dissatisfied with the food because they are dissatisfied with the hamburgers.
Why are customers dissatisfied with hamburgers?

The team looks at written feedback on forms or speaks with customers directly and discovers that many customers feel that their hamburgers were undercooked. The new answer is that customers are dissatisfied with hamburgers because the meat is undercooked.
Why is the meat undercooked?

An investigation into the kitchen reveals that the grill is not properly calibrated and is providing inconsistent results. At this point, you have the y=f(x) relationship, but the team could keep asking questions.
Why is the grill not properly calibrated?

Further investigation shows that the morning shift, responsible for calibrating the grill, has a new grill cook. During training, education on performing this function was omitted. The grill is not properly calibrated because the employee responsible was not properly trained. Now the team has a specific cause and a solution: train the grill cook.

In a Six Sigma environment, the team might move on with one more question: Why was the grill cook not properly trained? This might lead to the development of a consistent training policy so the problem doesn’t occur the next time a new grill cook is hired.

In the hamburger example, it only took four why questions to get to the root of the problem, and a fifth question started pointing to controls or long-term solutions. It isn’t always this easy; the tool is called the 5 Whys because it often leads to answers within five questions. However, teams could ask a dozen questions if they begin at a very high level and work down through a complex process.
When to Use 5 Whys

One benefit of 5 Whys is that it only costs your team a small amount of time to use—a team familiar with a process can conduct a complete 5 Whys session in less than an hour if a moderator keeps things on task. Because of its simplicity, the 5 Whys tool can be used for almost any problem. Use it to address a problem team members bring up, to address a problem a supervisor noticed, or to address the vague feeling that there is a problem when no one has been able to define what is actually wrong. At the very least, a 5 Whys session facilitates communication and thought.
In a Six Sigma project environment, 5 Whys is usually deployed when processes involve human interactions or people-powered inputs, though it can be an effective start to brainstorming on any process.
Conducting a 5 Whys Session

Since a 5 Whys session is usually based on input from subject-matter-experts, gather people who are close to the process. On a white board or web conference screen, display a basic problem statement as you understand it. This problem statement is not going to be detailed like the statements we’ll discuss in the next session—a 5 Whys session is often one of the tools you use to get to that detailed statement.
Examples of statements you might see in a 5 Whys station include:
Customers are not happy with the selection of produce
Customers are receiving orders late
The printing process is resulting in too many defects
Lead times on the bottling process are excessive
Employees are not happy with vacation schedules

These are all fairly general statements that simply say something about defects or dissatisfaction. Begin by asking the highest-level “Why?” question possible about the statement. “Why are employees not happy with vacation schedules?” Write this question down.

The team works together to provide a high level answer to the question. Employees are not happy with vacation schedules because it’s rare to get the exact time off requested. Write the answer down under the question, then create the highest-level “Why?” question you can about the new answer.

“Why are employees not getting their first choice time off for vacation?” Perhaps the answer is that supervisors take so long to approve vacation requests that other employees have also asked off for the same time, so it’s hard for supervisors to accommodate everyone.

The next question is “Why are supervisors taking too long to approve requests?” The answer might be that the time-off system is too cumbersome, so supervisors put off approvals until they have a lot of time to manage them.
“Why do supervisors see the system as cumbersome?” Because there are wait times when moving from screen to screen and each approval requires a vast number of clicks and entries.
