In the define phase, teams set rules, create a charter that will govern efforts moving forward, identify stakeholders and customers, define a process through process mapping, and prepare for a define tollgate before entering the measure phase.

zh.text: 六西格玛团队带着不同级别的信息进入项目流程。有时，在团队开始工作之前，问题就已经相当明确，特别是在使用六西格玛领导委员会来选择项目和创建团队的组织中。其他时候，团队在开始工作时几乎没有什么信息，只是因为流程的结果不符合预期而存在某种类型的问题。团队可能不知道错误发生在哪里，甚至在完全了解与流程相关的输入和输出的情况下开始项目。
无论知识团队从什么开始，定义阶段都是团队从有关流程或问题的非常基本的信息转向进入测量和后续其他阶段所需的知识和组织并奠定成功基础的阶段。在定义阶段，团队制定规则，创建一个章程来管理前进的工作，识别利益相关者和客户，通过流程图定义流程，并在进入测量阶段之前为定义收费站做好准备。

rows: 术语说明 Stakeholder（相关方）：会影响项目或被项目影响的人或组织。
zh.text: 术语说明 Stakeholder（相关方）：会影响项目或被项目影响的人或组织。
创建项目章程

en.text: A project charter, or team charter, is a short document that includes information about the team and what they plan to accomplish. The purpose of the charter is to set expectations that can be agreed upon by the team as well as the sponsor or executive leaders, keep the team focused on the goal, ensure the project remains aligned with the goals of the business, and documents the fact that control of a process is being moved from a business executive or manager to a Six Sigma project team.
Minimally, team charters should include:
A complete and concise problem statement that follows the guidelines set out in Chapter 6.
A list of critical to quality metrics, or those measurements that will ultimately determine project or process success. Critical to quality was introduced in chapter 8.
The names and roles of each person on the team. Selecting team members and appropriate team member roles are covered in Chapter 10.
A list of both internal and external process customers. Use a SIPOC, discussed in Chapter 7, to begin defining internal or external customers.
The name of a sponsor and/or champion.
A duration for the project.

Teams might also include information such as a list of non-customer stakeholders, an estimated schedule for each phase of the project, scope definitions for the process or project, and financial drivers for the project.

The information for the team charter usually can’t be gathered in a single brainstorming session; the charter is an outcome of the entire define phase, not a quick notation at the beginning. By taking time to properly consider all elements of a team charter, Six Sigma teams create a stronger foundation for the rest of their work.
Benefits of an Organizational Team Charter Template

Businesses that are implementing Six Sigma organization-wide might consider creating or using a specific template for team charters. Templates streamline define phases and make it easy for leadership teams and other employees to understand critical process components at a glance. While final team documentation is likely to be extensive, and even in the define phase, teams themselves might work with lengthy requirements documents, charters themselves should be as concise as possible. Some organizations distill charters to a single page while others use multipage documents. A sample one-page charter template is attached at the end of this chapter.
Details for Charter Elements

We’ve covered some of the most important elements of the charter in detail in previous chapters, but here’s a quick look at some of the items we didn’t cover in as much detail and are worth mentioning again.
Business Case

The business case might also be referred to as the financial drivers behind a project. Related closely to the problem statement, the business case is a short statement that provides a reason the project should be undertaken. The problem statement tells someone where, when, and how; the business case says why it’s important. If you think back to Chapter 6, we said dollar amounts or another financial metric were important to include in the problem statement. If you include a business case in your charter, you would build on that basic financial statement to explain why, specifically, the loss of money, efficiency, or quality is important to consumers, employees, or the organization. You might also make an argument for why the problem must be solved now; in essence, why is this project being run now in place of another project?
Project Scope

We introduced the concept of scope briefly in Chapter 6. For the purposes of the team charter, the scope should include a hard beginning and end of the process or problem being considered. You might also include a short list of items or activities that are in scope and out of scope for your project. A SIPOC diagram helps teams identify the parameters for a project, and you can also use the In and Out of the Box method described later in this chapter to understand the intended scope of a project.

The scope should be clear. Listing the scope for a project or process as “beginning at the order stage and ending with fulfillment” isn’t clear, because different people might consider different points the beginning of the order stage or the end of fulfillment. A better scope statement might be “beginning when a customer places an order and ending when the order is boxed for shipment.” Going even further, a team might deem return and replacement processes out of scope for a project so that they are only dealing with original orders. Successful projects have a well-defined scope that is approved and backed by a project sponsor or champion.
List the Stakeholders

Listing major stakeholders on the charter helps the team remember who and what they are likely to impact in addition to end customers. Having the list visible during meetings reduces the chance that the team will initiate changes that might have a negative or unwanted effect on other process owners or processes, and it helps direct the team to resources outside of the team that can provide help, access, or information to areas related to the project.
Team Member Roles

Team members and roles were covered in Chapter 10, and the team charter simply needs to list the names of all team members along with their role and expected time commitment. Adding time commitments to the charter helps sponsors and executive leadership understand the human resource requirements for the project; often a Six Sigma team leader has to seek approval for staff members from other areas to devote a specified amount of time to the project.

Time commitments can be listed in hours per week but are often listed as a percent of the employee’s overall time. For example, a subject matter expert who is expected to attend all of the team meetings to provide input, but is not expected to complete data collection, analysis, or improvement work, might be listed as providing 10 percent of his or her time to the team. A list of team members in a charter might look something like the list below. You don’t have to list all the staff members you might possibility consult during the course of the project.
Mike Smith, Black Belt, 100%
Chase Michaels, Green Belt, 100%
Lisa Javes, Green Belt, 100%
Rosalie Myers, Process Owner, 25%
Brent Reed, subject matter expert, 10%
Brenda Tran, subject matter expert, 10%
Milestones

In Chapter 10, we covered creating a draft schedule for a Six Sigma project. The diagram included in Chapter 10 that broke down the timeline for a project is called a Gantt chart. Adapted by Henry Gantt in the early 20th century, a Gantt chart is a bar chart that displays the phases of a project according to time. One of the benefits of using a Gantt chart to display a rough project schedule is that it can easily be included in a one-page project charter; anyone reviewing the charter can quickly visualize the time element required for the project.

Teams should ensure a date is provided for the end of each of the DMAIC phases and that all team members agree that the dates are plausible given what the group wants to do. In some cases, milestones might be set by the project sponsor or champion, but the team should agree that milestone dates are possible. If dates seem implausible, teams can present a counter schedule with logical arguments regarding why the original schedule wouldn’t work.

In addition to milestones at the end of each project phase, Six Sigma teams might also want to set milestones for work within each phase – specifically for the more laborious measure, analyze, and improve phases. While the team should document all milestones it agrees on, detailed milestones don’t necessarily belong in the one-page charter document.
Measurement of Success

Everyone needs to know how the team is going to measure success. If a sponsor is measuring success on customer satisfaction scores and the team is measuring success on internal quality scores, ideas about the outcome of the project are likely to differ. Usually, measures of success can be pulled from the critical to quality metrics discussed in Chapter 8. If teams can convert a CTQ to a measurement, they can understand what major metrics determine success of a project. While teams might begin to gather
measurements or look at existing measurements while in the define phase, finalization of metrics can extend into the measure phase.
Expected Financial Benefits

Financial information is already likely included on the charter in both the business case and the problem statement. Teams might include expected financial benefits in the business case section of a charter, but it must be included somewhere. For some sponsors and executive leaders, the financial benefit is the most important piece of information included in a charter. An estimated savings or increase in revenue also provides a measuring stick by which leaders can consider requests for resources for a project.

A Six Sigma expert should never over extend estimates regarding financial benefits; it’s almost always better to under-promise and over-perform. If you tell leaders a project will save $500,000 in the first year because a big number means you’re more likely to get project approval and all the resources you ask for, you’re the one that answers when the project saves only $80,000. As with any aspect of a Six Sigma project, be as accurate as possible, but be conservative with estimates when accuracy is in question.
Review the Charter with Success in Mind

Before a Six Sigma team presents a charter for approval, it should take time to review the document as a group to ensure the charter lays a foundation for success. Some questions a team might ask itself about a charter include:
Is everything—especially the goals, financial expectations, and timeline—challenging but realistic?
Can everyone on the team devote the committed amount of time to the project?
Is the project backed by a sponsor or champion with enough influence to drive critical assistance and resources?
Does the team expect to be supported by auxiliary departments such as information technology, human resources, compliance, accounting, or legal as necessary for project success?
Does the team expect to have the necessary freedom to implement a solution it designs after the solution is approved by the sponsor, champion, or executive steering committee?
Does the team have a leader who is well-versed in Six Sigma tools and project management?

If the answer to any of the questions above is no, then the team could be setting itself up for failure. Before moving forward, the team should address these concerns and, if possible, make changes that convert no answers to yes answers.

rows: 术语说明 Project Charter（项目章程）：确认问题、目标、范围、收益、团队和时间表的项目启动文件。
zh.text: 术语说明 Project Charter（项目章程）：确认问题、目标、范围、收益、团队和时间表的项目启动文件。
项目章程或团队章程是一份简短的文档，其中包含有关团队及其计划完成的任务的信息。章程的目的是设定团队以及发起人或执行领导者可以同意的期望，使团队专注于目标，确保项目与业务目标保持一致，并记录流程的控制权从业务主管或经理转移到六西格玛项目团队的事实。
团队章程至少应包括：
遵循第 6 章中规定的指导原则的完整而简洁的问题陈述。
关键质量特性（CTQ）指标列表，或最终决定项目或流程成功的那些衡量标准。 第8章介绍了关键质量特性（CTQ）。

rows: 术语说明 CTQ（关键质量特性）：把客户需求转化为可测量的质量要求。
zh.text: 术语说明 CTQ（关键质量特性）：把客户需求转化为可测量的质量要求。
团队中每个人的姓名和角色。第 10 章介绍了选择团队成员和适当的团队成员角色。
内部和外部流程客户的列表。使用第 7 章中讨论的 SIPOC 开始定义内部或外部客户。

rows: 术语说明 SIPOC：供应商、输入、流程、输出、客户；用于快速界定流程边界。
zh.text: 术语说明 SIPOC：供应商、输入、流程、输出、客户；用于快速界定流程边界。
赞助商和/或冠军的姓名。
项目的持续时间。
团队还可能包括非客户利益相关者列表、项目每个阶段的预计时间表、流程或项目的范围定义以及项目的财务驱动因素等信息。

rows: 术语说明 Scope（项目范围）：说明改进项目包括什么、不包括什么，避免目标扩张。
zh.text: 术语说明 Scope（项目范围）：说明改进项目包括什么、不包括什么，避免目标扩张。
团队章程的信息通常无法通过一次头脑风暴会议收集；章程是整个定义阶段的结果，而不是开始时的快速符号。通过花时间正确考虑团队章程的所有要素，六西格玛团队为其余工作奠定了更坚实的基础。
组织团队章程模板的好处
在整个组织范围内实施六西格玛的企业可能会考虑创建或使用特定的团队章程模板。模板简化了阶段定义，使领导团队和其他员工能够轻松一目了然地了解关键流程组件。虽然最终的团队文档可能很广泛，甚至在定义阶段，团队本身也可能使用冗长的需求文档，但章程本身应该尽可能简洁。一些组织将章程提炼为单页，而另一些组织则使用多页文档。本章末尾附有一页章程模板示例。

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
章程要素的详细信息
我们在前面的章节中详细介绍了章程的一些最重要的要素，但这里快速浏览了一些我们没有详细介绍但值得再次提及的项目。
商业案例

业务案例也可以称为项目背后的财务驱动因素。业务案例与问题陈述密切相关，是一个简短的陈述，提供了应该开展该项目的理由。问题陈述告诉某人地点、时间和方式；商业案例说明了它的重要性。如果你回想一下第 6 章，我们说过在问题陈述中包含美元金额或其他财务指标很重要。如果您在章程中包含一个商业案例，您将在基本财务报表的基础上解释为什么金钱、效率或质量的损失对消费者、员工或组织很重要。您还可以论证为什么现在必须解决问题；本质上，为什么现在运行这个项目而不是另一个项目？
项目范围
我们在第 6 章中简要介绍了范围的概念。出于团队章程的目的，范围应包括正在考虑的过程或问题的硬性开始和结束。您还可以包括项目范围内和项目范围外的项目或活动的简短列表。 SIPOC 图可帮助团队识别项目的参数，您还可以使用本章后面描述的开箱即用方法来了解项目的预期范围。
范围应该明确。将项目或流程的范围列为“从订单阶段开始到履行结束”并不明确，因为不同的人可能会认为订单阶段的开始或履行结束的时间点不同。更好的范围声明可能是“从客户下订单时开始，到订单装箱发货时结束”。更进一步，团队可能会认为退货和更换流程超出了项目范围，因此他们只处理原始订单。成功的项目有一个明确的范围，并得到项目发起人或支持者的批准和支持。
列出利益相关者
在章程中列出主要利益相关者可以帮助团队记住除了最终客户之外他们还可能影响谁和什么。在会议期间显示该列表可以减少团队启动可能对其他流程所有者或流程产生负面或不良影响的更改的机会，并且有助于引导团队使用团队外部的资源，这些资源可以为与项目相关的区域提供帮助、访问权限或信息。
团队成员角色
第 10 章介绍了团队成员和角色，团队章程只需要列出所有团队成员的姓名以及他们的角色和预期的时间投入。在章程中添加时间承诺有助于发起人和执行领导层了解项目的人力资源需求；六西格玛的团队领导常常必须征求其他地区的员工的批准，才能为该项目投入一定的时间。
时间投入可以按每周小时数列出，但通常以员工总时间的百分比列出。 例如，一位主题专家预计将参加所有团队会议以提供意见，但预计不会完成数据收集、分析或改进工作，则可能会被列为向团队提供了 10% 的时间。章程中的团队成员列表可能类似于下面的列表。您不必列出在项目过程中可能咨询的所有员工。
迈克·史密斯，黑带，100%
蔡斯·迈克尔斯，绿带，100%
丽莎·贾维斯，绿带，100%
Rosalie Myers，流程负责人，25%
布伦特·里德（Brent Reed），主题专家，10%
Brenda Tran，主题专家，10%
里程碑
在第 10 章中，我们介绍了为六西格玛项目创建时间表草案。第 10 章中包含的分解项目时间线的图表称为甘特图。甘特图由 Henry Gantt 在 20 世纪初改编，是一种根据时间显示项目阶段的条形图。使用甘特图显示粗略项目进度表的好处之一是它可以轻松包含在一页项目章程中；任何审查章程的人都可以快速想象出项目所需的时间要素。
团队应确保为每个 DMAIC 阶段的结束提供一个日期，并且所有团队成员都同意，考虑到团队想要做的事情，这些日期是合理的。在某些情况下，里程碑可能由项目发起人或支持者设定，但团队应该同意里程碑日期是可能的。如果日期看起来不可信，团队可以提出一个反时间表，并附上逻辑论证，说明为什么原始时间表行不通。
除了每个项目阶段结束时的里程碑之外，六西格玛团队可能还希望为每个阶段的工作设定里程碑——特别是针对更费力的测量、分析和改进阶段。虽然团队应该记录其同意的所有里程碑，但详细的里程碑不一定属于一页章程文件。
成功的衡量标准
每个人都需要知道团队将如何衡量成功。如果发起人根据客户满意度分数来衡量成功，而团队根据内部质量分数来衡量成功，那么关于项目结果的想法可能会有所不同。 通常，成功的衡量标准可以从第 8 章讨论的关键质量特性（CTQ）指标中提取。如果团队可以将 CTQ 转换为衡量标准，他们就可以了解哪些主要指标决定项目的成功。虽然团队可能开始聚集
在定义阶段进行测量或查看现有测量，指标的最终确定可以延伸到测量阶段。
预期财务效益
财务信息可能已经包含在章程的业务案例和问题陈述中。团队可能会在章程的业务案例部分中包含预期的财务收益，但它必须包含在某个地方。对于一些发起人和执行领导者来说，财务利益是章程中包含的最重要的信息。估计的节省或收入增加也提供了一个衡量标准，领导者可以通过它来考虑项目的资源请求。
六西格玛专家绝不能过度估计经济效益；承诺不足而表现出色几乎总是更好。如果你告诉领导者一个项目将在第一年节省 500,000 美元，因为数字越大意味着你更有可能获得项目批准和你要求的所有资源，那么当项目仅节省 80,000 美元时，你就是那个回答的人。与六西格玛项目的任何方面一样，要尽可能准确，但当准确性有问题时，要保守估计。
回顾章程，牢记成功
六西格玛团队在提交章程供批准之前，应该花时间作为一个团队来审查该文件，以确保章程为成功奠定基础。团队可能会问自己有关章程的一些问题，包括：
一切（尤其是目标、财务预期和时间表）是否都具有挑战性但又现实？
团队中的每个人都能为该项目投入承诺的时间吗？
该项目是否得到了具有足够影响力来推动关键援助和资源的赞助商或支持者的支持？
团队是否期望得到项目成功所需的辅助部门（如信息技术、人力资源、合规、会计或法律）的支持？
在解决方案获得赞助商、支持者或执行指导委员会批准后，团队是否期望拥有必要的自由来实施其设计的解决方案？
团队中是否有一位精通六西格玛工具和项目管理的领导者？
如果上述任何一个问题的答案是否定的，那么团队可能会失败。在继续前进之前，团队应解决这些问题，并在可能的情况下进行更改，将“否”答案转变为“是”答案。

en.text: Before moving forward with any work – even defining a team charter – it’s a good idea for a Six Sigma team to establish some basic rules and requirements for the team. We touched briefly on this in Chapter 10 when discussing management of a team. The ground rules for a project should be maintained in writing and approved by all team members, but they don’t have to be part of an official charter document. The reason for documenting the rules and having all team members approve them is because a single team member cannot later claim to be ignorant of the rules.

At the same time, rule generation on a Six Sigma team shouldn’t be a completely democratic process. Some of the more common sense or critical rules can be provided by the Black Belt or team leader. For example, ground rules should cover topics such as who should attend each meeting and the fact that team members should hold certain information confidential. A Black Belt might simply state that team members should observe confidentiality and attendance rules, be on time to meetings, and respect each other. The team itself will likely vote on the frequency of meetings and when meetings should be scheduled. Seeking team member input ensures that all team members can actually commit to meeting time slots. For consistency, it’s best to hold meetings on the same days and at the same time each week, but it’s understandably difficult to keep such a schedule through the entire life of some projects.

Black Belts might also provide some tips and suggestions for how team members should participate during meetings, particularly during group brainstorming sessions. For more information on running a brainstorming session, see Chapter 35. Black Belts should also dictate the rules for creating an agenda and running a meeting according to the agenda, though they might delegate some of these functions such as time keeper and secretary.

zh.text: 在进行任何工作之前——甚至是制定团队章程——对于六西格玛团队来说，建立一些基本的团队规则和要求是一个好主意。我们在第 10 章讨论团队管理时简要谈到了这一点。项目的基本规则应以书面形式维护并得到所有团队成员的批准，但它们不必成为正式章程文件的一部分。记录规则并让所有团队成员批准它们的原因是因为单个团队成员以后不能声称不了解规则。

同时，六西格玛团队的规则制定不应该是一个完全民主的过程。一些比较常识或者关键的规则可以由黑带或者组长提供。例如，基本规则应涵盖诸如谁应该参加每次会议以及团队成员应对某些信息保密等主题。黑带可能只是简单地规定团队成员应遵守保密和出勤规则，准时参加会议，并相互尊重。团队本身可能会就会议频率和会议安排时间进行投票。寻求团队成员的意见可确保所有团队成员都能真正承诺参加会议时间段。为了保持一致性，最好每周在同一天、同一时间举行会议，但在某些项目的整个生命周期中保持这样的时间表是很困难的，这是可以理解的。
黑带人员还可能就团队成员如何参与会议（特别是小组头脑风暴会议）提供一些提示和建议。有关举行头脑风暴会议的更多信息，请参阅第 35 章。黑带人员还应规定创建议程和根据议程召开会议的规则，尽管他们可能会委派其中一些职能，例如计时员和秘书。

en.text: We’ve covered a number of define tools in previous chapters, including the SIPOC diagram and the 5 Whys. Process maps and value stream mapping are two advanced Six Sigma tools that are often used in the define stage. Some Six Sigma teams begin using run charts to start defining a baseline in the define phase; run charts are covered in Chapter 13 on the measure phase.
In this section, we’ll cover three additional tools that are common to the define phase: the Stakeholder Analysis, the In and Out of the Box Method, and the Is/Is Not Matrix.
Stakeholder Analysis

A stakeholder analysis is a quick way to identify how various people within an organization relate to a project and how the team should keep them informed. Begin the analysis with a grid drawn over an x and y axis. The vertical axis represents the amount of power a person has in the organization. The horizontal axis represents the amount of interest a person has in the team’s project. The stakeholder analysis works best when teams conduct it on a whiteboard or large flipchart. Draw the basic diagram, as seen in the figure below, in large format.

Provide the team with sticky notes. Ask them to write down possible stakeholders for a project or process. Stakeholders are anyone who has an interest in the project, who might benefit from the outcome of the project, or who might be impacted by the work done via the project. Take a few minutes to discuss the names that were brainstormed and discard any the team feels are not actually stakeholders.

Once the team decides who the stakeholders are, begin placing the names on the chart according to power and interest. People with both low power and little interest will be placed in the lower left corner. People with higher power and low interest are placed in the upper left; people with less power but a lot of interest are placed in the lower right corner, and people with high levels of power and a lot of interest are placed in the upper right corner. A name might be placed high and to the right of the lower-left corner if the stakeholder has a moderate amount of interest and power, but the team doesn’t feel like the person quite crosses the line. Likewise, a name placed at the lower bounds of any quadrant might show lower amounts of power than those placed nearer the top of the box; the stakeholder analysis lets teams prioritize stakeholders in this manner, even within the four quadrants.
Each of the quadrants of the diagram correlate loosely to the type of stakeholder, providing some guidance on how the team might interact with each person or department listed.
Top Left: Keep Satisfied

Stakeholders that fall into the top left quadrant of the diagram have enough power that they could interfere with a project, but they aren’t extremely interested in the day-to-day outcomes. The team should ensure these individuals are satisfied in whatever way they do interact with the project. Teams

might also consult with these individuals at various times during the project. For example, a team working to solve problems of shipping in a warehouse might need to consult with procurement managers at some point because the team identifies a need for a different type of packing tape. Procurement leaders have power over obtaining the resource, but they might not have a great deal of interest in the project overall.

Sometimes, teams might identify a stakeholder that falls into this section and realize that it would be valuable to the team for that particular stakeholder to be more invested in the project. Six Sigma leaders can work with stakeholders to try to move someone from low interest to high interest categories – this is a political tactic that some teams use to bolster support for a project.
Bottom Left: Minimal Effort

Stakeholders that fall into the bottom left quadrant have the least important connection to a project. Teams will mostly communicate general information about a project via newsletters or email to these stakeholders. While these stakeholders take minimal effort from teams, some situations might exist where teams want to move stakeholders from this box to the lower-right box.
Bottom Right: Keep Informed

Individuals in the lower-right box have a strong tie or interest in the project, but do not have access to power to support projects from a resource standpoint. These stakeholders might include employees in departments related to the process being improved or subject matter experts that will be consulted about individual aspects of a project.
While stakeholders in the lower-right quadrant can’t usually bring resources to bear, they can act in support of a project, often in the form of a goodwill ambassador.
Top Right: Key Player

Individuals in the top-right quadrant are either key players regarding a process or executive leaders with the ability to assign resources to a project. These are the individuals teams will report to at various tollgates; often, the executives responsible for the ultimate success of a process or project appear in the top-right quadrant.
In and Out of the Box Method

The In and Out of the Box method is a quick and easy method that helps teams define project or process scope. Begin by drawing a large box on a whiteboard or flip chart. Provide markers and sticky notes for the team. Ask the team to write down elements of the process to be worked on, including resources, activities, and people. Each item should be written on a sticky note. Work together to create as complete a list as possible, placing the sticky notes on a wall or table as you go. Make sure everyone on the team understands that there are no wrong answers and the first phase of the exercise doesn’t require discussion. By brainstorming items with little-to-no discussion, teams can capture more ideas, leading to a more accurate picture of what is in and out of scope.

Once a comprehensive list is made, begin working as a team to assign each item to a place inside, on the line, or outside of the box. Items outside of the box are those that will be considered out of scope for

the project. Teams might place items outside of the box because they don’t have access or control over the items, they don’t have time to work on the items, or they have specifically been told not to include the item in the project.

Items that are within the box are considered in scope for the project. These should be elements of the process the team can reasonably be expected to influence. If the team isn’t sure yet where an item might fall, they should place it on the line. Items that go on the line might be ones that the team hopes to be able to impact but for which the team leader needs to seek permission or assistance from someone outside the team.

Once all items are placed on, in, or outside of the box, review the placements as a team and make any changes. Document the exercise by photographing the diagram or recreating it on a computer. The team might reflect back on the diagram when attempting to control scope creep or considering who they should approach for help with a project.
Is/Is Not Matrix

The Is/Is Not Matrix is another quick brainstorming tool teams can use to define scope. It can also be used to help define some of the information necessary to a problem statement. The matrix works by considering specific things about the process or project and coming up with both is and is not answers.
For example, if a Six Sigma team is tasked with determining why the furnaces in a certain factory are not heating to proper temperatures, they might create an Is/Is Not Matrix like the one below.
Is
Is Not
Where
South plant North or East plan
What
Steam furnaces Wood furnaces
When January 2015 Prior to January 2015

The matrix clearly shows that the scope of the project only includes work at the south plant on the steam furnaces. The problem was noted in January 2015, which provides the team with a starting point for gathering data. This is a very simple matrix; teams can ask as many questions as they like to narrow down scope or better understand processes and projects through the Is/Is Not structure.

zh.text: 我们在前面的章节中介绍了许多定义工具，包括 SIPOC 图和 5 个为什么。流程图和价值流图是定义阶段经常使用的两种高级六西格玛工具。一些六西格玛团队在定义阶段开始使用运行图来定义基线；第 13 章的测量阶段介绍了运行图。

rows: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。
zh.text: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。

rows: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
zh.text: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。

rows: 术语说明 5 Whys（五个为什么）：连续追问原因，帮助从表面症状深入到根本原因。
zh.text: 术语说明 5 Whys（五个为什么）：连续追问原因，帮助从表面症状深入到根本原因。
在本节中，我们将介绍定义阶段常见的三个附加工具：利益相关者分析、开箱即用方法以及是/否矩阵。
利益相关者分析
利益相关者分析是一种快速方法，可以确定组织内的各个人员与项目的关系以及团队应如何让他们了解情况。从在 x 和 y 轴上绘制的网格开始分析。纵轴代表一个人在组织中拥有的权力大小。横轴代表一个人对团队项目的兴趣程度。当团队在白板或大型活动挂图上进行利益相关者分析时，效果最佳。以大格式绘制基本图，如下图所示。
为团队提供便签。要求他们写下项目或流程的可能利益相关者。利益相关者是指对项目感兴趣、可能从项目结果中受益或可能受到项目完成的工作影响的任何人。花几分钟时间讨论头脑风暴得出的名称，并丢弃团队认为实际上不是利益相关者的任何名称。

一旦团队决定了利益相关者是谁，就开始根据权力和兴趣将名字放在图表上。权力低且兴趣低的人将被放置在左下角。权力高、兴趣低的人放在左上方；权力较小但兴趣较多的人放置在右下角，权力较高且兴趣较多的人放置在右上角。如果利益相关者有一定的兴趣和权力，那么名字可能会被放在左下角右侧较高的位置，但团队并不觉得这个人太越界了。同样，放置在任何象限下限的名称可能会比放置在靠近盒子顶部的名称显示出更低的能量。 利益相关者分析使团队能够以这种方式对利益相关者进行优先级排序，即使在四个象限内也是如此。
图表的每个象限都与利益相关者的类型松散相关，为团队如何与列出的每个人或部门互动提供一些指导。
左上：保持满意
属于图表左上象限的利益相关者拥有足够的权力，可以干预项目，但他们对日常结果并不十分感兴趣。团队应确保这些人以任何与项目互动的方式感到满意。团队
也可能在项目期间的不同时间与这些人进行协商。例如，致力于解决仓库运输问题的团队可能需要在某个时候咨询采购经理，因为该团队确定需要不同类型的包装胶带。采购领导者有权获取资源，但他们可能对整个项目没有太大兴趣。
有时，团队可能会识别属于此部分的利益相关者，并意识到该特定利益相关者在项目中投入更多对团队来说是有价值的。 六西格玛领导者可以与利益相关者合作，试图将某人从低兴趣类别转移到高兴趣类别——这是一些团队用来支持项目的政治策略。
左下：最小的努力
属于左下象限的利益相关者与项目的联系最不重要。团队主要通过新闻通讯或电子邮件向这些利益相关者传达有关项目的一般信息。虽然这些利益相关者需要团队付出最少的努力，但在某些情况下，团队可能希望将利益相关者从该框移动到右下框。
右下：随时了解情况
右下框中的个人与该项目有很强的联系或兴趣，但从资源的角度来看，他们无权支持项目。这些利益相关者可能包括与正在改进的流程相关的部门的员工或将就项目的各个方面进行咨询的主题专家。
虽然右下象限的利益相关者通常无法提供资源，但他们可以以亲善大使的形式支持项目。
右上：关键人物
右上象限中的个人要么是流程的关键参与者，要么是能够为项目分配资源的执行领导者。这些是团队将在各个收费站向其报到的个人；通常，负责流程或项目最终成功的高管出现在右上象限。
开箱即用方法

“开箱即用”方法是一种快速、简单的方法，可帮助团队定义项目或流程范围。首先在白板或挂图上画一个大盒子。为团队提供标记和便签。要求团队写下要处理的流程要素，包括资源、活动和人员。每个项目都应该写在便利贴上。共同创建尽可能完整的列表，并将便签贴在墙上或桌子上。确保团队中的每个人都明白没有错误的答案，并且练习的第一阶段不需要讨论。通过对几乎没有讨论的项目进行头脑风暴，团队可以捕获更多想法，从而更准确地了解范围内和范围外的内容。
一旦制定了全面的清单，就开始作为一个团队工作，将每个项目分配到框内、线上或框外的位置。盒子外的物品将被视为超出范围
该项目。团队可能会将项目放在盒子之外，因为他们无权访问或控制这些项目，他们没有时间处理这些项目，或者他们被明确告知不要将该项目包含在项目中。
框中的项目被视为在项目范围内。这些应该是团队可以合理预期影响的流程要素。如果团队还不确定某个物品可能会落在哪里，他们应该将其放在线上。上线的项目可能是团队希望能够产生影响的项目，但团队领导者需要寻求团队外部人员的许可或帮助。
将所有物品放置在盒子上、盒子内或盒子外后，以团队形式检查放置情况并进行更改。 通过拍摄图表或在计算机上重新创建它来记录练习。当尝试控制范围蔓延或考虑他们应该向谁寻求项目帮助时，团队可能会回顾该图。
是/不是矩阵
是/不是矩阵是团队可以用来定义范围的另一个快速头脑风暴工具。它还可用于帮助定义问题陈述所需的一些信息。该矩阵的工作原理是考虑有关流程或项目的具体事项，并得出“是”和“不是”的答案。
例如，如果六西格玛团队的任务是确定为什么某个工厂的熔炉没有加热到适当的温度，他们可能会创建一个如下所示的“是/不是”矩阵。
是
不是
哪里
南厂北或东规划
什么
蒸汽炉 燃木炉

en.text: A successful define phase ends with all of the following deliverables:

o A comprehensive project statement o A team charter o An understanding of the process and a project diagram or map o An understanding of the Voice of the Customer o A definition of what success will look like that has been agreed on by the team members and any sponsors or executive leaders

zh.text: 该矩阵清楚地表明，该项目的范围仅包括南厂蒸汽炉的工作。该问题于 2015 年 1 月被发现，为团队提供了收集数据的起点。这是一个非常简单的矩阵；团队可以提出任意多的问题，以缩小范围或通过“是/不是”结构更好地理解流程和项目。
定义收费站检查表
成功的定义阶段以以下所有可交付成果结束：
o 全面的项目陈述 o 团队章程 o 对流程和项目图表或地图的理解 o 对客户之声（VOC）的理解 o 团队成员和任何发起人或执行领导者一致同意的成功定义

source.extraction: python-docx body-order extraction; source-TOC sectionized bilingual content

en.text: Moving from the define phase to the measure phase of a project, Six Sigma teams continue to delve into the process, now coming to understand processes more fully through data. The measure phase is often the most laborious phase for the team, especially when data is not already available in digital formats. In this chapter, we’ll review some of the metrics covered in previous chapters and introduce some concepts for data collection. We’ll continue building on the concepts of measure introduced in later chapters on statistical analysis.

One of the first steps of the measure phase is determining the capability of a process. This step can be completed before a team formally leaves the define phase if the data needed to perform sigma level calculations is available. Calculating sigma levels for a process was covered in Chapter 1. In addition to sigma levels, teams might also calculate various metrics for a process, including defects per million opportunities, FTY, or RTY, which were all covered in Chapter 5.

zh.text: 从项目的定义阶段到测量阶段，六西格玛团队不断深入流程，现在通过数据更全面地了解流程。测量阶段通常是团队最费力的阶段，尤其是当数据尚未以数字格式提供时。在本章中，我们将回顾前几章中介绍的一些指标，并介绍一些数据收集的概念。我们将继续以后面统计分析章节中介绍的度量概念为基础。
测量阶段的第一步是确定流程的能力。如果执行西格玛水平计算所需的数据可用，则可以在团队正式离开定义阶段之前完成此步骤。第 1 章介绍了计算流程的 sigma 级别。除了 sigma 级别之外，团队还可以计算流程的各种指标，包括每百万机会的缺陷数、FTY 或 RTY，这些都在第 5 章中介绍。

rows: 术语说明 Sigma level（西格玛水平）：衡量过程缺陷率的尺度；水平越高，单位机会中产生缺陷的概率越低。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sigma level（西格玛水平）：衡量过程缺陷率的尺度；水平越高，单位机会中产生缺陷的概率越低。
失效模式及影响分析

en.text: The Failure Modes and Effect Analysis is a tool that can be applied by a Six Sigma team in any phase from define to analyze. Often, teams begin working with FMEAs in measure because it helps them identify risk priorities for various inputs and errors within a process. Used properly, the FMEA uses systemic data and team input to set the stage for root cause analysis in the next DMAIC phase. Remember, while tollgates do occur and teams move through five phases during a DMAIC project, hard borders don’t always exist between the phases. Teams might begin working on measure phase tasks before leaving the define phase, and it’s almost certain that teams will begin some analysis while still collecting data.

Ultimately, an FMEA tool should be used when teams need more detailed information about inputs and possible associated fail-points than the tools discussed in the define chapter allow. The FMEA offers some of the information that is offered by SIPOC, but it also provides evaluations of the inputs. Teams typically create FMEAs in a spreadsheet program, as some calculations are required during the process.
To create an FMEA, create a spreadsheet with the following column headers:

1. Process step 2. Potential failure 3. Potential failure effect 4. SEV 5. Potential cause of failure 6. OCC 7. Current monitor/control 8. DET 9. RPN 10. Recommended changes/actions 11. Who and When? 12. Action completed
13. SEV 14. DET 15. RPN
Columns 1 through 9 of the FMEA might be completed during the measure phase while columns 10 through 15 are more appropriate for the improve phase.

Begin by identifying all possible process steps, activities, or inputs in column one. In column two, indicate what might go wrong for each process step. Note that you can list process steps more than once if there are multiple opportunities for error within each step. If the team has created a detailed enough list of steps, however, this won’t likely be the case for a majority of the steps.

In column three, enter a short description of the impact of the failure on the customer. Incorrect measurement can result in increased variance in a product, for example. In the SEV column, rate the severity of the possible failure you described in the previous columns. Rate the severity from 1 to 10, with 1 being no effect, 5 being minor disruption to production, and 10 being severe enough to endanger a process or person.

In column five, enter the potential reasons the specific failure might occur, and in the OCC column, enter a numeric rating for how often the failure might be expected, with 1 being a very unlikely failure and 10 being an almost inevitable failure.

In column seven, create a short description of the current controls that are in place to monitor the process or prevent the failures the team has described. In the DET column, rate the ability of the process or staff to detect failure if it does occur. Rate detection between 1 and 10, with 1 being a process that includes automated detection that rarely fails and 10 being no detection at all.
Finally, calculate the risk priority number by multiplying the severity, occurrence, and detection ratings, as in the example below.
A Six Sigma team working on a project to improve the speed with which refunds are processed to customers is creating an FMEA. One row of the FMEA includes the following information:
Process step: Refund request is entered in system.
Potential failure: Incorrect amount is entered.
Potential failure effect: The customer receives more or less refund than anticipated.
SEV: 8
Potential cause of failure: Data-entry employee transposes numbers or makes a similar typing mistake.
OCC: 10
Current monitor/control: A supervisor randomly reviews a sample of refund requests to ensure accuracy.
DET: 7
RPN: (SEV * OCC * DET) = (8*10*7) = 560
The team completes a second row as follows:
Process step: Refund check is printed.
Potential failure: The printed check has defects that make it difficult to cash.
Potential failure effect: The customer can’t cash the check and has to call for a new one.
SEV: 9
Potential cause of failure: Printer is misaligned or out of ink.
OCC: 1
Current monitor/control: The person who signs the checks reviews the checks as they sign them.
DET: 2
RPN: (SEV * OCC * DET) = (9*1*2) = 18

The potential failure in the first example has a much higher risk priority number, which means, as the team moves forward, they are more likely to work on solving that potential failure. During analyze and improve phases, the team would recommend changes, implement the recommended actions, and rescore the process to determine if the RPN of the changed process is lower. If it is higher or the same, then the change was not a good one and the team might need to try again.

zh.text: 故障模式和影响分析是六西格玛团队在从定义到分析的任何阶段都可以应用的工具。通常，团队开始使用 FMEA 进行衡量，因为它可以帮助他们确定流程中各种输入和错误的风险优先级。如果使用得当，FMEA 可以使用系统数据和团队输入为下一个 DMAIC 阶段的根本原因分析奠定基础。请记住，虽然在 DMAIC 项目期间确实会出现收费站并且团队会经历五个阶段，但阶段之间并不总是存在硬边界。团队可能会在离开定义阶段之前开始处理测量阶段任务，并且几乎可以肯定团队将在收集数据的同时开始一些分析。

rows: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
zh.text: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。

rows: 术语说明 FMEA：失效模式与影响分析，用于提前识别风险、影响和控制措施。
zh.text: 术语说明 FMEA：失效模式与影响分析，用于提前识别风险、影响和控制措施。

rows: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
zh.text: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
最终，当团队需要比定义章节中讨论的工具更详细的有关输入和可能的相关故障点的信息时，应该使用 FMEA 工具。 FMEA 提供了 SIPOC 提供的一些信息，但它还提供了对输入的评估。团队通常在电子表格程序中创建 FMEA，因为在此过程中需要进行一些计算。

rows: 术语说明 SIPOC：供应商、输入、流程、输出、客户；用于快速界定流程边界。
zh.text: 术语说明 SIPOC：供应商、输入、流程、输出、客户；用于快速界定流程边界。
要创建 FMEA，请创建一个包含以下列标题的电子表格：
1. 过程步骤 2. 潜在故障 3. 潜在故障影响 4. SEV 5. 潜在故障原因 6. OCC 7. 电流监测/控制 8. DET 9. RPN 10. 建议的更改/操作 11. 谁以及何时？ 12. 行动完成
13. SEV 14. DET 15. RPN
FMEA 的第 1 至 9 列可能在测量阶段完成，而第 10 至 15 列更适合改进阶段。
首先确定第一列中所有可能的流程步骤、活动或输入。在第二栏中，指出每个流程步骤可能出现的问题。请注意，如果每个步骤中存在多次出错机会，则可以多次列出流程步骤。但是，如果团队创建了足够详细的步骤列表，则大多数步骤可能不会出现这种情况。
在第三列中，输入故障对客户影响的简短描述。例如，不正确的测量可能会导致产品差异增加。在 SEV 列中，对您在前列中描述的可能故障的严重性进行评级。将严重程度从 1 到 10 进行评级，1 表示没有影响，5 表示对生产造成轻微干扰，10 表示严重程度足以危及流程或人员。
在第五列中，输入可能发生特定故障的潜在原因，并在 OCC 列中，输入预期发生故障的频率的数字评级，其中 1 表示极不可能发生故障，10 表示几乎不可避免的故障。
在第七列中，创建当前控制措施的简短描述，这些控制措施用于监控流程或防止团队描述的故障。在 DET 列中，对流程或员工检测故障（如果确实发生）的能力进行评级。检测率介于 1 到 10 之间，1 表示包含很少失败的自动检测的过程，10 表示根本没有检测。
最后，通过将严重性、发生率和检测等级相乘来计算风险优先级编号，如下例所示。
六西格玛团队致力于提高客户退款处理速度的项目，正在创建 FMEA。 FMEA 的一行包含以下信息：
处理步骤：在系统中输入退款请求。
潜在故障：输入的金额不正确。
潜在的失败影响：客户收到的退款比预期多或少。
严重程度：8
潜在的失败原因： 数据输入员工调换了数字或犯了类似的打字错误。
奥委会：10
当前监控/控制：主管随机审查退款请求样本以确保准确性。

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
检测：7
RPN：(SEV * OCC * DET) = (8*10*7) = 560
该团队完成第二行如下：
处理步骤：打印退款支票。
潜在故障：打印的支票存在缺陷，导致难以兑现。
潜在的失败影响：客户无法兑现支票，必须要求开一张新支票。
严重程度：9
故障的潜在原因：打印机未对准或缺墨。
专科中心：1
当前监控/控制：签署支票的人在签署支票时检查支票。
检测：2
RPN：(SEV * OCC * DET) = (9*1*2) = 18
第一个示例中的潜在故障具有更高的风险优先级编号，这意味着随着团队的前进，他们更有可能致力于解决该潜在故障。在分析和改进阶段，团队将建议更改，实施建议的操作，并对流程重新评分，以确定更改后的流程的 RPN 是否较低。如果它更高或相同，则说明更改不是一个好的更改，团队可能需要再次尝试。

en.text: Creating a baseline metric for a process begins in the define phase, but teams cannot leave the measure phase without a strong understanding of current process performance. That understanding begins with figures such as sigma level, but teams should also define a process-specific metric where possible and gather historical data regarding that metric so they have something to compare future data against to prove that improvements were made.

Ideally, the team would have access to historical metrics for the process. In some cases, the team has to collect data from scratch. We’ll introduce data collection later in this chapter and cover it in depth in the units on sampling.

Continuous versus discrete data Before creating and displaying a baseline metric via graphical representation, you have to understand the type of data you are dealing with. Data is either discrete or continuous, and teams collect data either as a population sample or a process sample. How teams collect data and the type of data collected determine how the data can be viewed graphically and analyzed.
Discrete Data

Discrete data is categorical in nature; it is also referred to as qualitative data or attribute data. Discrete data falls into three categories: ordinal, nominal, and binary, or attribute, data; some data collected can be expressed in one or more of the discrete categories. For example, student test scores can be conveyed in an ordinal fashion via the grades A, B, C, D, and F or in a binary fashion via the Pass/Fail distinction.

Discrete data can be displayed via Pareto charts, pie charts, and bar charts. In some instances, the data can be converted to run and control charts using variation within the data or ratios as the item being charted. In the chapter on the control phase, you’ll begin understanding why a team might want to convert discrete data to be used in a control chart.

Within discrete data, binary or attribute data is usually the easiest data to collect. Attribute data records one of the other answers. Does the person choose paper or plastic? Is the room hot or cold? Is the glass empty or full? Is the light on or off? Depending on the scenario, attribute data can be very accurate. The light is either on or off; the switch position tells you that. Attribute data in this case can be automated with the right technology, which means it would be highly accurate. Whether the cup is empty or full is another story, because there are so many variations between completely empty and completely full. If the data is being collected by people, personal biases might enter the equation. Teams can remove some of those biases and better ensure accurate measurements, which will be covered in the sections on measurement systems.
Continuous Data

Continuous data is quantitative data and is measured in units. For example, the time of day is measured in hours. Temperature is measured in degrees, and almost anything can be converted to continuous data by making it a percentage.

Continuous data is visualized in graphs such as histograms and box plots. Box plots are discussed in chapter 14, and histograms are covered in depth in the chapters on statistics. Continuous data can also be viewed in the form of run and control charts.
Choosing Between Discrete and Continuous Data

Sometimes, a process or activity can be measured in both discrete or continuous data. Depending on the purpose of the measurements, teams might need to pick between the two data types. For example, if a Six Sigma team has identified room temperature as an input into the quality of product, they will want to monitor the temperature of the room. They can do so by recording the temperature in degrees every ten minutes; that data would be continuous. Alternatively, the team might create a tick sheet, having someone make a mark every hour to note whether the temperature was in the 40s, 50s, 60s, 70s, or 80s with regard to the Fahrenheit scale. That data would be discrete.

In this particular example, most teams would choose to record the continuous data. Exact temperature measurements every 10 minutes provides a lot more information than whether the temperature of the room was in the 70s at the turn of the hour. The continuous data could be converted to provide teams with the discrete data easily; the discrete data in this case – and in most cases – could not be converted to continuous data.
What is true in the example is true for most scenarios. When possible, teams should convert measurements to continuous data. Continuous data:
Provides more information than discrete data does.
Is typically more time-consuming to collect than discrete data unless teams have access to automated or computerized data collection.
Is more precise than discrete data.
Lets teams remove variation and errors inherent in estimation and rounding.
Levels of Data
Data can be classified at four basic levels: nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio. Attribute, or binary, data is actually a limited form of nominal data.
Nominal Data

Nominal is considered to be the lowest data classification level and simply involves applying number labels to a qualitative description so statistical analysis programs and tests can be applied to the data. The numbers assigned to each category don’t provide any information about whether the data is better or worse than other data in the listing – in nominal data, numbers don’t reflect a scale.
An example of nominal data might be applied in a list of birth states for a classroom. In a class of 30, the number of students born in various states breaks down as follows:
Texas: 6
Louisiana: 4
Arkansas: 10
Mississippi: 1
Oklahoma: 9
In nominal data, each state would be provided a numeric label:
1. Texas 2. Louisiana 3. Arkansas 4. Mississippi 5. Oklahoma
That doesn’t mean 5 students are from Oklahoma; it means 9 students fall into category 5 for the question “What state were you born in?”
For nominal data, central tendencies are calculated not with means or medians, but with mode. For example, a list of the nominal data in our example would be as follows:
The mode is the number that appears most in the set; in this case, 3.
Statistically, analysis is limited with regard to nominal data, but some tests can be performed with statistical analysis software.
Ordinal Data

Ordinal data is considered to be a higher form of data than nominal, though it still uses numbers and categories to identify data elements. With ordinal data, though, the numbers themselves actually provide some meaning. The numbers used in the FMEA scales at the beginning of this chapter were ordinal data. The numbers are qualitative in nature, but they are also ranked. Central tendencies with ordinal data are measured by either the mode or the median, and common uses for this type of data include ranking various things against each other or rating a specific thing, such as a movie or pain level.

Ordinal data can be arranged in an order that makes sense: on a 1 to 10 scale, Suzy rated the movies as 2, 5, 6, and 9. If one is the worst and 10 is the best, then we can assume Suzy liked the last movie best.

While ordinal data comes with a logical order, the intervals between the numbers don’t mean anything. If Suzy rated movie A as a 10 and movie B as a 9, the conclusion is that she liked movie A better. Exactly how much more she liked the movie is not discernible using ordinal data.
Interval Data

Interval data is an even higher form of data classification. Interval data provides numeric values that can be arranged in a logical order with meaning; the big difference between ordinal and interval data is that the difference between each interval value provides meaning. If Frank is keeping track of the temperature in his house and he sees that at 8:00 a.m. it was 76 degrees and at 9:00 a.m. it was 80 degrees, he not only knows that 9:00 a.m. was hotter; he knows that it was 4 degrees hotter at 9:00 a.m. than at 8:00 a.m. Interval data is continuous, or quantitative, and offers more flexibility when it comes to statistical analysis.
Ratio Data

Ratio data is considered to be the highest of the data classifications. Ratio data has an absolute zero point, can be both discrete or continuous in nature, and provides the highest capabilities for statistical analysis in many cases. Some examples of information that can be recorded using ratio data include force, defects per million opportunities, voltage, height, units per hour, and volume.
Choosing the Best Measurement Systems

Measurement systems analysis applies scientific principles to help teams analyze how much variation a system of measurement brings to a process. The purpose of the MSA is to identify errors of accuracy within data collection tools. Teams can then redress measurement systems to create more accurate data captures or, if that isn’t possible, take the possibility of errors into account when performing analysis on data.
During measurement systems analysis, teams should review multiple components of possible measurement error. Six Sigma teams analyze:
Whether bias occurs in the accuracy of measurements
Whether the measurement has the proper resolution
What measurement scale linearity exists
Whether measurement activities are stable over time
Whether measurements are repeatable and reproducible
Depending on the measurements the team is dealing with, the MSA can be time consuming and is often why the measure phase of a DMAIC project is one of the longest.
Creating Accuracy

In this stage of MSA, teams define the difference between the most accurate measurement possible and the data being collected by the current measurement system. The goal of a measurement system is accuracy: coming as close as possible to a defined target, if not the exact measurement. For example, in a computer manufacturing plant, one employee might solder a chip to the motherboard. For the rest of the chips and wires to be added to the motherboard, the chip must be placed within a 2 mm area. In this case, a measurement tool might be implemented with a required accuracy of plus or minus 0.5 mm to ensure the chip is placed within the area targeted.

Teams can ensure accuracy of data by verifying that the gauge used to collect data is performing accurately. If a digital scale is being used to weigh ingredients, teams should calibrate the scale using calibrated weights. If templates are used on a factory floor to make measurements more efficient, teams should ensure those templates are accurate by comparing them against known measurement tools such as verified rulers and scales. Note that, for the purpose of the MSA, accuracy reflects the performance of the measurement tool, not the operator. Whether the employee uses the measurement tool correctly or records the amount correctly is considered a concern of precision, which is covered later in this chapter under R&R Gages.

Once a Six Sigma team is confident that a measurement tool is properly calibrated, they can instruct employees or others who are responsible for recording data. Data should be accepted as it is collected for most efficient access and because early review can turn up specific problems with data collection. When possible, teams should not round data but collect it as it is recorded.

If data is being collected manually, employees should have a data collection template that prompts them to collect data at appropriate times and record information about the data collection event, including the person collecting the data, the machine or process involved, conditions of the environment – especially those that are different from normal conditions or might have a direct impact on measurements – and the measurement tool being used if multiple tools are an option. These details help Six Sigma teams rule out outliers, which are discussed in the next chapter.

Before measurements are passed to the analyze phase, Six Sigma experts should review data to ensure there are no misplaced or missing decimal points, that duplicate entries haven’t been recorded, that frequency-based measurements aren’t missing points, or that any other obvious issues haven’t occurred with the data. Addressing obvious data problems before beginning analyze phases reduces the chance that teams will come to false conclusions about root causes or viable solutions for a process.
Addressing Resolution

Measuring at the correct resolution ensures that a measurement system can detect change in the data or process appropriately. For example, if a Six Sigma team is working to improve a process that cuts pipe for bathroom fixture installations, it might be concerned with the length of the pipe. In reviewing the measurement system for the cut pipe, the team finds that the process includes measuring the pipe to the nearest centimeter. If, however, pipes that are off by several millimeters cause issues in the installation, then the nearest centimeter measurement is not a small enough resolution.

A good rule of thumb to follow for resolution is called the 10-bucket rule. Break your measurement resolution into a tenth of what is required. If the pipe must measure within a range of 5 mm to perform, the measuring tool should measure to the ½ mm. In another example, a food service department might be tasked with maintaining the correct temperature in a freezer. To monitor the temperature, an employee records the temperature once per hour. If temperatures fluctuate quickly in the freezer, a change in temperature that would impact quality of food or ingredients might come and go between recordings. In this case, the proper resolution might be gained by recording measurements every 10 minutes or every six minutes for 10 readings per hour. Even better, in a freezer with a digital thermostat connected to a network, teams might be able to access readings recorded every minute.

Resolution is usually one of the easiest things to correct within a measuring system, but it isn’t always cost-effective or plausible to measure at the most detailed resolutions. Teams should consider resource

requirements when developing a measurement system. If, however, the most detailed resolution is possible, measurements obtained will provide more information about the process and a larger sample size from which to work.
Adjusting for Errors of Linearity

Linearity describes how a measurement system performs across a range. A standard metric ruler in the hands of most people is fairly accurate at measuring centimeters, but is less accurate at measuring millimeters or kilometers. A scale with a range between 0 and 10 kilograms might measure less accurately at either end of the range.

Taking measurements at various ranges with an existing measurement system and comparing those measurements to data gathered with tools known to be accurate across all ranges can help teams find errors of linearity. In some cases, teams can develop mathematical equations to account for the discrepancies. For example, if the scale is accurate at 5 kilograms, but is off by an extra quarter of a kilogram for each kilogram thereafter, a measurement of 8.5 kilograms would actually be:
If mathematical adjustments are not possible, then teams should not use measurement systems to measure ranges where linearity errors regularly occur.
Stability

Stability describes the consistency of measurements over time. If operators are measuring in the same way and using the same tools – and those tools don’t have any of the other problems described above – then measurements should reflect stability on a control chart. Control charts are introduced in Chapter 16 and covered in depth in later chapters on statistical process control.

If the variation of measurements, as reflected on a control chart, do not indicate stability, then teams might want to first rule out a problem with the measurement system before determining that the process is out of control.
Gage R&R

Gage R&R tools are used to ensure repeatability and reproducibility with regard to measurement systems. In most cases, Gage R&R tools apply to measurement systems that involve human operators and appraisers. Six Sigma teams apply Gage R&R tests to find weaknesses within such measurement systems.

In Gage R&R testing, repeatability means that a single employee, using the same measurement system and appraising the same things, can repeat his or her measurements. Reproducibility means that multiple employees using the same measurement system and appraising the same things come up with measurements that match or are very close to matching.

Most Gage R&R tests fall into two types: attribute and variable. The premise for testing each type of measurement is the same, though the criteria and statistical analysis following the test differ slightly.
Attribute Gage R&R

An attribute Gage R&R is used when Six Sigma teams are analyzing measurement systems for go/no go data. For example, if operators review an item in the product line and decide simply to pass or fail it, this

would be an attribute measurement. In the example of the freezer measurements above, an employee might simply be tasked with recording whether the temperature was in an appropriate range: a yes/no measurement. As previously stated, attribute measurements provide the least information about a process, so in the case of the freezer temperature, it’s better to record the actual temperature. Whether that recording was within appropriate range can be determined systemically from the temperature data.
When attribute data is used, an attribute Gage R&R is used to test the measurement system following the steps below.

1. Select at least two appraisers. 2. Provide a number of samples. Label the samples in a way that you know which one is which but that wouldn’t identify the sample for the appraiser. 3. Record the actual attribute measurement for each sample according to the best possible (most accurate) measurement you have. 4. Have each appraiser record the attribute measurement for each sample provided (go/no go; yes/no; hot/cold; pass/fail; etc.). 5. Repeat the process with the same samples and appraiser, randomizing the order in which you present the samples. Randomizing sample order the second time appraisers are presented with them reduces the chance that appraisers remember what measurement they recorded the first time and record the same measurement by default. 6. Enter all data into a spreadsheet or Gage R&R file similar to the one below that shows a test of a pass/fail measurement.

From the Gage R&R above, you can see that the measurement system is reproducible only 50 percent of the time, making it a poor measurement system. It is repeatable 90 percent of the time for Appraiser 1

and 80 percent of the time for Appraiser 2, and the appraisers are accurate 80 percent and 70 percent of the time respectively. Given these results, a Six Sigma team might determine that there is some problem of clarity with instructions for how to determine whether a sample is a pass or a fail. The chart above only provides data for a set of 10 samples; more accurate attribute Gage R&R testing usually requires at least 20 data points.
Variable Gage R&R

Not all data is attribute data, which is why teams can also perform variable Gage R&R tests. While the raw data from a variable Gage R&R test can provide a Six Sigma team with a picture of whether a measurement system is obviously failing or not, statistical analysis is usually required to make a true determination about the performance of a measurement system. This is because, with variable measurements, some differences between measurements and operators is likely, particularly when measuring to very small or large figures or capturing data in a moving measurement.

Set up a variable Gage R&R test in much the same way you set up an attribute test, using two to three appraisers and at least five to ten outputs to be measured. Have each appraiser measure each sample two or three times, randomizing the order in which samples are presented to avoid appraisers remembering the measurements initially entered. Record all data on a variable Gage R&R template, such as the example below.
The statistical analysis performed in Excel SPC or Minitab by a Black Belt or Green Belt typically returns four figures:
% Study Variation
% Tolerance
% Contribution
Number of distinct categories

Teams should look to ensure all four elements of a variable Gage R&R test calculation are in what are considered “safe” ranges. Commonly, each element comes with a scale for safe, or green, zones along with caution zones and failure zones. If one of the elements falls into a caution zone and all others into

the green, then a team will likely conclude that the measurement system is sufficient. In some cases, all or a majority of caution zone scores might be deemed acceptable, particularly if making the measurement system any more accurate would be costly or cause application issues for other processes. Measurement systems that score in the failure zone for any element should probably be repaired or replaced.
Common criteria used to judge each element of the variable Gage R&R calculations are as follows:

Note: Another tool that is effective in identifying variation in a measurement system is called the ANOVA, or Analysis of Variance. ANOVA is also useful for analyzing variation of any type, and will be covered in Unit 5 on intermediate statistics.
Collecting Data Samples

Once teams are sure the best possible measurement tools are in place, they can begin collecting data to be used in the analyze phase of the DMAIC project. The most accurate conclusions come when a team can analyze data for the entire population, but that is rarely possible due to time and cost constraints. If you can gain access to automated data or data warehouses, you might be able to collect population data or extremely large sample sizes that better approximate population data. Otherwise, Six Sigma teams must randomly sample the population that is available and use those samples to draw conclusions about the population as a whole.

To ensure samples can be used to draw statistical conclusions, they must be handled correctly and be the appropriate size. In this section, we’ll simply cover the types of sampling strategies that Six Sigma teams might use and why.
Simple Random Sampling

Simple random sampling works when there is an equal chance that any item within the population will be chosen. For example, if you put 20 marbles of the exact size, weight, and texture in a bag and blindly select one, each marble in the bag has a 1 in 20 chance at being selected. If the marbles are different sizes or weights, those differing attributes can impact the chance that each marble will be selected. Heavier marbles might sort to the bottom of the bag; bigger marbles might be more likely to be picked up.

Random sampling for statistical analysis requires that the sample will represent similar attributes and percentages as the entire population. The population is “N” items large. The sample size is “n” items large. How big the sample needs to be to statistically represent the population is decided by a number of factors.
Stratified Sampling

Stratified sampling occurs when the population as a whole is divided or can be divided into subgroups with differing attributes. For example, if a shipping company wants to test the accuracy of its estimated shipping times against actual shipping times, it might assume that the results will vary according to the distance a package has to travel. By randomly selecting samples from the entire package population, there’s a chance the company might only end up with samples for packages delivered within a 200-mile radius.
To prevent bias in the data, the shipping company might divide the population into four subgroups:
Deliveries within 200-mile radius
Deliveries within 201 to 400-mile radius
Deliveries within 401 to 600-mile radius
Deliveries over 600 miles
By sampling randomly from the stratified subgroups, the team ensures a sample size with less bias.
Sequential Sampling

Sequential sampling involves selecting every X item for inclusion in the sampling. Sequential sampling can be used when teams are collecting data at intervals such as time. The team might collect data every 10 minutes. Sequential sampling can also be used to sample physical items; every 5th item on a product line might be reviewed. Given the right parameters and enough time, sequential sampling can provide valid statistical results. Teams must be cognizant, however, that the sequence of the sampling could, in rare cases, skew results. It is possible, for example, that something occurs during every 5th iteration of a process that causes a difference to occur.
Samples that Aren’t Random

Non-random sampling should not be used when dealing with statistical analysis because it is more likely to introduce user or sampling error. While all sampling comes with some form of error, random sampling errors can be calculated and accounted for in analysis. The same cannot be said of non-random samples.

Non-random sampling includes convenience or judgment sampling. Convenience sampling occurs when a team takes the most convenient measurements. “We want to know about the process right now, so let’s review the next dozen items that come off the line.” That type of analysis only truly tells the team how the process performed at that exact moment in time.

Judgment sampling occurs when an expert or knowledgeable person is tasked with “selecting” appropriate samples. A supervisor might say to his or her team members, “Select some of your work that represents the normal way you function in a given day.” In most cases, the team members select what they believe is better quality work, skewing any results from the sample.
Delivering a Baseline Metric

One of the major deliverables coming out of define and measure phases is the baseline metric. How is the process performing now, and what measurement will the team use to compare current performance to post-improvement performance?

Baseline metrics are numbers, but most teams find that presenting the metric graphically resonates best with business resources and executives. Visual representations also provide teams with a quick way to determine if progress is occurring.

The type of visual representation you use depends on whether your major metric is discrete or continuous. Discrete data can be displayed on Pareto charts (see Chapter 5) and continuous data can be displayed via run charts. You can also use variation or other calculations to convert discrete data to continuous data for display in run charts and control charts (see Chapter 16 for information about control charts).
Run Charts

A run chart can be used to monitor the performance of any variable or process over time. With a single, intuitive chart, Six Sigma teams can display trends, shifts, and cycles within a process; they can also monitor a process for concerns, though run charts are not as effective at this as the very similar control chart is.

A basic run chart is simply a line plot of the data over time, which means anyone can create the chart. Most Six Sigma run charts also feature a line representing the median of all data points for visual reference. Depending on the type of information being charted, you may need to convert data to a ratio for a more accurate run chart. For example, if you are plotting the temperature of a surface over time, there is no need to convert data. If you are plotting the number of patients readmitted to a hospital within 30 days of being discharged, then it helps to convert the data to a percentage of the number of patients discharged within the same time period. In a 30-day period where 10,000 patients were discharged, you can expect a higher number of returns than a period during which only 5,000 patients are discharged.

The figure below illustrates a run chart of temperature over time. You can see how temperature changes through time and begin to see some possible trends. A Six Sigma team would be able to zoom out, viewing the run chart over more time to validate trend assumptions. You can also see that the median temperature for the process is 33.

The run chart below indicates the number of returns per hundred sales for each month of the year. You can see that returns as a rate of sales increases steadily during the first part of the year before holding steady from May through November. The orange line indicates the median returns per hundreds sales, which is just under 7.
Create Basic Run Charts in Excel

Statistical analysis software, including Minitab and Excel SPC, creates all elements of a run chart automatically from entered data, but anyone can use basic Excel functions to create a run chart if needed.
First, create a data table.

Creating a data table for a single attribute, such as temperature, just requires entering the time labels in one column and the attribute measurements in another. For the example, we’ll walk through creating a rate data table, since it involves additional steps.

1. Enter the data labels (month, week, hour, etc.) in the first column of Excel. 2. In the next column, enter the corresponding measurements for the attribute you are interested in: in this case, the total number of returns per month. 3. In the third column, enter the total number of items you are comparing the attribute to: in this case, the total number of sales per month.

4. In the fourth column, calculate the percentage the first column of data is of the second. In this case, the percentage of returns per sales for each month. The calculation is achieved in this case by the formula =B2/C2 for January, =B3/C3 for February, and so forth.

5. Decide whether you want to create a run chart showing percentages, or if you would like to create the chart illustrating rate per 100, per 1,000, etc. 6. If you want to illustrate a rate per (x), multiply the percentage calculation in the fourth column by (x). In this case, the figures in column D are multiplied by 100.

7. Use Excel to calculate the median of the number you plan to chart. The median is calculated with the formula =Median(Number 1, Number 2,…), where the numbers in the formula correlate with the range of all the charted data points. In this case, the median is 6.96679 8. Highlight the data labels (in this case, column A) and the figures to be charted (column E)
9. Select Insert > Charts > Line Chart to insert a line chart of the attribute or attribute calculation.
10. Select Insert > Shape > Draw Line.
11. Draw a line on your run chart approximately where the median would be. Use Excel tools to select a color and thickness for the line that you desire.

The completed run chart can be used to present information to the Six Sigma team or include a graphical representation of baseline process performance in a measure phase tollgate presentation. Again, it should be noted that manual creation of a run chart is not required for most Black Belts and Green Belts, who will have access to statistical analysis software.

zh.text: 为流程创建基线指标从定义阶段开始，但如果团队对当前流程绩效没有深入了解，就无法离开衡量阶段。这种理解始于西格玛水平等数字，但团队还应该尽可能定义特定于流程的指标，并收集有关该指标的历史数据，以便他们可以比较未来的数据，以证明已做出改进。

rows: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
zh.text: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
理想情况下，团队可以访问流程的历史指标。在某些情况下，团队必须从头开始收集数据。我们将在本章后面介绍数据收集，并在采样单元中深入介绍它。
连续数据与离散数据 在通过图形表示创建和显示基线指标之前，您必须了解正在处理的数据类型。数据可以是离散的，也可以是连续的，团队可以将数据收集为总体样本或过程样本。 团队如何收集数据以及收集的数据类型决定了如何以图形方式查看和分析数据。

rows: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
离散数据
离散数据本质上是分类的；它也称为定性数据或属性数据。离散数据分为三类：有序数据、名义数据和二进制数据或属性数据；收集到的一些数据可以用一个或多个离散类别来表示。例如，学生考试成绩可以通过等级 A、B、C、D 和 F 以顺序方式表示，或者通过通过/失败区别以二进制方式表示。
离散数据可以通过帕累托图、饼图和条形图显示。在某些情况下，可以使用数据或比率内的变化作为绘制图表的项目，将数据转换为运行图和控制图。在有关控制阶段的章节中，您将开始理解为什么团队可能希望转换离散数据以在控制图中使用。

rows: 术语说明 Control Chart（控制图）：用时间序列和控制界限判断过程是否处于受控状态。
zh.text: 术语说明 Control Chart（控制图）：用时间序列和控制界限判断过程是否处于受控状态。

rows: 术语说明 Pareto（帕累托）：按影响大小排序，常用于找出“少数关键原因”。
zh.text: 术语说明 Pareto（帕累托）：按影响大小排序，常用于找出“少数关键原因”。

在离散数据中，二进制或属性数据通常是最容易收集的数据。属性数据记录其他答案之一。人们选择纸还是塑料？房间热还是冷？杯子是空的还是满的？灯亮还是灭？根据场景的不同，属性数据可能非常准确。灯要么亮，要么灭；开关位置告诉你这一点。在这种情况下，属性数据可以通过正确的技术实现自动化，这意味着它会非常准确。杯子是空的还是满的则是另一回事，因为完全空和完全满之间有很多差异。如果数据是由人们收集的，那么个人偏见可能会出现。团队可以消除其中一些偏差并更好地确保准确的测量，这将在测量系统部分中介​​绍。
连续数据
连续数据是定量数据，以单位计量。例如，一天中的时间以小时为单位。温度以度为单位测量，几乎任何东西都可以通过百分比转换为连续数据。
连续数据以直方图和箱线图等图表形式可视化。箱线图将在第 14 章中讨论，直方图将在统计章节中进行深入介绍。连续数据也可以以运行图和控制图的形式查看。
在离散数据和连续数据之间进行选择

有时，过程或活动可以用离散数据或连续数据来衡量。根据测量的目的，团队可能需要在两种数据类型之间进行选择。例如，如果六西格玛团队已将室温确定为产品质量的输入，他们将希望监控房间的温度。他们可以通过每十分钟记录一次温度（以度为单位）来做到这一点；该数据将是连续的。或者，团队可能会创建一个刻度表，让某人每小时做一个标记，以记录华氏温度是否在 40 度、50 度、60 度、70 度或 80 度之间。该数据将是离散的。
在这个特定的例子中，大多数团队会选择记录连续数据。每 10 分钟进行一次精确的温度测量可以提供比整点房间温度是否在 70 多度更多的信息。连续数据可以轻松转换为团队提供离散数据；在这种情况下以及在大多数情况下，离散数据无法转换为连续数据。
示例中的情况适用于大多数情况。如果可能，团队应将测量结果转换为连续数据。连续数据：
比离散数据提供更多信息。
除非团队能够进行自动化或计算机化的数据收集，否则收集数据通常比离散数据更耗时。
比离散数据更精确。
让团队消除估计和舍入中固有的变化和错误。
数据级别
数据可以分为四个基本级别：名义、序数、区间和比率。属性或二进制数据实际上是名义数据的有限形式。
标称数据
名义被认为是最低的数据分类级别，仅涉及将数字标签应用于定性描述，以便可以将统计分析程序和测试应用于数据。分配给每个类别的数字并不提供有关数据是否比列表中其他数据更好或更差的任何信息 - 在名义数据中，数字不反映规模。
名义数据的示例可以应用于教室的出生州列表。 在一个 30 人的班级中，出生在各个州的学生人数细分如下：
德克萨斯州：6
路易斯安那州：4
阿肯色州：10
密西西比州：1
俄克拉荷马州：9
在名义数据中，每个状态都会有一个数字标签：
1. 德克萨斯州 2. 路易斯安那州 3. 阿肯色州 4. 密西西比州 5. 俄克拉荷马州
这并不意味着 5 名学生来自俄克拉荷马州；这意味着对于“你出生在哪个州？”这个问题，有 9 名学生属于第 5 类。
对于名义数据，集中趋势不是用均值或中位数计算的，而是用众数计算的。例如，我们示例中的标称数据列表如下：
众数是集合中出现次数最多的数字；在这种情况下，3。
从统计学上来说，分析仅限于名义数据，但可以使用统计分析软件进行一些测试。
序数数据
序数数据被认为是比名义数据更高的形式，尽管它仍然使用数字和类别来标识数据元素。然而，对于序数数据，数字本身实际上提供了一些含义。本章开头的 FMEA 量表中使用的数字是序数数据。这些数字本质上是定性的，但它们也是排名的。序数数据的中心趋势通过众数或中位数来衡量，此类数据的常见用途包括对各种事物进行相互排名或对特定事物进行评级，例如电影或疼痛程度。
序数数据可以按有意义的顺序排列：在 1 到 10 的范围内，Suzy 将电影评分为 2、5、6 和 9。如果其中一个是最差的，10 是最好的，那么我们可以假设 Suzy 最喜欢最后一部电影。
虽然序数数据具有逻辑顺序，但数字之间的间隔没有任何意义。如果 Suzy 将电影 A 评为 10 分，将电影 B 评为 9 分，则结论是她更喜欢电影 A。使用序数数据无法辨别她到底有多喜欢这部电影。
区间数据

区间数据是数据分类的更高形式。区间数据提供可以按逻辑顺序排列且有意义的数值； 序数数据和区间数据之间的最大区别在于每个区间值之间的差异提供了意义。如果 Frank 记录他家里的温度，他发现上午 8:00 的温度为 76 度，上午 9:00 的温度为 80 度，那么他不仅知道上午 9:00 更热，而且还知道上午 9:00 的温度更高。他知道上午 9:00 的气温比上午 8:00 的气温高 4 度。间隔数据是连续的或定量的，并且在统计分析方面提供了更大的灵活性。
比率数据
比率数据被认为是最高的数据分类。比率数据具有绝对零点，本质上可以是离散的或连续的，并且在许多情况下提供最高的统计分析功能。可以使用比率数据记录的信息的一些示例包括力、每百万次机会的缺陷、电压、高度、每小时的单位和体积。
选择最佳的测量系统
测量系统分析应用科学原理来帮助团队分析测量系统给流程带来的变化程度。 MSA 的目的是识别数据收集工具中的准确性错误。然后，团队可以纠正测量系统以创建更准确的数据捕获，或者，如果不可能，则在对数据进行分析时考虑错误的可能性。
在测量系统分析期间，团队应检查可能的测量误差的多个组成部分。 六西格玛队分析：
测量精度是否存在偏差
测量分辨率是否合适
存在什么测量尺度线性
测量活动是否随时间稳定
测量结果是否可重复、可再现
根据团队正在处理的测量，MSA 可能非常耗时，这通常也是 DMAIC 项目的测量阶段最长的原因之一。
创造准确性
在 MSA 的这一阶段，团队定义可能的最准确测量与当前测量系统收集的数据之间的差异。测量系统的目标是准确性：即使不是精确的测量，也尽可能接近定义的目标。例如，在计算机制造工厂中，一名员工可能会将芯片焊接到主板上。 对于要添加到主板上的其余芯片和电线，芯片必须放置在 2 毫米的区域内。在这种情况下，测量工具可能需要达到正负 0.5 毫米的精度，以确保芯片放置在目标区域内。

rows: 术语说明 MSA（测量系统分析）：评估测量工具、人员和方法是否足以支持可靠决策。
zh.text: 术语说明 MSA（测量系统分析）：评估测量工具、人员和方法是否足以支持可靠决策。

团队可以通过验证用于收集数据的仪表是否准确运行来确保数据的准确性。如果使用数字秤来称量原料，团队应使用校准砝码来校准秤。如果在工厂车间使用模板来提高测量效率，团队应通过将这些模板与已知的测量工具（例如经过验证的尺子和秤）进行比较来确保这些模板的准确性。请注意，就 MSA 而言，精度反映的是测量工具的性能，而不是操作员的性能。员工是否正确使用测量工具或正确记录数量被认为是精度问题，这将在本章后面的 R&R 量具中介绍。
一旦六西格玛团队确信测量工具已正确校准，他们就可以指导员工或其他负责记录数据的人员。应接受数据，因为收集数据是为了最有效地访问，并且早期审查可能会发现数据收集的具体问题。如果可能，团队不应舍入数据，而应按记录收集数据。
如果是手动收集数据，员工应该有一个数据收集模板，提示他们在适当的时间收集数据并记录有关数据收集事件的信息，包括收集数据的人员、涉及的机器或过程、环境条件（尤其是那些与正常条件不同或可能对测量产生直接影响的环境条件）以及所使用的测量工具（如果可以选择多种工具）。这些细节有助于六西格玛团队排除异常值，这将在下一章中讨论。
在测量进入分析阶段之前，六西格玛专家应审查数据，以确保没有放错位置或丢失小数点，没有记录重复条目，基于频率的测量没有丢失点，或者数据没有出现任何其他明显问题。 在开始分析阶段之前解决明显的数据问题可以减少团队对流程的根本原因或可行解决方案得出错误结论的机会。
寻址分辨率
以正确的分辨率进行测量可确保测量系统能够正确检测数据或过程的变化。例如，如果六西格玛团队正在努力改进用于浴室固定装置安装的管道切割工艺，则可能会关心管道的长度。在审查切割管道的测量系统时，团队发现该过程包括将管道测量到最接近的厘米。然而，如果偏离几毫米的管道导致安装出现问题，那么最接近的厘米测量分辨率就不够小。

解决问题时需要遵循的一个好的经验法则称为“10 桶规则”。将您的测量分辨率分解为所需分辨率的十分之一。如果管道必须在 5 毫米范围内进行测量，则测量工具应测量到 ½ 毫米。在另一个例子中，食品服务部门的任务可能是保持冰箱内的正确温度。为了监控温度，员工每小时记录一次温度。如果冰箱内的温度波动很快，则在录制之间可能会出现影响食品或配料质量的温度变化。在这种情况下，可以通过每 10 分钟或每 6 分钟记录一次测量值（每小时 10 个读数）来获得适当的分辨率。更好的是，在带有连接到网络的数字恒温器的冰箱中，团队可以访问每分钟记录的读数。
分辨率通常是测量系统中最容易纠正的事情之一，但以最详细的分辨率进行测量并不总是具有成本效益或合理的。团队应该考虑资源
开发测量系统时的要求。然而，如果能够获得最详细的分辨率，获得的测量结果将提供有关过程的更多信息以及更大的样本量。
调整线性误差
线性度描述了测量系统在一定范围内的表现。大多数人手中的标准公尺在测量厘米时相当准确，但在测量毫米或公里时不太准确。 范围在 0 到 10 公斤之间的秤在范围两端的测量精度可能会降低。
使用现有测量系统在不同范围内进行测量，并将这些测量值与使用已知在所有范围内准确的工具收集的数据进行比较，可以帮助团队发现线性误差。在某些情况下，团队可以开发数学方程来解释差异。例如，如果秤的精确度为 5 公斤，但此后每公斤都会多出四分之一公斤，则 8.5 公斤的测量结果实际上是：
如果无法进行数学调整，则团队不应使用测量系统来测量经常出现线性误差的范围。
稳定性
稳定性描述了测量随时间的一致性。如果操作员以相同的方式进行测量并使用相同的工具 - 并且这些工具不存在上述任何其他问题 - 那么测量结果应该反映控制图上的稳定性。第 16 章介绍了控制图，并在后面有关统计过程控制的章节中深入介绍。

rows: 术语说明 SPC（统计过程控制）：用统计方法监控过程表现，区分普通波动和异常信号。
zh.text: 术语说明 SPC（统计过程控制）：用统计方法监控过程表现，区分普通波动和异常信号。
如果控制图上反映的测量变化并不表明稳定性，那么团队可能需要首先排除测量系统的问题，然后再确定过程失控。
量具R&R

rows: 术语说明 Gage R&R：量具重复性与再现性分析，用来判断测量系统本身带来的变异。
zh.text: 术语说明 Gage R&R：量具重复性与再现性分析，用来判断测量系统本身带来的变异。
量具 R&R 工具用于确保测量系统的重复性和再现性。在大多数情况下，Gage R&R 工具适用于涉及人类操作员和评估员的测量系统。 六西格玛团队应用量具 R&R 测试来查找此类测量系统中的弱点。
在 Gage R&R 测试中，可重复性意味着单个员工使用相同的测量系统并评估相同的事物，可以重复他或她的测量。可重复性意味着多个员工使用相同的测量系统并评估相同的事物，得出匹配或非常接近匹配的测量结果。
大多数量具 R&R 测试分为两种类型：属性测试和变量测试。测试每种类型的测量的前提是相同的，尽管测试后的标准和统计分析略有不同。
属性量具 R&R
当六西格玛团队分析测量系统的通过/不通过数据时，会使用属性Gage R&R。例如，如果操作员审查产品线中的一个项目并简单地决定通过或失败，则这
将是一个属性测量。在上面的冷冻室测量示例中，员工可能只是负责记录温度是否在适当的范围内：是/否测量。如前所述，属性测量提供的有关过程的信息最少，因此对于冷冻室温度，最好记录实际温度。可以根据温度数据系统地确定该记录是否在适当的范围内。
当使用属性数据时，属性量具 R&R 用于按照以下步骤测试测量系统。

1. 选择至少两名评估师。 2.提供一定数量的样品。以一种您知道哪个是哪个但无法为评估员识别样品的方式标记样品。 3. 根据您拥有的最佳（最准确）测量值记录每个样本的实际属性测量值。 4. 让每个评估员记录所提供的每个样品的属性测量（通过/不通过；是/否；热/冷；通过/失败；等等）。 5. 使用相同的样品和评估员重复此过程，随机显示样品的顺序。第二次向评估员提供随机样本顺序会减少评估员记住他们第一次记录的测量值并默认记录相同测量值的机会。 6. 将所有数据输入电子表格或 Gage R&R 文件中，类似于下面显示通过/失败测量测试的文件。
从上面的量具 R&R 中，您可以看到测量系统只有 50% 的时间可重复，这使其成为一个较差的测量系统。对于评估者 1，它在 90% 的时间内是可重复的
评估员 2 的准确率分别为 80% 和 80%，评估员的准确率分别为 80% 和 70%。鉴于这些结果，六西格玛团队可能会确定如何确定样本是通过还是失败的说明存在一些清晰度问题。上图仅提供了一组10个样本的数据；更准确的属性 Gage R&R 测试通常需要至少 20 个数据点。
可变量具 R&R
并非所有数据都是属性数据，这就是团队还可以执行可变量具 R&R 测试的原因。虽然可变量具 R&R 测试的原始数据可以为六西格玛团队提供测量系统是否明显失效的情况，但通常需要统计分析才能真正确定测量系统的性能。这是因为，对于可变测量，测量值和操作员之间可能存在一些差异，特别是在测量非常小或很大的数字或在移动测量中捕获数据时。
设置变量量具 R&R 测试的方式与设置属性测试的方式大致相同，使用两到三个评估员和至少五到十个要测量的输出。让每个评估员对每个样本进行两次或三次测量，随机化样本的呈现顺序，以避免评估员记住最初输入的测量值。在可变量具 R&R 模板上记录所有数据，如下例所示。
在 Excel SPC 或 Minitab 中由黑带或绿带执行的统计分析通常返回四位数：
研究变异百分比
% 公差

rows: 术语说明 Tolerance（公差）：产品或过程指标允许偏离目标值的范围。
zh.text: 术语说明 Tolerance（公差）：产品或过程指标允许偏离目标值的范围。
贡献百分比
不同类别的数量
团队应确保可变量具 R&R 测试计算的所有四个要素都在被认为是“安全”的范围内。通常，每个元素都带有安全区或绿色区以及警告区和故障区的范围。如果其中一个元素落入警戒区，而所有其他元素落入警戒区
绿色，那么团队可能会得出结论，测量系统是足够的。在某些情况下，所有或大部分警戒区分数可能被认为是可接受的，特别是如果使测量系统变得更准确将是昂贵的或导致其他过程的应用问题。对于任何元件在故障区域得分的测量系统可能应该进行维修或更换。
用于判断变量量具 R&R 计算的每个元素的通用标准如下：
注意：另一种有效识别测量系统变异的工具称为方差分析（ANOVA）。方差分析（ANOVA）对于分析任何类型的变异也很有用，并将在第 5 单元的中间统计中介绍。

rows: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。
zh.text: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。
收集数据样本
一旦团队确定最佳测量工具已就位，他们就可以开始收集数据以用于 DMAIC 项目的分析阶段。当团队可以分析整个人群的数据时，就会得出最准确的结论，但由于时间和成本的限制，这几乎是不可能的。如果您可以访问自动化数据或数据仓库，您也许能够收集人口数据或能够更好地近似人口数据的极大样本量。否则，六西格玛团队必须对现有人口进行随机抽样，并使用这些样本得出关于整个人口的结论。
为了确保样本能够用于得出统计结论，样本必须被正确处理并且大小合适。在本节中，我们将简单介绍六西格玛团队可能使用的抽样策略类型以及原因。
简单随机抽样
当总体中的任何项目被选择的机会均等时，简单随机抽样就有效。例如，如果您将 20 颗大小、重量和质地完全相同的弹珠放入一个袋子中并盲目选择一颗，那么袋子中的每个弹珠有二十分之一的机会被选中。如果弹珠的尺寸或重量不同，这些不同的属性可能会影响每个弹珠被选择的机会。较重的弹珠可能会被分类到袋子的底部；较大的弹珠可能更有可能被捡起。
用于统计分析的随机抽样要求样本代表与整个总体相似的属性和百分比。人口有“N”项大。样本量为“n”项大。需要多大的样本才能在统计上代表总体，由许多因素决定。
分层抽样
当总体总体被划分或可以被划分为具有不同属性的子组时，就会发生分层抽样。例如，如果运输公司想要测试其估计运输时间与实际运输时间的准确性，它可能会假设结果会根据包裹必须运输的距离而变化。通过从整个包裹群体中随机选择样本，该公司最终可能只会获得 200 英里半径内递送的包裹样本。
为了防止数据出现偏差，运输公司可能会将人群分为四个子组：
200英里半径内送货
201 至 400 英里半径内的送货
401 至 600 英里半径范围内的送货
送货超过 600 英里
通过从分层子组中随机抽样，团队确保了样本量的偏差较小。
顺序采样
顺序抽样涉及选择每个 X 项目以包含在抽样中。当团队按时间间隔收集数据时，可以使用顺序抽样。该团队可能每 10 分钟收集一次数据。顺序抽样也可用于对实物进行抽样；产品线上的每 5 个项目都可能会被审查。给定正确的参数和足够的时间，顺序采样可以提供有效的统计结果。然而，团队必须认识到，在极少数情况下，采样顺序可能会扭曲结果。例如，在流程的每 5 次迭代期间可能会发生一些导致差异发生的情况。
非随机样本
在进行统计分析时不应使用非随机抽样，因为它更可能引入用户或抽样误差。虽然所有抽样都存在某种形式的误差，但可以计算随机抽样误差并在分析中加​​以考虑。对于非随机样本则不能这样说。
非随机抽样包括方便抽样或判断抽样。当团队进行最方便的测量时，就会发生方便抽样。 “我们现在想了解整个流程，所以让我们回顾一下接下来下线的十几个项目。”这种类型的分析只能真实地告诉团队该流程在该特定时刻的执行情况。
当专家或知识渊博的人负责“选择”适当的样本时，就会发生判断抽样。主管可能会对他或她的团队成员说：“选择一些代表你在某一天的正常运作方式的工作。”在大多数情况下，团队成员会选择他们认为质量更好的工作，从而扭曲样本中的任何结果。
提供基准指标
定义和测量阶段的主要交付成果之一是基线指标。 该流程现在的表现如何？团队将使用什么衡量标准来比较当前绩效与改进后的绩效？
基线指标是数字，但大多数团队发现以图形方式呈现指标最能引起业务资源和高管的共鸣。视觉表示还为团队提供了一种快速方法来确定是否正在发生进展。
您使用的视觉表示类型取决于您的主要指标是离散的还是连续的。离散数据可以显示在 Pareto 图上（参见第 5 章），连续数据可以通过运行图显示。您还可以使用变化或其他计算将离散数据转换为连续数据，以便在运行图和控制图中显示（有关控制图的信息，请参阅第 16 章）。
运行图
运行图可用于监控任何变量或过程随时间的表现。通过一个直观的图表，六西格玛团队可以显示流程中的趋势、变化和周期；他们还可以监视流程中的问题，尽管运行图在这方面不如非常相似的控制图那么有效。

基本运行图只是数据随时间变化的线图，这意味着任何人都可以创建该图表。大多数六西格玛运行图还具有代表所有数据点中位数的线，以供视觉参考。根据绘制图表的信息类型，您可能需要将数据转换为比率以获得更准确的运行图。例如，如果您要绘制表面随时间变化的温度，则无需转换数据。如果您要绘制出院后 30 天内重新入院的患者人数，则有助于将数据转换为同一时间段内出院患者人数的百分比。在 30 天内，有 10,000 名患者出院，您预计的回访人数将高于仅 5,000 名患者出院的时期。
下图显示了温度随时间变化的运行图。您可以看到温度如何随时间变化，并开始看到一些可能的趋势。六西格玛团队将能够缩小范围，在更多时间内查看运行图以验证趋势假设。您还可以看到该过程的中位温度为 33。
下面的运行图显示了一年中每个月每百次销售的退货数量。 您可以看到，回报率在今年上半年稳步上升，然后从 5 月到 11 月保持稳定。橙色线表示每百次销售的平均回报率，略低于 7。
在 Excel 中创建基本运行图
统计分析软件（包括 Minitab 和 Excel SPC）会根据输入的数据自动创建运行图的所有元素，但如果需要，任何人都可以使用基本的 Excel 功能来创建运行图。
首先，创建一个数据表。
为单个属性（例如温度）创建数据表只需在一列中输入时间标签，在另一列中输入属性测量值。例如，我们将逐步创建费率数据表，因为它涉及其他步骤。
1. 在Excel的第一列中输入数据标签（月、周、小时等）。 2. 在下一列中，输入您感兴趣的属性的相应测量值：在本例中为每月退货总数。 3. 在第三列中，输入您要与属性进行比较的商品总数：在本例中为每月销售总数。
4. 在第四列中，计算第一列数据占第二列数据的百分比。在本例中，为每月每笔销售的退货百分比。在本例中，一月的计算公式为 =B2/C2，二月的公式为 =B3/C3，依此类推。
5. 决定是否要创建显示百分比的运行图，或者是否要创建说明每 100、每 1,000 等比率的图表。 6. 如果要说明每 (x) 的比率，请将第四列中的百分比计算乘以 (x)。在本例中，D 列中的数字乘以 100。
7. 使用 Excel 计算您计划绘制图表的数字的中位数。中位数使用公式 =Median(Number 1, Number 2,…) 计算，其中公式中的数字与所有图表数据点的范围相关。在本例中，中位数为 6.96679 8. 突出显示数据标签（在本例中为 A 列）和要绘制图表的数字（E 列）
9. 选择“插入”>“图表”>“折线图”以插入属性或属性计算的折线图。
10. 选择“插入”>“形状”>“绘制线条”。
11. 在运行图上大约中位数的位置画一条线。 使用 Excel 工具选择所需线条的颜色和粗细。
完成的运行图可用于向六西格玛团队提供信息，或在测量阶段收费站演示中包含基线过程性能的图形表示。再次值得注意的是，大多数黑带人员和绿带人员不需要手动创建运行图，他们可以使用统计分析软件。

en.text: Use the checklist below to determine whether a team is ready to move from the measure phase to the analyze phase of a DMAIC project.

o The team has agreed upon the key measurements and come up with a baseline measurement of process performance. o The team has analyzed measurement systems and identified any issues that might contribute to analysis errors. o Where possible, the team has corrected measurement systems to remove error risks. o The team has calculated process variation and sigma level. o The team has conducted appropriate sampling to allow for statistically valid conclusions in the next phase. o The sponsor or champion has reviewed and signed off on all elements of the measure phase.

zh.text: 使用下面的清单来确定团队是否准备好从 DMAIC 项目的测量阶段进入分析阶段。
o 团队已就关键衡量标准达成一致，并提出了流程绩效的基准衡量标准。 o 团队分析了测量系统并确定了可能导致分析错误的任何问题。 o 在可能的情况下，团队已纠正测量系统以消除错误风险。 o 团队已计算过程变异和西格玛水平。 o 该团队已进行适当的抽样，以便在下一阶段得出统计上有效的结论。 o 发起人或支持者已审查并签署了措施阶段的所有要素。
