Even when the raw data provides for easy analysis, however, many Six Sigma experts do take the time to create a graphical representation for the purpose of presentations. A graph is more visually appealing and quicker to read than a data table.
Sales Errors A 10 B 5 C 2 D 9 E 34 F 7 G 3
Employee

The simplest graphical analysis isn’t always the best choice, especially when data elements are not so obvious to note. More complex analysis might be required to discover outliers, relationships, and trends. Even when conclusions about data seem obvious, they aren’t always correct. This is especially true when dealing with the relationship or correlation between factors in a process.

As a Six Sigma expert, it takes time to be able to quickly choose which type of graphical analysis will best represent the data at hand. It’s also important to note that the best choice for graphical analysis relies equally on the questions being asked as much as it does on the data itself. In the example above, the basic bar chart is a good choice for a team that wants to know where errors might be coming from. If the team was comparing errors to shift times being worked, the bar chart would not be helpful.

While it’s true that you don’t want to waste time and space presenting data in a way that is not helpful – or could be considered muda of over-production – the same isn’t always true when dealing with analysis. At the analysis stage of a DMAIC project, Black Belts and other Six Sigma experts often work with numerous graphical analysis tools as they attempt to understand data. The need to view data in various ways is one reason statistical analysis software is helpful: such software takes some of the manual work out of creating these charts and graphs. Trial-and-error work with all types of Six Sigma analysis tools also helps you learn to identify which tools fit each situation best.

In this section, we’ll discuss some tools that haven’t been introduced so far, covering benefits, limitations, and how to create the tools in Excel without statistical analysis software when necessary.
Bar Charts

One of the biggest benefits of bar charts is that they are recognizable and easy to read. Almost any employee in a company can glean information from a properly formatted bar chart without instruction or guidance, making them a great choice for general use presentations and training materials. Bar charts are also easy to create, so they are a preferred method of illustration in presentations and reports. Other benefits include:
The ability to summarize large data sets in a simple visual format
The ability to clarify trends
The fact that most people can easily estimate important values on a well-formatted bar chart
The ability to visually check data and identify areas where data might be skewed
The ability to easily display data sets that range above and below zero on the same chart

Bar charts typically require nominal or ordinal data – data that is classified according to qualitative information. When displaying nominal data, it’s often beneficial to create a Pareto-style chart so the reader can see instantly how the population relates to the categories. Ordinal data usually involves its own logical order for presentation. For example, if individuals are asked to rank satisfaction with a product as very satisfied, somewhat satisfied, neutral, unsatisfied, and very unsatisfied, then you usually would not want to change the order of that presentation on a bar chart.

Bar charts are not without limitations. Depending on what is being presented, additional narrative might be required to explain the chart. Bar charts also often fail to reveal key information about trends that aren’t part of the specific design of the chart, and they rarely on their own provide detailed data about causes or patterns in data. Finally, it’s easy to manipulate colors, order, and layout of a bar chart to influence the message that your audience takes away. Six Sigma experts should always choose the best format for displaying fundamental truths, but should never format a chart to create an impression that might not be in keeping with the statistical data behind the graphical representation.
Create a Bar Chart in Excel

Create your own bar charts in Excel by starting with data tables of relevant nominal or ordinal data. Copy the data table below into Excel to practice bar chart creation, or use data relevant to your own processes or projects. The sample data table provides the total number of phone calls experienced by a regional customer service call-center team for each hour in a particular day.
1. Highlight the columns that contain the data you want to chart as well as the labels for that data. In this case, the data is the number of calls and the labels are the hours.
2. Select Insert > Chart > Bar Chart
3. For this example, the simplest form of bar chart is appropriate. 4. Use Excel formatting tools as desired to customize the chart title, colors, and labels.
Most versions of Excel offer several types of bar charts, and you can experiment with these various formats to see how they display your data. Some notes about common formats are included below.
Column versus Bar

Technically, what is commonly referred to as a bar chart – and what we’ve been calling a bar chart here – is a column chart. The visual columns representing each data category rise vertically. A literal bar chart displays the same information horizontally. Most versions of Excel let you choose between the two displays. The information from the example data table is presented below in a horizontal bar chart.
3-D Bar and Column Charts

The charts displayed so far in this chapter are all 2-dimensional. Excel can also help you create 3- dimensional charts to add visual appeal to a presentation. 3-D charts are helpful if you are presenting a number of similar-looking charts in a row, as it helps to differentiate between information in your presentation. It might also be helpful to create more visually appealing charts simply to capture audience attention more fully. Here’s an example of the same data used above in a 3-D chart.
Stacked Bar Charts

Stacked bar charts let you display the total nominal or ordinal data for each category while also breaking that information into color coded categories. For example, if the Six Sigma team analyzing phone calls per hour wanted to display data that included how many calls were handled by separate teams within a department, the bar chart might look something like the image below.
Create a Stacked Bar Chart

To create a stacked bar chart, you must have ordinal or nominal data that is broken into categories. The categories must be the same for each data set so that the total of the sub-category numbers equals the total of the main category. For example, if you have three bags of marbles, you might have data that says:
Upon further analysis, you realize you have marbles in three colors: red, blue, and yellow.

Bag 1 doesn’t include any blue marbles, but you must include blue as a category for Bag 1 because it is a category in the other two bags. You simply put a 0 in that data field. You’ll note that the totals of the subcategories under each bag add up to the amount of marbles in each bag. Following these guidelines – including all subcategories for each section and ensuring the subcategories total correctly – helps you create accurate stacked bar charts.
Examine the data table below, which displays the breakdown of calls by teams in the call center.
1. Highlight the cells containing the data you want to include in your stacked bar chart as well as the cells containing the data labels.
2. Select Insert > Charts > Bar Charts > Stacked Bar Chart
3. Use Excel’s format options to edit titles, colors, and data labels as desired.
Pie Charts

The pie chart is another visual tool that almost any employee in a business environment will be familiar with, making it a good choice for displaying certain types of information. Ordinal and nominal information can also be displayed in a pie chart with the main purpose of visually representing how each category relates to the whole. Conventional wisdom says to use a pie chart when the numbers you are charting add up to 100, but this doesn’t have to be the case. Excel converts values to percentages to create pie charts. Consider the pie chart below, which graphs the numbers 8, 5, and 4. Excel considers each of these numbers against the total “pie” of 17. The blue section, which represents 47%, corresponds to the raw data 8/17.
Benefits of pie charts include:
The ability to summarize large amounts of specific data types in a visual format.
Simplicity; the pie chart is one of the simplest types of graphs
The ability to identify obvious problems with data or calculations
Pie charts usually require very little extra explanation when labeled correctly
Pie charts display the relevance of subset data within a total data set

The fact that pie charts are used constantly in business environments is both an advantage and disadvantage for Six Sigma teams and experts. When data can be depicted in a pie chart, the Six Sigma expert usually has to create less narrative to get a potential point across. At the same time, pie charts don’t always carry the weight that a more advanced statistical representation might because business employees are so used to seeing them. Other disadvantages of pie charts include the fact that they can be manipulated in much the same way as bar charts, they usually fail to easily display changes over time, and it can be difficult to visualize exact values when presented with a pie chart.
Create a Pie Chart in Excel

Practice creating a pie chart in Excel using the following data table, which records how many minutes are spent on average for each step of a process. You can also use data relevant to your own process or project to create a practice pie chart if desired.
Process Step Minutes A 14 B 18 C 41 D 64 E 12 F 4 G 3 H 7 I 23 J 18
1. Highlight the column of data you want to chart as well as the data labels. In the example, we are charting the number of minutes and the process numbers are the data labels.
2. Click Insert > Chart > Pie Chart
3. Use Excel’s formatting tools to make changes to color, title, and labels as desired.

From the pie chart created for our sample data, we can see that approximately half of the total process time is related to only two steps in the 10-step process. We can also quickly see which steps take the longest on average and which are the shortest, although we can’t draw any conclusions about actual time from only this chart.

As with bar charts, you can take advantage of Excel options for creating three-dimensional pie charts. A particularly helpful function is the Pie of Pie function in Excel, which lets you create an overall pie chart and carve out a second pie chart to delve deeper into certain areas of the larger structure. Consider a Pie of Pie chart below, which represents the same data from the pie chart above.

The original pie chart depicted 10 steps. The illustration above shows a large pie chart with a secondary chart. The large pie chart appears to show 7 steps – but this pie chart shows the same information as the one above. However, four steps from the process are called out in a smaller chart so you can see how they relate to each other better. Those four steps combined make up the 7th, dark-blue pie piece in the larger pie. This makes it easier to pinpoint relevance of smaller data categories within the whole or to emphasize a certain area of the chart.

You do have to be careful, however, that the viewer doesn’t assign relevance or importance based solely on the size of the pie pieces. The gray pie piece in the secondary chart above is bigger than some of the other pieces in the larger pie, but that doesn’t mean that step takes longer. The smaller pie should be considered a large-scale view of a small piece of the overall graph. In this case, that small piece takes into account four of the steps.
X Y Scatter Diagrams

We introduced scatter diagrams very briefly in chapter 14, and we’ll revisit them in depth in later chapters on correlation and regression modeling. In this section, we’ll cover how to create a scatter diagram in Excel with any analysis add-on. In chapter 19, we’ll use an add-on for Excel to conduct some statistical analysis while creating scatter diagrams.

Scatter diagrams are beneficial because they can help teams visually see the relationship between two factors in a process. Does temperature decrease over time? Does a person’s productivity increase with his or her salary? These are examples of questions that might be answered by scatter diagrams, but it’s important to realize that correlation as depicted on a scatter diagram doesn’t necessarily mean causation. Two variables can be closely related without one causing changes in the other.

Scatter diagrams typically help teams see whether there is no correlation, weak correlation, or positive or negative correlation. Positive correlation occurs when variable 2 is related to an increase in variable 1, or vice versa. Negative correlation occurs when variable 2 is related to a decrease in variable 1, or vice versa. For example, it is commonly noted that the rate of crime and the average per-capita income of geographic areas in the United States are related. Areas that demonstrate higher crime rates often also demonstrate lower income statistics. The statement can be made the other direction: areas that demonstrate lower income statistics often demonstrate higher crime rates. That is not to say that either of these things necessarily causes the other.

Drawbacks of scatter diagrams are that they are not as familiar to business employees as bar, pie, or even line charts. There is also a risk that individuals who don’t have an understanding of statistical analysis will mistake correlation for causation, which can lead to incorrect decisions. Black Belts and other Six Sigma experts must be cognizant of these risks so they can appropriately explain scatter diagrams and provide further explanation when necessary.
Create a Scatter Diagram in Excel

Use the data table below, which includes the average number of errors per hour a process creates as output is increased per hour to create a scatter diagram in Excel. Copy the information from the table below into Excel, or use data from your own process or project if desired.
Parts Per
Hour
Defects
1. Highlight the data you want to chart. To create a scatter diagram, you will need to highlight two sets of data that you want to compare.
1. Select Insert > Chart > Scatter
2. Use Excel’s formatting functions to edit colors, titles, and labels as desired.

From the scatter diagram created, we can see that the two variables – production volume and errors – appear to be related in some manner. While it’s a good bet that increased production actually causes increased errors, you can’t simply assume causation from this graph. This assumption could be further tested using more advanced statistical techniques.

It should be noted that, even when correlation seems obvious on a scatter diagram, it isn’t always the case. Six Sigma experts should always test correlation assumptions to ensure they are correct statistically before presenting a scatter diagram to others as an illustration of a relationship. We’ll cover statistical regression and correlation tests in chapter 19.

zh.text: 与六西格玛方法论的许多其他领域一样，在处理图形分析时，特别是在向他人展示信息时，很容易走极端。有这么多可供您使用的工具，您很容易会选择新的、令人兴奋的或更复杂的图表。不过，这通常意味着，当一个更简单的工具可以完成这项工作时，却要花费非增值时间来创建一个可视化工具。如果您还记得第 4 章，这将是一个需要避免的过度处理实例。
请考虑下面的两个图表，以了解图形分析何时可能发生过度处理。
两个图表都是相同信息的图形表示。第一个是在 Excel 中单击几下即可创建的简单条形图。第二个是帕累托图，也是在 Excel 中创建的，但需要多次单击鼠标。在这种特殊情况下，帕累托图不需要传达所需的信息。数据足够多样化，任何人都可以通过查看条形图看出销售人员 E 造成了很多错误。在这种特殊情况下，帕累托图夸大了结论，如果需要额外的工作，则不需要创建帕累托图。
在这个例子中，可能根本不需要数据的可视化描述：结论是显而易见的。查看下面的原始数据，您很快就会发现员工 E 的错误是第二高员工的三倍。然而，即使原始数据可以轻松分析，许多六西格玛专家也会花时间创建图形表示以进行演示。与数据表相比，图表更具视觉吸引力且读取速度更快。
销售错误 A 10 B 5 C 2 D 9 E 34 F 7 G 3
员工
最简单的图形分析并不总是最好的选择，尤其是当数据元素不太明显时。可能需要更复杂的分析来发现异常值、关系和趋势。即使有关数据的结论看起来很明显，它们也并不总是正确的。在处理过程中因素之间的关系或相关性时尤其如此。

rows: 术语说明 Correlation（相关）：描述两个变量共同变化的程度，不直接证明因果关系。
zh.text: 术语说明 Correlation（相关）：描述两个变量共同变化的程度，不直接证明因果关系。
作为六西格玛专家，需要时间才能快速选择哪种类型的图形分析最能代表手头的数据。同样重要的是要注意，图形分析的最佳选择同样依赖于提出的问题和数据本身。在上面的示例中，对于想要了解错误可能来自何处的团队来说，基本条形图是一个不错的选择。如果团队将错误与正在工作的轮班时间进行比较，则条形图将没有帮助。

虽然您确实不想浪费时间和空间以无益的方式呈现数据（或者可能被认为是过度生产的混乱），但在处理分析时情况并非总是如此。 在DMAIC项目的分析阶段，黑带人员和其他六西格玛专家在试图理解数据时经常使用大量的图形分析工具。需要以各种方式查看数据是统计分析软件有用的原因之一：此类软件可以减少创建这些图表和图形的一些手动工作。使用各种类型的六西格玛分析工具进行反复试验还可以帮助您学习确定哪些工具最适合每种情况。

rows: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
zh.text: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
在本节中，我们将讨论一些到目前为止尚未介绍的工具，包括优点、局限性，以及如何在必要时在 Excel 中创建这些工具，而无需使用统计分析软件。
条形图
条形图的最大好处之一是它们易于识别且易于阅读。公司中几乎所有员工都可以从格式正确的条形图中收集信息，无需指导或指导，这使它们成为通用演示和培训材料的绝佳选择。条形图也很容易创建，因此它们是演示文稿和报告中的首选说明方法。其他好处包括：
以简单的视觉格式总结大型数据集的能力
澄清趋势的能力
事实上，大多数人可以轻松地在格式良好的条形图上估计重要值
能够目视检查数据并识别数据可能存在偏差的区域
能够在同一个图表上轻松显示高于和低于零的数据集
条形图通常需要名义数据或有序数据——根据定性信息分类的数据。显示名义数据时，创建帕累托式图表通常很有用，这样读者可以立即看到人口与类别的关系。序数数据通常涉及其自己的表示逻辑顺序。例如，如果要求个人将产品的满意度分为非常满意、有些满意、一般、不满意和非常不满意，那么您通常不想更改条形图上该演示文稿的顺序。

rows: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
条形图并非没有限制。根据所呈现的内容，可能需要额外的叙述来解释图表。 条形图通常也无法揭示不属于图表特定设计一部分的趋势的关键信息，而且条形图本身很少提供有关数据原因或模式的详细数据。最后，可以轻松地操纵条形图的颜色、顺序和布局来影响受众所获取的信息。 六西格玛专家应始终选择显示基本事实的最佳格式，但绝不应格式化图表来造成可能与图形表示背后的统计数据不一致的印象。
在 Excel 中创建条形图
从相关名义或有序数据的数据表开始，在 Excel 中创建您自己的条形图。将下面的数据表复制到 Excel 中以练习条形图创建，或使用与您自己的流程或项目相关的数据。示例数据表提供了区域客户服务呼叫中心团队在特定日期每小时经历的电话总数。

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
1. 突出显示包含要绘制图表的数据的列以及该数据的标签。在本例中，数据是呼叫次数，标签是小时数。
2. 选择“插入”>“图表”>“条形图”
3. 对于本例，最简单的条形图形式是合适的。 4. 根据需要使用 Excel 格式化工具自定义图表标题、颜色和标签。
大多数版本的 Excel 都提供多种类型的条形图，您可以尝试使用这些不同的格式，看看它们如何显示数据。下面包含一些有关常见格式的注释。
柱形与条形
从技术上讲，通常所说的条形图（以及我们在这里所说的条形图）是柱形图。代表每个数据类别的可视列垂直上升。文字条形图水平显示相同的信息。大多数版本的 Excel 允许您在两种显示之间进行选择。下面以水平条形图显示示例数据表中的信息。
3-D 条形图和柱形图
本章到目前为止显示的图表都是二维的。 Excel 还可以帮助您创建 3 维图表，为演示文稿增添视觉吸引力。如果您连续呈现多个外观相似的图表，3D 图表会很有帮助，因为它有助于区分演示文稿中的信息。 创建更具视觉吸引力的图表以更充分地吸引观众的注意力也可能会有所帮助。这是上面在 3D 图表中使用的相同数据的示例。
堆积条形图
堆叠条形图可让您显示每个类别的总名义或序数数据，同时还将该信息分解为颜色编码的类别。例如，如果六西格玛团队分析每小时的电话量，想要显示包括部门内不同团队处理的电话量在内的数据，则条形图可能如下图所示。
创建堆积条形图
要创建堆积条形图，您必须具有分为类别的序数或名义数据。每个数据集的类别必须相同，以便子类别数的总数等于主类别数的总数。例如，如果您有三袋弹珠，您的数据可能会显示：
经过进一步分析，您发现弹珠有三种颜色：红色、蓝色和黄色。
袋子 1 不包含任何蓝色弹珠，但您必须将蓝色作为袋子 1 的类别，因为它是其他两个袋子中的类别。您只需在该数据字段中输入 0 即可。您会注意到，每个袋子下的子类别总数加起来就是每个袋子中弹珠的数量。遵循这些准则（包括每个部分的所有子类别并确保子类别总计正确）可帮助您创建准确的堆叠条形图。
检查下面的数据表，其中显示了呼叫中心各团队的呼叫明细。
1. 突出显示包含要包含在堆叠条形图中的数据的单元格以及包含数据标签的单元格。
2. 选择“插入”>“图表”>“条形图”>“堆叠条形图”
3. 使用 Excel 的格式选项根据需要编辑标题、颜色和数据标签。
饼图

饼图是商业环境中几乎所有员工都会熟悉的另一种可视化工具，使其成为显示某些类型信息的不错选择。序数和名义信息也可以显示在饼图中，主要目的是直观地表示每个类别与整体的关系。传统观点认为，当您绘制图表的数字加起来达到 100 时，就应该使用饼图，但事实并非如此。 Excel 将值转换为百分比以创建饼图。考虑下面的饼图，其中绘制了数字 8、5 和 4。Excel 将这些数字中的每一个与 17 的总“饼图”进行比较。蓝色部分代表 47%，对应于原始数据 8/17。
饼图的好处包括：
以可视化格式汇总大量特定数据类型的能力。
简单；饼图是最简单的图表类型之一
识别数据或计算的明显问题的能力
当正确标记时，饼图通常不需要太多额外的解释
饼图显示总数据集中子集数据的相关性
商业环境中不断使用饼图对于六西格玛团队和专家来说既是优点也是缺点。当数据可以用饼图来描述时，六西格玛专家通常需要减少叙述才能表达潜在的观点。与此同时，饼图并不总是像更高级的统计表示那样具有重要性，因为企业员工已经习惯了看到它们。饼图的其他缺点包括它们的操作方式与条形图大致相同，它们通常无法轻松显示随时间的变化，并且在使用饼图呈现时很难可视化精确值。
在 Excel 中创建饼图
使用以下数据表练习在 Excel 中创建饼图，该数据表记录流程每个步骤平均花费的分钟数。如果需要，您还可以使用与您自己的流程或项目相关的数据来创建练习饼图。
处理步骤分钟 A 14 B 18 C 41 D 64 E 12 F 4 G 3 H 7 I 23 J 18
1. 突出显示要绘制图表的数据列以及数据标签。在示例中，我们绘制了分钟数图表，进程编号是数据标签。
2. 单击“插入”>“图表”>“饼图”
3. 使用 Excel 的格式设置工具根据需要更改颜色、标题和标签。
从为示例数据创建的饼图中，我们可以看到总处理时间的大约一半与 10 步流程中的仅两个步骤相关。 我们还可以快速查看哪些步骤平均耗时最长，哪些步骤最短，尽管我们无法仅从该图表得出有关实际时间的任何结论。
与条形图一样，您可以利用 Excel 选项来创建三维饼图。 Excel 中的 Pie of Pie 函数是一个特别有用的函数，它可让您创建一个整体饼图并绘制第二个饼图以更深入地研究较大结构的某些区域。考虑下面的饼图，它表示与上面饼图相同的数据。
原始饼图描绘了 10 个步骤。上图显示了一个带有辅助图表的大饼图。大饼图似乎显示了 7 个步骤 - 但此饼图显示的信息与上面的饼图相同。然而，该过程中的四个步骤在较小的图表中被标注出来，以便您可以更好地了解它们如何相互关联。这四个步骤组合起来构成了大饼中的第七个深蓝色饼块。这使得更容易查明整体中较小数据类别的相关性或强调图表的特定区域。
但是，您必须小心，观看者不会仅根据饼图的大小来分配相关性或重要性。上面第二个图表中的灰色饼图比较大饼图中的其他部分更大，但这并不意味着该步骤需要更长的时间。较小的饼图应被视为整个图表的一小部分的大比例视图。在本例中，该小部分考虑了其中四个步骤。
X Y 散点图
我们在第 14 章中非常简单地介绍了散点图，我们将在后面有关相关性和回归建模的章节中深入讨论它们。在本节中，我们将介绍如何使用任何分析插件在 Excel 中创建散点图。在第 19 章中，我们将使用 Excel 插件在创建散点图时进行一些统计分析。

rows: 术语说明 Scatter Diagram（散点图）：展示两个变量之间关系的图形；相关不等于因果。
zh.text: 术语说明 Scatter Diagram（散点图）：展示两个变量之间关系的图形；相关不等于因果。

rows: 术语说明 Regression（回归）：用模型估计变量之间的关系，并预测响应变量。
zh.text: 术语说明 Regression（回归）：用模型估计变量之间的关系，并预测响应变量。
散点图很有用，因为它们可以帮助团队直观地看到流程中两个因素之间的关系。温度会随着时间的推移而降低吗？一个人的生产力会随着他或她的薪水而提高吗？这些是可以通过散点图回答的问题示例，但重要的是要认识到散点图上描绘的相关性并不一定意味着因果关系。 两个变量可以密切相关，而一个变量不会引起另一个变量的变化。

散点图通常可以帮助团队了解是否存在不相关、弱相关、正相关或负相关。当变量 2 与变量 1 的增加相关时，就会出现正相关，反之亦然。当变量 2 与变量 1 的减少相关时，就会出现负相关，反之亦然。例如，人们普遍注意到美国各地理区域的犯罪率和人均收入是相关的。犯罪率较高的地区通常也表现出较低的收入统计数据。该说法也可以从另一个方向进行：收入统计数据较低的地区往往表现出较高的犯罪率。这并不是说其中任何一个必然导致另一个。
散点图的缺点是业务员工不像条形图、饼图甚至折线图那样熟悉它们。不了解统计分析的人还存在将相关性误认为因果关系的风险，这可能会导致错误的决策。 黑带人员和六西格玛的其他专家必须认识到这些风险，以便能够对散点图进行适当的解释，并在必要时提供进一步的解释。
在 Excel 中创建散点图
使用下面的数据表（其中包括随着输出每小时增加而导致流程每小时产生的平均错误数）在 Excel 中创建散点图。将下表中的信息复制到 Excel 中，或者根据需要使用您自己的流程或项目中的数据。
每份数
小时
缺陷
1. 突出显示要绘制图表的数据。要创建散点图，您需要突出显示要比较的两组数据。
1. 选择“插入”>“图表”>“散点图”
2. 使用 Excel 的格式化功能根据需要编辑颜色、标题和标签。
从创建的散点图中，我们可以看到两个变量——产量和错误——似乎以某种方式相关。虽然可以肯定的是，产量增加实​​际上会导致错误增加，但您不能简单地从该图中假设因果关系。可以使用更先进的统计技术进一步测试这一假设。
应该指出的是，即使相关性在散点图上看起来很明显，但情况并非总是如此。 六西格玛专家在向他人展示散点图以说明关系之前，应始终测试相关性假设，以确保它们在统计上是正确的。我们将在第 19 章介绍统计回归和相关性测试。

title.en: Creating an X-Bar Control Chart without Statistical Software
title.zh: 无需统计软件即可创建 X-Bar 控制图

en.text: The X-bar control chart plots the mean of a sample over time – or the mean of samples taken over time, in the case of an active process. X-bar control charts are one of the most frequently used control charts. Some control charts can also be created in Excel using these steps; more advanced control charts require statistical analysis software.
1. Create the following template in an empty Excel spreadsheet.
2. Enter your data points in column A. 3. In cell G1, enter the formula =STDEV(A2:A15)
a. The cell references A2:A15 should be edited to correspond to your actual data list. b. This calculates the standard deviation of your data set. 4. In cell G5, enter the formula =AVERAGE(A2:A15)
a. The cell references A2:A15 should be edited to correspond to your actual data list. b. This calculates the mean of your data set. 5. In cell G2, enter the formula: =G5+3*(G1)
a. This calculates an upper control limit that is three standard deviations above the mean. 6. In cell G3, enter the formula: =G5-3*(G1)

a. This calculates a lower control limit that is three standard deviations below the mean. 7. In the UCL column (B), direct Excel to create a column of numbers where every number is equal to the upper control limit by copying =$G$2 into each cell in a row that has an X-bar data point in column A. 8. In the LCL column (C), direct Excel to create a column of numbers where every number is equal to the lower control limit by copying =$G$3 into each cell in a row that has an X-bar data point in column A. 9. In the Mean column, direct Excel to create a column of numbers where every number is equal to the mean by copying =$G$5 into each cell in a row that has an X-bar data point in column A.
(Practice steps 1 through 9 with the following data.)
X-Bar Data Points
The result should be a spreadsheet that looks just like the one below.
10. Create a control chart by highlighting the data in columns A through D.
11. Select Insert > Charts > Line Chart > 2-D Line Chart with Markers

12. Right click on the data labels for the Y axis and select “Format Axis.” 13. Alter the bounds for the axis to remove excess white space in your control chart. In this case, we’ll change bounds from 0 to 35 to 15 to 35.
14. Format the chart as desired for color, titles, and data labels. You now have a very basic control chart.

One thing you’ll note missing from this control chart is the lines that denote one and two standard deviations above and below the mean. In chapter 16, you learned that those lines help you apply the tests that determine if a process is in control or not. You can easily add those lines by following the steps below.
Add Standard Deviation Lines
1. Add calculations to your spreadsheet for:

a. The mean plus one standard deviation b. The mean plus two standard deviations c. The mean minus one standard deviation d. The mean minus two standard deviations 2. Add four data columns, one for each of the calculations in step one. Copy the numbers from each of the calculations down the columns as you did with the upper and lower control limits and the mean in the original instructions above. 3. The spreadsheet should now appear similar to the image below.

4. Highlight all of the information in columns A through H. 5. Select Insert > Charts > Line Chart > 2-D Line Chart with Markers. 6. Change the Y-Axis to an appropriate range. 7. Edit the background standard deviation lines to be less intrusive by clicking on each and first selecting the gradient line option.
8. Click on the line again and select “Marker.” Select none to remove the markers for the background lines.
9. Edit the final chart as desired with titles and labels.

This process might seem very time consuming, but it only takes a few minutes once you are well-versed in the elements of a control chart. It’s a good idea to know how to create control charts in Excel, because Six Sigma experts can’t always rely on access to statistical analysis software.

zh.text: X 条形控制图绘制了一段时间内样本的平均值，或者在活动过程的情况下绘制了一段时间内采集的样本的平均值。 X 形控制图是最常用的控制图之一。还可以使用以下步骤在 Excel 中创建一些控制图；更先进的控制图需要统计分析软件。
1. 在空的 Excel 电子表格中创建以下模板。
2. 在 A 列中输入数据点。 3. 在单元格 G1 中，输入公式 =STDEV(A2:A15)
a.应编辑单元格引用 A2:A15 以对应于您的实际数据列表。 b.这将计算数据集的标准差。 4. 在单元格 G5 中，输入公式 =AVERAGE(A2:A15)

rows: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
zh.text: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
a.应编辑单元格引用 A2:A15 以对应于您的实际数据列表。 b.这将计算数据集的平均值。 5. 在单元格 G2 中，输入公式：=G5+3*(G1)
a.这将计算出比平均值高三个标准差的控制上限。 6. 在单元格 G3 中，输入公式：=G5-3*(G1)

a.这将计算出比平均值低三个标准差的控制下限。 7. 在 UCL 列 (B) 中，通过将 =$G$2 复制到 A 列中具有 X 条数据点的行中的每个单元格中，指示 Excel 创建一列数字，其中每个数字都等于控制上限。 8. 在 LCL 列 (C) 中，通过将 =$G$3 复制到 A 列中具有 X 条数据点的行中的每个单元格中，指示 Excel 创建一列数字，其中每个数字都等于控制下限。 9. 在平均值列，通过将 =$G$5 复制到 A 列中具有 X 条数据点的行中的每个单元格中，指示 Excel 创建一列数字，其中每个数字都等于平均值。
（使用以下数据练习步骤 1 到 9。）
X-Bar 数据点
结果应该是一个如下所示的电子表格。
10. 通过突出显示 A 至 D 列中的数据来创建控制图。
11. 选择“插入”>“图表”>“折线图”>“带标记的二维折线图”
12. 右键单击 Y 轴的数据标签，然后选择“设置轴格式”。 13. 更改轴的边界以删除控制图中多余的空白。在本例中，我们将范围从 0 更改为 35 到 15 到 35。
14. 根据需要设置图表的颜色、标题和数据标签格式。现在您已经有了一个非常基本的控制图。
您会注意到此控制图中缺少的一件事是表示高于和低于平均值的一个和两个标准差的线条。在第 16 章中，您了解到这些行可以帮助您应用测试来确定过程是否受控。您可以按照以下步骤轻松添加这些行。
添加标准偏差线
1. 将计算添加到电子表格中：
a.平均值加一标准差 B.平均值加上两个标准差 C.平均值减去一个标准差 D．平均值减去两个标准差 2. 添加四个数据列，每个数据列对应第一步中的每个计算。将每个计算中的数字复制到列中，就像上面原始说明中对控制上限和下限以及平均值所做的那样。 3. 电子表格现在应如下图所示。
4. 突出显示 A 至 H 列中的所有信息。 5. 选择“插入”>“图表”>“折线图”>“带标记的二维折线图”。 6. 将 Y 轴更改为适当的范围。 7. 单击每条背景标准偏差线并首先选择渐变线选项，编辑背景标准偏差线以减少干扰。
8. 再次单击该线并选择“标记”。选择“无”可删除背景线的标记。
9. 根据需要编辑最终图表的标题和标签。
这个过程可能看起来非常耗时，但一旦您精通控制图的元素，只需要几分钟。 了解如何在 Excel 中创建控制图是个好主意，因为六西格玛专家不能总是依赖统计分析软件。

title.en: Adding Free Data Analysis Tools to Excel
title.zh: 向 Excel 添加免费数据分析工具

en.text: You can complete these calculations by hand, but that can be time consuming and require extremely advanced statistical skill sets. We will start with manual calculations because it helps to understand the reasoning behind an analysis. But we’ll also rely heavily on tools that complete most of the calculations. Not only does statistical analysis software minimize the time it takes to conduct such analysis, but it also reduces the chance of calculation errors and increases the accuracy of analysis and conclusions offered by Six Sigma experts.
For lessons covered in the next few chapters, you can rely on the free data analysis tool available from Microsoft for Excel. To add this free tool in Excel 2013 and higher, follow the steps below.
1. Select File
2. Select options
3. Select Add-ins

4. Locate the Analysis ToolPak under inactive application add-ins. Note: if the Analysis ToolPak is located under Active Application Add-ins, then it is already active and you don’t need to take any of the next steps.
5. At the bottom of the dialogue box, ensure that “Excel Add-ins” is listed in the Manage box, and click “Go”.
6. In the new dialogue box, ensure the option for Analysis ToolPak is checked.
7. Click OK
8. Check that the Analysis ToolPak was installed by selecting “Data” from the Excel menu bar and looking for the Data Analysis option.

If the Analysis ToolPak doesn’t appear as an Add-In option, that means it wasn’t installed when your version of Excel was installed. If you are using Excel that was installed from a disk, you’ll need to locate your software CDs and either reinstall the software or complete a custom install of just the Analysis ToolPak file.
Note that there are two files with the Analysis ToolPak name. The file called Analysis ToolPak – VBA is not the file that provides analytical capability.

The Analysis ToolPak is also available in earlier versions of Excel. In 2003 and earlier versions of Excel, you’ll find the Add-in menu item under Tools. In 2007, the Add-in option is found by selecting the Office button and then Excel options. In 2010, select File, Options, and Manage Add-ins.

zh.text: 您可以手动完成这些计算，但这可能非常耗时，并且需要极其先进的统计技能。我们将从手动计算开始，因为它有助于理解分析背后的推理。但我们也会严重依赖完成大部分计算的工具。统计分析软件不仅最大限度地减少了进行此类分析所需的时间，而且还减少了计算错误的机会，提高了六西格玛专家提供的分析和结论的准确性。
对于接下来几章中介绍的课程，您可以依靠 Microsoft for Excel 提供的免费数据分析工具。要在 Excel 2013 及更高版本中添加此免费工具，请按照以下步骤操作。
1. 选择文件
2. 选择选项
3. 选择加载项
4. 在非活动应用程序加载项下找到分析工具库。注意：如果分析工具库位于活动应用程序加载项下，则它已处于活动状态，您无需执行任何后续步骤。
5. 在对话框底部，确保“Excel 加载项”列在“管理”框中，然后单击“执行”。
6. 在新对话框中，确保选中 Analysis ToolPak 选项。
7. 单击“确定”
8. 从 Excel 菜单栏中选择“数据”并查找“数据分析”选项，检查分析工具库是否已安装。
如果分析工具库没有作为加载项选项出现，则意味着在安装您的 Excel 版本时尚未安装它。如果您使用的是从磁盘安装的 Excel，则需要找到软件 CD 并重新安装软件或仅完成分析工具库文件的自定义安装。
请注意，有两个文件具有分析工具库名称。名为 Analysis ToolPak – VBA 的文件不是提供分析功能的文件。
早期版本的 Excel 中也提供了分析工具库。在 2003 版及更早版本的 Excel 中，您可以在“工具”下找到“加载项”菜单项。 2007 年，通过选择 Office 按钮然后选择 Excel 选项可以找到加载项选项。 在 2010 年，选择“文件”、“选项”和“管理加载项”。

title.zh: 第 18 章：正态概率分布

source.extraction: python-docx body-order extraction; source-TOC sectionized bilingual content

en.text: Now that you’ve downloaded the Analysis ToolPak in chapter 17, you can begin learning more detailed statistical analysis. In this chapter, we’ll discuss how and when to use a normal probability curve to draw inferential statistics. Now is a good time to know the difference between descriptive statistics and inferential statistics.

Descriptive statistics Descriptive statistics are numbers used to describe a set of data. Given a set of data about the employees in a company, for example, someone might say, “Over 50 percent of the employees work in sales or marketing departments,” or, “86 percent of the employees live in the same zip code that the office is located in.” These are descriptive statistics because they simply discuss the nature of the data at hand. Averages, means, and even standard deviations associated with that data are all descriptive in nature.

Inferential statistics Inferential statistics involve calculations and resulting numbers that draw more general conclusions from data. For example, if you have data about a sample of 150 employees in a city – not all the employees from a single business, but random employees from different companies – you might be able to use inferential statistics to draw conclusions about all the working individuals in that city based on the data in your sample.

Descriptive statistics can relate to population data or sample data. Inferential statistics are used to draw conclusions about the population from sample data. If you randomly select 20 people from an overall population and record their height, then the mean of the recorded heights is a descriptive statistic about the sample size. Descriptive statistics about the sample are used in calculations for inferential statistics about a population.

Probability distributions Probability is the likelihood that a certain event or outcome will occur given a specific set of data or a specific action. If you flip a coin one time, the probability of it coming up heads is 1 in 2, or 50 percent.

When discussing basic probability, we talk about outcomes and events. The outcome is the result of one trial: you flip the coin once or you randomly select a single item from the sample. The event is a specific outcome or outcomes. The coin coming up heads is an event. A person measuring 5 feet tall is an event if you are talking about selecting from a population or sample.
Basic probability can be calculated by the formula:
𝑃= # 𝑜𝑓 𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑠 𝑑𝑒𝑓𝑖𝑛𝑒𝑑 𝑎𝑠 𝑡ℎ𝑒 𝑜𝑢𝑡𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 # 𝑜𝑓 𝑡𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑠
In the case of the coin, there are two possible events, or outcomes, that can occur when you flip it. Only one of those events is coming up heads. Therefore, ½ = .5, or 50 percent.

In the case of rolling a six-sided die, there are six possible events. You can roll a 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6. The probability of rolling a 5 is 1/6, or .167. The probability of rolling either a 4 or a 5, however, is 2/6, or .33.

While probability measurements can be expressed as a percentage, they are calculated and usually communicated in statistics as a decimal point. Since probabilities are presented as fractions, the lowest probability possible is 0 and the highest is 1. A probability of 0 means an impossible event; a probability of 1 means a certain event.
Basic Probability Practice
Twenty marbles are placed in a bag. They are all the same size, but are different colors as follows:
- 3 red - 5 black - 8 blue - 4 green
What is the probability that a green marble would be pulled from the bag?
What is the probability a blue or a black marble would be pulled from the bag?
What is the probability a yellow marble would be pulled from the bag?
Note: This is an impossible task, because there are no yellow marbles in the bag.
Applying Basic Probability Concepts to Six Sigma Analysis

In a Six Sigma setting, you aren’t dealing with marbles and bags, but the ideas of probability are somewhat similar. If a process produces 100 products an hour and two of those products are defective, the probability of receiving a defective product within that hour is 2/100, or 0.02. However, it’s rare that a team or business unit would review all 100 products that were produced that hour to know that 2 out of 100 are defective. That information is also only useful for stating probabilities with relation to that particular hour of output, given that the sample wouldn’t be random.

Instead, Six Sigma experts randomly sample the population of all outputs of a process for a certain amount of time. They create descriptive statistics about that sample and use the descriptive statistics to further study and understand the nature of the data they are working with. Once they know the nature of the data, they can work with probability distributions and curves to determine probability via calculations and statistical analysis. In this chapter, we’ll discuss how to work with what is called normal probability distributions.
Histograms

Before moving forward with probability distributions, it’s important to understand histograms. Histograms are another graphical analysis tool. Technically, histograms are bar charts where each bar, or “bin,” corresponds to a data range. Data points within a sample or population are divided between the bins and are graphed accordingly.

Histograms are used to analyze continuous or variable data that is finite in nature or comes from population sampling. Histograms help Six Sigma experts understand how the spread, or distribution, of the data is shaped and where the center of that data might be. The center is the average or middle of the data spread, though the mean or median values aren’t always located at the center of a histogram. A histogram also shows the range of the data – the variation between the highest value of the data set and the lowest value of the data set. Finally, the histogram’s shape provides information about where the data is concentrated.

Consider the two histograms below. In the first, the data elements are concentrated toward the center. The histogram approximates what is called the bell curve, or normal curve. This shape is likely to indicate that the data used to create the histogram is normal data, which will be discussed further later in this chapter.

The second histogram depicts data that is concentrated at either end of the range. When two specific concentrations, or humps, are noted on a histogram, this is called bi-modal distribution. Often, when you see this shape of data, it means that you are measuring two processes that you believe are a single process. For example, if two different people are performing a piece of work, they might have different outcomes that result in two different humps on the histogram. If bi-modal data does come from a single process, then the data is not normal.

Histograms can also have several other common shapes, especially when presenting non-normal data or data that has an error or issue. If the histogram appears very random, as in the first example below, then it’s possible an error lies within the data measurement process. The measurement might not be adequate to capture the true picture of the process, or the definition of the operation and how to collect data might be at fault. The same is true if the histogram depicts an almost even distribution of data elements among bins.

A skewed distribution, seen in the second diagram above, features data that is clustered at only one end of the graph. Skewed histograms are common with certain types of data, particularly when data “falls off” at a natural cut-off time. For example, in a call center, you might measure the time it takes representatives to complete a certain type of phone call. A natural end-point for that data is 0 – it will always take more than 0 seconds to complete a phone call. Usually, it will take more than a few seconds, so you won’t have many data points in the lower end of the range. Perhaps this particular phone call follows a script and the majority of the calls end at the 7 to 8-minute mark. A histogram for length of calls might look something like the chart below.
Consider the basic example below to understand histograms further.

A local distribution company wants to understand more about how well delivery drivers are meeting estimated arrival times. The team pulls a random sample of deliveries from a week’s worth of information and considers two data points for each delivery: the estimated time of delivery and the actual time of delivery. They subtracted the actual time from the estimated time to determine how far off one way or the other the deliveries were. The result was the following data table.

To create a histogram, the team sorted the selection by difference in minutes and discovered the range as -27 to 48. The team decided to create a histogram from -30 to 50 with a bin size of 10. The resulting data is as follows:

To create the above table, the team simply counted how many data points fell in each bin. The data points -27 and -25 fell into the bin -20 (to -29); that is 2 data points. Eight data points fell into the 10 bin. When charted on a bar chart, this information creates a histogram.

When dealing with histograms, the number of bins used is important. If you have too few bins, the graph doesn’t provide any relevant information. If you have too many bins for the data set in question, you also lose informative functionality and your graph, rather than appearing as a rough curve, becomes something that looks like a comb. Usually, the more data you have, the more bins you can include on your histogram.
Consider the following four histograms that all represent the same set of data. The data features 150 random numbers ranging from 38.62 to 62.89.

The bin sizes get progressively smaller as the number of bins increase. In the first chart, bin sizes are 5; in the last chart, bin sizes are 0.5. For the purposes of understanding what type of data is represented in this histogram, the second or third charts are probably the most relevant. We’ll cover determining data type from a histogram and associated statistics later in this chapter.

zh.text: 现在您已经下载了第 17 章中的分析工具库，您可以开始学习更详细的统计分析。在本章中，我们将讨论如何以及何时使用正态概率曲线来绘制推论统计。现在是了解描述性统计和推论统计之间差异的好时机。
描述性统计 描述性统计是用于描述一组数据的数字。例如，给定一组有关公司员工的数据，有人可能会说，“超过 50% 的员工在销售或营销部门工作”，或者“86% 的员工居住在与办公室所在的同一邮政编码中”。这些是描述性统计数据，因为它们只是讨论手头数据的性质。与该数据相关的平均值、平均值，甚至标准差本质上都是描述性的。
推论统计 推论统计涉及从数据得出更一般结论的计算和结果数字。例如，如果您有一个城市 150 名员工样本的数据（不是来自单个企业的所有员工，而是来自不同公司的随机员工），您也许可以使用推论统计，根据样本中的数据得出有关该城市所有工作人员的结论。

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
描述性统计可以与总体数据或样本数据相关。推论统计用于从样本数据中得出有关总体的结论。如果您从总体人口中随机选择 20 个人并记录他们的身高，那么记录的身高平均值就是关于样本量的描述性统计数据。有关样本的描述性统计数据用于计算有关总体的推论统计数据。

rows: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
概率分布 概率是在给定一组特定数据或特定操作的情况下发生特定事件或结果的可能性。如果你抛一枚硬币一次，正面朝上的概率是二分之一，即 50%。
当讨论基本概率时，我们谈论结果和事件。结果是一次试验的结果：掷硬币一次或从样本中随机选择一个项目。该事件是一个或多个特定的结果。硬币正面朝上是一个事件。 如果您谈论的是从总体或样本中进行选择，那么身高 5 英尺的人就是一个事件。
基本概率可以通过以下公式计算：
𝑃=#𝑜𝑓𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑠𝑑𝑒𝑓𝑖𝑛𝑒𝑑𝑎𝑠𝑡ℎ𝑒 𝑜𝑢𝑡𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 # 𝑜𝑓 𝑡𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑠
就硬币而言，当您翻转硬币时，可能会发生两种可能的事件或结果。这些事件中只有一件正在发生。因此，1/2 = 0.5，即 50%。
在掷六面骰子的情况下，有六种可能的事件。您可以掷出 1、2、3、4、5 或 6。掷出 5 的概率为 1/6 或 0.167。然而，掷出 4 或 5 的概率是 2/6，即 0.33。
虽然概率测量可以用百分比表示，但它们是在统计中计算并通常以小数点表示的。由于概率以分数表示，因此最低概率为 0，最高概率为 1。概率为 0 表示不可能发生的事件；概率为 0 表示不可能发生的事件。概率为 1 表示某个事件。
基本概率练习
一个袋子里装有二十颗弹珠。它们的尺寸相同，但颜色不同，如下所示：
从袋子中取出绿色弹珠的概率是多少？
从袋子中取出蓝色或黑色弹珠的概率是多少？
从袋子中取出黄色弹珠的概率是多少？
注意：这是一项不可能完成的任务，因为袋子里没有黄色弹珠。
将基本概率概念应用于六西格玛分析
在六西格玛的环境中，你处理的不是弹珠和袋子，但概率的想法有些相似。如果一个流程每小时生产 100 个产品，其中两个产品有缺陷，则该小时内收到缺陷产品的概率为 2/100，即 0.02。然而，很少有团队或业务部门会审查当时生产的所有 100 件产品，以得知其中 100 件产品中有 2 件有缺陷。鉴于样本不是随机的，该信息也仅适用于说明与特定时间的输出相关的概率。
相反，六西格玛专家在一定时间内对某个过程的所有输出的总体进行随机抽样。 他们创建有关该样本的描述性统计数据，并使用描述性统计数据来进一步研究和理解他们正在使用的数据的性质。一旦他们了解了数据的性质，他们就可以利用概率分布和曲线，通过计算和统计分析来确定概率。在本章中，我们将讨论如何使用所谓的正态概率分布。
直方图
在继续研究概率分布之前，了解直方图很重要。直方图是另一种图形分析工具。从技术上讲，直方图是条形图，其中每个条形或“箱”对应一个数据范围。样本或总体内的数据点在各个箱之间划分并相应地绘制图表。
直方图用于分析本质上有限或来自总体抽样的连续或可变数据。直方图帮助六西格玛专家了解数据的传播或分布是如何形成的以及数据的中心可能在哪里。中心是数据分布的平均值或中间值，尽管平均值或中值并不总是位于直方图的中心。直方图还显示数据的范围——数据集的最高值和数据集的最低值之间的变化。最后，直方图的形状提供了有关数据集中位置的信息。

rows: 术语说明 Histogram（直方图）：展示数据分布形态和频数的柱状图。
zh.text: 术语说明 Histogram（直方图）：展示数据分布形态和频数的柱状图。
考虑下面的两个直方图。第一种情况，数据元素向中心集中。直方图近似于所谓的钟形曲线或正态曲线。这种形状可能表明用于创建直方图的数据是正常数据，本章稍后将进一步讨论。
第二个直方图描绘了集中在范围两端的数据。当直方图上标出两个特定浓度或驼峰时，这称为双峰分布。通常，当您看到这种形状的数据时，这意味着您正在测量两个您认为是单个流程的流程。例如，如果两个不同的人正在执行一项工作，他们可能会产生不同的结果，从而导致直方图上出现两个不同的驼峰。如果双峰数据确实来自单个进程，则该数据不正常。
直方图还可以具有其他几种常见形状，特别是在呈现非正常数据或存在错误或问题的数据时。如果直方图看起来非常随机，如下面的第一个示例所示，则数据测量过程中可能存在错误。测量可能不足以捕捉过程的真实情况，或者操作的定义以及如何收集数据可能有问题。如果直方图描绘了数据元素在箱之间几乎均匀的分布，情况也是如此。

如上面第二张图所示，偏态分布的特征是数据仅聚集在图的一端。倾斜直方图在某些类型的数据中很常见，特别是当数据在自然截止时间“下降”时。例如，在呼叫中心，您可以测量代表完成某种类型的电话呼叫所需的时间。该数据的自然终点是 0——完成一次电话呼叫总是需要 0 秒以上的时间。通常，这将需要几秒钟以上的时间，因此在范围的下端不会有很多数据点。也许这个特定的电话遵循一定的脚本，并且大多数电话都会在 7 到 8 分钟内结束。通话时长的直方图可能如下图所示。
考虑下面的基本示例以进一步理解直方图。
一家当地配送公司希望更多地了解送货司机满足预计到达时间的情况。该团队从一周的信息中随机抽取交货样本，并考虑每次交货的两个数据点：预计交货时间和实际交货时间。他们从估计时间中减去实际时间，以确定一侧或另一侧的交货时间有多远。结果是下面的数据表。
为了创建直方图，团队按分钟差异对选择进行排序，发现范围为 -27 到 48。团队决定创建一个从 -30 到 50 的直方图，bin 大小为 10。结果数据如下：
为了创建上表，团队简单地计算了每个容器中有多少个数据点。数据点 -27 和 -25 落入 bin -20（至 -29）；即 2 个数据点。 8 个数据点落入 10 个 bin。当绘制在条形图上时，此信息会创建直方图。
处理直方图时，使用的箱数很重要。如果分箱太少，图表不会提供任何相关信息。如果所讨论的数据集有太多的箱，您也会失去信息功能，并且您的图表不会显示为粗糙曲线，而是看起来像梳子。通常，您拥有的数据越多，您可以在直方图上包含的数据箱就越多。
考虑以下四个直方图，它们都代表同一组数据。该数据包含 150 个随机数，范围从 38.62 到 62.89。
随着分箱数量的增加，分箱的大小逐渐变小。在第一个图表中，bin 大小为 5；在最后一个图表中，bin 大小为 0.5。为了了解此直方图中表示的数据类型，第二个或第三个图表可能是最相关的。我们将在本章后面介绍从直方图和相关统计数据确定数据类型。
在 Excel 中创建直方图

title.zh: 在 Excel 中创建直方图

en.text: Before moving on with probability distributions, we’ll look at creating histograms in Excel using the Analysis ToolPak that was discussed in the last chapter.
1. Open Excel and copy the following data table into a blank workbook.
2. Copy the following bin designations into the second column in the same worksheet.
3. Select Data > Data Analysis
4. Select Histogram and click OK.
5. Click in the Input Range box, and then highlight the cells for the input range (the 50 data cells you copied from above).

6. Click in the Bin Range box, and then highlight the cells that include the bin data you copied from above. 7. Check the New Worksheet Ply: option. 8. Check the Chart Output option. 9. Ensure that your Histogram dialog box appears as below.
10. Click OK.

Excel automatically generates a bin and frequency table as well as a histogram graph in a new worksheet. The histogram design defaults to a standard bar graph, but you can create the look of the histograms previously shown by changing one setting on the design.
1. Click on the histogram. 2. Select Design from the top menu.
3. Under Quick Layout, choose the layout where the bars are stacked next to each other rather than with space between them.
Your result should look like the graph below.
Experiment with your own data, creating histograms with various bin sizes to begin to develop an understanding of how data converts to this type of chart.

zh.text: 在继续讨论概率分布之前，我们将使用上一章讨论的分析工具库在 Excel 中创建直方图。
1. 打开Excel，将以下数据表复制到空白工作簿中。
2. 将以下箱名称复制到同一工作表的第二列中。
3. 选择数据 > 数据分析
4. 选择直方图并单击确定。
5. 单击“输入范围”框，然后突出显示输入范围的单元格（从上面复制的 50 个数据单元格）。
6. 单击“Bin Range”框，然后突出显示包含您从上面复制的 bin 数据的单元格。 7. 选中新工作表层：选项。 8. 检查图表输出选项。 9. 确保直方图对话框如下所示。
10. 单击“确定”。
Excel 会在新工作表中自动生成 bin 和频率表以及直方图。直方图设计默认为标准条形图，但您可以通过更改设计上的一项设置来创建先前显示的直方图的外观。
1. 单击直方图。 2. 从顶部菜单中选择设计。
3. 在“快速布局”下，选择条形彼此相邻堆叠的布局，而不是条形之间留有空间。
您的结果应如下图所示。
使用您自己的数据进行试验，创建具有各种 bin 大小的直方图，以开始了解数据如何转换为此类图表。

en.text: Normal distribution, also called Gaussian distribution, is probably the most important distribution related to continuous data from a statistical analysis standpoint. A normal, or Gaussian, distribution is depicted below.

Normal data is shaped symmetrically surrounding the mean, represented above by the x-bar line. A normal curve is beneficial for determining the probability that a given data point in a population will fall inside a certain range within the distribution.

In a perfect normal distribution, 68.26 percent of all data points fall within plus or minus one standard deviation from the mean; this area is represented by the red arrow on the visual above. The blue arrow, which covers plus or minus two standard deviations from the mean, indicates the area under the curve that includes 95.46 percent of the data points. The purple area, which covers plus and minus three standard deviations from the mean, indicates the portion of the curve that covers 99.73 percent of the data. Less than 1 percent of data will fall under the curve outside of three standard deviations on either side.

Distributions – even normal distributions – vary a bit. To determine the exact probabilities of various data points, advanced statistics are required; Excel and other programs perform the calculations for you, making it easier to conduct analysis. Before we discuss probability calculations using Excel, we’ll look at determining whether your data is normal in the first place.

Testing whether data is normal is critical to many steps in statistical analysis, because the results of many tests can be invalid if you don’t account for the data you are working with. The most basic form of many of these tests are designed to work with normal data.
Normality Testing in Excel: Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit Test

We’ve touched on how visual inspection can help you determine whether data is normal or not. The histogram below, taken from earlier in the chapter, presents what seems to be obviously normal data. Data points converge around the center, and the histogram is roughly symmetrical in nature.
But what about this histogram, which is the one created in the practice exercise in the section on creating histograms in Excel?

The histogram above is an example of a chart that doesn’t fall neatly into any of the histogram shapes previously discussed. It seems to have elements of the normal curve, but it could also be skewed data. This is why we perform statistical tests to determine normality.

You’ll usually begin exploring probability data by following the instructions in the previous section to create a histogram for the data. For the example of the normality test, we’ll use the same data from the section above, which is:
Creating a histogram using the Analysis ToolPak generates a chart and a data table, as seen below.
Calculate descriptive statistics for the data.
Use the Descriptive Statistics option in the Analysis ToolPak to quickly generate descriptive statistics for your data set.
1. Select Data > Data Analysis > Descriptive Statistics
2. Click OK 3. Click in the Input Range box and select your input range using the mouse.

4. In this case, the data is grouped by columns. In most statistical analysis, that will be the case, but if you have data grouped by rows, you should change the Grouped By selection. 5. Select to output information in a new worksheet. 6. Ensure at least the Summary statistics box is checked. You can also check the Confidence level for mean and the Kth largest and smallest boxes, though that information isn’t required in the Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit test, which is the test we are running to test for normality of the data. If you check these extra boxes, Excel will simply provide you with additional information that we won’t be using at this time.
7. Click OK. 8. Excel returns descriptive summary statistics for your data set.

For the Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit test, you will need to note the sample size (or count), the same standard deviation, and the sample mean. The sample size is the number of items in the data set, which

was 50 for this example. If you don’t remember what the sample size was, you can refer to the count listed in the descriptive statistics. The other two figures are taken from the descriptive statistics above:
- Sample mean: 48.778 - Sample standard deviation: 11.444
Set up the hypothesis.

The Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit test is actually a hypothesis test. That means you are testing the data with regard to a null hypothesis and an alternative hypothesis. The two hypotheses for the Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit test are:
Null hypothesis: The data is normal.
Alternative hypothesis: The data is not normal.
If one is not true, then the other is. In statistical terms, we talk in terms of accepting or rejecting the null hypothesis. If we reject the null, we accept the alternative.
Understand the Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit test premise.

Basically, the Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit test takes the number of samples in each bin on the histogram and compares that to the number of samples you might expect to find in each bin given a normal curve. Using the actual number of samples in each bin and the expected number of samples, we can calculate what is called the Chi-Square Statistic in Excel. That number then lets us calculate a p- Value. In this case, it is the size of the p-Value that lets us decide whether to accept or reject the hypothesis that the data is normal.
For the purpose of the Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit test in this situation, if the p-Value is greater than 0.05, we will accept the null hypothesis that the data is normally distributed.
The Observed Bins
Having created a histogram via the Analysis ToolPak, you already have access to the observed bin distribution. That information is housed in the data table Excel creates to make the histogram.
The Expected Bins
We can use statistics related to the normal curve to calculate how we might expect bins to behave given the median and standard deviation of our sample.
To give you an idea of what is going on with the statistical calculations involved in determining expected size of bins, consider the graphic below.

This graphic roughly depicts the bins from our histogram drawn on the normal curve. Because mathematical formulations exist for determining the area under a curve, it’s possible to determine the area under the curve within a specific bin. Ultimately, that is done by calculating the total area and subtracting portions.

We begin with a calculation known as the Cumulative Distribution Function, or CDF. The CDF measures the total area under a curve to the left of the point we are measuring from. For example, the total area under the curve above that is to the left of 45 is 50 percent. Once we know the CDF at each border of our bins, it’s a matter of subtraction to calculate the CDF for each individual bin. For example, the CDF for the bin located between 40 and 45 would equal the CDF of 45 minus the CDF of 40.

One problem with this rough depiction is that the curve drawn above centers on 45, and we know from Excel that our mean is 48.778. We’ll use that number in our calculations to account for the slight shift.
Excel can calculate CDF with the formula:
=NORMDIST(x value, Sample Mean, Sample Standard Deviation, TRUE)

Set up the tables for calculating the CDF of each bin by copying the bin designations onto the descriptive statistics worksheet that Excel previously created for you and creating two columns, one for total CDF and one for bin CDF. Use the image below as an example.
Enter the formula for calculating CDF into column E, referencing the same mean and standard deviation for each row and using the numbers in D as X.

For the first row – in our case, the bin marked 10 -- the bin-only area is equal to the CDF because there is nothing left of the bin’s upper limit. For all other rows, the bin-only area is the CDF minus the CDF for the bin designation above. So, you would enter =E2 in the first data row for column F. The second data row would be calculated as E3-E2; the next would be E4-E3, and so forth. The result is the percentage of the curve in each bin.

Calculating the expected number of samples in each bin is as easy as multiplying the percentages of each bin by the sample size. Again, you can see from the descriptive statistics that the count for this set of data was 50.
To calculate the Chi-Squared statistic, you’ll use both the expected number of items in each bin and the actual or observed number. Copy the observed numbers over from your histogram worksheet.
Apply the following formula to each row:
(expected – observed)2 / expected
Calculate the final numbers for each row as desired in Excel.
Add up the final numbers to get the Chi-Squared statistic, denoted by X2. For our example, X2 is 16.7333.
To use the Chi-Squared statistic to find the p-Value, we also need one more item for the Excel formula to work: we need what is called the degrees of freedom.
Degrees of freedom = #bins – 1 - #calculated parameters
We have 14 bins. The parameters we used to arrive at the Chi-Squared statistic that we calculated from our sample were the mean and standard deviation: two parameters. For our example:
Degrees of freedom = 14 – 1 – 2 = 11
Now that we have both the degrees of freedom (df), and the Chi-Squared value, we can use Excel to calculate the p-Value. Simply enter the formula below, inputting the correct values.
In the case of our example, the resulting p-Value is 0.116. Because the p-Value is greater than 0.05, we accept the null hypothesis. Our data is normal.

zh.text: 从统计分析的角度来看，正态分布，也称为高斯分布，可能是与连续数据相关的最重要的分布。正态分布或高斯分布如下所示。

rows: 术语说明 Normal Distribution（正态分布）：常见钟形分布，许多统计推断方法以其为基础。
zh.text: 术语说明 Normal Distribution（正态分布）：常见钟形分布，许多统计推断方法以其为基础。
正常数据的形状围绕均值对称，如上面的 x 条线所示。正态曲线有利于确定总体中给定数据点落在分布内特定范围内的概率。

rows: 术语说明 X-Bar Chart（均值图）：监控样本均值随时间变化的控制图。
zh.text: 术语说明 X-Bar Chart（均值图）：监控样本均值随时间变化的控制图。
在完美正态分布中，68.26% 的数据点落在平均值正负 1 个标准差范围内；该区域由上图中的红色箭头表示。蓝色箭头覆盖了平均值的正负两个标准差，表示曲线下包含 95.46% 数据点的面积。紫色区域覆盖平均值的正负三个标准差，表示曲线中覆盖 99.73% 数据的部分。不到 1% 的数据将落在曲线两侧超出三个标准差的范围内。

rows: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
zh.text: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
分布——甚至是正态分布——略有不同。为了确定各种数据点的准确概率，需要高级统计； Excel 和其他程序为您执行计算，使分析变得更加容易。在我们讨论使用 Excel 进行概率计算之前，我们首先要确定您的数据是否正常。
测试数据是否正常对于统计分析中的许多步骤至关重要，因为如果不考虑正在使用的数据，许多测试的结果可能是无效的。其中许多测试的最基本形式旨在处理正常数据。
Excel 中的正态性检验：卡方拟合优度检验
我们已经介绍了目视检查如何帮助您确定数据是否正常。 下面的直方图取自本章前面的内容，显示了看似明显正常的数据。数据点集中在中心周围，直方图本质上大致对称。
但是这个直方图（它是在 Excel 中创建直方图部分的练习中创建的）又如何呢？
上面的直方图是一个图表示例，它并不完全属于前面讨论的任何直方图形状。它似乎具有正常曲线的元素，但也可能是扭曲的数据。这就是我们进行统计检验以确定正态性的原因。
您通常会按照上一节中的说明开始探索概率数据，为数据创建直方图。对于正态性检验的示例，我们将使用上一节中的相同数据，即：
使用分析工具库创建直方图会生成图表和数据表，如下所示。
计算数据的描述性统计。
使用分析工具库中的描述性统计选项可以快速生成数据集的描述性统计。
1. 选择数据 > 数据分析 > 描述性统计
2. 单击“确定” 3. 单击“输入范围”框，然后使用鼠标选择输入范围。
4. 在本例中，数据按列分组。在大多数统计分析中，情况都是如此，但如果您有按行分组的数据，则应更改“分组依据”选择。 5. 选择在新工作表中输出信息。 6. 确保至少选中“摘要统计”框。您还可以检查平均值以及第 K 个最大和最小框的置信水平，尽管卡方拟合优度检验不需要这些信息，这是我们正在运行的用于测试数据正态性的测试。如果您选中这些额外的框，Excel 将仅向您提供我们目前不会使用的其他信息。
7. 单击“确定”。 8. Excel 返回数据集的描述性汇总统计数据。
对于卡方拟合优度检验，您需要记下样本大小（或计数）、相同标准差和样本均值。样本量是数据集中的项目数，其中
本例中为 50。 如果您不记得样本量是多少，可以参考描述性统计中列出的计数。另外两个数字取自上面的描述性统计：
提出假设。
卡方拟合优度检验实际上是一种假设检验。这意味着您正在测试有关原假设和备择假设的数据。卡方拟合优度检验的两个假设是：

rows: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
zh.text: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
零假设：数据正常。
备择假设：数据不正常。
如果其中一个不正确，那么另一个就是正确的。用统计学术语来说，我们谈论的是接受或拒绝零假设。如果我们拒绝零，我们就接受替代方案。
了解卡方拟合优度检验前提。
基本上，卡方拟合优度检验获取直方图上每个箱中的样本数，并将其与给定正态曲线时您可能期望在每个箱中找到的样本数进行比较。使用每个箱中的实际样本数和预期样本数，我们可以计算 Excel 中的卡方统计量。然后我们可以通过该数字计算 p 值。在这种情况下，p 值的大小让我们决定是否接受或拒绝数据正常的假设。

rows: 术语说明 p-value（p 值）：在原假设成立时观察到当前或更极端结果的概率；常用于判断显著性。
zh.text: 术语说明 p-value（p 值）：在原假设成立时观察到当前或更极端结果的概率；常用于判断显著性。
在这种情况下，出于卡方拟合优度检验的目的，如果 p 值大于 0.05，我们将接受数据呈正态分布的原假设。
观察到的分箱
通过分析工具库创建直方图后，您已经可以访问观察到的箱分布。该信息存储在 Excel 创建的用于制作直方图的数据表中。
预期的分箱
我们可以使用与正态曲线相关的统计数据来计算在给定样本的中值和标准差的情况下我们预期箱的表现。
为了让您了解确定分箱预期大小所涉及的统计计算的情况，请考虑下图。
该图粗略地描绘了在正态曲线上绘制的直方图的箱。 由于存在用于确定曲线下面积的数学公式，因此可以确定特定箱内的曲线下面积。最终，这是通过计算总面积并减去部分来完成的。
我们从称为累积分布函数（CDF）的计算开始。 CDF 测量我们测量点左侧的曲线下的总面积。例如，上方曲线下方 45 左侧的总面积为 50%。一旦我们知道了每个 bin 边界的 CDF，就可以通过减法来计算每个单独 bin 的 CDF。例如，位于 40 和 45 之间的 bin 的 CDF 将等于 45 的 CDF 减去 40 的 CDF。
这种粗略描述的一个问题是上面绘制的曲线以 45 为中心，而我们从 Excel 中知道平均值是 48.778。我们将在计算中使用该数字来解释轻微的变化。
Excel 可以使用以下公式计算 CDF：
=NORMDIST(x 值, 样本均值, 样本标准差, TRUE)
通过将箱名称复制到 Excel 之前为您创建的描述性统计工作表并创建两列（一列用于总 CDF，一列用于箱 CDF），设置用于计算每个箱的 CDF 的表。使用下图作为示例。
在 E 列中输入计算 CDF 的公式，引用每行的相同平均值和标准差，并使用 D 中的数字作为 X。
对于第一行（在我们的例子中，bin 标记为 10），仅包含 bin 的区域等于 CDF，因为 bin 的上限已没有剩余。对于所有其他行，仅 bin 区域是 CDF 减去上面指定的 bin 的 CDF。因此，您将在 F 列的第一个数据行中输入 =E2。第二个数据行将计算为 E3-E2；接下来是 E4-E3，依此类推。结果是每个箱中曲线的百分比。
计算每个箱中的预期样本数就像将每个箱的百分比乘以样本大小一样简单。同样，从描述性统计中可以看到，这组数据的计数为 50。
要计算卡方统计量，您将使用每个箱中的预期项目数以及实际或观察到的数量。从直方图工作表中复制观察到的数字。
将以下公式应用于每一行：
（预期 – 观察到）2 / 预期
根据需要在 Excel 中计算每行的最终数字。
将最终数字相加即可得到卡方统计量，用 X2 表示。对于我们的示例，X2 是 16.7333。
要使用卡方统计量来查找 p 值，我们还需要一项才能使 Excel 公式发挥作用：我们需要所谓的自由度。
自由度 = #bins – 1 - #calculated 参数
我们有 14 个分箱。我们用于从样本计算得出卡方统计量的参数是平均值和标准差：两个参数。对于我们的例子：
现在我们已经有了自由度 (df) 和卡方值，我们可以使用 Excel 来计算 p 值。只需输入下面的公式，输入正确的值即可。
在我们的示例中，生成的 p 值为 0.116。由于 p 值大于 0.05，因此我们接受原假设。我们的数据是正常的。

en.text: Once you verify that your data is normal, you can use many statistical tests to draw conclusions about the data and the population as a whole from your sample. One of the things you can do is use Excel’s normal probability functions to draw conclusions about data.

For example, let’s assume that the data we’ve been working with throughout this chapter is a sample selection of the weights of boxes in a delivery channel. The 50 samples were taken from the same shipping center over the course of a week and were randomly selected. Given that we know the data is normal, what is the probability that any package selected from the distribution center will weigh over 50 pounds?
Excel provides the following formula:
=NORMDIST(x, mean, standard_dev, cumulative)
When using this formula:

- X = the value you are testing - Mean = sample mean from the descriptive statistics - Standard_dev = sample standard deviation from the descriptive statistics - Cumulative = FALSE if you want to test the probability of the exact occurrence; TRUE if you want to test the probability of any value left of x on the curve (or, in most cases, less than x)

Entering the formula in Excel for our data set with a FALSE cumulative tells us there is a 3.47 percent chance of a randomly selected box weighing 50 pounds. This is a real and important piece of information that might be beneficial to a Six Sigma team.

Entering the formula in Excel for our data set with a TRUE cumulative tells us there is a 54.25 percent chance of a randomly selected box weighing less than 50 pounds. Subtracting from 100 percent leaves a 45.75 percent chance that a box will weigh over 50 pounds.

Why might this be important? In this particular case, a shipping manager might be working to hire shipping reps. Because there is a strong likelihood that the employees will handle boxes over 50 pounds, the manager knows to include that requirement in job descriptions. Or, a Six Sigma team might be working on a process to increase production in a warehouse; the knowledge that many of the boxes are heavy enough to require special handling is relevant to how the team approaches the process.

Six Sigma teams can use Excel to generate a lot of information about the data. Excel also provides a formula for the inverse of the above calculation. What if the team wanted to know about the weight of the heaviest 80 percent of boxes in the shipping center?
The formula is:
=NORMINV(probability, mean, standard_dev)
Probability must be entered as a decimal point, not a percent. In the case of 0.8 in our example data set, all of the boxes in the heaviest 80 percent are above 58.40 pounds.
Because we are drawing statistical conclusions from samples, there is some error inherent in the process. Error is usually calculated for in advanced analysis.

How to apply statistical data and conclusions to a business or project approach is covered in depth in the next unit, where we’ll look at how these concepts can generate real information that is applicable to business processes.

zh.text: 一旦验证数据正常，您就可以使用许多统计检验从样本中得出有关数据和总体总体的结论。您可以做的事情之一是使用 Excel 的正态概率函数得出有关数据的结论。
例如，假设我们在本章中使用的数据是交付渠道中箱子重量的样本选择。这 50 个样本是在一周内从同一运输中心随机抽取的。假设我们知道数据是正常的，那么从配送中心选择的任何包裹重量超过 50 磅的概率是多少？
Excel 提供了以下公式：
=NORMDIST(x, 平均值, standard_dev, 累积)
使用此公式时：
- X = 您正在测试的值 - Mean = 描述性统计的样本均值 - Standard_dev = 描述性统计的样本标准差 - Cumulative = FALSE（如果您想测试确切发生的概率）； 如果要测试曲线上 x 左侧任何值的概率（或者在大多数情况下小于 x），则为 TRUE
在 Excel 中为我们的数据集输入带有 FALSE 累积值的公式后，我们得知随机选择一个重 50 磅的盒子的可能性为 3.47%。这是一个真实而重要的信息，可能对六西格玛团队有利。
在 Excel 中为我们的数据集输入具有 TRUE 累积值的公式后，我们会发现随机选择的盒子重量小于 50 磅的可能性为 54.25%。从 100% 中减去，盒子重量超过 50 磅的可能性为 45.75%。
为什么这很重要？在这种特殊情况下，运输经理可能正在努力雇用运输代表。由于员工很可能会搬运超过 50 磅的箱子，因此经理知道要将该要求纳入职位描述中。或者，六西格玛团队可能正在研究提高仓库产量的流程；许多箱子都足够重，需要特殊处理，这与团队处理流程的方式有关。
六西格玛团队可以使用Excel生成大量的数据信息。 Excel 还提供了上述计算的逆计算公式。如果团队想要了解运输中心最重 80% 的箱子的重量该怎么办？
公式为：
=NORMINV(概率、平均值、standard_dev)
概率必须以小数点形式输入，而不是百分比。在我们的示例数据集中为 0.8 的情况下，最重 80% 的所有箱子都超过 58.40 磅。
因为我们是从样本中得出统计结论，所以这个过程中存在一些固有的误差。通常在高级分析中计算误差。
下一个单元将深入介绍如何将统计数据和结论应用于业务或项目方法，其中我们将了解这些概念如何生成适用于业务流程的真实信息。
