en.text: Hypothesis tests are statistical tools used to draw conclusions based on data. With a basic understanding of the process and process performance, as well as real-time data – or as close to real- time data as possible – Six Sigma teams can draw statistical conclusions that are extremely accurate if they (1) ensure that the measurement systems are good, (2) have the right sample size, and (3) know how to set up the right type of hypothesis test.

Measurement systems analysis was discussed in Chapter 13. In this chapter, we’ll talk about different types of hypothesis tests, how to set them up, and how to read the results. In Chapter 22, we’ll cover how to choose the right sample size for various data types and hypothesis tests, and in the chapters on Minitab, you’ll find a step-by-step guide for running tests in the software program. You can also run some tests in Excel using the Analysis Toolpak you previously downloaded.

rows: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
zh.text: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
假设检验是用于根据数据得出结论的统计工具。通过对流程和流程性能以及实时数据（或尽可能接近实时数据）的基本了解，六西格玛团队可以得出极其准确的统计结论，如果他们（1）确保测量系统良好，（2）拥有正确的样本量，以及（3）知道如何设置正确类型的假设检验。

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
第 13 章讨论了测量系统分析。在本章中，我们将讨论不同类型的假设检验、如何设置它们以及如何读取结果。在第 22 章中，我们将介绍如何为各种数据类型和假设检验选择正确的样本量，在有关 Minitab 的章节中，您将找到在软件程序中运行检验的分步指南。您还可以使用之前下载的分析工具库在 Excel 中运行一些测试。
假设检验基础知识

en.text: Hypothesis tests cover three broad categories:

Testing whether the data you have fits a data model. In Chapter 18, we conducted a hypothesis test to determine whether data fit the normal curve. We used the Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit Test, but ultimately, it was a hypothesis test.
Comparing a statistic to a hypothesis about the data or population.

Answering the question whether something changed within the data, often after a team has modified an input or other part of the process. In the case of most Six Sigma projects, the team probably wants to find out whether the process or outcome is improved.
While the type of hypothesis test you use depends on the answers you are seeking and the type of data you have, all of the tests follow essentially the same guidelines:
You begin with a statistic or criteria that you usually compute from your sample data
You create a null hypothesis and an alternate hypothesis, in keeping with the type of test you are dealing with
o Remember, in chapter 18 when we ran the Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit Test:
 The null hypothesis was that the data was normal  The alternate hypothesis was that the data was not normal
The statistic or criteria is compared against a reference criteria or distribution
How the calculated statistic compares to the reference criteria determines whether you accept the null hypothesis or reject the null hypothesis in favor of the alternative hypothesis

Hypothesis tests are a large part of inferential statistics, where we draw conclusions about the overall process or population by analyzing the sample data and measurements. When stating hypotheses, we are not making statements about the sample. We are making statements about the population or entire process.
A hypothesis is “the population mean is 5.”
We don’t need to make a hypothesis about the sample mean – we can calculate the sample mean.
Null Versus Alternative
Hypothesis tests have two main parts: the null hypothesis and the alternative hypothesis.

The null hypothesis is abbreviated as H0 and is usually a statement about the data that reflects no effect or no difference. In chapter 18, we hypothesized that our data was normal. In effect, we were saying “there is no statistical difference between the distribution of our data and the distribution of data on a normal curve.”

The alternative hypothesis is abbreviated as Ha and is usually a statement that is likely to be true if the null hypothesis is not true. In chapter 18, the alternative hypothesis was “there is a statistical difference between the distribution of our data and the distribution of data on a normal curve.” In short, if we reject the null hypothesis, we accept the alternative hypothesis – in this case, that our data is not normal.
Typically, the null hypothesis is an equal statement of some type. The mean of the new process is equal to the mean of the new process. The distribution of the data is equal to the normal curve.

The alternative hypothesis is typically written as a not equals, a greater than, or a less than statement. The mean of the new process is greater than the mean of the old process. The distribution of the data is not equal to the normal curve. How you write the alternative hypothesis depends on the question you are asking and the type of hypothesis test you are running.
The Risk of Hypothesis Testing Error
Anytime you draw inferences about a population from sample data, there is at least some likelihood of error. With hypothesis testing, errors come in two types.
Type I Error: The null hypothesis is rejected when it is actually true.
o Also called producer risk o The probability of the risk is measured by alpha, where 𝛼 is a probability between 0 and 1.
Type II Error: The null hypothesis is accepted when it is actually false.
o Also called the consumer risk o The probability of the risk is measured by beta, where 𝛽 is a probability between 0 and 1.

To describe the risk – and set up our hypothesis test for what we deem to be an acceptable risk, a confidence level must be picked. The most common confidence level used is 95 percent, or 𝛼= 0.05. Typically, the confidence level is set with the Type I error in mind, so you use alpha for the confidence level. The value of 𝛽 then contributes to the sample size requirements and the power; sample size will be covered in the next chapter. Additional information about alpha and beta values is covered in the section in this chapter on running individual hypothesis tests.

zh.text: 假设检验涵盖三大类：
测试您拥有的数据是否适合数据模型。在第18章中，我们进行了假设检验以确定数据是否符合正态曲线。我们使用了卡方拟合优度检验，但最终它是一个假设检验。
将统计数据与有关数据或总体的假设进行比较。

rows: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
通常在团队修改输入或流程的其他部分之后，回答数据中是否发生某些变化的问题。对于大多数六西格玛项目来说，团队可能想知道过程或结果是否得到了改进。
虽然您使用的假设检验类型取决于您正在寻求的答案和您拥有的数据类型，但所有检验基本上都遵循相同的准则：
您从通常根据样本数据计算的统计数据或标准开始
您创建一个原假设和一个备择假设，以与您正在处理的测试类型保持一致
o 请记住，在第 18 章中，当我们运行卡方拟合优度检验时：
 原假设是数据正常  备择假设是数据不正常
将统计数据或标准与参考标准或分布进行比较
计算的统计量与参考标准的比较方式决定了您是接受原假设还是拒绝原假设而支持备择假设
假设检验是推论统计的重要组成部分，我们通过分析样本数据和测量结果得出有关整个过程或总体的结论。在陈述假设时，我们并不是对样本做出陈述。我们正在就人口或整个过程发表声明。
假设是“总体平均值为 5”。
我们不需要对样本均值做出假设——我们可以计算样本均值。
空与替代
假设检验有两个主要部分：原假设和备择假设。
原假设缩写为 H0，通常是关于反映没有影响或没有差异的数据的陈述。在第 18 章中，我们假设我们的数据是正常的。实际上，我们的意思是“我们的数据分布与正态曲线上的数据分布之间不存在统计差异。”
备择假设缩写为 Ha，通常是如果原假设不为真则可能为真的陈述。第 18 章中，备择假设是“我们的数据分布与正态曲线上的数据分布之间存在统计差异”。简而言之，如果我们拒绝零假设，我们就会接受备择假设——在这种情况下，我们的数据不正常。
通常，原假设是某种类型的等式陈述。新过程的均值等于新过程的均值。数据的分布等于正态曲线。
备择假设通常写成不等于、大于或小于语句。新过程的均值大于旧过程的均值。数据的分布不等于正态曲线。如何编写备择假设取决于您提出的问题以及您正在运行的假设检验的类型。
假设检验错误的风险
每当你从样本数据中得出关于总体的推论时，至少存在一定程度的错误可能性。通过假设检验，错误有两种类型。
第一类错误：原假设实际上为真时被拒绝。
o 也称为生产者风险 o 风险的概率用 alpha 来衡量，其中 𝛼 是 0 到 1 之间的概率。
II 类错误：原假设实际上是错误的，但它被接受。
o 也称为消费者风险 o 风险的概率通过 beta 来衡量，其中 𝛽 是 0 到 1 之间的概率。
为了描述风险并为我们认为可接受的风险建立假设检验，必须选择置信水平。最常用的置信水平是 95%，即 𝛼= 0.05。通常，设置置信水平时会考虑到 I 类错误，因此您可以使用 alpha 作为置信水平。 𝛽 的值会影响样本量要求和功效；样本大小将在下一章中介绍。有关 alpha 和 beta 值的其他信息将在本章有关运行单个假设检验的部分中介绍。

en.text: To determine which hypothesis test to run, you must know:
Which type of data you have (continuous/variable or discrete/attribute)
The number of levels of interest for the input in question (1, 2, or more than two)
Distribution of data (normal or non-normal)
What you are testing (means, medians, variance, count, or proportions)
In this chapter, the hypothesis tests that are covered deal with 1 or 2 levels of interest.
In the following section, we’ll introduce various types of hypothesis tests. Then, we’ll walk through running some of these tests using statistical software.
Hypothesis Tests for Discrete Data
1-Proportion Test
Used when there is only one factor for x
Used when there is one level of interest for x (the input)
o Typically, there is one set of data (the sample) and a target
Used when comparing proportions (percentage, rates) between samples and a target
Test is available in Minitab
o Requires samples in columns or summary of data to include number of events, number of trials, and hypothesized proportion
The hypothesis test is set up as:
H0 : Psample = Ptarget
Ha : Psample ≠ Ptarget

Note that in the above set-up, the alternative hypothesis is set in a default to “not-equal.” Minitab allows you to adjust the alternative hypothesis to a greater than or less than scenario if desired.
Example of the 1-Proportion Test:

A school administrator believes that the students in a certain classroom are being impacted by some environmental issue with that room. The administrator wants to see if the students in that room perform statistically lower on tests than the students throughout the school; she has already noted that different classes in that room are taught by other teachers who also teach in the other rooms, which means she has ruled out a teacher influence.

The administrator gathers data. The rate of failure for classes taught in any other classroom is 20 percent. Out of 142 students who have a class in the classroom in question, 38 are failing that particular class.
The hypothesis test would be written out with both a real-world statement and a statistical statement for both the null and alternative hypothesis.
H0: There is no difference in the failure rate between the classes taught in the specific classroom and those taught elsewhere.
o Pclassroom = Pelsewhere
o Pclassroom = 0.2 o
Ha: The rate of failure is greater for those classes taught in the specific classroom.
o Pclassroom > Pelsewhere
o Pclassroom > 0.2 o
2 Proportion Test
Used when there is only one factor for x
Used when there are 2 levels of interest for x (the input)
o Typically, there are two sets of data regarding the input and you are comparing those two sets of data
Used when comparing proportions (percentage, rates) between samples or between samples and a target
Test is available in Minitab
o Requires samples in columns
The hypothesis test is set up as:
Ha : P1 ≠ P2

Note that in the above set-up, the alternative hypothesis is set in a default to “not-equal.” Minitab allows you to adjust the alternative hypothesis to a greater than or less than scenario if desired.
Example of the 2-Proportion Test:

Reconsider the example above about the school administrator and the classroom. Perhaps the school administrator determines that students in that classroom do in fact perform statistically differently from students in other classrooms. The administrator makes a decision to move the students in that classroom to another classroom. She then gathers data about test performance in the new classroom.

Now, the administrator has two sets of test performance data for the same students. The only variable that has changed is the classroom location. She wants to know, is there a difference between performance in the old room versus the new room?
H0: There is no difference in the failure rate between the old room and the new room.
o Pold = Pnew
Ha: The rate of failure is less in the new classroom.
o Pold > Pnew
Hypothesis Tests for Continuous Normal Data
1-Sample T Test (or Paired T Test)
Used when comparing means
Typically used when dealing with smaller samples or when standard deviation is known.
o When dealing with larger samples or when standard deviation is not known, use the 1- Sample Z-Test.
1-Sample T Test compares the mean of a sample to a target mean

Paired T Test compares the mean of a sample against the mean of another sample for the same factor of X. Usually, the first sample is the baseline and the second sample is taken after a change is made; the test is run to see if the change made a difference.
1-Sample T Test is available in Minitab
o Requires standard deviation, mean, and sample size as well as the target, or hypothesized mean.
Paired T Test is available in Minitab and Excel’s Analysis TookPak
The hypothesis test is set up as:
Ha : 𝜇 1 ≠ 𝜇 2

Note that in the above set-up, the alternative hypothesis is set in a default to “not-equal.” Minitab allows you to adjust the alternative hypothesis to a greater than or less than scenario if desired.
Example of the 1-Sample T Test

An agriculture company growing corn produced an average of 168 bushels of corn per acre each year for five years. The company made a change to its seeding process in an attempt to increase the yield of its fields. The harvest following the seed change resulted in an average of 175 bushels of corn per acre each year. Did the seeding change result in a statistically different outcome in yield per acre?
H0: There is no difference between the outcome after the seeding change and the outcome before it.
o 𝜇old= 𝜇new
Ha: The seeding change resulted in an increase in the average amount yielded per field.
o 𝜇old< 𝜇new
Chi Square Test (or 1-Variance Test)
Used when comparing standard deviation or variance

Compares the standard deviation or variance between two samples of the same x factor (usually one sample is taken after a change to the process has been made) or compares the standard deviation or variance to a target or hypothesized statistic.
Is available in Minitab
o Requires sample data in two columns or summary data to include sample size with either sample standard deviation or sample variance and a hypothesized standard deviation or variance.
The hypothesis test is set up as:
Ha : 𝜗 1 ≠ 𝜗 2

Note that in the above set-up, the alternative hypothesis is set in a default to “not-equal.” Minitab allows you to adjust the alternative hypothesis to a greater than or less than scenario if desired.
Example of the Chi-Square Test (or 1-Variance Test)

A manufacturing plant makes the wheels that are put on dolly carts. The plant provides three sizes of wheels to a partner plant, which makes the dolly itself and uses the wheels to complete the process. The partner plant makes a complaint: The smallest wheels coming from the factory have enough variation in size to cause stability problems with the final product, resulting in defective dollies or wasted wheel parts.

The wheel-making plant launches a Six Sigma improvement project to reduce the variation of the size in the smallest wheels. The team measures for a baseline and finds that the standard deviation in the size of the wheels is 0.0409. The team makes changes to the process and measures again to compare the new standard deviation to the old.
H0: There is no difference between the standard deviations; the changes did nothing to statistically reduce the variation in the process.
o 𝜗 new= 0.0409
Ha: The improvements reduced the standard deviation in the process.
o 𝜗new< 0.0409
2-Sample T Test
Used when comparing means
Compares the means between two samples of the different x factors
Is available in Minitab and Excel
o Requires sample data in two columns or summary data to include sample size, sample mean, and standard deviation for both samples
Requires you to note whether equal variances between the two samples are assumed or not

The 2-Sample T Test might seem like the 1-Sample T Test, because in the 1-Sample T Test, we discussed how you might compare a sample from before a change to a sample from after a change. However, the 2-Sample T Test is slightly different, because you are comparing two samples from different populations. For example, you might compare the mean body temperature for children who didn’t receive the influenza vaccine to mean body temperature for children who did receive the vaccine. This is different

than comparing mean body temperature for the same group of children from last year, when they didn’t receive the vaccine, to this year, when they did receive the vaccine. The first example would use the 2- Sample T Test; the second (year-over-year) example would use the 1-Sample T Test.
The hypothesis test is set up as:
Ha : 𝜇1 ≠ 𝜇 2

Note that in the above set-up, the alternative hypothesis is set in a default to “not-equal.” Minitab allows you to adjust the alternative hypothesis to a greater than or less than scenario if desired.
Example of the 2-Sample T Test

Two attorneys practicing in the same law firm are turning in very different amounts of billable hours, even though the partners note that the two lawyers have similar caseloads. One partner hypothesized that Attorney A’s legal staff is taking too long to handle some tasks; because the firm bills at a certain time for administrative-style tasks regardless of how long those tasks take, if legal staff is taking too long for such tasks, they are spending time on work that can’t be billed for.

To test the theory, data is gathered about the time legal staff spends on tasks. Specifically, a team looks at time spent on three tasks for which the legal staff is allowed to bill 15 minutes each: certain types of simple administrative phone, copying, or filing tasks.
H0: There is no difference between the average time Attorney A’s staff takes and the average time Attorney B’s staff takes for such tasks.
o H0 : 𝜇AttorneyA= 𝜇AttorneyB
Ha: Attorney B’s staff takes, on average, less time for these tasks.
o Ha : 𝜇 AttorneyA > 𝜇 AttorneyB
Hypothesis Tests for Continuous Non-Normal Data
Chi-Square Test
The Chi-Square Test, which was described in the above section, can also be used to compare standard deviation between a sample and a hypothesized standard deviation when data is not normal.
One Sample Wilcox
Used when comparing medians
Compares the medians between a sample and a hypothesized sample or a new sample to a previous sample before changes were made
Is available in Minitab
o Requires sample data in a column and hypothesized mean
Typically used when data is somewhat symmetrical; otherwise, you can use the 1-Sample Sign test in Minitab for the same thing
The hypothesis test is set up as:
H0 : 𝑀1 = 𝑀𝑡
Ha : 𝑀1 ≠ 𝑀𝑡

Note that in the above set-up, the alternative hypothesis is set in a default to “not-equal.” Minitab allows you to adjust the alternative hypothesis to a greater than or less than scenario if desired.
Example of the One-Sample Wilcox

A real estate company wants to enter a high-dollar market, so they want to build some regional websites that cater to homebuyers looking for larger homes. Specifically, they want to cater to homebuyers who are purchasing homes with 4 or more bedrooms. Before the company can build websites and begin marketing, it needs to find neighborhoods where homes for sale typically have 4 or more bedrooms.
The real estate company gathers information about the median number of bedrooms in homes in a specific area.
H0: The median number of bedrooms is equal to 3.
o H0 : 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠= 3
Ha: The median number of bedrooms is greater than 3 (4 or more).
o Ha : 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠 > 3
Mann-Whitney Test
Used when comparing medians
Compares the medians between samples of two factors of x
Is available in Minitab
o Requires sample data in columns
The hypothesis test is set up as:
Ha : 𝑀1 ≠ 𝑀2

Note that in the above set-up, the alternative hypothesis is set in a default to “not-equal.” Minitab allows you to adjust the alternative hypothesis to a greater than or less than scenario if desired.
Example of the Mann-Whitney Test

Consider the real estate company example above again. Now, imagine that the real estate company has limited resources, so they can only launch a campaign for one neighborhood at a time. To maximize potential results of their first campaign, the company wants to choose the neighborhood with the highest number of bedrooms in the highest number of homes.
The company gathers information about homes in two neighborhoods. They then compare the samples to determine whether one sample has a statistically higher median number of bedrooms.
H0: The median number of bedrooms in neighborhood A is equal to the median number of bedrooms in neighborhood B.
o H0 : 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠𝐴= 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠𝐵
Ha: The median number of bedrooms in neighborhood A is higher than that in neighborhood B.
o Ha : 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠𝐴 > 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠𝐵

zh.text: 要确定要运行哪个假设检验，您必须知道：
您拥有哪种类型的数据（连续/变量或离散/属性）
相关输入的感兴趣级别数（1、2 或两个以上）
数据分布（正态或非正态）
您正在测试的内容（均值、中位数、方差、计数或比例）
在本章中，所涉及的假设检验涉及 1 或 2 个兴趣级别。
在下一节中，我们将介绍各种类型的假设检验。然后，我们将逐步使用统计软件运行其中一些测试。
离散数据的假设检验
1-比例测试
当 x 只有一个因子时使用
当 x（输入）有一个级别的兴趣时使用
o 通常，有一组数据（样本）和一个目标
用于比较样本和目标之间的比例（百分比、比率）
Minitab 中提供了测试
o 要求列中的样本或数据摘要包括事件数量、试验数量和假设比例
假设检验设置为：
H0：P样本=P目标
Ha : Psample ≠ Ptarget
请注意，在上述设置中，备择假设默认设置为“不等于”。如果需要，Minitab 允许您将备择假设调整为大于或小于场景。
1-比例测试示例：
学校管理员认为某个教室的学生受到该房间的某些环境问题的影响。管理员想了解该房间的学生在测试中的统计成绩是否低于全校学生；她已经注意到，那个房间里的不同课程是由也在其他房间里教书的其他老师讲授的，这意味着她已经排除了老师的影响。
管理员收集数据。在任何其他教室教授的课程的失败率为 20%。在该教室上课的 142 名学生中，有 38 名学生未能通过该课程。
假设检验将用真实世界的陈述和针对原假设和备择假设的统计陈述来写出。
H0：在特定教室教授的课程与在其他教室教授的课程之间的不及格率没有差异。
o Pclassroom = Pelsewhere
o P 课堂 = 0.2 o
哈：在特定教室授课的课程失败率更高。
o Pclassroom > Pelsewhere
o P 课堂 > 0.2 o
2 比例测试
当 x 只有一个因子时使用
当 x（输入）有 2 个兴趣级别时使用
o 通常，有两组关于输入的数据，并且您正在比较这两组数据
用于比较样本之间或样本与目标之间的比例（百分比、比率）
Minitab 中提供了测试
o 需要列中的样本
假设检验设置为：
哈：P1≠P2
请注意，在上述设置中，备择假设默认设置为“不等于”。如果需要，Minitab 允许您将备择假设调整为大于或小于场景。
2-比例测试的示例：
重新考虑上面关于学校管理员和教室的例子。也许学校管理人员确定该教室的学生实际上在统计上与其他教室的学生表现不同。管理员决定将该教室的学生转移到另一个教室。然后，她收集有关新教室测试表现的数据。
现在，管理员拥有同一学生的两组测试成绩数据。唯一改变的变量是教室位置。她想知道，旧房间和新房间的性能有区别吗？
H0：旧房间和新房间的故障率没有差异。
o Pold = Pnew
哈：新教室的失败率比较低。
o Pold > Pnew
连续正态数据的假设检验
1-样本 T 检验（或配对 T 检验）
比较平均值时使用
通常在处理较小的样本或已知标准偏差时使用。

rows: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
zh.text: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
o 当处理较大样本或标准偏差未知时，请使用 1 样本 Z 检验。
1-样本 T 检验将样本平均值与目标平均值进行比较
配对 T 检验将相同 X 因子的一个样本的均值与另一个样本的均值进行比较。通常，第一个样本是基线，第二个样本是在进行更改后获取的； 运行测试以查看更改是否产生影响。

rows: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
zh.text: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
Minitab 中提供 1 样本 T 检验
o 需要标准差、平均值、样本量以及目标或假设平均值。
Minitab 和 Excel 的 Analysis TakePak 中提供配对 T 检验
假设检验设置为：
哈 : 𝜇 1 ≠ 𝜇 2
请注意，在上述设置中，备择假设默认设置为“不等于”。如果需要，Minitab 允许您将备择假设调整为大于或小于场景。
1 样本 T 检验示例
一家种植玉米的农业公司五年来平均每英亩生产 168 蒲式耳玉米。该公司改变了播种流程，试图提高田地的产量。换种后的收获结果是，每年每英亩平均收获 175 蒲式耳玉米。播种量的变化是否导致每英亩产量出现统计上不同的结果？
H0：种子变更后的结果与变更前的结果没有差异。
o 𝜇旧= 𝜇新
Ha：播种方式的改变导致每块田地的平均产量增加。
o 𝜇旧< 𝜇新
卡方检验（或 1-方差检验）
在比较标准差或方差时使用
比较相同 x 因子的两个样本之间的标准差或方差（通常在对过程进行更改后采集一个样本），或者将标准差或方差与目标或假设统计量进行比较。
在 Minitab 中可用
o 需要两列中的样本数据或汇总数据，以包括样本量、样本标准差或样本方差以及假设的标准差或方差。
假设检验设置为：
哈 : 𝜗 1 ≠ 𝜗 2
请注意，在上述设置中，备择假设默认设置为“不等于”。如果需要，Minitab 允许您将备择假设调整为大于或小于场景。
卡方检验（或 1-方差检验）示例
一家制造厂生产装在手推车上的轮子。 该工厂向合作工厂提供三种尺寸的轮子，该工厂自行制造小车并使用轮子来完成整个过程。合作工厂提出投诉：工厂生产的最小车轮的尺寸差异很大，会导致最终产品出现稳定性问题，从而导致台车出现缺陷或浪费车轮零件。
该车轮制造厂启动了六西格玛改进项目，以减少最小车轮的尺寸变化。该团队测量了基线，发现车轮尺寸的标准偏差为 0.0409。团队对流程进行了更改，并再次进行测量，以将新标准偏差与旧标准偏差进行比较。
H0：标准差没有差异；这些变化并没有从统计上减少过程中的变异。
Ha：这些改进减少了过程中的标准偏差。
2样本T检验
比较平均值时使用
比较不同 x 因子的两个样本之间的平均值
可在 Minitab 和 Excel 中使用
o 需要两列样本数据或汇总数据，包括两个样本的样本量、样本均值和标准差
要求您注意是否假设两个样本之间的方差相等
2 个样本 T 检验可能看起来像 1 个样本 T 检验，因为在 1 个样本 T 检验中，我们讨论了如何将更改前的样本与更改后的样本进行比较。但是，2 样本 T 检验略有不同，因为您要比较来自不同群体的两个样本。例如，您可以将未接种流感疫苗的儿童的平均体温与接种疫苗的儿童的平均体温进行比较。这是不同的
比较去年未接种疫苗时与今年接种疫苗时同一组儿童的平均体温。第一个示例将使用 2 样本 T 检验；第二个（同比）示例将使用单样本 T 检验。
假设检验设置为：
哈： 𝜇1 ≠ 𝜇2
请注意，在上述设置中，备择假设默认设置为“不等于”。如果需要，Minitab 允许您将备择假设调整为大于或小于场景。
2 样本 T 检验示例
尽管合伙人指出，在同一家律师事务所执业的两名律师的计费时间相差很大，但两位律师的案件量相似。一位合伙人推测 A 律师的法律人员处理某些任务的时间太长；因为公司会在特定时间对行政类任务进行计费，无论这些任务需要多长时间，如果法律人员在此类任务上花费的时间太长，他们就会将时间花在无法计费的工作上。
为了检验该理论，收集了有关法律人员执行任务的时间的数据。具体来说，团队会查看三项任务所花费的时间，法律人员可以为每项任务计费 15​​ 分钟：某些类型的简单管理电话、复印或归档任务。
H0：A律师的工作人员执行此类任务的平均时间与B律师的工作人员执行此类任务的平均时间没有差异。
o H0 : 𝜇律师A= 𝜇律师B
Ha：平均而言，B 律师的员工花在这些任务上的时间更少。
o Ha : 𝜇 律师 A > 𝜇 律师 B
连续非正态数据的假设检验
卡方检验
上一节中描述的卡方检验也可用于在数据不正常时比较样本之间的标准偏差和假设的标准偏差。
一份威尔科克斯样本
比较中位数时使用
比较更改前样本与假设样本或新样本与先前样本之间的中位数
在 Minitab 中可用
o 需要一列中的样本数据和假设平均值
通常在数据有些对称时使用；否则，您可以使用 Minitab 中的 1-Sample Sign 检验来实现同样的效果
假设检验设置为：
H0 : 𝑀1 = 𝑀𝑡
哈 : 𝑀1 ≠ 𝑀𝑡
请注意，在上述设置中，备择假设默认设置为“不等于”。如果需要，Minitab 允许您将备择假设调整为大于或小于场景。
单样本 Wilcox 示例
一家房地产公司想要进入高价市场，因此他们想要建立一些区域性网站，以满足寻找更大房屋的购房者的需求。具体来说，他们希望迎合购买 4 间或更多卧室房屋的购房者。在公司建立网站并开始营销之前，需要找到待售房屋通常拥有 4 间或更多卧室的社区。
房地产公司收集有关特定区域房屋中卧室数量的信息。
H0：卧室数量中位数等于 3。
H0：𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠= 3
Ha：卧室数量中位数大于 3（4 间或更多）。
o 哈 : 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠 > 3
曼惠特尼测试
比较中位数时使用
比较 x 的两个因子的样本之间的中位数
在 Minitab 中可用
o 需要列中的样本数据
假设检验设置为：
哈 : 𝑀1 ≠ 𝑀2
请注意，在上述设置中，备择假设默认设置为“不等于”。如果需要，Minitab 允许您将备择假设调整为大于或小于场景。
曼-惠特尼检验的示例
再考虑一下上面的房地产公司的例子。现在，想象一下房地产公司的资源有限，因此他们一次只能针对一个社区发起一项活动。为了最大限度地提高首次活动的潜在效​​果，该公司希望选择住宅数量最多、卧室数量最多的社区。
该公司收集两个社区的房屋信息。然后，他们对样本进行比较，以确定某个样本的卧室中位数是否在统计上更高。
H0：社区 A 的卧室中位数等于社区 B 的卧室中位数。
o H0 : 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠𝐴= 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠𝐵
Ha：A 区的卧室数量中位数高于 B 区。
o 哈 : 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠𝐴 > 𝑀𝑏𝑒𝑑𝑟𝑜𝑜𝑚𝑠𝐵

en.text: At this point, a common question about hypothesis tests comes up: Why go through the trouble to run statistical tests to determine if things are different?
Consider the example problem presented for the 1-Sample T Test:

An agriculture company growing corn produced an average of 168 bushels of corn per acre each year for five years. The company made a change to its seeding process in an attempt to increase the yield of its fields. The harvest following the seed change resulted in an average of 175 bushels of corn per acre each year. Did the seeding change result in a statistically different outcome in yield per acre?

The question is: Is the mean corn yield per acre statistically higher following the seed change? Just looking at the numbers presented in this problem, we can see that the mean following the seed change is 175. When you compare that to 168, it is obviously higher. Why would we go through the trouble of setting up and running a hypothesis test?

When introducing the normal curve in chapter 18, we noted that it’s not always enough to see that a histogram appears to be symmetric and following the normal curve. Raw data can be deceptive, which is why we ran the Chi-Squared Goodness-of-Fit test in Excel to ensure the data was normal. The same principle is true when comparing statistics from samples. Yes, 175 is higher than 168. But in the case of the corn yield – is it statistically different?

Six Sigma teams can’t just answer the question “Is this number different?” They must answer the question “Is this number so statistically different that we can take action on this information?” Statistically different doesn’t have to do with scale. In one process, the difference between 10 and 23 might not be statistically different. In another, the difference between 10 and 10.5 might be statistically different.

zh.text: 此时，出现了一个关于假设检验的常见问题： 为什么要费力地进行统计测试来确定情况是否不同呢？
考虑 1 样本 T 检验提出的示例问题：
一家种植玉米的农业公司五年来平均每英亩生产 168 蒲式耳玉米。该公司改变了播种流程，试图提高田地的产量。换种后的收获结果是，每年每英亩平均收获 175 蒲式耳玉米。播种量的变化是否导致每英亩产量出现统计上不同的结果？
问题是：在更换种子后，每英亩玉米的平均产量在统计上是否更高？只要看看这个问题中呈现的数字，我们就可以看到种子更改后的平均值是 175。当你将其与 168 进行比较时，它显然更高。为什么我们要经历设置和运行假设检验的麻烦？
在第 18 章介绍正态曲线时，我们注意到，仅仅看到直方图看起来对称并遵循正态曲线并不总是足够的。原始数据可能具有欺骗性，这就是我们在 Excel 中运行卡方拟合优度检验以确保数据正常的原因。比较样本统计数据时，同样的原理也是如此。是的，175 高于 168。但就玉米产量而言，统计上有差异吗？

rows: 术语说明 Histogram（直方图）：展示数据分布形态和频数的柱状图。
zh.text: 术语说明 Histogram（直方图）：展示数据分布形态和频数的柱状图。
六西格玛团队不能只回答“这个数字不同吗？”的问题。他们必须回答这样的问题：“这个数字在统计上是否差异如此之大，以至于我们可以根据这些信息采取行动？”统计上的差异与规模无关。在一个过程中，10 和 23 之间的差异可能没有统计学差异。在另一个例子中，10 和 10.5 之间的差异可能在统计上有所不同。

en.text: To run a hypothesis test, you typically follow the same series of steps:

1. State the null and alternative hypotheses. The null hypothesis is always written from the perspective that no change or difference occurs or is present. The alternative hypothesis is always written from the perspective that a change or difference is present – either not equal, greater than, or less than. 2. Set the confidence level for alpha. Usually, the confidence level is set at 95 percent for alpha, but other common settings are 99 and 99.9 percent. A confidence level of 95 percent means an alpha value of 0.05; a confidence interval level of 99 percent means an alpha value of 0.01; a confidence interval of 99.9 percent means an alpha value of 0.001. 3. Decide which hypothesis test you are going to use. Use the information in the above section to find the appropriate test based on:

a. The type of data you have b. The statistic you are dealing with (mean, variation, etc.) c. How many sets of data you have (level of interest in x) d. Remember, testing more than two sets of data is covered in Unit 7 on Design of Experiments and ANOVA 4. Decide whether your sample size is fixed or whether you can select a sample sized based on your beta setting. Setting sample size is covered in Chapter 22. 5. Run the test in Minitab. Specific steps for each hypothesis test – as well as other Minitab functions – are covered in Unit 6. 6. Interpret the p-value against your alpha setting, which tells you whether or not to reject the null hypothesis. 7. Translate the statistical analysis into real-world, business-relevant language.

For each statistical test, the calculations – handled automatically for us in Minitab or other software programs – return several values. Some calculations, such as the Chi-Squared tests, return specific statistics. In the case of the Chi-Squared tests, that statistic is the Chi-Squared value. These statistics can be valuable in applications and analyses, but for the purpose of hypothesis testing, we are primarily concerned with the p-value. The p-value is returned for each test, and we compare the p-value to the alpha value we set before we ran the test.

If the p-value returned for a test is less than the alpha value you set, then you reject the null hypothesis and accept the alternative hypothesis. If the p-value returned is more than the alpha value you set, then you fail to reject the null hypothesis and you do reject the alternative hypothesis.

Above, we talked about setting a confidence interval for alpha. If the confidence interval is 95 percent, then the alpha value is 0.05. Given a p-value of 0.02, we would reject the null hypothesis and accept the alternative. Given a p-value of 0.13, we would fail to reject the null hypothesis and would reject the alternative.
Hypothesis Test Examples
We’ll go through the steps for setting up and evaluating hypothesis tests using some of the example tests from the section above.
1-Proportion Test

Problem: A school administrator believes that the students in a certain classroom are being impacted by some environmental issue with that room. The administrator wants to see if the students in that room perform statistically lower on tests than the students throughout the school; she has already noted that different classes in that room are taught by other teachers.

The administrator gathers data. The rate of failure for classes taught in any other classroom is 20 percent. Out of 142 students who have a class in the classroom in question, 38 are failing that particular class.
Step 1: State Null and Alternative Hypothesis
H0: There is no difference between the failure rate of students in the classroom and those in other classrooms.
Ha: The failure rate of students in the classroom is statistically higher than the failure rate of students in other classrooms.
Step 2: Set Confidence Level
Unless otherwise stated in this book, the confidence level will always be 95 percent, or alpha = 0.05.
Step 3: Select Appropriate Test

Our data takes the form of pass/fail, which is attribute or discrete data. We have one set of data that we are comparing to the hypothesized rate of failure of 20 percent. Given that information, the 1- Proportion Test is the appropriate test.
Step 4: Select Sample Size

In this case, we aren’t worried about sample size because we have been provided with the data. Since Six Sigma teams usually gather their own data, they need to worry about the proper sample size. How to determine sample size is covered in the next chapter.
Step 5: Run the Test and Analyze P-Value
The test is run in Minitab with the following information:

- Number of trials: 142 (there are 142 students) - Number of events: 38 (there are 38 students meeting the event parameter “fail”) - Hypothesized proportion: 0.20 (20 percent of students in other classrooms meet the event parameter “fail”) - Alternative hypothesis: proportion > hypothesized proportion (we are testing whether the fail rate of students within the classroom is higher than the 20 percent fail rate of those outside the classroom)
Given all of the information above, Minitab returns a p-value of 0.031.
Test and CI for One Proportion
Test of p = 0.2 vs p > 0.2
Exact
Sample X N Sample p 95% Lower Bound P-Value 1 38 142 0.267606 0.207083 0.031
Since the p-value is less than the alpha value of 0.05, we reject the null hypothesis and accept the alternative. Yes, the rate of failure is statistically higher for students in the classroom.
1-Sample T Test

Problem: An agriculture company growing corn produced an average of 168 bushels of corn per acre each year for five years. The company made a change to its seeding process in an attempt to increase the yield of its fields. The harvest following the seed change resulted in an average of 175 bushels of corn per acre each year over 500 acres of fields. The standard deviation is 2.2 bushels. Did the seeding change result in a statistically different outcome in yield per acre?
Step 1: State Null and Alternative Hypothesis
H0: There is no difference in the mean yield after the seed change as compared to before the seed change.
Ha: Following the seed change, the acres produce a statistically higher mean yield of corn.
Step 2: Set Confidence Level
Again, we set the confidence level for alpha at 95 percent, so alpha is 0.05.
Step 3: Select Appropriate Test

We are testing the mean of a sample against a hypothesized (or historic, or population) mean. Assume for the purpose of this example that our data is normal. The correct test would be the 1-Sample T Test or the Z Test if we didn’t have the standard deviation.
Step 4: Select Sample Size

In this case, we aren’t worried about sample size because we have been provided with the data. Since Six Sigma teams usually gather their own data, they need to worry about the proper sample size. How to determine sample size is covered in the next chapter.
Step 5: Run the Test and Analyze P-Value
The test is run in Minitab with the following information:

- Sample size: 500 (there were 500 acres of corn fields) - Sample mean: 175 (following the seed change, the 500 acres yielded an average of 175 bushels) - Standard deviation: 2.2 (provided in our problem) - Hypothesized mean: 168 (prior to the seed change, the average yield was 168 bushels) - Alternative hypothesis: mean > hypothesized mean (we are testing whether the average yield after the seed change is statistically higher than the yield prior to the seed change).
Given all of the information above, Minitab returns a p-value of 0.000.
One-Sample T Test of μ = 168 vs > 168 N Mean StDev SE Mean 95% Lower Bound T P 500 175.000 2.200 0.098 174.838 71.15 0.000

Since the p-value is less than the alpha value of 0.05, we reject the null hypothesis and accept the alternative. Yes, the mean yield is statistically higher following the seed change. It should be noted that the p-value in this case is exceptionally low. For our purposes, it is zero. That means there is only a very tiny chance of a Type 1 hypothesis testing error in this case. Why is this important? When making a decision that involves resources of money, leadership is always happy to hear that analysts are especially confident in their conclusions.
2-Sample T Test

Problem: Two attorneys practicing in the same law firm are turning in very different amounts of billable hours, even though the partners note that the two lawyers have similar caseloads. One partner hypothesizes that Attorney A’s legal staff is taking too long to handle some tasks; because the firm bills at a certain time for administrative-style tasks regardless of how long those tasks take, if legal staff is taking too long for such tasks, they are spending time on work that can’t be billed for.

To test the theory, data is gathered about the time legal staff spends on tasks. Specifically, a team looks at time spent on three tasks for which the legal staff is allowed to bill 15 minutes each: certain types of simple administrative phone, copying, or filing tasks. The following data is collected.
Minutes Spent on Quarter-Hour Tasks Attorney A's Team Attorney B's Team 10 8 8 7 15 10 16 12 17 18 5 6 8 9 16 12 12 11 11 11 8 9 9 8 15 14 17 15
Step 1: State Null and Alternative Hypothesis
H0: There is no difference in the average time it takes Attorney A’s team to handle quarter-hour tasks as compared to the time it takes Attorney B’s team.
Ha: Attorney A’s team takes on average longer to perform quarter-hour tasks than Attorney B’s team.
Step 2: Set Confidence Level
Again, we set the confidence level for alpha at 95 percent, so alpha is 0.05.
Step 3: Select Appropriate Test

We are testing the mean of one sample against the mean of another. For this problem, we will assume the data was tested to be normal. The data is time, so it is continuous. The appropriate test is the 2- Sample T test.
Step 4: Select Sample Size

In this case, we aren’t worried about sample size because we have been provided with the data. Since Six Sigma teams usually gather their own data, they need to worry about the proper sample size. How to determine sample size is covered in the next chapter.
Step 5: Run the Test and Analyze P-Value
The test is run in Minitab by indicating the two columns with the same data in it and setting the alternative hypothesis to greater than.
Given all of the information above, Minitab return a p-value of 0.255

Two-Sample T-Test and CI: A, B Two-sample T for A vs B N Mean StDev SE Mean A 34 13.76 6.56 1.1 B 34 12.82 5.07 0.87 Difference = μ (A) - μ (B) Estimate for difference: 0.94 95% lower bound for difference: -1.43 T-Test of difference = 0 (vs >): T-Value = 0.66 P-Value = 0.255 DF = 62

Since the p-value is greater than the alpha value of 0.05, we fail to reject the null hypothesis and we do reject the alternative. The average time spent on the quarter-hour tasks is not statistically different for each team. Minitab also calculates the means for us: Attorney A’s team spends an average of 13.76 minutes on each task while Attorney B’s team spends an average of 12.82 minutes on each task. Both teams, in fact, spend less time on the tasks than they can bill for the tasks, which means neither team is technically wasting any time.
One Sample Wilcox

Problem: A real estate company wants to enter a high-dollar market, so they want to build some regional websites that cater to homebuyers looking for larger homes. Specifically, they want to cater to homebuyers who are purchasing homes with 4 or more bedrooms. Before the company can build websites and begin marketing, it needs to find neighborhoods where homes for sale typically have 4 or more bedrooms.
The real estate company gathers information about the number of bedrooms in homes in a neighborhood. That data is presented below.
Number of Bedrooms
Step 1: State Null and Alternative Hypothesis
H0: The median number of bedrooms is equal to 3.
Ha: The median number of bedrooms is greater than 3.
Step 2: Set Confidence Level
Again, we set the confidence level for alpha at 95 percent, so alpha is 0.05.
Step 3: Select Appropriate Test
We are testing the median of a sample against a hypothesized median. We only have one factor for X, so we will use the One Sample Wilcox.
Step 4: Select Sample Size

In this case, we aren’t worried about sample size because we have been provided with the data. Since Six Sigma teams usually gather their own data, they need to worry about the proper sample size. How to determine sample size is covered in the next chapter.
Step 5: Run the Test and Analyze P-Value
The test is run in Minitab by indicating the column where the sample data resides, notating a hypothesized median of 3, and setting the alternative hypothesis to greater than 3.
Given all of the information above, Minitab returns a p-value of 0.000.
Wilcoxon Signed Rank Test: Bedrooms Test of median = 3.000 versus median > 3.000 N for Wilcoxon Estimated N Test Statistic P Median Bedrooms 52 40 726.0 0.000 4.000
Since the p-value is less than the alpha value of 0.05, we reject the null hypothesis and accept the alternative. Yes, the median number of bedrooms for homes in the neighborhood is 4 or more.
Hypothesis Testing in Analyze, Improve, and Control

For Six Sigma teams, hypothesis testing is an activity typically found in the latter three phases of a DMAIC project, and most specifically in Analyze or Improve. During Analyze, teams might use hypothesis testing to help determine if certain inputs or factors could be a root cause. Given historical data, teams who can separate factors within a process can use that data to run a hypothesis test. For example, a team might believe that one employee is responsible for defects in a process. By comparing defect rates
when that employee is on shift to defect rates when the employee is not working, teams can draw conclusions from data.

During Improve, hypothesis tests are often used to validate solutions that are being tested in beta environments or in small batches. The team compares the results of that solution with previous data about the process to determine whether the solution has created a statistically different result – and whether that result is what the team wanted.

Because hypothesis tests can begin to sound very mathematical, it’s important to start with a business- friendly statement and end with a business-relevant statement regarding the conclusions. Chapter 24 covers some tips for converting various Six Sigma and statistical conclusions to business-friendly presentations and knowledge, which is especially helpful when a Six Sigma team is proposing changes based on statistical analysis. The ability to convert that analysis into business-friendly language helps teams explain to decision-makers why a change is likely to help.
A Review of Hypothesis Testing Terms

This chapter introduced a lot of new statistical terms, many of which will come up again in future chapters. Before moving to the next chapter, take a few minutes to review the terms that came up in this chapter.

zh.text: 要运行假设检验，您通常遵循相同的一系列步骤：

1. 陈述原假设和备择假设。原假设始终是从没有发生或不存在任何变化或差异的角度编写的。备择假设总是从存在变化或差异的角度出发——要么不等于、大于或小于。 2. 设置 alpha 的置信水平。通常，alpha 的置信水平设置为 95%，但其他常见设置为 99% 和 99.9%。 95% 的置信水平意味着 alpha 值为 0.05； 99% 的置信区间水平意味着 alpha 值为 0.01； 99.9% 的置信区间意味着 alpha 值为 0.001。 3. 决定您要使用哪种假设检验。使用上一节中的信息根据以下内容找到适当的测试：

a.您拥有的数据类型 B.您正在处理的统计数据（均值、方差等） c．您有多少组数据（对 x 的兴趣程度） d．请记住，关于实验设计和方差分析（ANOVA）4 的第 7 单元涵盖了测试两组以上的数据。确定样本大小是固定的还是可以根据 beta 设置选择样本大小。第 22 章介绍了如何设置样本大小。 5. 在 Minitab 中运行测试。第 6 单元介绍了每个假设检验以及其他 Minitab 函数的具体步骤。 6. 根据 alpha 设置解释 p 值，该设置告诉您是否拒绝原假设。 7. 将统计分析翻译成现实世界的业务相关语言。

rows: 术语说明 p-value（p 值）：在原假设成立时观察到当前或更极端结果的概率；常用于判断显著性。
zh.text: 术语说明 p-value（p 值）：在原假设成立时观察到当前或更极端结果的概率；常用于判断显著性。

rows: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。
zh.text: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。

rows: 术语说明 DOE（实验设计）：系统安排实验以识别因素、水平和交互作用对结果的影响。
zh.text: 术语说明 DOE（实验设计）：系统安排实验以识别因素、水平和交互作用对结果的影响。
对于每个统计检验，计算（在 Minitab 或其他软件程序中自动为我们处理）返回多个值。某些计算（例如卡方检验）会返回特定的统计数据。在卡方检验的情况下，该统计量就是卡方值。这些统计数据在应用和分析中可能很有价值，但出于假设检验的目的，我们主要关注 p 值。每次测试都会返回 p 值，我们将 p 值与运行测试之前设置的 alpha 值进行比较。
如果测试返回的 p 值小于您设置的 alpha 值，则您拒绝原假设并接受备择假设。如果返回的 p 值大于您设置的 alpha 值，则您无法拒绝原假设，但会拒绝备择假设。
上面，我们讨论了设置 alpha 的置信区间。如果置信区间为 95%，则 alpha 值为 0.05。给定 p 值为 0.02，我们将拒绝原假设并接受替代假设。给定 p 值为 0.13，我们将无法拒绝原假设，并且会拒绝替代假设。
假设检验例子
我们将使用上一节中的一些示例检验来完成设置和评估假设检验的步骤。
1-比例测试
问题：学校管理员认为某个教室的学生受到该房间的某些环境问题的影响。管理员想了解该房间的学生在测试中的统计成绩是否低于全校学生；她已经注意到那个房间里的不同课程是由其他老师教的。
管理员收集数据。在任何其他教室教授的课程的失败率为 20%。在该教室上课的 142 名学生中，有 38 名学生未能通过该课程。
步骤 1：陈述原假设和备择假设
H0：本教室学生的不及格率与其他教室学生的不及格率没有差异。
哈：从统计上看，该教室学生的不及格率比其他教室学生的不及格率要高。
第 2 步：设置置信度
除非本书另有说明，否则置信水平始终为 95%，即 alpha = 0.05。
第 3 步：选择适当的测试
我们的数据采用通过/失败的形式，即属性或离散数据。我们将一组数据与 20% 的假设失败率进行比较。鉴于该信息，1-比例测试是合适的测试。
第 4 步：选择样本量
在这种情况下，我们并不担心样本大小，因为我们已经获得了数据。由于六西格玛团队通常收集自己的数据，因此他们需要担心样本量是否合适。下一章将介绍如何确定样本量。
第 5 步：运行测试并分析 P 值
该测试在 Minitab 中运行，并包含以下信息：
- 试验次数：142（有 142 名学生） - 事件数量：38（有 38 名学生满足事件参数“不及格”） - 假设比例：0.20（其他教室 20% 的学生满足事件参数“不及格”） - 备择假设： 比例>假设比例（我们正在测试课堂内学生的不及格率是否高于课堂外学生20%的不及格率）
鉴于上述所有信息，Minitab 返回 p 值为 0.031。
某一比例的测试和 CI
准确
由于 p 值小于 alpha 值 0.05，因此我们拒绝原假设并接受替代假设。是的，从统计数据来看，课堂上学生的失败率更高。
1-样本 T 检验
问题：一家种植玉米的农业公司五年来平均每年每英亩生产 168 蒲式耳玉米。该公司改变了播种流程，试图提高田地的产量。换种后的收获结果是，500 英亩的土地上每年平均每英亩收获 175 蒲式耳玉米。标准偏差为 2.2 蒲式耳。播种量的变化是否导致每英亩产量出现统计上不同的结果？
步骤 1：陈述原假设和备择假设
H0：与换种前相比，换种后的平均产量没有差异。
Ha：更换种子后，从统计数据来看，这些英亩土地的玉米平均产量更高。
第 2 步：设置置信度
我们再次将 alpha 的置信水平设置为 95%，因此 alpha 为 0.05。
第 3 步：选择适当的测试
我们正在根据假设（或历史或总体）平均值来测试样本的平均值。出于本示例的目的，假设我们的数据是正常的。如果我们没有标准差，正确的检验将是 1 样本 T 检验或 Z 检验。
第 4 步：选择样本量
在这种情况下，我们并不担心样本大小，因为我们已经获得了数据。由于六西格玛团队通常收集自己的数据，因此他们需要担心样本量是否合适。下一章将介绍如何确定样本量。
第 5 步：运行测试并分析 P 值
该测试在 Minitab 中运行，并包含以下信息：
- 样本数量：500（有 500 英亩玉米田） - 样本均值： 175（更换种子后，500 英亩土地的平均产量为 175 蒲式耳） - 标准差：2.2（在我们的问题中提供） - 假设平均值：168（更换种子之前，平均产量为 168 蒲式耳） - 替代假设：平均值 > 假设平均值（我们正在测试更换种子后的平均产量是否在统计上高于更换种子之前的产量）。
鉴于上述所有信息，Minitab 返回 p 值为 0.000。
由于 p 值小于 alpha 值 0.05，因此我们拒绝原假设并接受替代假设。是的，从统计上看，更换种子后平均产量更高。应该指出的是，这种情况下的 p 值非常低。就我们的目的而言，它为零。这意味着在这种情况下，出现 1 类假设检验错误的可能性非常小。为什么这很重要？当做出涉及资金资源的决策时，领导层总是很高兴听到分析师对他们的结论特别有信心。
2样本T检验
问题：在同一家律师事务所执业的两名律师的计费时间差异很大，尽管合伙人指出这两名律师的案件量相似。一位合伙人推测 A 律师的法律人员处理某些任务的时间太长；因为公司会在特定时间对行政类任务进行计费，无论这些任务需要多长时间，如果法律人员在此类任务上花费的时间太长，他们就会将时间花在无法计费的工作上。
为了检验该理论，收集了有关法律人员执行任务的时间的数据。具体来说，团队会查看三项任务所花费的时间，法律人员可以为每项任务计费 15​​ 分钟：某些类型的简单管理电话、复印或归档任务。收集以下数据。
步骤 1：陈述原假设和备择假设
H0： 律师 A 的团队处理一刻钟任务所需的平均时间与律师 B 的团队所花费的时间没有差异。
Ha：A 律师的团队执行一刻钟任务的平均时间比 B 律师的团队要长。
第 2 步：设置置信度
我们再次将 alpha 的置信水平设置为 95%，因此 alpha 为 0.05。
第 3 步：选择适当的测试
我们正在将一个样本的平均值与另一个样本的平均值进行比较。对于这个问题，我们假设测试数据是正常的。数据是时间，因此是连续的。适当的检验是 2 样本 T 检验。
第 4 步：选择样本量
在这种情况下，我们并不担心样本大小，因为我们已经获得了数据。由于六西格玛团队通常收集自己的数据，因此他们需要担心样本量是否合适。下一章将介绍如何确定样本量。
第 5 步：运行测试并分析 P 值
通过指示其中包含相同数据的两列并将备择假设设置为大于，在 Minitab 中运行检验。
鉴于上述所有信息，Minitab 返回 p 值为 0.255
两个样本 T 检验和 CI：A、B A 与 B 的两个样本 T N 平均值 StDev SE 平均值 A 34 13.76 6.56 1.1 B 34 12.82 5.07 0.87 差异 = μ (A) - μ (B) 差异估计：0.94 95% 差异下限：-1.43 差异 T 检验 = 0（vs >）： T 值 = 0.66 P 值 = 0.255 DF = 62

由于 p 值大于 alpha 值 0.05，因此我们无法拒绝原假设，并且拒绝替代方案。每个团队在每刻钟任务上花费的平均时间在统计上没有差异。 Minitab 还为我们计算均值：律师 A 的团队在每项任务上平均花费 13.76 分钟，而律师 B 的团队在每项任务上平均花费 12.82 分钟。事实上，两个团队在任务上花费的时间都少于他们可以为任务计费的时间，这意味着两个团队在技术上都没有浪费任何时间。
一份威尔科克斯样本
问题：一家房地产公司想要进入高价市场，因此他们想要建立一些区域性网站，以满足寻找更大房屋的购房者的需求。具体来说，他们希望迎合购买 4 间或更多卧室房屋的购房者。 在公司建立网站并开始营销之前，需要找到待售房屋通常拥有 4 间或更多卧室的社区。
房地产公司收集有关附近房屋的卧室数量的信息。该数据如下所示。
卧室数量
步骤 1：陈述原假设和备择假设
H0：卧室数量中位数等于 3。
Ha：卧室数量中位数大于 3 间。
第 2 步：设置置信度
我们再次将 alpha 的置信水平设置为 95%，因此 alpha 为 0.05。
第 3 步：选择适当的测试
我们正在根据假设的中位数来测试样本的中位数。我们只有一个 X 因子，因此我们将使用一个样本 Wilcox。
第 4 步：选择样本量
在这种情况下，我们并不担心样本大小，因为我们已经获得了数据。由于六西格玛团队通常收集自己的数据，因此他们需要担心样本量是否合适。下一章将介绍如何确定样本量。
第 5 步：运行测试并分析 P 值
该检验在 Minitab 中运行，方法是指示样本数据所在的列，标注假设中位数 3，并将备择假设设置为大于 3。
鉴于上述所有信息，Minitab 返回 p 值为 0.000。
Wilcoxon 符号秩检验：卧室检验 中位数 = 3.000 与中位数 > 3.000 N 进行 Wilcoxon 估计 N 检验统计 P 中位数卧室 52 40 726.0 0.000 4.000
由于 p 值小于 alpha 值 0.05，因此我们拒绝原假设并接受替代假设。是的，附近住宅的卧室数量中位数为 4 间或更多。
分析、改进和控制中的假设检验
对于六西格玛团队来说，假设检验通常是在 DMAIC 项目的后三个阶段中进行的活动，尤其是在分析或改进中。在分析过程中，团队可能会使用假设检验来帮助确定某些输入或因素是否可能是根本原因。 给定历史数据，能够在流程中分离因素的团队可以使用该数据来运行假设检验。例如，团队可能认为一名员工应对流程中的缺陷负责。通过比较缺陷率

zh.text: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。

rows: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
zh.text: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
当该员工轮班时，当该员工不工作时，团队可以从数据中得出结论。
在改进过程中，假设检验通常用于验证在 beta 环境或小批量中测试的解决方案。团队将该解决方案的结果与之前有关流程的数据进行比较，以确定该解决方案是否创建了统计上不同的结果，以及该结果是否是团队想要的。
由于假设检验一开始听起来非常数学化，因此以业务友好的陈述开始并以与结论相关的业务相关陈述结束非常重要。第24章介绍了将各种六西格玛和统计结论转化为商业友好的演示和知识的一些技巧，这在六西格玛团队根据统计分析提出变更建议时特别有用。将分析转换为业务友好语言的能力有助于团队向决策者解释为什么变革可能会有所帮助。
假设检验术语回顾
本章介绍了许多新的统计术语，其中许多术语将在以后的章节中再次出现。在进入下一章之前，请花几分钟回顾一下本章中出现的术语。

en.text: Accuracy in inferential statistics requires that you have the right sample size. A retail company with thousands of customers would never make a major product decision based on feedback from five people, and Six Sigma teams should never make decisions based on statistical analysis when the sample size is not big enough to create an acceptable margin of error.
To understand the importance of sample size, we’ll first review some important notes about sampling as related to inferential statistics.

Six Sigma teams take samples because they want to determine information about a population. With some exceptions, it is very difficult, very expensive, or impossible to run statistical calculations on the entire population of data. However, when we sample the population and draw inferences from those samples about means, proportions, or variances, there is always some risk of error. Some level of uncertainty will always exist when we draw conclusions about a population from sample data. In many cases, a larger sample size helps reduce the uncertainty of our conclusions.

The uncertainty associated with sampling is defined by something called the Confidence Interval, which is also called the margin of error in some applications. You’ve likely read or seen survey or poll results reported with a margin of error: The fans are 93 percent in favor of the new team colors, +/- 2 percent. The margin of error in that particularly survey was 2 percent, or 0.02.

In chapter 21, we discussed hypothesis testing and we set our alpha value at 0.05, which means we were at least 95 percent confident in choosing to reject or not reject the null hypothesis. We can make our hypothesis tests even more accurate by defining power and sample size for our data – particularly when we are pulling the data ourselves and can choose how many samples to take.

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
推论统计的准确性要求您拥有正确的样本量。一家拥有数千名客户的零售公司永远不会根据五个人的反馈做出重大产品决策，而六西格玛团队也不应该在样本量不足以产生可接受的误差范围时根据统计分析做出决策。
为了了解样本量的重要性，我们首先回顾一下与推论统计相关的抽样的一些重要注意事项。
六西格玛团队采集样本是因为他们想要确定有关种群的信息。除了一些例外，对整个数据群进行统计计算是非常困难、非常昂贵或不可能的。然而，当我们对总体进行抽样并从这些样本中得出关于均值、比例或方差的推论时，总是存在一些错误的风险。当我们从样本数据中得出关于总体的结论时，总会存在一定程度的不确定性。在许多情况下，更大的样本量有助于减少我们结论的不确定性。

rows: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Population（总体）：研究对象的全部集合；实际项目中常通过样本推断总体。
与采样相关的不确定性由置信区间定义，在某些应用中也称为误差幅度。您可能读过或看到过有误差范围的调查或民意调查结果：93% 的球迷支持新球队颜色，+/- 2%。该调查的误差幅度为 2%，即 0.02。
在第 21 章中，我们讨论了假设检验，并将 alpha 值设置为 0.05，这意味着我们至少有 95% 的信心选择拒绝或不拒绝原假设。我们可以通过定义数据的功效和样本量来使我们的假设检验更加准确——特别是当我们自己提取数据并可以选择要采集的样本数量时。

rows: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
zh.text: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
假设检验错误的回顾

title.en: A Review of Hypothesis Testing Errors

en.text: The concept of hypothesis testing errors is key to selecting sample sizes for various hypothesis testing. The two types of errors were introduced in Chapter 21, but warrant a quick review before moving on with sample size calculations.
Type I Error

A Type I error occurs when you reject the null hypothesis during a hypothesis test when, in fact, the null hypothesis is true. You might reject the null hypothesis that the mean of the sample is statistically the same as the hypothesized mean, deciding instead that the sample mean is statistically greater than the hypothesized mean. If the sample mean is, in fact, statistically the same as the hypothesized mean, you have a Type I error.
Type I error risks are denoted by alpha.

As stated in chapter 21, the Type I error is also called the producer risk. In a manufacturing environment, if the null hypothesis is true but it is rejected due to a Type I error, then material that is within specification (material that is statistically the same as the target) will be rejected. This is a producer risk – it causes waste, extra cost, and lower employee morale.
Type II Error

A Type II error occurs when you accept the null hypothesis when it is, in fact, not true. If you accept the hypothesis that the sample mean is statistically the same as the hypothesized mean when, in fact, the sample mean is statistically greater than the hypothesized mean, then you have a Type II error.
Type II error risks are denoted by beta.

As stated in chapter 21, the Type II error is also called the consumer risk. In a manufacturing environment, if the null hypothesis is false but it is accepted anyway due to a Type II error, then material that is not within specification (material that is not statistically the same as the target) is accepted. That means product that doesn’t meet target is passed onto the consumer. That is a consumer risk – it could cause returns, unsatisfied customers, poor brand reputation.
The appropriate sample size can reduce the risk of either type of error.
What Information is Required for Choosing Sample Size?
To perform sample size calculations – or have Minitab perform them for you – certain information and assumptions are required.
Standard
deviation
Type of Data
Discrete or continuous?
Type of Test
Which hypothesis test are you using (see Chapter 21 for selecting the right test)

Before you can move onto calculating sample size, some practical considerations must be made with regard to alpha, beta, and delta. In our review of Type I and Type II errors, we covered the difference between producer and consumer risks, but that breakdown doesn’t work in all organizations or with all hypothesis tests.

Because the outcome of your hypothesis tests are going to be used to make real-world decisions, you have to truly understand what the test and its outcomes means for the business, employee, and consumer. That real-world understanding helps you set appropriate Alpha and Beta values.
Questions to Ask About Alpha, Beta, and Delta Values

In most cases, you can set the alpha value at 0.05. However, in specific experiments, accuracy is extremely important. In medical or drug trials, for example, alpha values could be set to 0.99 or 0.999, making it very unlikely that a researcher would accept an alternative hypothesis (that a drug caused a change, for example) if it was not correct.
When selecting alpha, Six Sigma teams should ask:
- What are the costs associated with an unnecessary change if the team makes a mistake in rejecting the null hypothesis?

o In a manufacturing environment, what are the costs with rejecting materials that actually fit specifications? o In a non-manufacturing environment, what are the costs associated with accepting the hypothesis that change did occur?
 Are there dangers or costs associated with concluding that a statistical change occurred? What are they?
When selecting a beta value, Six Sigma teams should ask:
- What is the potential costs of a Type II error if the team makes a mistake in not rejecting the null hypothesis?

o What is the potential damage or cost if defective materials are passed to the customer? If a defective spoon is passed along, the ultimate cost might be minimal compared to a defective piece of medical equipment or car engine. o Are there costs associated with lost time or resources in correcting a problem that comes from a Type II error?
When selecting a value for delta, Six Sigma teams should ask:

- How small does the difference have to be before it becomes practically insignificant to the customer? In a pizza restaurant, for example, a customer who orders a 14-inch pizza is going to care if he or she receives a 12-inch pizza. If the pizza that is delivered measures 13.9 inches, the customer is unlikely to care.

- What is the smallest delta that provides the best chance at exposing all benefits or information but is not so small as to be unfeasible. Working at smaller delta values means larger sample sizes or smaller measurement requirements, which take time to gather. - Ultimately, Six Sigma teams should ask themselves: what margin of error is tolerable in results? The type of test you are running, the decisions you are making, and the processes you are dealing with help you make this determination.

The delta, or critical difference, should always be based on business needs. It is helpful in Minitab to express the delta in terms of sigma, or standard deviation. We want to detect differences of .5 σ or 2 σ, for example.
Guidelines for Setting Various Numbers When Calculating Sample Size

While values for various factors in sample size calculations should be guided by the business needs and purposes of the test, here are some guidelines Six Sigma teams can follow for baseline values or if no other information or needs are present.
When Testing Means for Continuous Data
If you have some information about the population, including standard deviation:

- Set alpha at 0.05. - Set beta at 0.10 or 0.20 (which will give you a power of 0.90 or 0.80 – a power of 0.90 is less likely to produce a Type II error than a power of 0.80, but it will also require a much larger sample size in most cases). - Set delta with absolute values as required by business needs or, more often in Minitab, as a function of standard deviation (.5 σ, 1 σ, and 2 σ.)
When Testing Variance for Continuous Data

- Set alpha at 0.05. - Set beta at 0.10 or 0.20 (which will give you a power of 0.90 or 0.80 – a power of 0.90 is less likely to produce a Type II error than a power of 0.80, but it will also require a much larger sample size in most cases). - Set delta greater than 1 and according to business needs, or, more often in Minitab, as a function of standard deviation (.5 σ, 1 σ, and 2 σ.) -
When Testing Proportions for Discrete/Binomial Data
If you have some information about the population, including standard deviation:

- Set alpha at 0.05. - Set beta at 0.10 or 0.20 (which will give you a power of 0.90 or 0.80 – a power of 0.90 is less likely to produce a Type II error than a power of 0.80, but it will also require a much larger sample size in most cases). - Set delta logically according to business needs, or, more often in Minitab, as a function of standard deviation (.5 σ, 1 σ, and 2 σ.)

Note: General sampling on an unknown population, also called survey sampling, requires a different approach to sample-size calculation. Typically, you begin by setting alpha to 0.05, beta to 0.5, and delta to a proportion in keeping with a standard deviation of 1. This provides an initial sample size so you can better understand the population, but you might have to do additional sampling based on the statistics calculated from your first sample.

zh.text: 假设检验误差的概念是为各种假设检验选择样本量的关键。这两种类型的错误在第 21 章中介绍过，但在继续样本量计算之前需要快速回顾一下。
I 类错误
当您在假设检验期间拒绝原假设，而实际上原假设为真时，就会出现 I 类错误。 您可能会拒绝样本均值在统计上与假设均值相同的零假设，而是决定样本均值在统计上大于假设均值。事实上，如果样本均值在统计上与假设均值相同，则存在 I 类错误。
I 类错误风险用 alpha 表示。
正如第 21 章所述，I 类错误也称为生产者风险。在制造环境中，如果原假设为真，但由于 I 类错误而被拒绝，则符合规格的材料（统计上与目标相同的材料）将被拒绝。这是生产者风险——它会导致浪费、额外成本和员工士气下降。
II 类错误
当您接受原假设而事实上它并不成立时，就会出现 II 类错误。如果您接受样本均值在统计上与假设均值相同的假设，而实际上样本均值在统计上大于假设均值，则您会出现 II 类错误。
II 类错误风险用 beta 表示。
正如第 21 章所述，II 类错误也称为消费者风险。在制造环境中，如果原假设为假，但由于 II 类错误而无论如何都被接受，则不符合规格的材料（在统计上与目标不同的材料）将被接受。这意味着不符合目标的产品将转嫁给消费者。这是一种消费者风险——它可能会导致退货、客户不满意、品牌声誉不佳。
适当的样本量可以降低任一类型错误的风险。
选择样本量需要哪些信息？
要执行样本量计算（或让 Minitab 为您执行这些计算），需要某些信息和假设。
标准型
偏差
数据类型
离散还是连续？
测试类型
您使用哪种假设检验（请参阅第 21 章选择正确的检验）
在开始计算样本量之前，必须对 alpha、beta 和 delta 进行一些实际考虑。 在我们对第一类和第二类错误的审查中，我们涵盖了生产者风险和消费者风险之间的差异，但这种细分并不适用于所有组织或所有假设检验。
由于假设检验的结果将用于做出现实世界的决策，因此您必须真正了解测试及其结果对企业、员工和消费者意味着什么。这种对现实世界的理解可以帮助您设置适当的 Alpha 和 Beta 值。
关于 Alpha、Beta 和 Delta 值的问题
大多数情况下，您可以将 alpha 值设置为 0.05。但在具体实验中，准确性极其重要。例如，在医学或药物试验中，α 值可以设置为 0.99 或 0.999，这使得研究人员不太可能接受不正确的替代假设（例如，药物引起变化）。
在选择alpha时，六西格玛团队应该问：
- 如果团队在拒绝零假设方面犯了错误，那么与不必要的变更相关的成本是多少？
o 在制造环境中，拒绝实际符合规格的材料的成本是多少？ o 在非制造环境中，接受变革确实发生的假设相关的成本是多少？
▪ 得出统计数据发生变化的结论是否存在相关的危险或成本？这些是什么？
在选择 Beta 值时，六西格玛团队应询问：
- 如果团队在不拒绝零假设方面犯了错误，那么第二类错误的潜在成本是多少？
o 如果有缺陷的材料被传递给客户，潜在的损害或成本是多少？如果有缺陷的勺子被传递，与有缺陷的医疗设备或汽车发动机相比，最终的成本可能是最小的。 o 纠正 II 类错误引起的问题是否会因时间或资源损失而产生成本？
在选择 delta 值时，六西格玛团队应询问：
- 差异必须小到什么程度才会对客户变得几乎无关紧要？例如，在一家披萨餐厅，订购 14 英寸披萨的顾客会关心他或她是否收到 12 英寸披萨。如果送来的披萨尺寸为 13.9 英寸，顾客不太可能在意。
- 提供展示所有好处或信息的最佳机会但又不至于小到不可行的最小增量是多少。使用较小的增量值意味着较大的样本量或较小的测量要求，这需要时间来收集。 - 最终，六西格玛团队应该问自己：结果中可以容忍的误差范围是多少？您正在运行的测试类型、您正在做出的决定以及您正在处理的流程可以帮助您做出此决定。
增量或关键差异应始终基于业务需求。在 Minitab 中，用 sigma 或标准差来表示 delta 很有帮助。例如，我们想要检测 0.5 σ 或 2 σ 的差异。

rows: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
zh.text: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
计算样本量时设置各种数字的指南
虽然样本量计算中各种因素的值应以业务需求和测试目的为指导，但六西格玛团队可以遵循以下一些准则来获取基线值或在没有其他信息或需求的情况下。

rows: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
zh.text: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
当测试连续数据的方法时
如果您有一些有关总体的信息，包括标准差：
- 将 alpha 设置为 0.05。 - 将 beta 设置为 0.10 或 0.20（这将为您提供 0.90 或 0.80 的幂 - 0.90 的幂比 0.80 的幂更不可能产生 II 类错误，但在大多数情况下也需要更大的样本量）。 - 根据业务需求将 delta 设置为绝对值，或者在 Minitab 中更常见的是，将 delta 设置为标准差的函数（0.5 σ、1 σ 和 2 σ）。
测试连续数据的方差时
- 将 alpha 设置为 0.05。 - 将 beta 设置为 0.10 或 0.20（这将为您提供 0.90 或 0.80 的幂 - 0.90 的幂比 0.80 的幂更不可能产生 II 类错误，但在大多数情况下也需要更大的样本量）。 - 根据业务需求将 delta 设置为大于 1，或者在 Minitab 中更常见的是，将其设置为标准差的函数（0.5 σ、1 σ 和 2 σ。）-
测试离散/二项式数据的比例时
如果您有一些有关总体的信息，包括标准差：
- 将 alpha 设置为 0.05。 - 将 beta 设置为 0.10 或 0.20（这将为您提供 0.90 或 0.80 的幂 - 0.90 的幂比 0.80 的幂更不可能产生 II 类错误，但在大多数情况下也需要更大的样本量）。 - 根据业务需求逻辑设置增量，或者更常见的是在 Minitab 中设置为标准差的函数（0.5 σ、1 σ 和 2 σ）。
注意：对未知总体的一般抽样（也称为调查抽样）需要采用不同的方法来计算样本量。通常，您首先将 alpha 设置为 0.05，将 beta 设置为 0.5，将 delta 设置为与标准差 1 保持一致的比例。这提供了初始样本大小，以便您可以更好地了解总体，但您可能需要根据第一个样本计算的统计数据进行额外采样。

title.en: Sample Size Calculations: Choosing the Right Method
title.zh: 样本量计算：选择正确的方法

en.text: Just as there were numerous hypothesis tests to cover a range of data types and questions, there are numerous sample size calculations – all of which can be performed in Minitab. Review the list below to determine what type of calculation to perform when determining sample size for various hypothesis tests.
1-Sample T Test Sample Size Calculation
- Means testing - Comparing to a target value - You already have sample statistics about the population
1-Sample Z Test Sample Size Calculation
- Means testing - Comparing to a target - You do not have sample statistics about the population (standard deviation is not known)
2-Sample T Test Sample Size Calculation
- Means testing - Comparing means from two sets of data
2-Sample Variance Test Sample Size Calculation
- Variance testing - Comparing variance from two sets of data
1-Sample Variance Test Sample Size Calculation
- Variance testing - Comparing variance of one data set to a target
1-Sample Proportion Test Sample Size Calculation
- Proportion testing (rate, x per y) - Comparing rate of one data set to a target
2-Sample Proportion Test Sample Size Calculation
- Proportion testing (rate, x per y) - Comparing rate from two sets of data
Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) Sample Size Calculation
- Means testing - More than 2 sets of data - Only one factor for x
Design of Experiment (DOE) Sample Size Calculation
- Means testing - More than 2 sets of data - More than one factor for x

zh.text: 正如有大量的假设检验涵盖一系列数据类型和问题一样，也有大量的样本量计算 - 所有这些都可以在 Minitab 中执行。查看下面的列表以确定在确定各种假设检验的样本量时要执行的计算类型。
1-样本 T 检验样本量计算
- 经济状况调查 - 与目标值比较 - 您已经拥有有关人口的样本统计数据
1-样本 Z 测试样本大小计算
- 经济状况调查 - 与目标比较 - 您没有关于人口的样本统计数据（标准差未知）
2-样本 T 检验样本量计算
- 经济状况调查 - 比较两组数据的平均值
2 样本方差检验样本量计算
- 方差测试 - 比较两组数据的方差
1-样本方差测试样本量计算
- 方差测试 - 将一个数据集的方差与目标进行比较
1-样本比例测试样本量计算
- 比例测试（比率，x/y） - 将一个数据集的比率与目标进行比较
2样本比例测试样本量计算
- 比例测试（比率，x/y） - 比较两组数据的比率
方差分析（ANOVA）(ANOVA) 样本量计算

rows: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。
zh.text: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。
- 经济情况调查 - 超过 2 组数据 - x 只有一个因素

title.en: Running and Analyzing Sample Size Tests in Minitab
title.zh: 实验设计 (DOE) 样本量计算

en.text: To understand more about running and analyzing sample size tests in Minitab, we’ll walk through the process for most of the test types above.
To find the Power and Sample Size calculation tools in Minitab, select Stat > Power and Sample Size

First, let’s look at the 1-Sample Z power and sample size calculation. Remember, you would use this calculation in conducting survey sampling for means testing, especially if you don’t know the standard deviation for the process or population. This calculation provides you with a “jumping off point” – a sample size that provides you some relevant information that is as accurate as you define and that you can use as a basis for future sampling.
Selecting the 1-Sample Z calculation from the menu brings up the following dialogue box.
Minitab lets you calculate sample sizes or back into power values if a sample size is fixed, which is why it asks you to fill in only two of the three first values.

Because we don’t yet know much about our data, we leave sample size blank and provide several parameters for differences and power values for Minitab. This causes Minitab to provide a number of answers – one for each difference and power value combination – so we can make a more viable decision about sample size.

In this case, we’ve set the differences at .5 σ, 1 σ, and 2 σ. We’ve set power values at 0.8 and 0.9 (or, beta to either 0.2 or 0.1 respectively). Standard deviation for this first pass is assumed to be 1; if we had information about standard deviation, we could run a more precise calculation or use the 1-Sample T Test calculation.
If you click options, you can change the confidence level or alternative hypothesis (to a greater or less than scenario).
In this case, we’re leaving the options as is.
After we click OK on the Power and Sample Size box, Minitab returns the following information.

Power and Sample Size 1-Sample Z Test Testing mean = null (versus ≠ null) Calculating power for mean = null + difference α = 0.05 Assumed standard deviation = 1 Sample Target Difference Size Power Actual Power 0.5 32 0.8 0.807430 0.5 43 0.9 0.906375 1.0 8 0.8 0.807430 1.0 11 0.9 0.912556 2.0 2 0.8 0.807430 2.0 3 0.9 0.933727

The data table returned by Minitab shows us the required sample size for each difference and target power. You can see that for the lowest difference and highest target power, the sample size required is only 43. But to determine a difference of 1 σ at a target power of 0.9, you only need 11 in the sample size.

Here, the team has to make a business-centric decision. Perhaps samples are easy and inexpensive to capture, so the team selects the maximum sample size. But if each sample takes an hour to obtain and costs the company $26, then the team might make the call to select a smaller sample size.
Again, it is important to note with this particular test that more sampling might be required to draw further conclusions.
Sample Calculations for a 1-Sample T Test
To see the 1-Sample T Test sample calculation in action, let’s revisit a problem from Chapter 21.

An agriculture company growing corn produced an average of 168 bushels of corn per acre each year for five years. The standard deviation is 2.2 bushels. The company made a change to its seeding process in an attempt to increase the yield of its fields. Did the seeding change result in a statistically different outcome in yield per acre?
How many acres would the company need to sample to determine if the new process made a difference? This could help the company know how many acres to plant with the new seed process.
To generate data for this problem, select the 1-Sample T calculation from the Power and Sample Size menu in Minitab.
For this calculation, enter the differences as a multiple of standard deviation, the power values as desired, and the standard deviation provided in the data.
Change the alternative hypothesis to greater than. The company wants to determine whether the yield of the new process is greater than the average yield of the old process.
After you click OK, and then OK again, Minitab generates the following set of information.

Power and Sample Size 1-Sample t Test Testing mean = null (versus > null) Calculating power for mean = null + difference α = 0.05 Assumed standard deviation = 2.2 Sample Target Difference Size Power Actual Power

We can see from the above table that the company would have to plant 168 acres to achieve a target power of 0.9 and be able to discern differences of 0.5 standard deviations. That’s a lot of acreage to plant with an experimental seeding process. The company might weigh its options and resources and decide, instead, to plant 43 or 32 acres. By planting 43 acres, the company can detect differences as low as one standard deviation at 0.9 target power. Given that the standard deviation is assumed to be 2.2 bushels, that is still a relatively small detection range, and the company saves a ton of money by planting over 100 fewer acres for the test.

Backing into target power What if the agriculture company didn’t have a choice regarding the number of acres it could plant for a test? Finances, resources, or corporate leadership might collude to decide that the test could be run on a total of 18 acres – and only 18 acres.
Minitab can take the information about the test and calculate the target power. The test is run the same way, but sample size is entered and power values are left blank.

Power and Sample Size 1-Sample t Test Testing mean = null (versus > null) Calculating power for mean = null + difference α = 0.05 Assumed standard deviation = 2.2 Sample Difference Size Power 0.5 18 0.236131 1.0 18 0.581637 2.0 18 0.979934
Sample Calculations for a 1-Sample Proportion Test
Proportion tests are a bit different for two major reasons:

- Proportion tests are run using attribute data. Attribute data almost always requires a larger sample size for accurate results than continuous data does. - Because you are dealing with attributes – and rates – you don’t need to provide any information about population parameters such as sigma levels or standard deviation.
To calculate the sample size required for accurate 1-Sample Proportion hypothesis testing, you simply set alpha and beta and enter the proportions for both the null and alternative hypothesis.
Let’s consider a real-world example to better understand the calculations in Minitab.

A grocery store chain discovers it has a problem with prices being entered correctly into the system when product is placed in the store or prices are updated. In population sampling, the store found that approximately 8 percent of the prices are entered incorrectly. The store provided ½ of the employees who do pricing entry with new scanners to attempt to mitigate the problem. Before the store commits to a full-time lease on the new equipment for all employees, it wants to confirm that the new scanners bring the error-rate down by 5 percent – to 3 percent or less.

Select the 1-Proportion calculation from the Power and Sample Size menu in Minitab. The comparison proportion is entered as 1 – the probability of failure for a single trial. In this case, 1 – 0.03, since the company wants to reduce the error rate to 3 percent.
The hypothesized proportion is entered as 1 – the probability of a failure for a single trial, or 1 – 0.08, since the original process had an 8 percent failure rate.
Under options, select “greater than” as the alternative hypothesis.
Click OK and then OK, and Minitab generates the information seen below.
Power and Sample Size Test for One Proportion Testing p = 0.92 (versus > 0.92) α = 0.05 Sample Target Comparison p Size Power Actual Power 0.97 140 0.8 0.802942 0.97 177 0.9 0.900362
To run a valid 1-Proportion test, the company needs to gather between 140 and 177 data samples.
Sample Size Calculations for a 2-Sample T Test
Consider again the 2-Sample T test problem from the last chapter:

Two attorneys practicing in the same law firm are turning in very different amounts of billable hours, even though the partners note that the two lawyers have similar caseloads. One partner hypothesizes that Attorney A’s legal staff is taking too long to handle some tasks; because the firm bills at a certain time for administrative-style tasks regardless of how long those tasks take, if legal staff is taking too long for such tasks, they are spending time on work that can’t be billed for.

To test the theory, data is gathered about the time legal staff spends on tasks. Specifically, a team looks at time spent on three tasks for which the legal staff is allowed to bill 15 minutes each: certain types of simple administrative phone, copying, or filing tasks. The following data is collected.
Minutes Spent on Quarter-Hour Tasks Attorney A's Team Attorney B's Team 10 8 8 7 15 10 16 12 17 18 5 6 8 9 16 12 12 11 11 11 8 9 9 8 15 14 17 15 22 16
For the purposes of this problem, the firm team collected a sample of 34 data elements from each team. But is that enough to provide a strong conclusion in the 2-Sample T Test?

First, let’s look at the sample size calculations given what we know of the data in question. The standard deviation of the data is 5.83, and we want to test whether the mean for Attorney A’s team is greater than the mean for Attorney B’s team. We would set up a 2-Sample T Test in Minitab as pictured below.
Minitab generates the following information:

Power and Sample Size 2-Sample t Test Testing mean 1 = mean 2 (versus >) Calculating power for mean 1 = mean 2 + difference α = 0.05 Assumed standard deviation = 5.83 Sample Target Difference Size Power Actual Power 0.5 1682 0.8 0.800045 0.5 2330 0.9 0.900079 1.0 421 0.8 0.800038 1.0 583 0.9 0.900075 2.0 106 0.8 0.800820 2.0 147 0.9 0.901369 The sample size is for each group.

You can see above, the minimal sample size – for each group – that Minitab calculates is 106, and that is for a difference of 2 with target power of 0.8. While a 2-Sample T test can be run on the data set provided above – and we did, in fact, run that test in chapter 21 – the likelihood of a type II error for that test was fairly high.
Just how high was the likelihood for an error running the 2-Sample T test with a sample size of 34 for each group? Minitab can tell us that answer.
Instead of entering power values in the power and sample size dialog box, enter the sample size of 34 and click OK.

Power and Sample Size 2-Sample t Test Testing mean 1 = mean 2 (versus >) Calculating power for mean 1 = mean 2 + difference α = 0.05 Assumed standard deviation = 5.83 Sample Difference Size Power 0.5 34 0.097685 1.0 34 0.172364 2.0 34 0.403259

The highest power for a sample size of 34 given this data is 0.40, which means a beta of 0.60 – more than 50 percent chance of a Type II error in the hypothesis test. In this particular case, before the firm makes any decisions, they should gather a larger sample of data.
A Reminder Regarding Random Samples

Before moving on from sample size calculations, it’s worth noting again the importance of random sampling. Even if you perform a calculation to determine an appropriate sample size – and you choose to ere on the side of caution by sampling at the largest count size returned by Minitab – you can draw the wrong inferences because the sample was not random.

rows: 术语说明 DOE（实验设计）：系统安排实验以识别因素、水平和交互作用对结果的影响。
zh.text: 术语说明 DOE（实验设计）：系统安排实验以识别因素、水平和交互作用对结果的影响。
- 经济情况调查 - 超过 2 组数据 - x 的因素超过一个
在 Minitab 中运行和分析样本量检验
为了了解有关在 Minitab 中运行和分析样本量检验的更多信息，我们将逐步完成上述大多数检验类型的过程。
要在 Minitab 中查找功效和样本量计算工具，请选择统计 > 功效和样本量
首先，我们来看看 1-Sample Z power 和样本量的计算。请记住，您将在进行经济状况调查的调查抽样时使用此计算，尤其是在您不知道过程或总体的标准差的情况下。此计算为您提供了一个“起点”——一个样本大小，可为您提供一些与您定义的一样准确的相关信息，并且您可以将其用作未来抽样的基础。
从菜单中选择 1-Sample Z 计算将弹出以下对话框。
Minitab 允许您计算样本大小，或者如果样本大小固定，则返回功效值，这就是为什么它要求您仅填写前三个值中的两个。
由于我们对数据还不太了解，因此我们将样本大小留空，并为 Minitab 提供多个差异参数和功效值。这使得 Minitab 提供许多答案（每个差异和功效值组合都有一个答案），以便我们可以就样本大小做出更可行的决定。
在本例中，我们将差异设置为 0.5 σ、1 σ 和 2 σ。我们将功效值设置为 0.8 和 0.9（或者将 beta 分别设置为 0.2 或 0.1）。假设第一遍的标准差为 1；如果我们有有关标准差的信息，我们可以进行更精确的计算或使用 1-Sample T 检验计算。
如果单击选项，您可以更改置信水平或备择假设（大于或小于场景）。
在这种情况下，我们将保留选项不变。
在我们单击功效和样本大小框上的确定后，Minitab 将返回以下信息。
Minitab 返回的数据表向我们显示了每个差异和目标功效所需的样本量。您可以看到，对于最低差异和最高目标功效，所需的样本量仅为 43。但要在目标功效为 0.9 时确定 1 σ 的差异，则仅需要 11 个样本量。
在这里，团队必须做出以业务为中心的决策。也许样本采集起来既简单又便宜，因此团队选择了最大样本量。但如果每个样本需要一个小时才能获取，并且公司花费 26 美元，那么团队可能会决定选择较小的样本量。
再次强调，在这个特定的测试中，可能需要更多的采样才能得出进一步的结论。
1 样本 T 检验的示例计算
为了了解 1-Sample T 检验样本计算的实际效果，让我们回顾一下第 21 章中的一个问题。
一家种植玉米的农业公司五年来平均每英亩生产 168 蒲式耳玉米。标准偏差为 2.2 蒲式耳。 该公司改变了播种流程，试图提高田地的产量。播种量的变化是否导致每英亩产量出现统计上不同的结果？
公司需要取样多少英亩才能确定新工艺是否产生影响？这可以帮助公司了解采用新种子工艺种植多少英亩土地。
要生成此问题的数据，请从 Minitab 的功效和样本量菜单中选择 1-样本 T 计算。
对于此计算，请输入标准差倍数的差值、所需的功效值以及数据中提供的标准差。
将备择假设更改为大于。该公司想要确定新工艺的良率是否大于旧工艺的平均良率。
单击“确定”，然后再次单击“确定”后，Minitab 会生成以下信息集。
功效和样本量 1-样本 t 检验 测试均值 = null（与 > null） 计算功效 均值 = null + 差值 α = 0.05 假设标准差 = 2.2 样本目标差值大小 功效 实际功效

从上表中我们可以看出，该公司需要种植 168 英亩才能实现 0.9 的目标功效，并能够辨别 0.5 个标准差的差异。通过实验播种过程可以种植大量面积。该公司可能会权衡其选择和资源，然后决定种植 43 或 32 英亩的土地。通过种植 43 英亩的土地，该公司可以在 0.9 的目标功效下检测低至 1 个标准偏差的差异。鉴于标准偏差假设为 2.2 蒲式耳，这仍然是一个相对较小的检测范围，该公司通过减少 100 多英亩的测试种植面积节省了大量资金。
返回目标功率 如果农业公司无法选择可以种植用于测试的英亩数怎么办？财务、资源或公司领导层可能会串通决定该测试可以在总共 18 英亩的土地上进行——而且只有 18 英亩。
Minitab 可以获取有关检验的信息并计算目标功效。测试以相同的方式运行，但输入样本大小并将功效值留空。
功效和样本量 1-样本 t 检验 检验均值 = 零（与 > 零） 计算功效 均值 = 零 + 差值 α = 0.05 假设标准差 = 2.2 样本差值大小 功效 0.5 18 0.236131 1.0 18 0.581637 2.0 18 0.979934
1-样本比例检验的计算示例
比例测试略有不同，主要有两个原因：
- 使用属性数据运行比例测试。与连续数据相比，属性数据几乎总是需要更大的样本量才能获得准确的结果。 - 因为您正在处理属性和比率，所以不需要提供有关总体参数的任何信息，例如西格玛水平或标准差。

rows: 术语说明 Sigma level（西格玛水平）：衡量过程缺陷率的尺度；水平越高，单位机会中产生缺陷的概率越低。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sigma level（西格玛水平）：衡量过程缺陷率的尺度；水平越高，单位机会中产生缺陷的概率越低。
要计算准确的 1 样本比例假设检验所需的样本量，您只需设置 alpha 和 beta 并输入原假设和备择假设的比例。
让我们考虑一个现实世界的示例，以更好地理解 Minitab 中的计算。
一家连锁杂货店发现，当产品放入商店或更新价格时，价格无法正确输入系统。在总体抽样中，商店发现大约 8% 的价格输入错误。该商店为 1/2 负责定价输入的员工配备了新扫描仪，以试图缓解该问题。在商店承诺为所有员工全职租赁新设备之前，它希望确认新扫描仪将错误率降低了 5%，即 3% 或更低。
从 Minitab 的功效和样本量菜单中选择 1-比例计算。比较比例输入为 1——单次试验失败的概率。在本例中，为 1 – 0.03，因为公司希望将错误率降低到 3%。
假设的比例输入为 1 – 单次试验失败的概率，或 1 – 0.08，因为原始过程的失败率为 8%。
在选项下，选择“大于”作为备择假设。
单击“确定”，然后单击“确定”，Minitab 将生成如下所示的信息。
要运行有效的 1-比例测试，该公司需要收集 140 到 177 个数据样本。
2 样本 T 检验的样本量计算
再次考虑上一章中的 2 样本 T 检验问题：
尽管合伙人指出，在同一家律师事务所执业的两名律师的计费时间相差很大，但两位律师的案件量相似。一位合伙人推测 A 律师的法律人员处理某些任务的时间太长；因为公司会在特定时间对行政类任务进行计费，无论这些任务需要多长时间，如果法律人员在此类任务上花费的时间太长，他们就会将时间花在无法计费的工作上。
为了检验该理论，收集了有关法律人员执行任务的时间的数据。具体来说，团队会查看三项任务所花费的时间，法律人员可以为每项任务计费 15​​ 分钟：某些类型的简单管理电话、复印或归档任务。收集以下数据。
为了解决这个问题，公司团队从每个团队收集了 34 个数据元素的样本。但这足以在 2 样本 T 检验中提供强有力的结论吗？
首先，根据我们对相关数据的了解，我们来看看样本量的计算。数据的标准差是 5.83，我们想要测试律师 A 团队的均值是否大于律师 B 团队的均值。我们将在 Minitab 中设置 2 样本 T 检验，如下图所示。
Minitab 生成以下信息：
您可以在上面看到，Minitab 计算的每组的最小样本量为 106，即差异为 2，目标功效为 0.8。虽然可以在上面提供的数据集上运行 2 样本 T 检验（事实上，我们在第 21 章中运行过该检验），但该检验出现 II 类错误的可能性相当高。
每组样本量为 34 时，运行 2 样本 T 检验时出错的可能性有多高？ Minitab 可以告诉我们这个答案。
不要在功效和样本量对话框中输入功效值，而是输入样本量 34，然后单击“确定”。
功效和样本量 2-样本 t 检验 检验均值 1 = 均值 2（与 >） 计算均值 1 的功效 = 均值 2 + 差值 α = 0.05 假设标准差 = 5.83 样本差值大小 功效 0.5 34 0.097685 1.0 34 0.172364 2.0 34 0.403259
给定此数据时，样本量为 34 的最高功效为 0.40，这意味着 beta 值为 0.60——假设检验中出现 II 类错误的可能性超过 50%。在这种特殊情况下，公司在做出任何决定之前，应该收集更大的数据样本。
关于随机样本的提醒
在继续样本量计算之前，值得注意的是随机抽样的重要性。即使您执行计算以确定适当的样本量（并且您选择谨慎起见，以 Minitab 返回的最大计数大小进行采样），您也可能会得出错误的推论，因为样本不是随机的。
