零件
Minitab 生成一系列图形分析来帮助确定测量系统是否有效。底部的两个图表显示每个运算符相当接近地复制另一个运算符的结果。每个操作员的 Xbar 图表看起来几乎相同，零件操作员交互图表非常接近，您甚至看不到 Ann 的红色下方 Mike 的蓝色方块和线条。数据的箱线图还显示，每个操作员的信息非常相似——平均值似乎相同，数据的分布也是如此。

rows: 术语说明 Interaction（交互作用）：一个因素的效果会随另一个因素水平不同而变化。
zh.text: 术语说明 Interaction（交互作用）：一个因素的效果会随另一个因素水平不同而变化。
第一张图详细说明了测量中出现变化的位置。 对于迈克和安来说，当将第一次运行中一个零件的测量值与第二次运行中同一零件的测量值进行比较时，就会出现变化。然而，即使运行之间存在微小的差异，总体差异也非常小，并且该量具 R & R 表明测量系统正在工作。

title.en: Chapter 27: The Stat Menu in Minitab
title.zh: 第 27 章：Minitab 的 Stat 菜单

source.extraction: python-docx body-order extraction; source-TOC sectionized bilingual content

en.text: Chapter 21 introduced hypothesis testing and included information on how to select the right test and how to interpret the results of a test based on p-value. Chapter 19 covered correlation and regression and how to interpret the results of tests run in Excel. In this chapter, you’ll learn how to run all these tests in Minitab.

Because interpreting test results and setting up hypothesis testing has been covered in detail in other chapters, this chapter simply offers some guidance for using Minitab to run the tests, including where to find the tests and how to enter information for various tests. Each individual test isn’t covered in detail, since the entry of data for the various hypothesis tests is very similar.

zh.text: 第 21 章介绍了假设检验，并包含有关如何选择正确的检验以及如何根据 p 值解释检验结果的信息。第 19 章介绍了相关性和回归以及如何解释在 Excel 中运行的测试结果。在本章中，您将学习如何在 Minitab 中运行所有这些测试。

rows: 术语说明 Correlation（相关）：描述两个变量共同变化的程度，不直接证明因果关系。
zh.text: 术语说明 Correlation（相关）：描述两个变量共同变化的程度，不直接证明因果关系。

rows: 术语说明 Regression（回归）：用模型估计变量之间的关系，并预测响应变量。
zh.text: 术语说明 Regression（回归）：用模型估计变量之间的关系，并预测响应变量。

rows: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
zh.text: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
由于解释检验结果和设置假设检验已在其他章节中详细介绍，因此本章仅提供一些使用 Minitab 运行检验的指导，包括在哪里查找检验以及如何输入各种检验的信息。由于各种假设检验的数据输入非常相似，因此没有详细介绍每个单独的检验。
基础统计

en.text: Many of the tools used to run hypothesis tests are found under Basic Statistics in the Stat menu.
Descriptive and Store Descriptive Statistics

Sometimes, you need to know the mean, standard deviation, or some other descriptive statistic about your data sample. When running many hypothesis tests, you need such data. Minitab’s Descriptive Statistics function lets you run all of the most common statistics at one time, and you can store them in a worksheet for reference as you work on analysis.
Copy the data above into Minitab.
Select Stat > Basic Statistics > Display Descriptive Statistics.
Select the temperature column as the variable.
Click Statistics.
Check the box next to each statistic you would like to display.
Click OK. Click OK again.
Minitab displays the statistics in the session window.
Descriptive Statistics: Temperature A
Variable N N* Mean SE Mean StDev Minimum Q1 Median Q3 Maximum Temperature A 12 0 49.33 3.55 12.31 30.00 37.75 51.50 61.25 65.00
Complete the exact same series of steps, but choose Store Descriptive Statistics instead of Display Descriptive Statistics. Minitab will place the statistics in the workbook.
Regression Analysis
Use the data of temperature over time you copied above to run a regression analysis in Minitab. The question you want to answer is: Is there a correlation between the temperature and the time?
Select Stat > Regression > Fitted Line Plot.
Choose the temperature column as the response variable and the time column as the predictor variable.
Click OK.
Fitted Line Plot
Temperature A = 27.56 + 3.350 Time
Temperature A
Time
Minitab returns the fitted line plot along with R-squared values, which you can interpret following the information from chapter 19.

zh.text: 许多用于运行假设检验的工具都可以在“统计”菜单中的“基本统计”下找到。
描述性和存储描述性统计数据
有时，您需要了解数据样本的平均值、标准差或其他一些描述性统计数据。在运行许多假设检验时，您需要此类数据。 Minitab 的描述统计功能可让您一次运行所有最常见的统计数据，并且您可以将它们存储在工作表中，以供分析时参考。

rows: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
zh.text: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
将以上数据复制到 Minitab 中。
选择统计 > 基本统计 > 显示描述性统计。
选择温度列作为变量。
单击统计。
选中您想要显示的每个统计数据旁边的框。
单击“确定”。再次单击“确定”。
Minitab 在会话窗口中显示统计数据。
描述性统计：温度 A
完成完全相同的一系列步骤，但选择“存储描述性统计信息”而不是“显示描述性统计信息”。 Minitab 会将统计数据放入工作簿中。
回归分析
使用上面复制的温度随时间变化的数据在 Minitab 中运行回归分析。你要回答的问题是：温度和时间之间有相关性吗？
选择统计 > 回归 > 拟合线图。
选择温度列作为响应变量，选择时间列作为预测变量。
单击“确定”。
拟合线图
温度A
时间
Minitab 返回拟合线图以及 R 平方值，您可以根据第 19 章中的信息对其进行解释。

en.text: Most of the relevant hypothesis tests in Minitab are found either under Stat > Basic Statistics or Stat > Nonparametics.
The most relevant tests found under Stat > Basic Statistics are:
- 1 Sample Z - 1 Sample t - 2 Sample t - Paired t - 1 Proportion - 2 Proportion - 1 Variance - 2 Variance - Normality Test

You might notice that the tests found under Basic Statistics tend to be the tests used for discrete or normally distributed continuous data. The test for nonnormally distributed continuous data can be found under Stat > Nonparametrics.
Running Hypothesis Tests
Example 1: 1 Proportion Test
Let’s look at a few examples of hypothesis tests in Minitab. We’ll look again at some of the problems discussed in the hypothesis testing chapter.

Problem: A school administrator believes that the students in a certain classroom are being impacted by some environmental issue with that room. The administrator wants to see if the students in that room perform statistically lower on tests than the students throughout the school; she has already noted that different classes in that room are taught by other teachers.

The administrator gathers data. The rate of failure for classes taught in any other classroom is 20 percent. Out of 142 students who have a class in the classroom in question, 38 are failing that particular class.

The question is, is the rate of failure in the classroom statistically greater than the rate in other classrooms? You can look back to the 1 Proportion section of chapter 21 to see how the null and alternative hypotheses are created.
To run the test in Minitab, select Stat > Basic Statistics > 1 Proportion.
In the drop down, select “Summarized data,” since you don’t have information in columns.
The number of events is the number of outcomes that you are measuring. Here, the administrator was measuring failure rates, and there were 38 failures.
The number of trials is the total number of data points. In this case, the school administrator looked at scores for 142 students.

Check the box to perform a hypothesis test and enter the hypothesized proportion. In this case, you are comparing the actual failure rate of the classroom group to the overall failure rate of 20 percent. The hypothesized portion is written as a decimal between 0 and 1.
Click Options.
Minitab usually defaults the confidence level to 95, which would set alpha at 0.05. Unless you have a specific need for greater confidence, you can leave this as is.
Select the appropriate alternative hypothesis from the drop down menu. In this case, we are testing whether the proportion of our data is greater than the hypothesized proportion.
Click OK. Click OK again.
Minitab displays the results, including a p-value you can use to decide to reject or not reject the null hypothesis.
Example 2: 1 Sample T Test

Problem: An agriculture company growing corn produced an average of 168 bushels of corn per acre each year for five years. The company made a change to its seeding process in an attempt to increase the yield of its fields. The harvest following the seed change resulted in an average of 175 bushels of corn per acre each year over 500 acres of fields. The standard deviation is 2.2 bushels. Did the seeding change result in a statistically different outcome in yield per acre?
Select Stat > Basic Statistics > 1 Sample T.
Choose summarized data.

Enter the sample size, sample mean, and sample standard deviation from the problem. Check the box to perform a hypothesis test and enter the hypothesized mean. In this case, you want to know whether the new mean is greater than the old mean, so the old mean is the hypothesized mean.
Click Options.
Choose the appropriate hypothesis test from the drop down menu.
Click OK. Click OK again.
Minitab displays the results, including a p-value you can use to decide to reject or not reject the null hypothesis.
Example 3 – 2 – Sample T Test

Copy the data from the table below into Minitab. If you recall from chapter 21, a law firm believed there was a statistical difference between the time employees on two different teams were spending on tasks.
Select Stat > Basic Statistics > 2 – Sample T Test.
From the drop down, select that each sample is in its own column. You can also choose to analyze samples when the data is all in one column or you can enter summarized data about each sample.
Select the appropriate columns into the Samples 1 and 2 boxes. In this case, you are selecting the columns that hold the data for each of the teams.
Click Options.

In this case, we only want to know if there is a difference between the two teams, so we’ll leave the alternative hypothesis as simply not equal to the hypothesized difference. In this case, we are assuming there is no difference, so we’ll leave the hypothesized difference as 0.
Click OK. Click OK again.
Minitab displays the results, including a p-value you can use to decide to reject or not reject the null hypothesis.
Hypothesis Test Steps Are Similar for all Types of Tests

All of the hypothesis tests in Minitab are run using basically the same steps as in the examples above. For most test types, you can choose to test the raw data or you can enter summarized data. Remember, each type of hypothesis test requires different summarized data. Proportion testing requires rates, means testing usually requires standard deviations and means of samples, and variance testing requires sample variance. Some of the tests you might use on data that isn’t normally distributed require the median instead of the mean.

If you have the right descriptive statistics or have your data in columns in Minitab, it is a matter of a few clicks to set up and run a hypothesis test. The challenge isn’t in running, or interpreting, the tests. It is usually in determining which test is the right option for your data. It’s worth reviewing the information in chapter 21 again to understand what types of hypothesis tests are used with different data types and scenarios. It’s also important to test your data for normality before running hypothesis tests. Remember that you can easily test for normality by running the Graphical Summary under Basic Statistics.

Some other things to keep in mind when dealing with hypothesis tests in Minitab are that you should always click the Options button to verify that your confidence interval is set appropriately and that the appropriate hypothesis test is selected. Minitab defaults to a confidence interval of 95% and an alternative hypothesis of not equal. However, if you are working during the same session – meaning you haven’t closed Minitab since the last time you ran that specific hypothesis test, the program will remember what settings you entered and apply them automatically to the new test you are running. This can be a problem if you want to run a test on different data or run a test on the same data with a different confidence interval or alternative hypothesis and you forgot to make the change.
It’s also worth noting that a few of the means tests are also located under Stat > Equivalence Test.
If you are working in Minitab and have questions about these tests, you can access the Help menu for information about choosing, running, and interpreting each test.
Select Help > StatGuide.
The topics are organized just like the Minitab menus. Simply select the test you want help with and Minitab offers a dozen or so pages of help content for each.

zh.text: Minitab 中的大多数相关假设检验可在统计 > 基本统计或统计 > 非参数下找到。
在统计 > 基本统计下找到的最相关的测试是：
- 1 个样本 Z - 1 个样本 t - 2 个样本 t - 配对 t - 1 个比例 - 2 个比例 - 1 个方差 - 2 个方差 - 正态性检验
您可能会注意到，在“基本统计”下找到的检验往往是用于离散或正态分布连续数据的检验。非正态分布连续数据的检验可以在统计 > 非参数下找到。
运行假设检验
示例1：1比例测试
让我们看一下 Minitab 中假设检验的几个示例。我们将再次看看假设检验章节中讨论的一些问题。
问题：学校管理员认为某个教室的学生受到该房间的某些环境问题的影响。管理员想了解该房间的学生在测试中的统计成绩是否低于全校学生；她已经注意到那个房间里的不同课程是由其他老师教的。
管理员收集数据。在任何其他教室教授的课程的失败率为 20%。在该教室上课的 142 名学生中，有 38 名学生未能通过该课程。
问题是，从统计数据来看，该教室的失败率是否高于其他教室的失败率？您可以回顾第 21 章的 1 比例部分，了解原假设和备择假设是如何创建的。
要在 Minitab 中运行检验，请选择统计 > 基本统计 > 1 比例。
在下拉列表中，选择“汇总数据”，因为列中没有信息。
事件的数量就是您正在测量的结果的数量。这里，管理员正在测量故障率，结果有 38 次故障。
试验次数是数据点的总数。在本例中，学校管理人员查看了 142 名学生的成绩。
选中该框以执行假设检验并输入假设比例。在本例中，您将课堂小组的实际不及格率与 20% 的总体不及格率进行比较。假设部分被写成 0 到 1 之间的小数。
单击选项。
Minitab 通常将置信水平默认为 95，这会将 alpha 设置为 0.05。除非您特别需要增强信心，否则可以保持原样。
从下拉菜单中选择适当的备择假设。在这种情况下，我们正在测试我们的数据比例是否大于假设比例。
单击“确定”。再次单击“确定”。
Minitab 显示结果，包括可用于决定拒绝或不拒绝原假设的 p 值。

rows: 术语说明 p-value（p 值）：在原假设成立时观察到当前或更极端结果的概率；常用于判断显著性。
zh.text: 术语说明 p-value（p 值）：在原假设成立时观察到当前或更极端结果的概率；常用于判断显著性。
示例 2：1 个样本 T 检验
问题：一家种植玉米的农业公司五年来平均每年每英亩生产 168 蒲式耳玉米。该公司改变了播种流程，试图提高田地的产量。换种后的收获结果是，500 英亩的土地上每年平均每英亩收获 175 蒲式耳玉米。标准偏差为 2.2 蒲式耳。播种量的变化是否导致每英亩产量出现统计上不同的结果？
选择统计 > 基本统计 > 1 个样本 T。
选择汇总数据。
输入问题的样本量、样本均值和样本标准差。选中该框以执行假设检验并输入假设平均值。在这种情况下，您想知道新均值是否大于旧均值，因此旧均值就是假设均值。
单击选项。
从下拉菜单中选择适当的假设检验。
单击“确定”。再次单击“确定”。
Minitab 显示结果，包括可用于决定拒绝或不拒绝原假设的 p 值。
示例 3 – 2 – 样本 T 检验
将下表中的数据复制到 Minitab 中。如果您还记得第 21 章，一家律师事务所认为两个不同团队的员工花在任务上的时间存在统计差异。
选择统计 > 基本统计 > 2 – 样本 T 检验。
从下拉列表中，选择每个样本都位于其自己的列中。当数据全部在一列中时，您还可以选择分析样品，或者您可以输入有关每个样品的汇总数据。
在示例 1 和 2 框中选择适当的列。在本例中，您选择保存每个团队的数据的列。
单击选项。
在这种情况下，我们只想知道两支球队之间是否存在差异，因此我们将备择假设简单地不等于假设的差异。在本例中，我们假设没有差异，因此我们将假设的差异保留为 0。
单击“确定”。再次单击“确定”。
Minitab 显示结果，包括可用于决定拒绝或不拒绝原假设的 p 值。
所有类型的检验的假设检验步骤都是相似的
Minitab 中的所有假设检验都使用与上述示例基本相同的步骤运行。对于大多数测试类型，您可以选择测试原始数据，也可以输入汇总数据。请记住，每种类型的假设检验都需要不同的汇总数据。比例检验需要比率，均值检验通常需要标准差和样本均值，方差检验需要样本方差。您可能对非正态分布的数据使用的一些检验需要中位数而不是均值。
如果您拥有正确的描述性统计数据或将数据存储在 Minitab 的列中，则只需单击几下即可设置并运行假设检验。挑战不在于运行或解释测试。通常是为了确定哪种测试最适合您的数据。 值得再次回顾第 21 章中的信息，以了解不同数据类型和场景使用哪些类型的假设检验。在运行假设检验之前测试数据的正态性也很重要。请记住，您可以通过运行基本统计下的图形摘要来轻松测试正态性。

在 Minitab 中处理假设检验时需要记住的其他一些事情是，您应该始终单击“选项”按钮来验证您的置信区间设置是否正确以及是否选择了适当的假设检验。 Minitab 默认置信区间为 95%，备择假设为不相等。但是，如果您在同一个会话期间工作 - 这意味着自上次运行特定假设检验以来您尚未关闭 Minitab，程序将记住您输入的设置并自动将它们应用到您正在运行的新测试。如果您想要对不同的数据运行测试，或者使用不同的置信区间或备择假设对相同的数据运行测试，而您忘记进行更改，则这可能会出现问题。
还值得注意的是，一些经济状况测试也位于统计 > 等价测试下。
如果您使用 Minitab 并对这些测试有疑问，您可以访问“帮助”菜单以获取有关选择、运行和解释每个测试的信息。
选择帮助 > 统计指南。
这些主题的组织方式与 Minitab 菜单类似。只需选择您需要帮助的测试，Minitab 就会为每个测试提供十几页左右的帮助内容。

title.zh: 第 28 章：方差分析（单因素 ANOVA）

source.extraction: python-docx body-order extraction; source-TOC sectionized bilingual content

en.text: Analysis of variance, often referred to as ANOVA, is a hypothesis test that deals with more than two populations or factors of X. If you recall from chapter 21, we discussed a variety of hypothesis tests for dealing with single factors of x or two factors of x. For example, you learned to conduct a hypothesis test to determine if the mean of a sample was statistically equal to a target mean. You also learned to compare the means of two groups of data with each other for the same reason. ANOVA lets you perform this test with more than two groups of data.

Analysis of variance is often used when you have a combination of both discrete and continuous variables – if the independent variance is discrete – a list of employees, for example – and the response variable is continuous – a list of errors or length of time worked – ANOVA can be a valuable tool for analysis.
Before learning more about ANOVA, let’s review the other tools for testing means.

- When testing for one mean – usually against a target – you would use the Z test or T test. Remember, the Z test is often used for testing large samples when you don’t know what the standard deviation is. The Z test often provides the “lay of the land,” letting you gather some information so you can run more accurate analysis with other tests. - When testing for two means – which involves comparing two samples against each other – you would use the 2 sample T test or the paired T test. - When testing for three or more means, you can use a specific ANOVA test called the 1-way Anova.
To better understand these designations, consider the real-world applications below.

A Six Sigma team is working to reduce the process time in a manufacturing process. The team was provided with historic baseline metrics for the process. Specifically, in the last quarter, the process averaged 35.8 minutes per output. After working through Define, Measure, and Analyze phases, the team has put an improvement in place they believe will reduce the average time per output. After piloting the change in Improve, the team takes new measurements. To compare the new sample to the historical baseline, the team would use the 1-sample T test.

In a different scenario, a Six Sigma team is working to increase the number of calls that can be handled by a call center team in a given day. Call center management has repeatedly asked for additional employees, but executive leadership wants to find out whether efficiency improvements can increase production without adding additional employees. After the first half of the DMAIC process, a Six Sigma team is ready with possible solutions. To verify these solutions, the team decides to implement the changes in half the call center. Over the course of one month, the team measures performance for both halves of the call center. At the end of the month, the team has two sets of data and wants to answer
the question: Is the average production for the altered group greater than the average for the unaltered group? In this case, the team would use the 2-sample T test.
The two examples above are in line with many of the examples used in the chapter on hypothesis testing. Consider the scenario below, which is slightly different.

A restaurant chain wants to know if its branding and customer-facing activities are working equally across all locations. Specifically, the company decides to look at the customer satisfaction scores for each restaurant location. The chain includes five locations. Satisfaction scores are collected via phone, web, and written surveys at each location. The company averages the scores each week and reports those numbers. After several months, the company has five sets of averages. If the company wants to know whether the scores for any location differs statistically from the scores from other locations, then the correct test is the 1-way ANOVA.
Preparing for a 1-Way ANOVA

The way a Six Sigma expert approaches a 1-Way ANOVA is the very similar to the way he or she would approach any other hypothesis test. First, you begin with a real-world problem – a practical or business problem. In the example above, that problem is whether there is variation in customer satisfaction among the various restaurant locations.
As with any hypothesis test, a null and alternative hypothesis is required. The null hypothesis is that there is no difference in the means of the samples.
The alternative hypothesis is that at least one of the means is not statistically the same.

After stating the null and alternative hypothesis, a Six Sigma expert should verify that any assumptions within the model are appropriate. This includes assumptions about errors as well as some basic assumptions about the ANOVA model.
First, the samples used for the ANOVA must be randomly selected. Remember, this is always a necessary assumption for inferential statistics.
After ensuring samples are randomly selected, Six Sigma experts must validate six other assumptions about data before running a 1-way ANOVA test.

1. The dependent variable, or outcome, must be continuous in nature. This means that it is a ratio or an interval. In previous examples, the dependent variable included the customer satisfaction score (numerical), the number of calls handled per day, and the cycle

time for a specific output. All of these are examples of continuous data. Anything that can be measured in time, temperature, feet and inches (or centimeters and meters), money, or ratios is typically continuous in nature. 2. The independent variable list contains two or more unrelated groups or categories. Teams A, B, and C are three independent variables. If you are measuring performance for five different workers, then you might have five independent variables. In the example about restaurants, the five different restaurants are independent variables. Categorical variables can include people, times, shifts, teams, departments, locations, various demographic groups (age, gender, ethnicity), or professions. 3. Observations are independent. First, you must ensure there are no dependencies within groups. If you are measuring the performance of three different teams in a department but several employees work on multiple teams, then the results are not completely independent. Second, you must ensure that observations within groups are not dependent. 4. Significant outliers don’t exist in your data. Outliers throw off the accuracy of the 1-way ANOVA, but you don’t have to scrap the entire test because of a single explainable outlier. As a Six Sigma expert, you do have to review your data, investigate outliers, and discard them appropriately. For example, if a Six Sigma expert is reviewing the response times for customer emails, he or she might review data samples from nine different employees. The data for one employee includes three outliers where emails were responded to after a much longer amount of time than all other samples seem to indicate. The Six Sigma expert might investigate this and note that the employee in question was on a short medical leave, which skewed results. The Six Sigma expert could remove those three data points from his or her calculations, but it would be important to note these outliers. The fact that this particular problem can happen is still important to the overall process improvement. The Six Sigma team might recommend instituting a process change that addresses this issue, but they might analyze data minus these outliers to draw other conclusions. 5. The dependent variable (as described in number 1 above) should be normally distributed or approximate the normal curve within each group. In the example above, the Six Sigma team gathers data for nine employees. Within each of these nine data sets, the data should be normally distributed. 6. The variance within each set has to be statistically equal to the variances of other sets – also called homogeneity of variances. The test for equal variance can be run in Minitab and will be covered later in this chapter.

After validating assumptions and setting up the hypotheses, a Six Sigma expert can run the 1-way ANOVA manually or using statistical analysis software. In this chapter, we’ll use Minitab to run the tests. When using statistical analysis software, you typically spend more time on validating some of the assumptions than you do running the test itself.
Running a 1-Way ANOVA
To understand how to validate all the assumptions, run the 1-Way ANOVA, and interpret the results, consider a real-world example.
A mail-order book company wants to improve customer satisfaction with deliveries. A Six Sigma team working on the process has identified packing materials as a possible factor in negative customer

satisfaction scores. The mail-order company currently ships books via cardboard boxes with no other protection, which the team believes is contributing to an increased amount of damage to shipments before they reach the customer or when the customer opens the package.

To test this theory and pilot two proposed solutions, the team implements additional packing options. Some books will be shipped in the regular packaging – the box without any additional materials. Others will be shipped in boxes with packing peanuts and still others in padded envelopes. The team implements the different packing methods at three different shipping stations and records an overall customer satisfaction score for each shipment based on a scale from 1 to 10 with 10 being the most satisfied. That data is presented below.
Original
Packaging
Padded
Envelope
To follow along with the 1-Way ANOVA for this example, copy the above data into Minitab.
Validate Assumptions:
Is the dependent variable continuous?
Yes, it is measured in interval form.
Is the independent variable made up of two or more unrelated groups?
Yes, the independent variable is how the book is packaged.
Is there independence of observation?
Yes, the three packing methods don’t have anything to do with each other and are not combined. The team wouldn’t pack one book in a padded envelop and also put it in a box, for example.

It is worth noting here that the team has implemented the various packing at three different stations. It would be a good idea for the team to ensure that the packing stations were otherwise treated identically to keep any other factors from influencing the outcome of the pilot.
Are there any significant outliers in the data?

Graphical analysis can be an easy way to check for outliers. The box plot (also called the box and whisper plot) is a good way to check quickly for outliers. Boxplotting in Minitab was covered in Unit 6, but you can run one on the data above now.
1. Click Graph > Boxplot 2. Select multiple Ys 3. Click OK 4. Select all three data columns into the Graph Variables box 5. Click OK.

Minitab returns the graph above, which shows no outliers for any of the three sets of data. What if someone recording the customer satisfaction scores made a mistake and entered the number 20 for one shipment? The boxplot analysis changes.

In the graph above, you can see an outlier dot high above the box for packing peanuts. A Six Sigma expert reviewing this graph would see that outlier and realize that it was a measurement error – remember, the scale for customer satisfaction was only supposed to go up to 10. Because there is an explanation for the outlier, it can be removed.
Is the data in each group normal?
The quickest way to test normality for each set of data is probably to run a graphical analysis in Minitab. This was also covered in Unit 6.
1. Select Stat > Basic Statistics > Graphical Summary
2. Select the column for the first set of data into the Variables box. 3. Make sure the confidence level is set to 95.0. 4. Click OK.
Minitab returns the following chart and data.
Summary Report for Original Packaging

The p-value for the Anderson-Darling normality test is 0.405. Remember, when testing for normalcy, the null hypothesis is that there is no difference between the data and the normal curve. Since the p-value is above our alpha level (0.05), then we fail to reject the null hypothesis and accept that the data is normal.

The graphs generated for the other two sets of data are included below. You can see that the p-values for each also allow us to accept the null hypothesis and validate this assumption. Yes, the data for each group approximates the normal distribution.
Summary Report for Padded Envelope
Summary Report for Packing Peanuts
Is there a homogeneity of variances? Are the variances between groups relatively equal?

Minitab includes an option for testing for equal variances that provides two different p-values. One is the p-value for the Multiple Comparisons test and one is for Levene’s test. Levene’s test is commonly used to test for equal assumptions when data within each group is normal. This test was not previously covered in Unit 6.
1. Select Stat > ANOVA > Test for Equal Variances
2. In the drop down box, select “Response data are in a separate column for each factor level.”
3. Select all the data columns into the Responses box.
4. Click OK.
Minitab returns an interval graph of all the columns of data along with two statistical values.

For the test for equal variance, you are usually concerned with the p-value for Levene’s Test. In this case, it is above 0.05 (Minitab’s default alpha setting), so you can fail to reject the null hypothesis, which is that there is no difference in the variance among the data. In this case, even a look at the interval graph helps you make this determination. The ranges are certainly different, but the intervals are not extremely different in length.
To consider a contrasting example, copy the following data table into Minitab.
Run the test for equal variance under the ANOVA menu using just the two columns of data from the above table.

You can quickly see that these two sets of data do not have equal variance. First, the intervals make it fairly obvious. The variance in set B is much smaller. The p-value for Levene’s Test is much smaller than the alpha value, which means you reject the null hypothesis and accept the alternative hypothesis that there is a difference in the variance.
You can change the alpha value for the tests by selecting Options on the Test for Equal Variances dialogue box.
You can change the type of graph displayed by selecting Graphs from the Test for Equal Variances dialogue box.
Here are the original three sets of data graphed via box plot.
Data
Run the 1-Way ANOVA Test
Now that all the assumptions are verified, you can run the 1-Way ANOVA test.
Select Stat > ANOVA > One-Way.
Select the drop down option that response data are in separate columns.
Select all three of the data columns (the data for the original packing, padded envelopes, and packing peanuts) into the Responses box.
You can click Options to set the confidence interval or ensure it is still set at 95%, if desired. Then click OK.
Click OK on the main dialogue box. Minitab will perform the 1-Way ANOVA calculations. Depending on how much data is in columns when you run this test, it can take a few seconds.
Minitab defaults to returning two things: a graphical analysis of the data and the ANOVA results in the session window.

The data from the session window is included above. Highlighted are the hypothesis and the p-value. The null hypothesis is that all the means are equal. The alternative is that at least one of the means is statistically different.
The p-value is less than the alpha value of 0.05, which means you reject the null hypothesis and accept the alternative hypothesis that at least one of the means is different.

In business terms, the Six Sigma team now knows that “one of these things is not like the other.” That might prompt them to work on additional analysis or indicate that there might be an issue with one or more processes. If the team was comparing mean production times between various teams on the same process, they might assume that the teams would complete work in roughly the same time. If the 1-Way ANOVA indicates that isn’t true, the Six Sigma team can then ask itself: What is it that makes the outcome statistically different? Is there a team doing it better than everyone else? If so, can the Six Sigma team implement solutions from that group across the other groups? Is there a team performing worse than everyone else? If so, what is going on with that team and how can an improvement be made?
What if Variances Aren’t Equal?

If you can’t validate the sixth assumption about equal variances between your subgroups, you can still run a 1-Way ANOVA test in Minitab. Minitab uses a different statistical calculation, known as Welch’s test, to provide a p-value in such a case.
Use the data we previously found to have unequal variances to run this test in Minitab.
Select Stat > ANOVA > One-Way.
Select the drop down option that response data are in separate columns.
Select the data columns for A and B into the Responses box.
Select Options.
Uncheck the box for assume equal variances.
Click OK. Click OK again.
As in the previous example, Minitab generates both a graphical analysis and the test data in the session window.

You can see from the Minitab results, equal variance was not assumed for this data. You also have a p- value that is effectively 0, which means you reject the null hypothesis and accept the alternative that one of the means is statistically different: something we already did just by looking at the graphical interpretation of this data before. As stated previously, data doesn’t always look so different when viewed graphically, which often necessitates statistical analysis.
The Hypothesis Test Assistance in Minitab

At the end of the last unit, we noted that you could use the help menu in Minitab to look up information about all of the functions and read in-depth instructions and summaries on various types of tests. You can also use the Minitab Assistant to help you choose a hypothesis test – including a 1-Way ANOVA – and run that test.
Select Assistant > Hypothesis Tests.

You can have Minitab help you choose a test to compare one sample with a target, two samples with each other, or more than two samples. Minitab will apply the same information included in this chapter and the previous chapter on hypothesis testing to help you make this choice, but this is a good tool to
use if you get confused, don’t remember which test to choose, or think you might need to use a less common test that wasn’t covered in detail in this book.
If you click “Help me choose” under the option for comparing more than two samples, Minitab presents you with a second diagram.

If you are comparing the means for more than two samples of continuous data, you would use the 1- Way ANOVA described in this chapter. If you are comparing standard deviations, however, you use a different test. If you are comparing attributed data, then you would use the chi-square tests. You can hover over any of the tests and click and Minitab will open the dialogue box for performing that test.
Minitab offers several assistant wizards. For example, below is a screenshot of the graphical analysis assistant menu.

Here, Minitab helps you choose a graph or visual analysis that best matches your data and purposes. You can use the assistant tool for help with regression analysis, measurement system analysis, control charts, and design of experiments, which will be covered in the next chapter.

rows: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。
zh.text: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。
方差分析（ANOVA），通常称为方差分析（ANOVA），是一种处理两个以上总体或 X 因子的假设检验。如果您还记得第 21 章，我们讨论了处理 x 的单个因子或 x 的两个因子的各种假设检验。例如，您学习了如何进行假设检验以确定样本平均值在统计上是否等于目标平均值。出于同样的原因，您还学会了比较两组数据的均值。方差分析（ANOVA）允许您使用两组以上的数据执行此检验。

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。

rows: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
zh.text: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
当您同时拥有离散变量和连续变量时，通常会使用方差分析（ANOVA）– 如果独立方差是离散的 – 例如员工列表 – 并且响应变量是连续的 – 错误列表或工作时间长度 – 方差分析（ANOVA）可以是一个有价值的分析工具。
在详细了解方差分析（ANOVA）之前，我们先回顾一下用于测试手段的其他工具。

- 当测试一个均值时（通常针对目标），您将使用 Z 检验或 T 检验。请记住，当您不知道标准差是多少时，Z 检验通常用于测试大样本。 Z 测试通常提供“实际情况”，让您收集一些信息，以便您可以与其他测试一起运行更准确的分析。 - 当测试两种均值时（涉及将两个样本相互比较），您将使用 2 个样本 T 检验或配对 T 检验。 - 当测试三个或更多均值时，您可以使用称为单向方差分析（ANOVA）的特定方差分析（ANOVA）测试。

rows: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
zh.text: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
为了更好地理解这些名称，请考虑下面的实际应用。
六西格玛团队正在努力减少制造过程中的处理时间。该团队获得了该流程的历史基线指标。具体来说，在上个季度，每个输出的流程平均需要 35.8 分钟。经过定义、测量和分析阶段后，团队进行了改进，他们认为这将减少每个输出的平均时间。在改进中试点变革后，团队采取了新的衡量标准。为了将新样本与历史基线进行比较，团队将使用 1 样本 T 检验。

rows: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
zh.text: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
在另一个场景中，六西格玛团队正在努力增加呼叫中心团队在一天内可以处理的呼叫数量。呼叫中心管理层一再要求增加员工，但执行领导层希望了解提高效率是否可以在不增加员工的情况下提高产量。在 DMAIC 流程的前半部分完成后，六西格玛团队已准备好可能的解决方案。为了验证这些解决方案，团队决定在一半的呼叫中心实施更改。在一个月的时间里，团队衡量了呼叫中心两部分的绩效。月底团队有两组数据想要解答

rows: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
zh.text: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
问题：改变组的平均产量是否大于未改变组的平均产量？在这种情况下，团队将使用 2 样本 T 检验。
上面的两个例子与假设检验章节中使用的许多例子一致。考虑下面的场景，它略有不同。
一家连锁餐厅想知道其品牌推广和面向客户的活动是否在所有地点均等地发挥作用。具体来说，该公司决定查看每个餐厅地点的客户满意度得分。该连锁店包括五个地点。满意度分数是通过电话、网络和书面调查在每个地点收集的。该公司每周平均得分并报告这些数字。几个月后，公司有了五组平均值。如果公司想知道任何地点的分数是否与其他地点的分数存在统计差异，那么正确的测试是单向方差分析（ANOVA）。
准备单向方差分析（ANOVA）
六西格玛专家进行单向方差分析（ANOVA）的方式与他或她进行任何其他假设检验的方式非常相似。首先，你从一个现实世界的问题开始——一个实际的或商业的问题。在上面的例子中，问题是不同餐厅地点的顾客满意度是否存在差异。
与任何假设检验一样，需要原假设和备择假设。原假设是样本均值没有差异。
另一种假设是至少其中一个平均值在统计上不相同。
在陈述原假设和备择假设后，六西格玛专家应验证模型中的任何假设是否正确。这包括关于误差的假设以及关于方差分析（ANOVA）模型的一些基本假设。
首先，用于方差分析（ANOVA）的样本必须是随机选择的。请记住，这始终是推论统计的必要假设。
在确保随机选择样本后，六西格玛专家必须在运行单向方差分析（ANOVA）检验之前验证有关数据的其他六个假设。
1. 因变量或结果本质上必须是连续的。这意味着它是一个比率或一个区间。在前面的示例中，因变量包括客户满意度评分（数字）、每天处理的呼叫数量以及周期

特定输出的时间。所有这些都是连续数据的例子。任何可以用时间、温度、英尺和英寸（或厘米和米）、金钱或比率来衡量的东西本质上通常都是连续的。 2.自变量列表包含两个或多个不相关的组或类别。 A、B、C 队是三个自变量。如果您正在衡量五名不同员工的绩效，那么您可能有五个自变量。在有关餐馆的示例中，五家不同的餐馆是自变量。分类变量可以包括人员、时间、轮班、团队、部门、地点、各种人口群体（年龄、性别、种族）或职业。 3. 观察是独立的。首先，您必须确保组内不存在依赖关系。如果您正在衡量一个部门中三个不同团队的绩效，但有多名员工在多个团队中工作，那么结果并不完全独立。其次，您必须确保组内的观察结果不相互依赖。 4. 您的数据中不存在显着的异常值。异常值会降低单向方差分析（ANOVA）的准确性，但您不必因为单个可解释的异常值而放弃整个测试。作为六西格玛专家，你必须检查你的数据，调查异常值，并适当地丢弃它们。例如，如果六西格玛专家正在检查客户电子邮件的响应时间，他或她可能会检查来自九个不同员工的数据样本。 一名员工的数据包括三个异常值，其中电子邮件的回复时间比所有其他样本显示的时间要长得多。六西格玛专家可能会对此进行调查，并指出该员工正在休短期病假，这会扭曲结果。六西格玛专家可以从他或她的计算中删除这三个数据点，但重要的是要注意这些异常值。事实上，这个特定问题可能发生对于整个流程的改进仍然很重要。六西格玛团队可能会建议进行流程更改来解决此问题，但他们可能会分析减去这些异常值的数据以得出其他结论。 5. 因变量（如上面第 1 项所述）应呈正态分布或近似于每组内的正态曲线。在上面的示例中，六西格玛团队收集了 9 名员工的数据。在这九个数据集中，数据应呈正态分布。 6. 每个集合内的方差必须在统计上等于其他集合的方差——也称为方差同质性。等方差检验可以在 Minitab 中运行，本章稍后将介绍。
在验证假设并建立假设后，六西格玛专家可以手动或使用统计分析软件运行单向方差分析（ANOVA）。在本章中，我们将使用 Minitab 来运行测试。使用统计分析软件时，您通常会花费比运行测试本身更多的时间来验证某些假设。
运行单向方差分析（ANOVA）
要了解如何验证所有假设、运行单向方差分析（ANOVA）并解释结果，请考虑一个现实世界的示例。
一家邮购图书公司希望提高客户对递送的满意度。负责该流程的六西格玛团队已确定包装材料可能是造成负面客户的因素
满意度分数。该邮购公司目前通过纸板箱运送书籍，没有其他保护，该团队认为，这导致货物在到达客户之前或客户打开包裹时造成的损坏增加。
为了测试这一理论并试验两个提出的解决方案，该团队实施了额外的包装选项。有些书籍将采用常规包装运输——即没有任何其他材料的盒子。 其他的将装在装有花生的盒子中运输，还有一些则装在带衬垫的信封中。该团队在三个不同的运输站实施不同的包装方法，并根据 1 到 10 的等级记录每批货物的总体客户满意度评分，其中 10 表示最满意。该数据如下所示。
原创
包装
带衬垫
信封
要遵循本示例的单向方差分析（ANOVA），请将上述数据复制到 Minitab 中。
验证假设：
因变量是连续的吗？
是的，它是以区间形式测量的。
自变量是否由两个或多个不相关的组组成？
是的，自变量是这本书的包装方式。
观察有独立性吗？
是的，这三种打包方式没有任何关系，也不组合。例如，团队不会将一本书装在带衬垫的信封中，然后将其放入盒子中。
这里值得注意的是，团队在三个不同的站点实施了各种包装。对于团队来说，最好确保包装站得到相同的处理，以防止任何其他因素影响试点结果。
数据中是否存在显着的异常值？
图形分析是检查异常值的简单方法。箱线图（也称为箱线图和耳语图）是快速检查异常值的好方法。第 6 单元介绍了 Minitab 中的箱线图，但您现在可以对上面的数据运行箱线图。
1. 单击图形 > 箱线图 2. 选择多个 Y 3. 单击确定 4. 将所有三个数据列选择到图形变量框中 5. 单击确定。
Minitab 返回上面的图表，其中显示三组数据中的任何一组都没有异常值。如果记录客户满意度分数的人犯了一个错误，为一批货物输入了数字 20，该怎么办？箱线图分析发生变化。
在上图中，您可以看到花生包装盒上方高处有一个离群点。 六西格玛专家审阅此图时会看到该异常值，并意识到这是一个测量错误 - 请记住，客户满意度的等级只能达到 10。因为对异常值有解释，所以可以将其删除。
各组数据是否正常？
测试每组数据正态性的最快方法可能是在 Minitab 中运行图形分析。第 6 单元也对此进行了介绍。
1. 选择统计 > 基本统计 > 图形摘要
2. 在变量框中选择第一组数据的列。 3. 确保置信水平设置为 95.0。 4. 单击“确定”。
Minitab 返回以下图表和数据。
原包装汇总报告
Anderson-Darling 正态性检验的 p 值为 0.405。请记住，在检验正态性时，原假设是数据与正态曲线之间没有差异。由于 p 值高于我们的 alpha 水平 (0.05)，因此我们无法拒绝零假设并接受数据是正态的。

rows: 术语说明 p-value（p 值）：在原假设成立时观察到当前或更极端结果的概率；常用于判断显著性。
zh.text: 术语说明 p-value（p 值）：在原假设成立时观察到当前或更极端结果的概率；常用于判断显著性。
下面包括为其他两组数据生成的图表。您可以看到每个 p 值也允许我们接受原假设并验证该假设。是的，每组的数据近似正态分布。

rows: 术语说明 Normal Distribution（正态分布）：常见钟形分布，许多统计推断方法以其为基础。
zh.text: 术语说明 Normal Distribution（正态分布）：常见钟形分布，许多统计推断方法以其为基础。
软垫信封总结报告
包装花生总结报告
方差是否具有同质性？各组之间的方差是否相对相等？
Minitab 包含一个用于检验等方差的选项，该选项提供两个不同的 p 值。一种是多重比较检验的 p 值，一种是 Levene 检验的 p 值。当每组内的数据正常时，Levene 检验通常用于检验相等假设。之前第 6 单元中未涵盖此测试。
1. 选择统计 > 方差分析（ANOVA）> 等方差检验
2. 在下拉框中，选择“响应数据位于每个因子水平的单独列中”。
3. 选择“响应”框中的所有数据列。
4. 单击“确定”。
Minitab 返回所有数据列的区间图以及两个统计值。
对于等方差检验，您通常关心 Levene 检验的 p 值。 在本例中，它高于 0.05（Minitab 的默认 alpha 设置），因此您无法拒绝原假设，即数据之间的方差没有差异。在这种情况下，即使查看区间图也能帮助您做出决定。范围当然不同，但间隔的长度并没有太大差异。
要考虑对比示例，请将以下数据表复制到 Minitab 中。
仅使用上表中的两列数据在方差分析（ANOVA）菜单下运行等方差检验。
您很快就会发现这两组数据的方差并不相等。首先，间隔使其相当明显。 B 组的方差要小得多。 Levene 检验的 p 值远小于 alpha 值，这意味着您拒绝原假设并接受方差存在差异的备择假设。
您可以通过选择“等方差检验”对话框中的“选项”来更改检验的 alpha 值。
您可以通过从“等方差检验”对话框中选择“图形”来更改显示的图形类型。
以下是通过箱线图绘制的原始三组数据。
数据
运行单向方差分析（ANOVA）测试
现在所有假设均已验证，您可以运行单向方差分析（ANOVA）检验。
选择统计 > 方差分析（ANOVA）> 单向。
选择响应数据位于单独列中的下拉选项。
将所有三个数据列（原始包装、填充信封和包装花生的数据）选择到“响应”框中。
如果需要，您可以单击选项来设置置信区间或确保其仍设置为 95%。然后单击“确定”。
单击主对话框上的“确定”。 Minitab 将执行单向方差分析（ANOVA）计算。根据运行此测试时列中的数据量，可能需要几秒钟的时间。
Minitab 默认返回两件事：数据的图形分析和会话窗口中的方差分析（ANOVA）结果。
会话窗口中的数据包含在上面。突出显示的是假设和 p 值。原假设是所有均值相等。 另一种方法是至少其中一个平均值在统计上是不同的。
p 值小于 alpha 值 0.05，这意味着您拒绝原假设并接受至少一个均值不同的备择假设。

从商业角度来说，六西格玛团队现在知道“其中一个与另一个不同”。这可能会促使他们进行额外的分析，或者表明一个或多个流程可能存在问题。如果团队比较同一流程中不同团队之间的平均生产时间，他们可能会假设这些团队将在大致相同的时间内完成工作。如果单向方差分析（ANOVA）表明情况并非如此，那么六西格玛团队就可以问自己：是什么导致结果在统计上有所不同？有没有一个团队比其他人做得更好？如果是这样，六西格玛团队能否将该组的解决方案应用于其他组？有没有一个团队表现比其他团队差？如果是这样，该团队的情况如何以及如何改进？
如果方差不相等怎么办？
如果您无法验证关于子组之间方差相等的第六个假设，您仍然可以在 Minitab 中运行单向方差分析（ANOVA）检验。 Minitab 使用不同的统计计算（称为 Welch 检验）在这种情况下提供 p 值。
使用我们之前发现的方差不等的数据在 Minitab 中运行此检验。
选择统计 > 方差分析（ANOVA）> 单向。
选择响应数据位于单独列中的下拉选项。
在“响应”框中选择 A 和 B 的数据列。
选择选项。
取消选中假设方差相等的框。
单击“确定”。再次单击“确定”。
与前面的示例一样，Minitab 在会话窗口中生成图形分析和测试数据。
您可以从 Minitab 结果中看到，未假设该数据具有相等方差。您还有一个实际上为 0 的 p 值，这意味着您拒绝原假设并接受其中一个均值在统计上不同的替代方案：我们之前通过查看此数据的图形解释已经做到了这一点。 如前所述，以图形方式查看数据时，数据并不总是看起来如此不同，这通常需要统计分析。
Minitab 中的假设检验辅助
在上一个单元结束时，我们注意到您可以使用 Minitab 中的帮助菜单来查找有关所有函数的信息，并阅读有关各种类型测试的深入说明和摘要。您还可以使用 Minitab Assistant 帮助您选择假设检验（包括单向方差分析（ANOVA））并运行该检验。
选择助手 > 假设检验。
您可以让 Minitab 帮助您选择一种检验来比较一个样本与目标、两个样本之间或两个以上样本。 Minitab 将应用本章和上一章有关假设检验的相同信息来帮助您做出选择，但这是一个很好的工具
如果您感到困惑，不记得要选择哪个测试，或者认为您可能需要使用本书中未详细介绍的不太常见的测试，请使用该测试。
如果您单击用于比较两个以上样本的选项下的“帮助我选择”，Minitab 会向您显示第二个图表。
如果您要比较两个以上连续数据样本的均值，您将使用本章中描述的单向方差分析（ANOVA）。但是，如果您要比较标准差，则需要使用不同的检验。如果您要比较归因数据，那么您将使用卡方检验。您可以将鼠标悬停在任何测试上并单击，Minitab 将打开用于执行该测试的对话框。
Minitab 提供了多个辅助向导。例如，下面是图形分析助手菜单的屏幕截图。
在这里，Minitab 可以帮助您选择最适合您的数据和目的的图形或可视化分析。您可以使用辅助工具来帮助进行回归分析、测量系统分析、控制图和实验设计，这些将在下一章中介绍。

rows: 术语说明 MSA（测量系统分析）：评估测量工具、人员和方法是否足以支持可靠决策。
zh.text: 术语说明 MSA（测量系统分析）：评估测量工具、人员和方法是否足以支持可靠决策。

rows: 术语说明 Control Chart（控制图）：用时间序列和控制界限判断过程是否处于受控状态。
zh.text: 术语说明 Control Chart（控制图）：用时间序列和控制界限判断过程是否处于受控状态。

rows: 术语说明 Regression（回归）：用模型估计变量之间的关系，并预测响应变量。
zh.text: 术语说明 Regression（回归）：用模型估计变量之间的关系，并预测响应变量。

en.text: In the past few chapters, you’ve learned how to analyze various types of data, conduct hypothesis tests, and draw conclusions from those tests. But simply analyzing data – no matter how accurate you can be – won’t help you bring about improvements to the process. It can be easy to get lost in the analysis and forget about the need to make improvements, but continuous improvement is the very heart of the Six Sigma and DMAIC methodology.

Experiments are one way that Six Sigma experts and teams bring analysis and improvements together. Douglas C. Montgomery, author of Design and Analysis of Experiments provides a definition for the type of experiment we talk about in relation to a Six Sigma project.
An experiment is a test or series of tests in which purposeful changes are made to input variables of a process or system so that changes in the output responses can be observed and identified.

The fundamental concept of design of experiments, or DOE, is that all processes can be ultimately distilled to the Y = f(x) equation. There are some factors, x, that work together to create some output, Y. The job of the Six Sigma team, as has been stated throughout this book, is to find out what factors (x) actually have an impact on Y and how changes in those factors will change the outcome.

One way teams can achieve this is to run a designed experiment. This involves making purposeful changes in one or more of the input variables – Xs – and then measuring and analyzing what occurs with the output, Y. Designed experiments have to be controlled and well-documented or improper conclusions can be drawn. The process for planning and running such experiments will be covered in this chapter, and you’ll probably see similarities between DOE and the scientific method that most learn in primary school.

rows: 术语说明 DOE（实验设计）：系统安排实验以识别因素、水平和交互作用对结果的影响。
zh.text: 术语说明 DOE（实验设计）：系统安排实验以识别因素、水平和交互作用对结果的影响。
在过去的几章中，您学习了如何分析各种类型的数据、进行假设检验并从这些检验中得出结论。但仅仅分析数据——无论你有多准确——都无法帮助你改进流程。人们很容易在分析中迷失方向，忘记需要改进，但持续改进是六西格玛和 DMAIC 方法论的核心。

rows: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
zh.text: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
实验是六西格玛专家和团队将分析和改进结合起来的一种方式。 《实验设计与分析》一书的作者道格拉斯·蒙哥马利 (Douglas C. Montgomery) 为我们谈论的与六西格玛项目相关的实验类型提供了定义。
实验是一种测试或一系列测试，其中对过程或系统的输入变量进行有目的的更改，以便可以观察和识别输出响应的变化。
实验设计 (DOE) 的基本概念是所有过程最终都可以归结为 Y = f(x) 方程。有一些因素 x 共同作用，创造出一些输出 Y。正如本书中所述，六西格玛团队的工作是找出哪些因素 (x) 实际上对 Y 有影响，以及这些因素的变化将如何改变结果。
团队实现这一目标的一种方法是进行设计好的实验。这涉及到对一个或多个输入变量（Xs）进行有目的的更改，然后测量和分析输出 Y 发生的情况。设计的实验必须受到控制，并有详细记录，否则可能会得出不正确的结论。本章将介绍规划和运行此类实验的过程，您可能会发现能源部与大多数人在小学学习的科学方法之间的相似之处。
何时可以对现有数据进行分析

title.en: When Analysis Can Occur on Existing Data
title.zh: 何时可以对现有数据进行分析

en.text: Sometimes, enough data exists or enough change exists within the current process that a Six Sigma team can make decisions about factors without running a designed experiment. In these cases, the data is already available or can be captured in a way to isolate various inputs so that a Six Sigma team can run analysis and hypothesis tests without recreating physical scenarios. For example, in an agricultural setting, a company might already be using various types of planting processes to grow the same crop. Teams could gather data about each type of planting process, ensure no other variables exist between those processes, and then draw valid conclusions about how the planting processes impact the harvest outcomes.

There are benefits and disadvantages to analyzing existing data rather than running a designed experiment. The first benefit is that basing primary analysis and initial solution selection on existing data typically reduces the timeline for the entire DMAIC process. A team might still have to pilot a solution, measuring and analyzing the results to ensure they are in keeping with initial predictions, but they don’t

have to implement a designed experiment to make those first predictions. Second, basing decisions on existing data or processes can reduce the overall cost of a DMAIC project. This is especially true in processes that involve machines or parts that would have to be retooled to create a change in some inputs. In some cases, it simply isn’t possible to make such changes solely so a team can gather information upon which to draw such conclusions, and the Six Sigma expert must use other tools to analyze the process so the team can draw conclusions and suggest improvements for a more formal pilot or test.

The disadvantage of using existing data is that it isn’t always clean. You can use the existing data in a process when a process includes multiple changes to a factor. You would even run the same analysis that you might run if you made those changes yourself as part of an experiment. The problem is that you don’t have control over the other variables in the process. For example, a Six Sigma team might be working on how document printing is managed in a large law firm. The team wants to know if the copy- print station to which documents are sent impacts the speed with which the jobs are processed. The copy and print machines have internal logs that record when a job is received and when it finished printing, so the team can compare the speed of the process from all the stations using ANOVA or DOE analysis without actually designing and running an experiment. The team could simply access the historical data for the process from last week – or any time period.

What isn’t recorded for the copy-print process are a variety of external factors including how many staff were working at a given time, whether machines experience maintenance needs or paper jams, and whether staff were interrupting printing jobs to handle copying, scanning, or faxing jobs at the same machines. While the historical data might provide some valuable information about the difference in productivity for each machine, the team might not be able to rule out some of these other factors as causation without a designed experiment that captures all the possibly relevant data.
Why Run an Experiment?

A well-designed experiment helps you understand what variables provide the most influence on the process output. This helps a Six Sigma team understand where time, money, and other resources should be spent. In the example above, the experiment might help the team rule out external factors and understand if the type or location of the copy-print station is a major factor in productivity.

Another reason to run an experiment is that it can help you understand how to set inputs to create the best possible outcome. In one of the earliest chapters of this book, we used the example of a manufacturing process that makes chocolate bars. Each of the inputs – the ingredient amounts, the mixing time, and the mixing temperature – has to be set at the right level to create an optimal product. A Six Sigma team working on that process might conduct experiments to test various levels of inputs to find the right combination for the desired output.

Sometimes, Six Sigma teams want to understand how to control or set inputs that are simply influential to a process but don’t necessarily directly feed the outcome. In the chocolate bar example, if the team has found optimal settings for the ingredients, time, and mixing temperature, and they still see variation in the outcome of the process, they might then consider influential inputs. Does the temperature in the room influence the process of mixing and setting the chocolate? If the team decides it might, they could conduct an experiment to test various room temperatures to find the right level for the best process outcome.

Finally, every input is not within a Six Sigma team or process owner’s control. You can control the temperature in a climate-controlled room, but you can’t control the temperature outside, for example. Experiments can help teams understand how to set all the inputs they can control to reduce any impact on variation and outcome from the inputs that cannot be controlled.

zh.text: 有时，当前流程中存在足够的数据或存在足够的变化，六西格玛团队可以在不进行设计实验的情况下就因素做出决策。在这些情况下，数据已经可用，或者可以通过隔离各种输入的方式捕获数据，以便六西格玛团队可以运行分析和假设检验，而无需重新创建物理场景。例如，在农业环境中，公司可能已经在使用各种类型的种植过程来种植相同的作物。 团队可以收集有关每种种植过程的数据，确保这些过程之间不存在其他变量，然后就种植过程如何影响收获结果得出有效的结论。
分析现有数据而不是运行设计的实验有好处也有坏处。第一个好处是，基于现有数据进行初步分析和初始解决方案选择通常会缩短整个 DMAIC 流程的时间。团队可能仍然需要试验解决方案，测量和分析结果以确保它们与最初的预测一致，但他们不需要
必须实施一个设计好的实验来做出这些初步预测。其次，基于现有数据或流程的决策可以降低 DMAIC 项目的总体成本。在涉及机器或零件的流程中尤其如此，必须重新装备这些机器或零件才能改变某些输入。在某些情况下，仅仅进行此类更改是不可能的，以便团队可以收集信息来得出此类结论，六西格玛专家必须使用其他工具来分析流程，以便团队可以得出结论并为更正式的试点或测试提出改进建议。

使用现有数据的缺点是它并不总是干净的。当流程包含对某个因素的多项更改时，您可以使用流程中的现有数据。您甚至可以运行与您在实验中自己进行这些更改时可能运行的分析相同的分析。问题是您无法控制流程中的其他变量。例如，六西格玛团队可能正在研究如何在大型律师事务所中管理文档打印。该团队想知道发送文档的复印打印站是否会影响作业的处理速度。复印机和打印机具有内部日志，记录何时收到作业以及何时完成打印，因此团队可以使用方差分析（ANOVA）或 DOE 分析来比较所有工作站的流程速度，而无需实际设计和运行实验。该团队可以简单地访问上周或任何时间段的流程历史数据。

rows: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。
zh.text: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。
复印打印过程中没有记录各种外部因素，包括在给定时间有多少员工在工作、机器是否需要维护或卡纸，以及员工是否中断打印作业以在同一台机器上处理复印、扫描或传真作业。虽然历史数据可能提供一些有关每台机器生产率差异的有价值的信息，但如果没有设计实验来捕获所有可能相关的数据，团队可能无法排除其中一些其他因素作为因果关系。
为什么要进行实验？
精心设计的实验可以帮助您了解哪些变量对过程输出影响最大。这有助于六西格玛团队了解时间、金钱和其他资源应该花在哪里。在上面的示例中，实验可能有助于团队排除外部因素，并了解复印打印站的类型或位置是否是生产力的主要因素。
进行实验的另一个原因是它可以帮助您了解如何设置输入以创造最佳结果。在本书最早的章节之一中，我们使用了巧克力棒制造过程的示例。每个输入（成分量、混合时间和混合温度）都必须设置在正确的水平，才能创造出最佳的产品。负责该流程的六西格玛团队可能会进行实验来测试不同级别的输入，以找到所需输出的正确组合。
有时，六西格玛团队想要了解如何控制或设置仅对流程有影响但不一定直接产生结果的输入。在巧克力棒的例子中，如果团队找到了成分、时间和混合温度的最佳设置，并且他们仍然看到过程结果的变化，那么他们可能会考虑有影响力的输入。房间温度是否会影响巧克力的混合和凝固过程？如果团队决定这样做，他们可以进行实验来测试各种室温，以找到最佳工艺结果的合适水平。
最后，每个输入都不在六西格玛团队或流程所有者的控制范围内。例如，您可以控制气候控制房间内的温度，但无法控制室外温度。 实验可以帮助团队了解如何设置他们可以控制的所有输入，以减少无法控制的输入对变化和结果的影响。

title.zh: 最佳猜测试错法与阶乘实验

en.text: A designed experiment is just that – it is designed statistically to test a variety of factors. Although a designed experiment does have, at its very basis, the idea of trial-and-error, teams that use design of experiment are not simply taking a best guess approach to the matter. A best-guess trial-and-error approach is actually not efficient in most cases, although some people might think so.

To better understand the different between a designed experiment and a best-guess approach, consider a real-word application. A team is working to improve a process for a regional furniture warehouse that delivers furniture to retail stores based on weekly orders from the stores. The team wants to reduce the average delivery turnaround by 24 hours and does have the baseline measurement – the average turnaround time for orders is currently 72 hours.

Using a best-guess approach, the team would identify what it thinks are the combination of inputs that are most likely to impact delivery times. The team might decide that factors include the delivery driver, the dispatcher who provides instructions and directions, whether the driver has a GPS in the truck, and how many pieces of furniture are being delivered. To find out which factor most contributes to the delivery time or discover which factors can be changed to decrease average times, the team would then have to run a series of trial-and-error tests.

The team sets the factors and runs a scenario. If the delivery time is greater than 48 hours, the result is not as desired. The team changes one of the factors and runs the test again. The team has to repeat the process until it finds a magic combination. Not only is this inefficient, but it uses an approach called the OFAT Approach, or One-Factor-At-a-Time approach. Only one input setting is changed for each trial and a measurement is taken. The measurements are compared to make decisions about each factor. In the furniture example, the team might note that delivery times are faster when a GPS is on board the truck. The might also note that deliver times are faster when a more experienced driver is on board the truck, regardless of the inclusion of a GPS.

One of the major problems with testing one factor at a time—other than inefficiency--is that Six Sigma experts don’t gain a true understanding of the interaction between changes. Yes, the presence of a GPS increases efficiency. Yes, the presence of an experienced driver increases efficiency. But what happens when both a GPS and an experienced driver are on board a truck? Does efficiency increase at some exponential level, or does it simply increase to the maximum amount of either the experience-related increase or the GPS-related increase? Likewise, what happens when a negative factor is coupled with a positive factor? If delivery times increase with each item added to the order, what happens to the impact of the experience of the driver on efficiency? One-factor-at-a-time approaches don’t usually answer these questions.
Why Do People Think This is a Good Approach?
Based on the scenario above, you can see that the best-guess approach is cumbersome and time consuming. The problem is, many people within an organization often favor this approach because it has

worked well before. If you are very familiar with a process, you might be able to get a good result within the first few guesses. In our furniture moving example, you don’t even have to be extremely familiar with the process to guess that having a GPS on board each delivery truck is likely to reduce delivery times or that it’s going to take longer to deliver more furniture.

Because of a tendency to favor a best-guess approach, Six Sigma experts might have to work to explain the importance of using statistical analysis to help with problem solving. It’s important for teams to understand that a best guess approach does usually lead to more trials and tests than are necessary, which creates waste, or muda. Yes, teams might get lucky, especially if someone on the team or a subject-matter-expert being utilized by the team is extremely familiar with the process and, thus, understands how the inputs impact the outcomes at a granular level. However, that familiarity with the process can create the opposite impact. The person might be so close to the process and so ingrained with the traditional way of doing things that he or she cannot make valuable best guesses.

Even if the first guess regarding changes to the inputs yields a result that is within the parameters the team was looking for, it might not be the best possible guess. The team in the delivery example wanted to reduce deliver times by an average of 24 hours. Based on historical data, any combination of inputs that averages 48 hours or less would achieve this goal. But what if there is a combination of inputs that averages 24 hours or less? Wouldn’t that be something valuable for the team to know? If the combination of inputs is viable to achieve, then the team could not only deliver on the original goal, but deliver well above the original goal. It’s not something teams should always set out to do, because setting the bar too high can have negative results on a project. But knowing that excellent results are possible is valuable because it lets the team make a more educated decision about the solution.
What is Factorial Experimentation?

The solution to all the problems of inefficiency and lackluster data described above is design of experiment, or factorial experimentation. Design of experiment is also called statistically designed experiments, and it involves running a series of experimental runs that vary all the input factors in an organized way. The information isn’t analyzed until you complete all the planned runs, which means you have all the data for all the changes to inputs at that time.

For example, consider the factors from the furniture delivery example again: number of pieces being delivered, experience of the driver, whether a GPS is on board, and the method of providing instruction or direction from the dispatcher. For this example, assume that each of these factors could be set with a low value and a high value as follows.
Experience of driver: 0 years, 1 year
GPS: No, Yes
Dispatcher: No policy, follows a policy
Number of pieces of furniture: 1, 5

In a grid, you would create a list of all possible runs. Each run creates a unique grouping of the possible factors, which means the number of runs you have is determined by the number of factors and possible settings that you have. The grid for the delivery factors is shown below.
Run Number
Driver Experience GPS Dispatcher Pieces of Furniture
1 0 years Yes Follows Procedure 1 piece
2 0 years Yes Follows Procedure 5 pieces
3 0 years Yes No Procedure 1 piece
4 0 years Yes No Procedure 5 pieces
5 0 years No Follows Procedure 1 piece
6 0 years No Follows Procedure 5 pieces
7 0 years No No Procedure 1 piece
8 0 years No No Procedure 5 pieces
9 1 year Yes Follows Procedure 1 piece
10 1 year Yes Follows Procedure 5 pieces
11 1 year Yes No Procedure 1 piece
12 1 year Yes No Procedure 5 pieces
13 1 year No Follows Procedure 1 piece
14 1 year No Follows Procedure 5 pieces
15 1 year No No Procedure 1 piece
16 1 year No No Procedure 5 pieces

This information is then used to generate a worksheet for running the experiment; once data is collected, a Six Sigma expert analyzes the outcome. Minitab actually has tools that help you create the worksheet and analyze the experiment outcome, which we’ll cover at the end of this chapter and in the next chapter.

Note that the above experiment is an example of a 2k factorial designed experiment. In 2k factorial design, you limit the factor levels to two: a high and low factor. The driver has less than a year (0 years) experience or 1 or more years of experience. The GPS is there or it isn’t.

Experiments that include more than two levels – more than a low and a high – for one or more factors are full factorial experiments. The examples in this chapter all deal with 2k factorials; examples of full factorials can be seen in chapter 30.

Step-by-Step Guide for Creating a Designed Experiment As with hypothesis testing, most experiments follow a road map of steps required to define the elements, set up data collection, and analyze the data collected. These steps are virtually the same for
any type of experiment, though you’ll see in chapter 30 that some requirements are slightly different when dealing with full factorials.
Step 1: What, in business or practical terms, is the problem?

As with anything in Six Sigma, you begin with a practical business consideration or issue. This isn’t necessarily the big problem statement that the team created in the Define phase for the overall project. It’s more likely to be a smaller issue within the larger problem. For example, a healthcare facility might want to address the rate of staph infections for admitted patients. This might be the overall project for the team: reduce the current rate of staph infections, which is 17 percent.

During the project, the team discovers that clinical staff are not always compliant with sanitation protocols when treating patients. For this particular situation, the practical problem might be stated as:
In a 10-point evaluation for sanitation compliance, clinical healthcare workers are averaging a score of 7.1. The hospital requires an average of at least 9.0.

The business problem or concern that the experiment is addressing should be related to the Six Sigma team’s overall problem statement. In this case, the lack of sanitation compliance could be linked to the fact that patients are getting staph infections.
Step 2: What is the objective of running a designed experiment?

Unlike some of the other analysis discussed in this book, designed experiments do often take the time and resources of someone besides the Six Sigma expert, which means there is a larger cost associated with DOE than there might be with graphical analysis or even hypothesis testing. Because of this, teams don’t want to run experiments without a detailed objective – a detailed understanding of why the experiment is necessary and what the team hopes to achieve via the analysis.
The objective is directly tied to the practical problem. In the healthcare example, the problem was:
In a 10-point evaluation for sanitation compliance, clinical healthcare workers are averaging a score of 7.1. The hospital requires an average of at least 9.0.
An objective might be written as:
Increase sanitation compliance scores from 7.1 to at least 9.0.

That increase would meet the requirements from the leadership team and, since the Six Sigma team believes sanitation is related to staph infections, would hopefully improve the overall problem. Understanding the amount of change the team wants to create in the process will be important in determining the sample size required for the DOE. The objective should be specific enough that the team can use it later to measure success and involve the scale of the change for sample size calculations.

It’s important to note that the objective of any given experiment within a Six Sigma project is not going to be the same as the overall project initiative. Instead, each experiment objective should be related to – or supportive of – the project objective. In this case, the team hopes that increasing sanitation compliance will help decrease staph infection rates.
Step 3: What is the appropriate Y for your experiment?

For a designed experiment, you almost always select the output response, or Y, first. The response needs to be related to the goals of the overall project; because there might be multiple output responses related to the project and the team’s experiment objective, the team should ask some questions about the response and possible associated inputs.

First, is the measurement system capable enough to support the experiment? You can test the measurement system with a gage analysis. The less capable a measurement system is, the bigger the sample size and more experimental runs you will need. If a measurement system’s capability is too poor, then the time or expense for an experiment can be prohibitive.

Second, what is important for the team to address? Does the team want to address variation within a process or address the center of the process? Is the purpose to bring variation down or to move the mean of the process up or down? In the case of the healthcare scenario provided above, the purpose is to increase the rate of compliance.

The team must also understand how the response might relate to other responses in the process or whether changes to one response will cause changes to another – particularly if an experiment is being conducted within the live environment of a process. This helps the team manage any downstream changes that could impact other processes or the customer.
The rate of compliance with the sanitation protocol is the Y for the Six Sigma healthcare team’s problem.
Step 4: What inputs will be studied?

In the DMAIC chapters, we covered some brainstorming tools for identifying inputs within a process, including the fishbone diagram. More brainstorming tools are covered in chapter 31. Teams can use such tools, along with process maps, to identify the inputs associated with the output decided upon in the previous step. Once you have a list of inputs, you can select the inputs that are going to be studied in the experiment. Note that not all the inputs to a process will be studied, and teams should choose inputs that are:
Believed to be most influential on the response (consider the Pareto, or 80/20, rule)

Are quantitative or qualitative in nature (it’s harder to work with attribute data due to sample size requirements, and involving too many people in the measurement system can also add variation, so keep these considerations in mind)
Can be set and controlled appropriately for the experiment

For the healthcare scenario, the team believes that several factors might contribute to a lack of compliance: education of the staff, the length of time a particular staff member has been on shift, and the number of patients on the floor at a given time.
Step 5: What are the factor levels for the experiment?

What levels will the input variables be set at for the purpose of the experiment? If you are running an experiment that involves baking cookies, then oven temperature is an input, or factor. Levels for the factor might be 325 degrees F, 350 degrees F, and 375
degrees F. Remember that in the case of a 2k factorial, each factor only has two levels.
In the healthcare example, the team decides to set each factor at a low and a high:
Staff education: Received annual compliance update training in the last 6 months versus has not received the training in the last six months
Time on shift: Less than or equal to 4 hours versus 5 or more hours
Number of patients on the floor: Below 80 percent of maximum capacity versus above 80 percent of maximum capacity

The levels set for each factor depend on a variety of considerations. If the team is performing a screening experiment – which means they only want to test input impact on outputs or better understand the process – they would set levels at wide intervals. For optimization, the team likely already has an understanding of the input impact on the process; the team might already have conducted analysis or experiments, and that information helps them set the levels appropriately at smaller intervals.

The process itself will dictate how some levels are set. If a Six Sigma team is working with a baking process, for example, it can’t set the temperature any lower than 0 degrees and most ovens also have a maximum temperature range. Teams also probably know the temperature around which baked goods will still be raw, so they are unlikely to set temperatures at something like 100 degrees F. Safety should always be a concern when setting levels. An oven might heat to 550 degrees F maximum, but is it safe to attempt to bake cookies at that level?

Finally, the levels should correlate with the type of information the team is looking for and the size of change the team wants to see. If a team is working with a manufacturing process that cuts lumber, it likely wants to see changes at small increments. Setting tool levels several feet apart isn’t going to show the change the team wants to see if they are working with the cutting process for 2-by-4 lumber piece that are to be cut into 6-inch pieces.
Step 6: What is the appropriate sample size?

In the case of the design of experiment, a sample size isn’t the number of data elements you collect but the number of times you replicate the experiment. The more replications, the greater the accuracy of the statistics, but each run in the DOE takes time and possibly other resources. Six Sigma experts have to balance the use of resources with the need for strong experiment conclusions. When you create a design of experiment in Minitab, the software program generates some predictions about the experiment even before you run the experiment, which lets you decide on size and replication before you begin. Specifically, Minitab tells you the size of effect, as measured in standard deviation, that you are 80 percent likely to capture with the experiment as you have designed it.
Step-by-Step Guide to Running a 2k Factorial Experiment in Minitab

To understand more about sample size and replication – and how to run a 2k factorial experiment in Minitab, begin with the Assistant menu. You can also access design of experiments under Stat > DOE, but the assistant wizard is helpful because it reminds you of all the steps previously discussed in this chapter, helps you decide on the proper type of experiment, and helps you create the data collection form and analysis.
For the purpose of setting up this experiment, use the healthcare example discussed above.
Problem: In a 10-point evaluation for sanitation compliance, clinical healthcare workers are averaging a score of 7.1. The hospital requires an average of at least 9.0.
Objective: Increase compliance scores to at least 9.0.
Response: Rate of compliance with sanitation protocols
Factors: Staff education, time on shift, number of patients on floor
Levels:
Factor Low (-1) High (1)
Staff education Didn’t receive compliance training in last 6 months
Did receive compliance training in last 6 months
Time on shift Less than or equal to 4 hours 5 or more hours
Number of patients on floor Below 80 percent maximum capacity
At or above 80 percent maximum capacity
Select Assistant > DOE > Plan and Create.
Select “Plan optimization experiment” since the team is looking to improve, or optimize, the process.

Minitab provides some instructions on identifying factors, setting levels, and choosing sample size. This information is all valuable and worth reviewing, particularly if you are conducting a DOE when it has been a long time since you last did so. It is also all information that has been included in this chapter.

One good note from the above Minitab menu is that it’s a good idea to obtain approval from any stakeholders, project owners, or sponsors associated with the project or process. Key stakeholders should understand what will happen during the experiment and why it is necessary to the project. If resources are required, sponsors or other leaders should sign off as required by organization guidelines.

In the particular healthcare scenario being used as the example, running a standalone experiment isn’t an option. A Six Sigma team certainly isn’t going to give patients a staph infection just to find out about inputs. In this particular case, the team would need to use existing data that matched the experiment parameters. This is something that might be an option – or a requirement – for teams in any number of scenarios.
After reviewing any Minitab notes desired, click “Back.”
Select “Create Modeling Design”.
Minitab displays a Modeling Design dialogue box, requesting all the information needed to create an experiment. Enter the information for the healthcare scenario.

1. Name the response variable. The team is measuring compliance with the sanitation protocols, so name it “Compliance.” The name isn’t important to Minitab, so you can name the variable anything that will make sense to yourself or the Six Sigma team. 2. Choose an option for the response goal. Minitab lets you select minimize the response, maximize the response, or achieve a target. In this case, the team wants to improve compliance to a score of 9, but going above 9 would also be a positive outcome, so select “maximize the response.” 3. Enter the number of factors. Remember, factors are inputs, and the healthcare scenario identifies 3 inputs. 4. Enter names and information for the factors. Even though some of the factors are continuous in nature, since this is a 2k factorial experiment, we’ve divided them into categories. 5. Select a number of times to replicate the experiment (remember, this is relevant to sample size). For this purpose, select 2. 6. Ensure your dialogue box matches the one below and select OK.
Minitab offers you the option to print the data form to make it easier to collect data. For the example, select no.
Minitab generates three items. The first is a modeling design report card that provides next steps and tips for completing the experiment.
Create Modeling Design

rows: 术语说明 Factorial（因子实验）：同时考察多个因素及其组合影响的实验设计。
zh.text: 术语说明 Factorial（因子实验）：同时考察多个因素及其组合影响的实验设计。
设计好的实验就是这样的——它是为了测试各种因素而进行统计设计的。尽管设计的实验从根本上来说确实具有试错的思想，但使用实验设计的团队并不是简单地对问题采取最佳猜测方法。在大多数情况下，最佳猜测试错方法实际上效率不高，尽管有些人可能这么认为。
为了更好地理解设计的实验和最佳猜测方法之间的区别，请考虑一个真实的应用程序。一个团队正在努力改进区域家具仓库的流程，该仓库根据商店的每周订单向零售店运送家具。该团队希望将平均交货周转时间缩短 24 小时，并且确实有基准测量 – 目前订单的平均周转时间为 72 小时。

rows: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
zh.text: 术语说明 Baseline（基线）：改进前的当前表现水平，是后续比较改进效果的参照。
使用最佳猜测方法，团队将确定其认为最有可能影响交付时间的输入组合。团队可能会决定因素包括送货司机、提供指示和方向的调度员、司机的卡车上是否有 GPS，以及要运送多少件家具。为了找出哪个因素对交付时间影响最大​​，或者发现可以改变哪些因素来减少平均时间，团队必须进行一系列的试错测试。

团队设定因素并运行场景。如果交货时间超过48小时，结果将达不到预期。团队更改了其中一个因素并再次运行测试。团队必须重复这个过程，直到找到神奇的组合。这不仅效率低下，而且使用了一种称为“OFAT 方法”或“一次单因素方法”的方法。每次试验仅更改一个输入设置并进行测量。对测量结果进行比较以针对每个因素做出决策。在家具示例中，团队可能会注意到，当卡车上装有 GPS 时，交货时间会更快。他们可能还会注意到，如果卡车上有经验丰富的司机，无论是否配备 GPS，送货时间都会更快。

一次测试一个因素的主要问题之一（除了效率低下之外）是六西格玛专家无法真正理解变化之间的相互作用。是的，GPS 的存在提高了效率。是的，经验丰富的司机的存在可以提高效率。但是，当卡车上同时配备 GPS 和经验丰富的驾驶员时，会发生什么呢？效率是否以某种指数级增长，或者只是增长到与体验相关的增长或与 GPS 相关的增长的最大量？同样，当负面因素与正面因素结合时会发生什么？如果订单中添加的每个商品的交付时间都会增加，那么司机的体验对效率的影响会怎样？一次单一因素的方法通常不能回答这些问题。

rows: 术语说明 Interaction（交互作用）：一个因素的效果会随另一个因素水平不同而变化。
zh.text: 术语说明 Interaction（交互作用）：一个因素的效果会随另一个因素水平不同而变化。
为什么人们认为这是一个好方法？
根据上面的场景，您可以看到最佳猜测方法既麻烦又耗时。问题是，组织内的许多人通常喜欢这种方法，因为它具有
以前工作得很好。如果你对一个过程非常熟悉，你也许能够在最初的几次猜测中得到一个好的结果。在我们的家具运输示例中，您甚至不必非常熟悉该流程，就能猜测每辆送货卡车上安装 GPS 可能会减少送货时间，或者需要更长的时间才能运送更多家具。

由于倾向于采用最佳猜测方法，六西格玛专家可能必须努力解释使用统计分析来帮助解决问题的重要性。对于团队来说，重要的是要明白，最佳猜测方法通常会导致不必要的试验和测试，从而造成浪费或浪费。是的，团队可能会很幸运，特别是如果团队中的某个人或团队使用的主题专家非常熟悉该流程，从而了解输入如何在粒度上影响结果。然而，对流程的熟悉可能会产生相反的影响。这个人可能如此接近这个过程，并且如此根深蒂固地遵循传统的做事方式，以至于他或她无法做出有价值的最佳猜测。

即使关于输入更改的第一个猜测产生的结果在团队正在寻找的参数范围内，它也可能不是最好的猜测。 交付示例中的团队希望将交付时间平均缩短 24 小时。根据历史数据，任何平均 48 小时或更短的输入组合都可以实现此目标。但是，如果输入组合的平均时间为 24 小时或更短怎么办？这对于团队来说不是很有价值吗？如果投入的组合是可行的，那么团队不仅可以实现最初的目标，而且可以远远高于最初的目标。这不是团队应该总是去做的事情，因为将标准设置得太高会给项目带来负面结果。但知道可能取得优异的结果是很有价值的，因为它可以让团队对解决方案做出更明智的决策。
什么是析因实验？
解决上述所有低效和数据乏善可陈的问题的方法是实验设计或析因实验。实验设计也称为统计设计实验，它涉及运行一系列实验运行，以有组织的方式改变所有输入因素。在您完成所有计划运行之前，不会对信息进行分析，这意味着您拥有当时输入的所有更改的所有数据。
例如，再次考虑家具交付示例中的因素：交付的件数、驾驶员的经验、车上是否有 GPS 以及调度员提供指示或方向的方法。对于此示例，假设这些因素中的每一个都可以设置为低值和高值，如下所示。
司机经验：0年、1年
GPS： 否，是
调度员：无政策，遵循政策
家具数量: 1, 5
在网格中，您将创建所有可能运行的列表。每次运行都会创建一个唯一的可能因素分组，这意味着您拥有的运行次数由因素数量和您拥有的可能设置决定。交付系数的网格如下所示。

然后，该信息用于生成用于运行实验的工作表；收集数据后，六西格玛专家将对结果进行分析。 Minitab 实际上有一些工具可以帮助您创建工作表并分析实验结果，我们将在本章末尾和下一章中介绍这些工具。
请注意，上述实验是 2k 因子设计实验的示例。在 2k 因子设计中，您将因子级别限制为两个：高因子和低因子。驾驶员的经验不足一年（0 年）或 1 年或以上经验。 GPS 有或没有。
对于一个或多个因素包含两个以上水平（不止一个低水平和一个高水平）的实验是完全析因实验。本章的例子都涉及2k阶乘；完整阶乘的例子可以在第 30 章中看到。
创建设计实验的分步指南 与假设检验一样，大多数实验都遵循定义元素、设置数据收集和分析收集的数据所需的步骤路线图。这些步骤实际上是相同的

rows: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
zh.text: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
任何类型的实验，尽管你会在第 30 章中看到，在处理全阶乘时，一些要求略有不同。
第 1 步：从业务或实际角度来看，问题是什么？
与六西格玛中的任何事情一样，您从实际的业务考虑或问题开始。 这不一定是团队在整个项目的定义阶段创建的大问题陈述。它更有可能是大问题中的小问题。例如，医疗机构可​​能希望解决入院患者的葡萄球菌感染率问题。这可能是该团队的总体项目：降低目前 17% 的葡萄球菌感染率。
在项目过程中，团队发现临床工作人员在治疗患者时并不总是遵守卫生协议。对于这种特殊情况，实际问题可以表述为：
在卫生遵守情况的 10 分评价中，临床医护人员的平均得分为 7.1。医院要求平均分至少9.0。
实验要解决的业务问题或关注点应该与六西格玛团队的整体问题陈述相关。在这种情况下，缺乏卫生依从性可能与患者感染葡萄球菌有关。
第 2 步：运行设计实验的目的是什么？
与本书中讨论的其他一些分析不同，设计实验确实经常占用六西格玛专家之外的其他人的时间和资源，这意味着与图形分析甚至假设检验相比，与 DOE 相关的成本更大。因此，团队不想在没有详细目标的情况下进行实验——详细了解为什么实验是必要的以及团队希望通过分析实现什么目标。
目标与实际问题直接相关。在医疗保健示例中，问题是：
在卫生遵守情况的 10 分评价中，临床医护人员的平均得分为 7.1。医院要求平均分至少9.0。
目标可以写成：
将卫生合规性分数从 7.1 提高到至少 9.0。
这一增加将满足领导团队的要求，并且由于六西格玛团队认为卫生条件与葡萄球菌感染有关，因此有望改善整体问题。了解团队希望在此过程中创建的变更量对于确定 DOE 所需的样本量非常重要。 目标应该足够具体，以便团队稍后可以使用它来衡量成功，并涉及样本量计算的变化规模。

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
值得注意的是，六西格玛项目中任何特定实验的目标都不会与整个项目计划相同。相反，每个实验目标都应该与项目目标相关或支持项目目标。在这种情况下，该团队希望提高卫生合规性将有助于降低葡萄球菌感染率。
第 3 步：适合您的实验的 Y 是多少？
对于设计的实验，您几乎总是首先选择输出响应或 Y。响应需要与整个项目的目标相关；因为可能有多个与项目和团队的实验目标相关的输出响应，所以团队应该询问一些有关响应和可能的相关输入的问题。
首先，测量系统是否足够支持实验？您可以通过量具分析来测试测量系统。测量系统的能力越低，您需要的样本量就越大，并且需要进行更多的实验。如果测量系统的能力太差，那么实验的时间或费用可能会令人望而却步。

rows: 术语说明 MSA（测量系统分析）：评估测量工具、人员和方法是否足以支持可靠决策。
zh.text: 术语说明 MSA（测量系统分析）：评估测量工具、人员和方法是否足以支持可靠决策。
其次，团队需要解决的重要问题是什么？团队想要解决流程中的变异还是解决流程的中心问题？目的是降低变异性还是提高或降低过程的均值？在上述医疗保健场景中，目的是提高合规率。
团队还必须了解响应如何与流程中的其他响应相关，或者一个响应的更改是否会导致另一个响应的更改 - 特别是当实验是在流程的实时环境中进行时。这有助于团队管理可能影响其他流程或客户的任何下游变更。
六西格玛医疗团队的问题是，卫生协议的遵守率是Y。
第 4 步：将研究哪些输入？
在 DMAIC 章节中，我们介绍了一些用于识别流程中输入的头脑风暴工具，包括鱼骨图。第 31 章介绍了更多头脑风暴工具。 团队可以使用此类工具以及流程图来识别与上一步中决定的输出相关的输入。获得输入列表后，您可以选择要在实验中研究的输入。请注意，并非会研究流程的所有输入，团队应选择以下输入：

rows: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。
zh.text: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。

rows: 术语说明 Fishbone / Ishikawa（鱼骨图）：按类别梳理潜在原因的根因分析工具。
zh.text: 术语说明 Fishbone / Ishikawa（鱼骨图）：按类别梳理潜在原因的根因分析工具。
据信对响应影响最大（考虑帕累托或 80/20 规则）

rows: 术语说明 Pareto（帕累托）：按影响大小排序，常用于找出“少数关键原因”。
zh.text: 术语说明 Pareto（帕累托）：按影响大小排序，常用于找出“少数关键原因”。
本质上是定量或定性的（由于样本量要求，使用属性数据比较困难，并且测量系统中涉及太多人也会增加变化，因此请记住这些注意事项）
可以针对实验进行适当的设置和控制
对于医疗保健场景，该团队认为有几个因素可能会导致缺乏合规性：员工的教育程度、特定员工轮班的时间长度以及特定时间在场的患者数量。
第 5 步：实验的因子水平是多少？
为了实验的目的，输入变量将设置在什么水平？如果您正在进行涉及烘烤饼干的实验，那么烤箱温度就是一个输入或因素。该因子的级别可能为 325 华氏度、350 华氏度和 375 度
华氏度。请记住，在 2k 阶乘的情况下，每个因子只有两个水平。
在医疗保健示例中，团队决定将每个因素设置为低值和高值：
员工教育：过去 6 个月内接受过年度合规更新培训与过去 6 个月内未接受过培训
轮班时间：小于或等于 4 小时 vs 5 小时或以上
楼层患者人数：低于最大容量的 80% 与高于最大容量的 80%
为每个因素设定的水平取决于多种考虑因素。如果团队正在进行筛选实验（这意味着他们只想测试输入对输出的影响或更好地了解过程），他们会以较宽的间隔设置水平。对于优化，团队可能已经了解输入对流程的影响；该团队可能已经进行了分析或实验，这些信息可以帮助他们以较小的间隔适当地设置水平。
该过程本身将决定如何设置某些级别。例如，如果六西格玛团队正在进行烘焙过程，则它无法将温度设置为低于0度，并且大多数烤箱也有最高温度范围。团队还可能知道烘焙食品仍处于生状态的温度，因此他们不太可能将温度设置为 100 华氏度之类的温度。在设置温度水平时，安全始终应该是一个问题。烤箱最高温度可能会达到 550 华氏度，但尝试在该温度下烘烤饼干是否安全？
最后，这些级别应与团队正在寻找的信息类型以及团队希望看到的变化规模相关。如果一个团队正在使用切割木材的制造流程，那么它可能希望看到小增量的变化。将工具水平设置为相距几英尺并不会显示团队希望看到的变化，如果他们正在处理将被切割成 6 英寸块的 2 x 4 木材块的切割过程。
第 6 步：合适的样本量是多少？

在实验设计中，样本量不是您收集的数据元素的数量，而是您重复实验的次数。重复次数越多，统计数据的准确性就越高，但 DOE 中的每次运行都需要时间和可能的其他资源。 六西格玛专家必须平衡资源的利用与强有力的实验结论的需要。当您在 Minitab 中创建实验设计时，软件程序甚至在运行实验之前就会生成一些有关实验的预测，这使您可以在开始之前决定大小和重复性。具体来说，Minitab 会告诉您效应的大小（以标准差衡量），您在设计实验时有 80% 的可能性捕获该效应。

rows: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
zh.text: 术语说明 Standard Deviation（标准差）：衡量数据离散程度的统计量；标准差越大，波动越明显。
在 Minitab 中运行 2k 因子实验的分步指南
要了解有关样本大小和复制的更多信息，以及如何在 Minitab 中运行 2k 因子实验，请从“助手”菜单开始。您还可以在统计 > DOE 下访问实验设计，但助手向导非常有用，因为它会提醒您本章前面讨论的所有步骤，帮助您决定正确的实验类型，并帮助您创建数据收集表格和分析。
为了设置此实验，请使用上面讨论的医疗保健示例。
问题：在卫生遵守情况的 10 分评价中，临床医护人员的平均得分为 7.1。医院要求平均分至少9.0。
目标：将合规性分数提高到至少 9.0。
回应：卫生协议的遵守率
因素：员工教育程度、轮班时间、楼层患者数量
级别：
员工教育 过去 6 个月内未接受过合规培训
在过去 6 个月内是否接受过合规培训
轮班时间 小于或等于 4 小时 5 小时或以上
楼层患者人数 低于最大容量的 80%
等于或高于最大容量的 80%
选择助手 > DOE > 计划和创建。
选择“计划优化实验”，因为团队希望改进或优化流程。
Minitab 提供了一些有关识别因素、设置水平和选择样本量的说明。这些信息都很有价值，值得审查，特别是如果您距离上次执行 DOE 已有很长时间了。这也是本章中包含的所有信息。
上述 Minitab 菜单中的一个好注意事项是，最好获得与项目或流程相关的任何利益相关者、项目所有者或发起人的批准。主要利益相关者应该了解实验期间会发生什么以及为什么它对项目是必要的。如果需要资源，发起人或其他领导者应按照组织指南的要求签字。

rows: 术语说明 Stakeholder（相关方）：会影响项目或被项目影响的人或组织。
zh.text: 术语说明 Stakeholder（相关方）：会影响项目或被项目影响的人或组织。
在用作示例的特定医疗保健场景中，运行独立实验不是一种选择。六西格玛团队当然不会仅仅为了了解输入信息而给患者带来葡萄球菌感染。在这种特殊情况下，团队需要使用与实验参数相匹配的现有数据。对于任何情况下的团队来说，这都可能是一种选择或要求。
查看所需的任何 Minitab 注释后，单击“返回”。
选择“创建建模设计”。
Minitab 显示“建模设计”对话框，请求创建实验所需的所有信息。输入医疗保健场景的信息。

1. 命名响应变量。该团队正在衡量对卫生协议的遵守情况，因此将其命名为“合规性”。该名称对于 Minitab 来说并不重要，因此您可以将变量命名为对您自己或六西格玛团队有意义的任何名称。 2. 选择响应目标的选项。 Minitab 允许您选择最小化响应、最大化响应或实现目标。在本例中，团队希望将合规性提高到 9 分，但高于 9 分也将是一个积极的结果，因此选择“最大化响应”。 3. 输入因子的数量。请记住，因素是输入，而医疗保健场景确定了 3 个输入。 4. 输入因素的名称和信息。尽管有些因子本质上是连续的，但由于这是一个 2k 阶乘实验，我们将它们分为几类。 5. 选择重复实验的次数（记住，这与样本大小有关）。为此，请选择 2。 6. 确保您的对话框与下面的对话框相符，然后选择“确定”。
Minitab 为您提供打印数据表单的选项，以便更轻松地收集数据。对于示例，选择否。
Minitab 生成三个项目。第一个是建模设计报告卡，提供完成实验的后续步骤和技巧。
创建造型设计
报告卡
检查
状态说明
在原始设计中运行并再次运行它们。实验运行，同时在同一运行期间进行重复。当您将仿行添加到设计中时，您将在相同的因子设置下进行所有的仿行和重复测量，但仿行是在单独的过程中进行的。
