View the Pre-Experiment Checklist. Print a data collection form for this experiment. Fit the linear model.
The second item is a summary report. The report summarizes all the information you entered for the experiment and provides an estimated detection ability, which helps you choose a sample size.

The final item is a worksheet to help you gather results from the experiment. Minitab randomizes the order of the runs, which would be required if you were completing the experiment in real-time.
For the healthcare scenario we entered, the detection ability based on 2 replicas will detect standard deviations of 1.36 or more.
If the team wanted to detect smaller changes, it would need to add more replicates. Four replications would detect 0.91 standard deviations, and 5 would detect as little as 0.81 standard deviations.

To make the best possible choice about sample size, it does help to know what the current standard deviation is to understand what measurement is going to be meaningful. Remember, two replicates have an 80 percent chance of detecting 1.36 standard deviations, not a 1.36 shift in the actual compliance score.
Before moving forward with the experiment, the team might consider some sample data. Here are the sanitation compliance scores from all of the clinical audits for one week.
Copy the data into Excel or Minitab to calculate the standard deviation of that data set.
The standard deviation is 1.028.

According to the first summary, based on the setting for 2 replicates, the detection ability of the experiment is only an 80 percent chance at detecting changes of 1.36 standard deviations. That’s too big to make the difference the healthcare team needs to see. At three replicates, the difference is right on the line, and in any other situation, a Six Sigma expert would probably want to go with 4 or more replicates. For the purpose of this exercise, we’ll stay with 2 replicates for the rest of the example.
On the Report Card screen, select “View the pre-experiment checklist”.
Minitab provides some important tips for completing a designed experiment in the checklist.

First, anyone involved in the experiment should be properly trained to remove variation from operator error. Individuals involved must know how to measure the output, or response, how to set factor levels if that is required during the experiment, and how to make changes to equipment or processes between runs to affect the experiment. In the healthcare example we are using, the only training that might be required is ensuring the audit – which applies the compliance score – is handled correctly. In another situation, however, a team might be running a production process in a manufacturing plant. The team might want to try different materials, different tools, and different operators as part of the experiment, and they would have to ensure everyone knew how to use the materials and tools.

Measurement systems should be validated for both the response and the levels for every factor. In our healthcare example, the Six Sigma team should have already validated the measurement system used to rate compliance. They might have used a Gage R & R to determine if separate auditors with separate replications would rate the same situation the same way more than once, for example. Teams should also validate the measurements used to set levels. If the speed of a wheel in a manufacturing process is to be set at 1 rpm, 1.2 rpm, and 1.5 rpm as levels of a factor, then calibrations for those settings should be checked.

When running actual experiments, Six Sigma teams should review all possible combinations in the experiment to ensure they are all both safe and possible. Perfectly safe or possible levels for one factor might become unsafe or impossible when combined with another, for example. The team might want to perform a few trial runs to work out troubleshooting issues with the experiment and finalize validations before moving on to the actual experiment.

Finally, the team runs the designed experiment according to the random worksheet created by Minitab. In the healthcare scenario, the team reviewed the data for the last month in random order and recorded the first set of data that fit each experiment scenario. For example, the first run in the list below required that the staff member being audited had received compliance training in the last six months, was over the 4th hour into a shift when the interaction being audited occurred, and was working at a time when the particular floor was at 80 percent or more of capacity. The first score that met those parameters was 7.9. The results of the data gathering are included below, and you can copy this table into Minitab if you want to follow along with the analysis.

more 8.1
80% or
15 15 1 2 No Over 4
more 5.6 12 16 1 2 Yes Over 4 Below 80% 8.9
To analyze the results in Minitab, select Assistant > DOE > Analyze and Interpret.
Click “Fit Linear Model.”
Select “Maximize the response” (Or the response goal that matches the goal for your experiment.)
Click OK.
Minitab generates five items:
A report card
A prediction and optimization report
A diagnostic report
An effects report
A summary report

The report card provides overall information about the analysis and some tips for understanding the results. In this case, the report card notes that no unusual data points, or possible outliers, are noted and that there is only one significant factor in the experiment according to statistical analysis.

The prediction and optimization report compares the means associated with significant factors and provides an optimized level setting for those factors. In this case, only one factor – whether or not the staff had received compliance training in the past six months – was statistically significant.
Fit Linear Model for Compliance
Prediction and Optimization Report
Goal: Maximize Compliance
Solution: Optimal Setting
Predicted Y 9.0625 StaffEd Yes 95% CI (8.3878, 9.7372)
Main Effects Plot for StaffEd
Compare the means and intervals. The green interval shows the optimal setting for StaffEd.
Level
N Mean 95% CI for Mean
No
StaffEd
Yes
Compliance

The effects report provides more information about all the factors. In the lower part of the diagram below, you can see the main effects plots for each factor. The main effect is the difference between the mean response at each factor setting. You can see that only a slight difference occurs between the means of the responses for different amounts of time spent on the shift and almost no difference occurs between the means of the two settings for amount of patients on the floor. The largest difference is seen with regard to the compliance education status. The same is true in the top part of the graph, where different factors are graphed together.
Fit Linear Model for Compliance
Effects Report
Interaction Plots for Compliance
Describes how the mean of Compliance changes if you change the settings of two factors.
Mean of Compliance
A gray background represents a term that was removed from the model because it is not statistically significant.

The summary report provides a Pareto breakdown of the influence on the response. In this case, the biggest influence on the compliance number is whether the staff received compliance training in the last six months. The next factor is how long the staff member had been working that shift. The third most influential factor is a combination of A (staff training) and B (time worked on the shift). Number of patients on the floor doesn’t make an appearance until the fourth influence.

The summary notes that setting the staff education factor at Yes – the staff member received training within the last 6 months – results in a predicted Y (response) that is above 9. The summary also notes that this model explains 68.64 percent of the variation.

Based on these results, the Six Sigma team might run further tests and analysis, but it would also likely make a recommendation about supplying staff with compliance training. If you remember, the training that was mentioned originally was an annual training. However, this experiment seems to indicate that the training has more impact if staff members have received it within the past 6 months. The team might recommend that staff receive the training biannually going forward.
Next Steps

Remember that an experiment is not usually what solves the large problem that the Six Sigma team is working on. It simply helps approach one part of a solution to that problem. In the case of the healthcare Six Sigma team, recommending the additional training might increase compliance rates with the sanitation protocol, which could in turn decrease the rate of staph infections in the hospital. But that’s not likely the only root cause the team has found and not the only solution it will recommend.

After drawing conclusions from experiments, Six Sigma teams must take steps to validate results, implement solutions or work the results into the overall project, and create controls. These steps will be covered in more detail at the end of the next chapter.

最后一项是一个工作表，可帮助您收集实验结果。 Minitab 随机化运行的顺序，如果您要实时完成实验，则需要这样做。
对于我们进入的医疗保健场景，基于2个副本的检测能力将检测到1.36或更大的标准差。
如果团队想要检测较小的变化，则需要添加更多的重复。重复 4 次将检测到 0.91 个标准差，重复 5 次将检测到低至 0.81 个标准差。
为了对样本量做出尽可能最佳的选择，了解当前的标准差确实有助于了解什么测量将是有意义的。请记住，两次重复有 80% 的机会检测到 1.36 个标准偏差，而不是实际合规性分数的 1.36 个偏差。
在继续实验之前，团队可能会考虑一些样本数据。以下是一周内所有临床审核的卫生合规性分数。
将数据复制到 Excel 或 Minitab 中以计算该数据集的标准差。
标准差为 1.028。
根据第一个总结，基于 2 个重复的设置，实验的检测能力只有 80% 的机会检测到 1.36 个标准差的变化。这太大了，无法产生医疗团队需要看到的变化。三个重复的时候，差别就很明显了，在其他情况下，六西格玛高手可能会想要四个或更多的重复。出于本练习的目的，我们将在示例的其余部分保留 2 个重复。
在报告卡屏幕上，选择“查看实验前清单”。
Minitab 在清单中提供了一些完成设计实验的重要提示。

首先，参与实验的任何人都应该接受适当的培训，以消除操作员错误带来的变化。相关人员必须知道如何测量输出或响应，如何在实验过程中需要时设置因子水平，以及如何在运行之间更改设备或流程以影响实验。在我们使用的医疗保健示例中，唯一可能需要的培训是确保正确处理应用合规性分数的审核。 然而，在另一种情况下，团队可能正在制造工厂中运行生产流程。作为实验的一部分，团队可能想要尝试不同的材料、不同的工具和不同的操作员，并且他们必须确保每个人都知道如何使用这些材料和工具。
应对测量系统的响应和每个因素的水平进行验证。在我们的医疗保健示例中，六西格玛团队应该已经验证了用于评估合规性的测量系统。例如，他们可能使用量具 R&R 来确定具有单独重复的单独审计员是否会多次以相同的方式对同一情况进行评级。团队还应该验证用于设置级别的测量结果。如果将制造过程中车轮的速度设置为 1 rpm、1.2 rpm 和 1.5 rpm 作为因子级别，则应检查这些设置的校准。
在进行实际实验时，六西格玛团队应该审查实验中所有可能的组合，以确保它们都是安全且可行的。例如，当一个因素与另一个因素结合时，一个因素的完全安全或可能的水平可能会变得不安全或不可能。团队可能希望进行几次试运行，以解决实验中的问题并完成验证，然后再进行实际实验。

最后，团队根据 Minitab 创建的随机工作表运行设计的实验。在医疗保健场景中，团队以随机顺序回顾了上个月的数据，并记录了适合每个实验场景的第一组数据。例如，下面列表中的第一次运行要求接受审核的工作人员在过去六个月内接受过合规培训，在接受审核的交互发生时处于轮班第 4 个小时以上，并且在特定楼层的容量达到或超过 80% 时工作。满足这些参数的第一个分数是 7.9。数据收集的结果如下所示，如果您想继续分析，可以将此表复制到 Minitab 中。

摘要报告提供了对响应的影响的帕累托分解。在这种情况下，对合规数量影响最大的是员工在过去六个月内是否接受过合规培训。下一个因素是该工作人员轮班工作了多长时间。第三个最有影响力的因素是 A（员工培训）和 B（轮班工作时间）的组合。直到第四次影响时，地板上的病人数量才出现。
摘要指出，将员工教育因素设置为“是”（员工在过去 6 个月内接受过培训）会导致预测 Y（响应）高于 9。摘要还指出，该模型解释了 68.64% 的变异。
根据这些结果，六西格玛团队可能会进行进一步的测试和分析，但也可能会提出有关为员工提供合规培训的建议。如果你还记得的话，最初提到的培训是年度培训。然而，这个实验似乎表明，如果员工在过去 6 个月内接受过培训，那么培训的效果会更大。团队可能会建议员工今后每两年接受一次培训。
下一步
请记住，实验通常并不能解决六西格玛团队正在研究的大问题。它只是帮助解决该问题的一部分。就六西格玛医疗团队而言，建议额外培训可能会提高卫生协议的遵守率，从而降低医院内的葡萄球菌感染率。但这可能不是团队发现的唯一根本原因，也不是他们推荐的唯一解决方案。

rows: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
zh.text: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
从实验中得出结论后，六西格玛团队必须采取措施验证结果，实施解决方案或将结果纳入整个项目，并创建控制措施。这些步骤将在下一章末尾更详细地介绍。

title.en: Chapter 30: Interactions, Multi-Level Factors, and Experiment Creation
title.zh: 第 30 章：交互作用、多水平因子与实验创建

source.extraction: python-docx body-order extraction; source-TOC sectionized bilingual content

title.en: Interactions, Multi-Level Factors, and Experiment Creation
title.zh: 交互作用、多水平因子与实验创建

en.text: Factorials, and Creating Experiments

In the previous two chapters, you saw how designed experiments could lead to the use of an ANOVA or a 2k (two-level) factorial. This chapter will cover more information on the use of factorials, including multi-level factorials, and provide information for setting up designed experiments.

rows: 术语说明 Interaction（交互作用）：一个因素的效果会随另一个因素水平不同而变化。
zh.text: 术语说明 Interaction（交互作用）：一个因素的效果会随另一个因素水平不同而变化。
阶乘和创建实验
在前两章中，您了解了设计的实验如何导致方差分析（ANOVA）或 2k（两级）阶乘的使用。本章将介绍有关使用阶乘（包括多级阶乘）的更多信息，并提供有关设置设计实验的信息。

rows: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。
zh.text: 术语说明 ANOVA（方差分析）：比较多个组均值差异是否显著的统计方法。

rows: 术语说明 Factorial（因子实验）：同时考察多个因素及其组合影响的实验设计。
zh.text: 术语说明 Factorial（因子实验）：同时考察多个因素及其组合影响的实验设计。
理解交互的重要性

en.text: Chapter 29 briefly touched on the concept of interactions. Remember, one reason design of experiments is such a powerful tool for Six Sigma teams is that it doesn’t only let experts analyze how certain factors and levels impact results. Design of experiments also lets you understand how the combination of certain factors and levels impact results. Because designed experiments run each factor and level against all other factors and levels, they can unearth interactions that other types of analysis, which consider only a single factor at a time, can miss. Understanding interactions helps Six Sigma teams come up with the strongest possible solution while also mitigating potential issues such as waste or error.

In the sample healthcare experiment run in the previous chapter, there wasn’t a great deal of interaction between the individual factors, but the interaction plots did show a slight interaction between the education and shift factors. If a staff member had received the compliance education within the last 6 months, they were more likely to be compliant with the sanitation procedures. If the staff member, who had received the training in the past 6 months, was also within the first four hours of the shift, the increase in the likelihood of compliance associated with the training was slightly higher than the same increase in someone who was over four hours into a shift.

Interactions are indicated in the interaction plots by lines that are not parallel. The less parallel the lines are, the greater the interaction. As you can see, most of these lines are close to parallel, which means that any interaction that does exist here is fairly weak. Consider the bottom left graph. The blue line indicates measurements taken when the department was below 80 percent capacity for patients. The red line indicates measurements taken when the department was above 80 percent capacity for patients. Regardless of the capacity status, the behavior of the measurements is roughly the same depending on whether the staff member had received the compliance training within the past six months or not. This means there is not much of an interaction – the setting of one factor does not seem to impact the way the settings for the other factor changes the result.
Understanding the Main Effect

To better understand how interactions are measured, you first have to understand the main effect. Consider a real-world example, again with a 2-level factorial. In this example, a Six Sigma team within a chain of movie theaters is trying to positively impact the performance of concession stands. Specifically, the team has been tasked with increasing profits in the concession stand.

One area the team is concentrating on is the popcorn process, and they have defined waste as a root cause of the problem in this part of the concession stand. The team believes the current popcorn- making process is generating waste because so many of the kernels introduced into the popcorn machine never pop. Those kernels are swept out of the machine regularly and thrown away, and the team believes the amount of kernels thrown away each day amounts to at least 10 large popcorn orders.

While that might seem like a small amount of waste, the team calculates that the concession stand earns a profit of $2.65 for each large popcorn order, which means the stand is losing approximately $9,600 each year in lost popcorn kernels. If the team can use a designed experiment to identify a quick, inexpensive way to reduce kernel waste, it might be worth the effort.

After finding out more about the kernel process, the team decides that the temperature of the machine at the time the popcorn is introduced is a factor. They also decide that the number of kernels placed in the machine and the brand of popcorn are factors as well.
The team designs the following experiment:
Row Number
Brand Temperature Kernels
1 A 400 degrees F 1 scoop
2 A 400 degrees F 2 scoops
3 A 600 degrees F 1 scoop
4 A 600 degrees F 2 scoops
5 B 400 degrees F 1 scoop
6 B 400 degrees F 2 scoops
7 B 600 degrees F 1 scoop
8 B 600 degrees F 2 scoops

Following good experiment protocol, the team decides on a sample size. Remember, the sample size for a designed experiment is what tells you how many replications of the overall experiment to run. In this case, the Six Sigma team decides to run 4 replications. Given the above design, that means the team is going to record 32 runs. Also following good experiment protocol, the team randomizes the runs so they aren’t recording the information in the same order each time through the experiment.

The Six Sigma expert on the team also knew that simply recording the amount of kernels left after each batch wouldn’t provide the ability to draw statistical conclusions about the three factors the team was considering. This is because one of the factors the team was considering was the volume of kernels entering the process. Naturally, if more kernels enter the process, there are likely to be more waste kernels – the team shouldn’t need an experiment to test if that is true. The team really wants to find out if a larger ratio of unpopped kernels is left when popping one or two scoops of certain popcorn brands at certain temperatures. The Six Sigma expert ensures the team records both total number of kernels and the percent, or ratio, of those kernels to the number of kernels that entered the process.

The information recorded by the team is shown in the table below. Note that the data has been sorted by brand, then temperature, and then number of scoops for the purpose of analysis. This would not be the order in which the team ran the experiment since it would have been randomized.
Row
Number Brand Temperature Kernels Est. No Kernals
Kernels
Left Percent Left

1 A 400 degrees F 1 scoop 2300 125 5.43% 9 A 400 degrees F 1 scoop 2300 115 5.00% 17 A 400 degrees F 1 scoop 2300 118 5.13% 25 A 400 degrees F 1 scoop 2300 127 5.52% 2 A 400 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 298 6.48% 10 A 400 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 275 5.98% 18 A 400 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 298 6.48% 26 A 400 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 275 5.98% 3 A 600 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 98 2.13% 11 A 600 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 101 2.20% 19 A 600 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 99 2.15% 27 A 600 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 105 2.28% 4 A 600 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 187 4.07% 12 A 600 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 192 4.17% 20 A 600 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 175 3.80% 28 A 600 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 201 4.37% 5 B 400 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 145 3.15% 13 B 400 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 129 2.80% 21 B 400 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 138 3.00% 29 B 400 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 142 3.09%

6 B 400 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 324 7.04% 14 B 400 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 298 6.48% 22 B 400 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 307 6.67% 30 B 400 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 399 8.67% 7 B 600 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 109 2.37% 15 B 600 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 138 3.00% 23 B 600 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 109 2.37% 31 B 600 degrees F 1 scoop 4600 124 2.70% 8 B 600 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 202 4.39% 16 B 600 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 225 4.89% 24 B 600 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 198 4.30% 32 B 600 degrees F 2 scoops 4600 225 4.89%

The effect in a designed experiment is the measurement of the change in the result based on changing the levels of factors. The main effect describes the measurement of change in the result based on changes in a single factor. The main effect of each individual factor can be roughly calculated by comparing the average of the results at both the low and high end of the factor.

For example, the average percent left for Brand A is 4.45. The average left for Brand B is 4.36. The difference is 0.09. Immediately, it appears that there is not much of an effect for kernel-popping performance between the two brands of popcorn. Remember, though, that a statistical difference doesn’t necessarily require a large numerical difference. Whether the difference is worth considering depends on other factors, such as the range of difference the team is hoping to record and the main effect of other factors in the experiment.

The same calculation can be completed for each of the factors in the experiment. The average percent of kernels left for runs with a temperature of 400 degrees F is approximately 5.43; the average percent left for runs with a temperature of 600 degrees F is 3.38. The main effect for this factor is larger than that for brand; the difference between the averages for 400 degrees and 600 degrees is 2.05.
The average percent of kernels left when the team started with one scoop was approximately 3.27. The
percent left when starting with two scoops was approximately 5.54; the difference is 2.27.
Understanding Interaction Plots

When plotted on a line graph the main effects from the above experiment look something like the image below. These images are generated in Excel, but Minitab and other statistical analysis software generate such graphs as part of the overall analysis when running designed experiments.

This image makes it easy to see: there isn’t much of a main effect for the brand of popcorn, but there are major changes based on temperature and scoop. Since the team wants to reduce the percentage of unpopped kernels, it would seem that they would want to set temperature at a higher level but use only one scoop of popcorn at a time.

But the main effects graph doesn’t always take into account any interaction between the factors, which is why it’s important to consider interaction plots. You can use Minitab to generate interaction plots, or create your own in Excel. To create interaction plots in Excel, follow the steps below.

1. Import your data, with headers, into Excel. For this example, you can use the data grid provided for the popcorn scenario above. 2. Calculate the average of the result for each combination of two factors. In the popcorn scenario, you would calculate the average of the percent results for three sets of graph data.
a. Graph 1 (Scoops * Temperature)

i. All rows where scoops equals 1 and temperature equals 400 ii. All rows where scoops equals 1 and temperature equals 600 iii. All rows where scoops equals 2 and temperature equals 400 iv. All rows where scoops equals 2 and temperature equals 600 b. Graph 2 (Scoops * Brand)
i. All rows where scoops equals 1 and brand equals A ii. All rows where scoops equals 1 and brand equals B iii. All rows where scoops equals 2 and brand equals A
iv. All rows where scoops equals 2 and brand equals B c. Graph 3 (Brand * Temperature)

i. All rows where brand equals A and temperature equals 400 ii. All rows where brand equals A and temperature equals 600 iii. All rows where brand equals B and temperature equals 400 iv. All rows where brand equals B and temperature equals 600 3. Arrange the results into three small data tables that resemble the data table for the temperature and scoop set below.
Temperature Scoops Average 400 1 4.14 600 1 2.4 400 2 6.72 600 2 4.36
4. Use each of the small data tables to create a line graph.
a. Select Insert > Charts > Line Charts > Multi line graph with markers
b. Click on “Select Data.”
c. Click “Add” under “Legend Entries (Series)”
d. Enter “1 scoop” as the name for the first series. Select the averages for 1 scoop from your small data table into the range for “series values”.
e. Click OK. f. Click Add again, and repeat step d to create a series for 2 scoops. g. Click OK. h. Click Edit under “Horizontal (Category) Axis Labels.
i. Select two cells that hold the names for the other factor settings (in this case, it is the temperature settings) into the Axis labels box.
j. Click OK. k. Click OK again. l. Use Excel chart options to add a title and legends as desired so that your chart looks something like the image below.

These are the same style of interaction plots that are displayed by Minitab if you run DOE analysis in that program, and they show some interaction that was not apparent when simply considering the raw data or even the main effects plots.

Analysis of the first interaction plot shows what the Six Sigma team might already have gleaned from the main effects. The lowest leftover kernel percentage seems to come when you combine 1 scoop of kernels and the higher temperature setting. This plot doesn’t show an interaction – the lines are almost parallel. Regardless of the number of scoops you start with, the highest temperature yields the least waste. Regardless of the temperature setting, the lowest scoop start yields the least waste.

The second graph, to the right of the first, shows the interaction between brand and temperature. Remember, the main effects analysis seemed to indicate that the choice of brand of popcorn was not important. When brand is analyzed with another factor, though, this doesn’t seem to hold true. In this graph, you can see that brand does make a difference in how the change in temperature setting works.

At 600 degrees F, brand A performs better than brand B. At 400 degrees F, brand B performs better. Even though all of this is true, both brands generate less waste at the higher temperature setting when compared to their own performance at the lower temperature setting.

The third graph also shows some interaction between brand and number of scoops. Brand B performs better at the lower scoop setting than brand A, and the reverse is true at the higher scoop setting. Again, both brands generate less waste at the lower scoop setting than when compared to their own performance at the higher scoop setting.

Based on all of the information above, the Six Sigma team is likely to choose to start the popcorn process with 1 scoop and use the 600 degree F temperature setting. Choosing a brand of popcorn, however, is a little tougher. Opting for brand A saves approximately 0.5 percent of waste over brand B when considering the interaction with temperature. However, brand B saves almost 1 percent of waste over brand A when considering the interaction with number of scoops.

Given all of the information and analysis in this experiment, a Six Sigma team might either say brand is not a big enough factor (so the concession stand could choose brand based on other factors, such as cost) or recommend that brand B be purchased when it is equal to or less than brand A in price because brand B is associated with the highest percentage of waste savings. If brand B is more expensive than brand A, the small waste savings associated with brand B would likely be mitigated, though, making brand A the better choice. This is a good illustration of how other data should play a role in decision making – a designed experiment doesn’t exist in a vacuum.

This was a basic analysis to help you understand how interactions work. Minitab and other statistical analysis software runs all of this analysis automatically, reducing the workload on the Six Sigma expert. Most such software will also consider all possible interactions and present the factors with the most impact in the analysis. If you have a 2k factorial experiment with three factors, each with two settings, and those factors are A, B, and C, you could have the following possible effects on the result:
A (the impact of changing only A)
B (the impact of changing only B)
C (the impact of changing only C)
AB (the interaction of A and B)
AC (the interaction of A and C)
BC (the interaction of B and C)
ABC (the interaction of all three factors)
Adding additional factors makes this analysis increasingly complicated; adding additional levels at each factor does the same thing.
2k Factorials Versus Multi-Level Factorials

So far in this unit, all the DOE scenarios used have been two-level (or 2k) factorial experiments. In each experiment, Six Sigma teams chose to set the levels of each factor at no more than two – typically at a high and a low.
Why would a Six Sigma team choose to do this? Wouldn’t an experiment with multiple factor levels provide more information for the team? In the popcorn example, why would the team choose the high
and low temperature settings? What happens when the temperature is set at 500 degrees F? What about 425, 450, 475, etc.? How does the team know that 400 and 600 are the two most optimal settings?

The truth is that the team doesn’t know those are the most optimal settings, but adding multiple levels for one or more factors adds to the number of runs that have to be completed. One replication of the popcorn experiment previously described required eight runs. Just adding one more factor for temperature – 500 degrees F – adds four runs to the experiment.
Row Number
Brand Temperature Kernels
1 A 400 degrees F 1 scoop
2 A 400 degrees F 2 scoops
3 A 500 degrees F 1 scoop
4 A 500 degrees F 2 scoops
5 A 600 degrees F 1 scoop
6 A 600 degrees F 2 scoops
7 B 400 degrees F 1 scoop
8 B 400 degrees F 2 scoops
9 B 500 degrees F 1 scoop
10 B 500 degrees F 2 scoops
11 B 600 degrees F 1 scoop
12 B 600 degrees F 2 scoops

Now, instead of 32 runs for a designed experiment that includes four replications, the team would have 48 runs. That’s 16 additional runs where the time and resources of the team and the company are being used. If the team wanted to also test temperature settings of 425, 450, 475, 525, 550, and 575, that would entail 24 additional runs for every replication, or 96 additional runs for four replications. If the team also decided to add additional levels to other factors, such as a third brand or an attempt at 1.5 scoops of kernels, the team could be looking at an experiment with hundreds of runs required to achieve the appropriate sample size.

For these reasons, 2k factorial experiments are typically the most commonly used among Six Sigma experts. Minitab lets you run these experiments with up to 15 different factors, providing a powerful ability to analyze processes. Minitab also lets you run experiments with more than two factors.

Multi-factor experiments are handled in much the same way 2k factorial experiments are handled. Obviously, if you are handling analysis manually, you’ll want to limit the levels and factors as much as possible. Minitab removes the need to limit data because of analysis requirements – as long as the team

can define a need for including factors and levels in an experiment and has been given permission and access to use the resources needed to run an appropriate number of replications for accurate statistical conclusions, then Six Sigma teams can certainly run multi-level factor experiments.

zh.text: 第29章简要介绍了交互的概念。请记住，实验设计对于六西格玛团队来说是如此强大的工具，原因之一是它不仅可以让专家分析某些因素和水平如何影响结果。实验设计还可以让您了解某些因素和水平的组合如何影响结果。由于设计的实验将每个因素和水平与所有其他因素和水平进行比较，因此它们可以发现其他类型的分析（每次只考虑一个因素）可能会错过的相互作用。了解交互有助于六西格玛团队提出最有力的解决方案，同时减少浪费或错误等潜在问题。

rows: 术语说明 DOE（实验设计）：系统安排实验以识别因素、水平和交互作用对结果的影响。
zh.text: 术语说明 DOE（实验设计）：系统安排实验以识别因素、水平和交互作用对结果的影响。
在上一章进行的医疗保健样本实验中，各个因素之间没有大量的相互作用，但相互作用图确实显示了教育和轮班因素之间存在轻微的相互作用。如果工作人员在过去 6 个月内接受过合规教育，他们更有可能遵守卫生程序。如果在过去 6 个月内接受过培训的工作人员也在轮班的前 4 小时内，则与培训相关的合规可能性的增加略高于轮班超过 4 小时的人员的相同增加。

rows: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。
zh.text: 术语说明 Sample / Sampling（样本/抽样）：从总体中选取部分数据进行分析；样本应尽量代表总体。

交互作用在交互作用图中用不平行的线表示。线的平行度越低，相互作用就越大。正如您所看到的，这些线中的大多数都接近平行，这意味着这里确实存在的任何相互作用都相当弱。考虑左下图。蓝线表示当科室的患者容量低于 80% 时进行的测量。红线表示当科室的患者容量超过 80% 时进行的测量。 无论容量状态如何，测量的行为大致相同，具体取决于工作人员在过去六个月内是否接受过合规培训。这意味着没有太多的交互作用——一个因素的设置似乎不会影响另一因素的设置改变结果的方式。
了解主要效果
为了更好地理解如何衡量交互作用，您首先必须了解主效应。考虑一个现实世界的例子，同样是一个 2 级阶乘。在此示例中，连锁电影院内的六西格玛团队正试图对特许摊位的绩效产生积极影响。具体来说，该团队的任务是增加特许摊位的利润。
该团队重点关注的一个领域是爆米花加工过程，他们将浪费定义为特许摊位这部分问题的根本原因。该团队认为，当前的爆米花制作过程正在产生废物，因为许多放入爆米花机的玉米粒从未爆开。这些玉米粒会定期从机器中扫出并扔掉，该团队相信每天扔掉的玉米粒数量至少相当于 10 个大份爆米花订单。

rows: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
zh.text: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
虽然这看起来只是少量浪费，但该团队计算出，每个大爆米花订单的特许摊位可赚取 2.65 美元的利润，这意味着该摊位每年因爆米花仁的丢失而损失约 9,600 美元。如果团队可以使用设计好的实验来找到一种快速、廉价的方法来减少内核浪费，那么这可能是值得的。
在了解有关内核过程的更多信息后，团队确定引入爆米花时机器的温度是一个因素。他们还认为，放入机器中的玉米粒数量和爆米花的品牌也是因素。
该团队设计了以下实验：
行号
品牌温度内核
1 A 400 华氏度 1 勺
2 A 400 华氏度 2 勺
3 A 600 华氏度 1 勺
4 A 600 华氏度 2 勺
5 B 400 华氏度 1 勺
6 B 400 华氏度 2 勺
7 B 600 华氏度 1 勺
8 B 600 华氏度 2 勺
根据良好的实验方案，团队决定样本量。请记住，设计实验的样本量告诉您要运行整个实验的重复次数。在这种情况下，六西格玛团队决定进行 4 次重复。鉴于上述设计，这意味着球队将记录 32 场比赛。此外，遵循良好的实验方案，团队将运行随机化，这样他们就不会在每次实验中都以相同的顺序记录信息。

团队中的六西格玛专家也知道，仅仅记录每批后剩余的玉米粒数量并不能提供关于团队正在考虑的三个因素的统计结论。这是因为团队考虑的因素之一是进入该过程的内核数量。当然，如果更多的内核进入这个过程，可能会有更多的废弃内核——团队不需要进行实验来测试这是否属实。该团队确实想知道，在特定温度下爆开一两勺某些品牌的爆米花时，是否会留下更大比例的未爆开的玉米粒。六西格玛专家确保团队记录玉米粒总数以及这些玉米粒占进入流程的玉米粒数量的百分比或比率。
团队记录的信息如下表所示。请注意，出于分析目的，数据已按品牌、温度和勺数排序。这不会是团队进行实验的顺序，因为它是随机的。
行
编号 品牌 温度内核 估计值 无内核
内核
左侧百分比
设计实验中的效果是基于因素水平变化对结果变化的测量。主效应描述了基于单个因素变化的结果变化的测量。通过比较该因素低端和高端结果的平均值，可以粗略地计算出每个因素的主效应。
例如，品牌 A 的平均剩余百分比为 4.45。品牌 B 的平均剩余值为 4.36。差异为0.09。立即看来，这两个品牌的爆米花对爆米花性能没有太大影响。但请记住，统计差异并不一定需要很大的数值差异。这种差异是否值得考虑取决于其他因素，例如团队希望记录的差异范围以及实验中其他因素的主要影响。
对于实验中的每个因素都可以完成相同的计算。 在 400 华氏度温度下运行时留下的谷粒的平均百分比约为 5.43；温度为 600 华氏度时运行的平均剩余百分比为 3.38。该因素的主效应大于品牌； 400 度和 600 度的平均值之间的差异为 2.05。
当团队开始用一勺时，剩余的谷粒平均百分比约为 3.27。的
从两勺开始时剩余百分比约为 5.54；差异为2.27。
了解交互作用图
当绘制在线图时，上述实验的主要效果如下图所示。这些图像是在 Excel 中生成的，但 Minitab 和其他统计分析软件在运行设计的实验时会生成此类图表作为整体分析的一部分。
这张图很容易看出：爆米花的品牌没有太大的主要影响，但根据温度和勺子有很大的变化。由于该团队希望减少未爆开的玉米粒的百分比，因此他们似乎希望将温度设置在更高的水平，但一次只使用一勺爆米花。
但主效应图并不总是考虑因素之间的任何相互作用，这就是为什么考虑相互作用图很重要。您可以使用 Minitab 生成交互作用图，或在 Excel 中创建自己的交互作用图。要在 Excel 中创建交互作用图，请按照以下步骤操作。
1. 将带标题的数据导入 Excel。对于此示例，您可以使用为上面的爆米花场景提供的数据网格。 2. 计算两个因素的每个组合的结果平均值。在爆米花场景中，您将计算三组图形数据的百分比结果的平均值。
a.图 1（勺数 * 温度）
岛勺数等于 1 且温度等于 400 ii 的所有行。勺数等于 1 且温度等于 600 的所有行 iii.勺数等于 2 且温度等于 400 iv 的所有行。 b. 勺数等于 2、温度等于 600 的所有行。图2（勺数*品牌）
岛勺子等于 1 且品牌等于 A 的所有行 ii。勺数等于 1 且品牌等于 B 的所有行 iii。勺数等于 2 且品牌等于 A 的所有行
四.勺数等于 2 且品牌等于 B 的所有行 c。 图3（品牌*温度）
岛品牌等于 A、温度等于 400 ii 的所有行。品牌等于 A、温度等于 600 的所有行 iii。品牌等于 B、温度等于 400 iv 的所有行。品牌等于 B、温度等于 600 的所有行 3. 将结果排列到三个小数据表中，类似于下面设置的温度和勺的数据表。
4. 使用每个小数据表创建折线图。
a.选择“插入”>“图表”>“折线图”>“带标记的多线图”
b.单击“选择数据”。
c.单击“图例条目（系列）”下的“添加”
d.输入“1 scoop”作为第一个系列的名称。从小数据表中选择 1 勺的平均值到“系列值”范围内。
e.单击“确定”。 f.再次单击“添加”，然后重复步骤 d 以创建 2 勺的系列。 g。单击“确定”。 h.单击“水平（类别）轴标签”下的编辑。
岛选择两个保存其他因子设置名称（在本例中为温度设置）的单元格到“轴标签”框中。
j。单击“确定”。 k.再次单击“确定”。湖使用 Excel 图表选项根据需要添加标题和图例，以便您的图表如下图所示。
如果您在该程序中运行 DOE 分析，这些交互作用图与 Minitab 显示的交互作用图的样式相同，并且它们显示了一些在仅考虑原始数据甚至主效应图时并不明显的交互作用。
对第一个交互图的分析显示了六西格玛团队可能已经从主效应中收集到了什么。当您将 1 勺玉米粒和较高的温度设置结合起来时，剩余玉米粒的百分比似乎会达到最低。该图没有显示交互作用——线条几乎是平行的。无论您开始使用多少勺，最高温度都会产生最少的浪费。无论温度设置如何，最低的勺启动产生的浪费最少。
第二张图位于第一张图的右侧，显示了品牌与温度之间的相互作用。请记住，主效应分析似乎表明爆米花品牌的选择并不重要。然而，当用另一个因素来分析品牌时，这似乎并不成立。 在此图中，您可以看到品牌确实对温度设置的变化产生影响。
在 600 华氏度时，品牌 A 的表现优于品牌 B。在 400 华氏度时，品牌 B 的表现更好。尽管所有这些都是事实，但与较低温度设置下的性能相比，两个品牌在较高温度设置下产生的废物较少。
第三张图还显示了品牌和勺数之间的一些相互作用。品牌 B 在较低勺设置下的表现优于品牌 A，而在较高勺设置下则相反。同样，与在较高勺设置下的性能相比，两个品牌在较低勺设置下产生的浪费更少。
根据上述所有信息，六西格玛团队很可能选择从 1 勺开始爆米花过程并使用 600 华氏度的温度设置。然而，选择爆米花品牌有点困难。考虑到与温度的相互作用，选择品牌 A 比品牌 B 节省约 0.5% 的浪费。然而，考虑到与勺数的相互作用，品牌 B 比品牌 A 节省了近 1% 的浪费。
考虑到本实验中的所有信息和分析，六西格玛团队可能会说品牌不是一个足够大的因素（因此特许摊位可以根据其他因素（例如成本）来选择品牌），或者建议当品牌B的价格等于或低于品牌A时购买，因为品牌B与最高的浪费百分比相关。如果品牌 B 比品牌 A 更贵，那么与品牌 B 相关的少量浪费可能会减少，从而使品牌 A 成为更好的选择。这很好地说明了其他数据应如何在决策中发挥作用——设计好的实验并不存在于真空中。
这是一个基本分析，可帮助您了解交互如何工作。 Minitab等统计分析软件自动运行所有这些分析，减少了六西格玛专家的工作量。大多数此类软件还将考虑所有可能的相互作用，并呈现在分析中影响最大的因素。如果您有一个包含三个因子的 2k 阶乘实验，每个因子有两个设置，并且这些因子是 A、B 和 C，则可能会对结果产生以下影响：
A（仅更改 A 的影响）
B（仅改变B的影响）
C（仅更改 C 的影响）
AB（A 和 B 的相互作用）
AC（A 和 C 的相互作用）
BC（B 和 C 的相互作用）
ABC（所有三个因素的相互作用）
添加额外的因素会使分析变得更加复杂；在每个因素上添加额外的水平会起到同样的作用。
2k 阶乘与多级阶乘
到目前为止，在本单元中，使用的所有 DOE 场景都是二级（或 2k）阶乘实验。在每个实验中，六西格玛团队选择将每个因素的水平设置为不超过两个——通常是高和低。
六西格玛团队为何会选择这么做？多因素水平的实验不会为团队提供更多信息吗？在爆米花的例子中，为什么团队会选择高的
和低温设置？当温度设置为 500 华氏度时会发生什么？ 425、450、475 等呢？团队如何知道 400 和 600 是两个最佳设置？
事实上，团队并不知道这些是最佳设置，但为一个或多个因素添加多个级别会增加必须完成的运行次数。先前描述的爆米花实验的一次重复需要运行八次。只需再添加一个温度因素（500 华氏度）即可增加实验四次。
行号
品牌温度内核
1 A 400 华氏度 1 勺
2 A 400 华氏度 2 勺
3 A 500 华氏度 1 勺
4 A 500 华氏度 2 勺
5 A 600 华氏度 1 勺
6 A 600 华氏度 2 勺
7 B 400 华氏度 1 勺
8 B 400 华氏度 2 勺
9 B 500 华氏度 1 勺
10 B 500 华氏度 2 勺
11 B 600 华氏度 1 勺
12 B 600 华氏度 2 勺

现在，该团队将进行 48 次运行，而不是包含四次重复的设计实验的 32 次运行。这需要 16 次额外的运行，需要使用团队和公司的时间和资源。 如果团队还想测试 425、450、475、525、550 和 575 的温度设置，则每次复制需要额外运行 24 次，或者四次复制需要额外运行 96 次。如果团队还决定对其他因素添加额外的水平，例如第三个品牌或尝试使用 1.5 勺玉米粒，则团队可能会考虑进行数百次运行的实验，以达到适当的样本量。
由于这些原因，2k 因子实验通常是六西格玛专家中最常用的。 Minitab 允许您使用多达 15 个不同因素运行这些实验，从而提供强大的过程分析能力。 Minitab 还允许您运行两个以上因子的实验。
多因子实验的处理方式与 2k 因子实验的处理方式大致相同。显然，如果您手动处理分析，您将希望尽可能地限制级别和因素。 Minitab 消除了由于分析要求而限制数据的需要 – 只要团队
如果能够定义一个实验中需要包含的因素和水平，并且已被授予使用运行适当数量的重复次数以获得准确统计结论所需的资源的权限和权限，那么六西格玛团队当然可以运行多水平因素实验。

title.en: Tips for Creating Successful Designed Experiments
title.zh: 创建成功设计实验的技巧

en.text: As with almost any other tool in the Six Sigma arena, ANOVA and design of experiment are only as strong as the foundation you create each time you run an experiment and associated analysis. In addition to the instructions and information in this unit, this section provides some tips for successful experiments and analysis.

Take Time to Think About the Y, or Response, of Your Experiment In chapter 29, defining the Y, or response, of the experiment is one of the first steps in the procedure for conducting a DOE. Remember, a good response is related to the overall DMAIC process or the overall need associated with the experiment. The team must know what it wants to address and whether the response it is considering is a good measurement.
Often, training materials or tips for DOE in Six Sigma provide two pieces of common advice:
Ensure that the Y is continuous for the purpose of later analysis
Take the Y from brainstorming or other work done in previous phases – specifically from Define or Analyze tools such as fishbone diagrams.

While those are both pieces of good advice, Six Sigma teams shouldn’t be afraid to think outside of the box when it comes to the response for an experiment. Ultimately, the response should be something that will help you truly understand what you set out to understand at the beginning of the experiment. In the popcorn experiment used as an example in this chapter, the team could have chosen any number of responses. Possible responses from the popcorn popping process might include how long the overall process took, how many kernels were popped, how many orders were generated by each process, or customer satisfaction with the taste of the popcorn that was popped. But the team wasn’t seeking answers to questions related to production efficiency or product performance with this particular experiment. The team was asking questions about waste of raw materials, and the amount of unpopped kernels let the team know exactly how much waste was created in the process.

When considering an appropriate Y for your experiment, don’t get too caught up in the current metrics a process or department uses to measure success. The concession stand probably never considered unpopped popcorn before this experiment – it likely used number of sales or sales dollars as a metric for success. If you’re having trouble identifying a Y that you think will help answer the questions the team is asking, consider conducting a brand new brainstorming exercise focused solely on coming up with an appropriate result type.
Plan Ahead, and Keep Excellent Documents

You’ve seen throughout this unit that experiments and ANOVA analysis can become complex quickly. First, you have to validate all assumptions, select the right method, and choose an appropriate sample size. Then, you have to run the experiments and record all the data. Finally, you have to conduct the analysis. At any point in the process, you might introduce an error or create confusion. Planning and documentation are critical to keeping this from happening, and they can be equally important when you
present the conclusions of your experiment to others. Some specific areas where documentation is especially important are highlighted below.

Take notes when working through either the ANOVA steps in chapter 28 or the DOE steps from chapter 29. You will forget or confuse some number or element of the analysis at some point and need to refer back to the notes. Consider recording:

o Your reasons for choosing a specific method or test. o Lists of responses, inputs, factors, and levels with a reason for choosing each. You might need this information to justify the number of runs in your experiment. o The process for choosing sample size and any data or assumptions used in that process.

Record any outside assumptions made regarding the experiment, the components of the experiment, or the result. You might need to review these assumptions or data linked to the assumptions to rule out outliers or understand what might seem like otherwise unexplainable experiment behavior.

Document any instructions or information provided to any operator within the experiment. Your sponsor or other decision-makers will likely want to understand exactly how the experiment was performed before they make decisions based off of the results.
The outcome of any measurement system analysis, particularly as it relates to the confidence in experiment outcomes.
Account for Confidence Levels

In any type of statistical analysis, testing, or DOE, the Six Sigma expert is always mindful of confidence levels. There is always some chance of an incorrect conclusion – the alpha and beta errors covered in the chapters on hypothesis testing tell us this. More accurate data, larger sample sizes, and choosing the right test or experiment help reduce the chance of these errors, but decision-makers do need to know how big of a possibility such an error might be.

When reporting the outcome of a designed experiment, make sure you take all confidence levels into account. The confidence interval for the actual experiment is not always the only relevant consideration. Did you make assumptions based on previous analysis to help you set up the experiment? What were the confidence intervals associated with those analyses? Consider the popcorn example again: how sure is the team that each scoop holds an average of 2,300 kernels? What is the variation of that measurement? The answers to both of these questions contribute to the accuracy of the experiment analysis.

If enterprise leadership or a Six Sigma team will be basing resource-heavy or resource-contingent decisions on DOE analysis, then those decision-makers must know how confident the Six Sigma expert is in what he or she is presenting. You should always do everything you can statistically to increase confidence – such as validating data and assumptions and choosing proper sample sizes – but you should never falsely inflate confidence in a conclusion to encourage leadership to support a certain decision.
Use Multiple Analysis and Experiments Together

You should not – and often cannot – do everything you want to do in a single experiment. If there are 25 possible factors, do you really want them all in a single, unwieldy experiment that could become costly to run and impossible to control? Often, when you are considering running an experiment with so many

factors, you are actually trying to integrate multiple experiments into one. Stop and consider the possibility that you are comparing apples and oranges in an experiment when you really should run separate experiments for various product types or processes.

If you are actually working on a single product type or process, consider that you might need to run experiments serially. You might need to find out a certain piece of information or narrow down interactions and factors to better set up other experiments with new factors and level settings. For example, the Six Sigma team in the popcorn example isn’t done with their project just because they’ve found out how to best pop the popcorn to reduce waste. There are other ways to increase profits. Since brand wasn’t a major factor in the waste-related experiment, the team might conduct a second experiment to find out which brand customers like more and whether increased customer satisfaction drives increased sales.

Designed experiments are one of the more challenging aspects of Six Sigma to learn because exact rules don’t always exist. Six Sigma teams can set up experiments in a variety of ways, which increases the flexibility of the tool but can also make it difficult to know exactly how to proceed. This is an area where experience pays off, so the more experiments and analysis you handle, the better you are at setting up DOE. This is also a reason it’s a good idea to work with a more experienced Six Sigma expert or a Master Black Belt, who can act as a sounding board for your ideas and guide you in creating the best possible experiment.

zh.text: 与六西格玛领域中的几乎任何其他工具一样，方差分析（ANOVA）和实验设计的可靠性取决于您每次运行实验和相关分析时创建的基础。除了本单元中的说明和信息之外，本节还提供了一些成功实验和分析的提示。
花时间思考实验的 Y（即响应） 在第 29 章中，定义实验的 Y（即响应）是进行 DOE 过程中的首要步骤之一。请记住，良好的响应与整个 DMAIC 流程或与实验相关的总体需求有关。团队必须知道它想要解决什么问题，以及它正在考虑的响应是否是一个很好的衡量标准。

rows: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
zh.text: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
通常，六西格玛的 DOE 培训材料或提示提供两条常见建议：
确保Y连续，以便后面分析
从头脑风暴或之前阶段完成的其他工作中获取 Y，特别是从定义或分析工具（例如鱼骨图）中获取 Y。

rows: 术语说明 Fishbone / Ishikawa（鱼骨图）：按类别梳理潜在原因的根因分析工具。
zh.text: 术语说明 Fishbone / Ishikawa（鱼骨图）：按类别梳理潜在原因的根因分析工具。

虽然这些都是很好的建议，但六西格玛团队在考虑实验响应时不应该害怕跳出框框思考。最终，响应应该能够帮助你真正理解你在实验开始时想要理解的内容。在本章用作示例的爆米花实验中，团队可以选择任意数量的响应。爆米花过程的可能响应可能包括整个过程需要多长时间、爆出多少粒玉米粒、每个过程生成多少订单或客户对爆米花味道的满意度。但该团队并不是通过这个特定的实验来寻找与生产效率或产品性能相关的问题的答案。该团队询问了有关原材料浪费的问题，未爆开的玉米粒的数量让团队确切地知道在此过程中产生了多少废物。
在为您的实验考虑合适的 Y 时，不要太关注流程或部门用来衡量成功的当前指标。在这次实验之前，小卖部可能从未考虑过未爆开的爆米花——它很可能使用销售额或销售额作为成功的衡量标准。如果您无法确定您认为有助于回答团队提出的问题的 Y，请考虑进行一次全新的头脑风暴练习，仅专注于提出适当的结果类型。
提前计划并保留优秀的文件
在本单元中您已经看到，实验和方差分析（ANOVA）可能很快就会变得复杂。首先，您必须验证所有假设，选择正确的方法，并选择适当的样本量。然后，您必须运行实验并记录所有数据。最后，你必须进行分析。在此过程中的任何时候，您都可能会引入错误或造成混乱。规划和文档对于防止这种情况发生至关重要，并且当您
向他人展示你的实验结论。下面重点介绍了文档特别重要的一些特定领域。
在执行第 28 章中的 ANOVA 步骤或第 29 章中的 DOE 步骤时记笔记。在某些时候，您可能会忘记或混淆分析的某些数字或元素，需要回顾注释。考虑记录：
o 您选择特定方法或测试的原因。 o 反应、输入、因素和水平的列表以及选择每个因素的原因。您可能需要此信息来证明实验中运行次数的合理性。 o 选择样本量以及该过程中使用的任何数据或假设的过程。
记录有关实验、实验组成部分或结果的任何外部假设。您可能需要检查这些假设或与假设相关的数据，以排除异常值或了解可能看似无法解释的实验行为。
记录实验中向任何操作员提供的任何说明或信息。您的申办者或其他决策者可能希望在根据结果做出决策之前准确了解实验是如何进行的。
任何测量系统分析的结果，尤其是与实验结果的置信度相关的结果。

rows: 术语说明 MSA（测量系统分析）：评估测量工具、人员和方法是否足以支持可靠决策。
zh.text: 术语说明 MSA（测量系统分析）：评估测量工具、人员和方法是否足以支持可靠决策。
考虑置信水平
在任何类型的统计分析、测试或 DOE 中，六西格玛专家始终关注置信度。总是有可能得出错误的结论——假设检验章节中涵盖的阿尔法和贝塔错误告诉我们这一点。更准确的数据、更大的样本量以及选择正确的测试或实验有助于减少出现这些错误的可能性，但决策者确实需要知道这种错误的可能性有多大。

rows: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
zh.text: 术语说明 Hypothesis Test（假设检验）：用样本数据判断某个统计假设是否有足够证据被拒绝。
在报告设计实验的结果时，请确保考虑到所有置信水平。实际实验的置信区间并不总是唯一相关的考虑因素。您是否根据之前的分析做出假设来帮助您设置实验？与这些分析相关的置信区间是多少？再次考虑爆米花的例子：团队对每勺平均含有 2,300 颗爆米花的把握有多大？该测量的变化是什么？这两个问题的答案都有助于实验分析的准确性。
如果企业领导层或六西格玛团队将根据DOE分析做出资源密集型或资源偶然型决策，那么决策者必须知道六西格玛专家对他或她所展示的内容有多大信心。您应该始终尽一切努力从统计角度来增强信心，例如验证数据和假设以及选择适当的样本量，但您绝不能错误地夸大结论的信心，以鼓励领导层支持某个决策。
结合使用多种分析和实验
您不应该（而且通常也不能）在一次实验中完成您想做的所有事情。如果有 25 个可能的因素，您是否真的希望将所有这些因素都集中在一个笨拙的实验中，而且运行成本可能会很高并且无法控制？通常，当您考虑进行如此多的实验时
因素，你实际上是在尝试将多个实验整合为一个。当您确实应该对各种产品类型或流程进行单独的实验时，停下来考虑一下您在实验中比较苹果和橙子的可能性。

如果您实际上正在研究单一产品类型或流程，请考虑您可能需要连续运行实验。您可能需要找出某条信息或缩小交互作用和因素的范围，以便更好地使用新因素和水平设置来设置其他实验。例如，爆米花示例中的六西格玛团队并不仅仅因为找到了如何最好地爆爆米花以减少浪费而完成了他们的项目。还有其他方法可以增加利润。由于品牌并不是浪费相关实验的主要因素，因此团队可能会进行第二次实验，以找出客户更喜欢哪个品牌，以及客户满意度的提高是否会带动销量的增加。
设计实验是六西格玛中更具挑战性的方面之一，因为确切的规则并不总是存在。 六西格玛团队可以通过多种方式设置实验，这增加了工具的灵活性，但也可能让人很难确切地知道如何进行。这是一个经验丰富的领域，因此您处理的实验和分析越多，您就越能更好地设置 DOE。 这也是与更有经验的六西格玛专家或黑带大师合作的好主意，他们可以为你的想法提供共鸣，并指导你创造最好的实验。

title.en: Chapter 31: Brainstorming and Process Improvement Tools
title.zh: 第 31 章：头脑风暴与流程改进工具

source.extraction: python-docx body-order extraction; source-TOC sectionized bilingual content

title.zh: 头脑风暴与流程改进工具

en.text: Throughout this book, you’ve learned to deploy a number of Six Sigma tools, including brainstorming tools. Some tools you’ve already learned to use include the SIPOC diagram, the in-and-out-of-the-box method, and the 5 Whys brainstorming model. This chapter provides an appendix of additional tools and methods that can be integrated into meetings for DMAIC projects or simply to help a team or department gain insight into a problem or process.

As a Six Sigma expert or process leader, having access to multiple process improvement and brainstorming tools is helpful. While all of the tools previously covered in this book are extremely powerful when used correctly, if you use the same approach all the time, you risk coming up with the same solutions and hitting the same obstacles. Sometimes, switching the brainstorming method or tool can help break a team out of a rut, encouraging a different perspective or sparking different discussions or debates.

zh.text: 在本书中，您学习了如何部署许多六西格玛工具，包括头脑风暴工具。您已经学会使用的一些工具包括 SIPOC 图、现成方法和 5 个为什么头脑风暴模型。本章提供了附加工具和方法的附录，这些工具和方法可以集成到 DMAIC 项目的会议中，或者只是为了帮助团队或部门深入了解问题或流程。

rows: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
zh.text: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。

rows: 术语说明 SIPOC：供应商、输入、流程、输出、客户；用于快速界定流程边界。
zh.text: 术语说明 SIPOC：供应商、输入、流程、输出、客户；用于快速界定流程边界。

rows: 术语说明 5 Whys（五个为什么）：连续追问原因，帮助从表面症状深入到根本原因。
zh.text: 术语说明 5 Whys（五个为什么）：连续追问原因，帮助从表面症状深入到根本原因。
作为六西格玛专家或流程领导者，能够使用多种流程改进和头脑风暴工具会很有帮助。虽然本书前面介绍的所有工具在正确使用时都非常强大，但如果您一直使用相同的方法，您就有可能提出相同的解决方案并遇到相同的障碍。有时，转换头脑风暴方法或工具可以帮助团队打破陈规，鼓励不同的观点或引发不同的讨论或辩论。
活动网络图

en.text: An activity network diagram helps a Six Sigma team chart either the activity involved in a project or the activity involved in a process. The point of the diagram is to understand sequential and time relationships within a project or process. Which items come first? Which activities happen at the same time?

When applied to an overall project, the activity network diagram helps teams and project leaders understand what resources will be required at each point during the project. It can also provide project managers with the ability to better estimate project timelines.

When applied to a process, the diagram helps teams identify all the activities in a process. It also helps teams understand which activities are up- and downstream and what activities happen together. Once teams know these things, they can begin to discuss whether the current sequence is the most effective sequence and understand how changes to one activity might impact the outcome of another.
An activity network diagram is created by following these steps:

1. List all of the activities involved in the process. Teams can draw from their own knowledge or from previous tools used by the group such as SIPOC or process maps. 2. Write each activity on a separate card or sticky note. 3. Place activities in order from left to right. Activities that are simultaneous are placed in a vertical line in one horizontal space on the diagram. 4. Write an estimated time for each activity.

5. Draw a critical path line through each step. Where vertical (simultaneous steps) are noted, the line goes through the step that takes the longest. 6. Add up the times listed along the critical path to get an idea of the overall estimate of time for the process. In the example below, the total time for the critical path in minutes is: 5 + 2 + 12 + 9 + 10 + 5, or 43.

zh.text: 活动网络图可以帮助六西格玛团队绘制项目中涉及的活动或流程中涉及的活动的图表。该图的目的是理解项目或流程中的顺序和时间关系。哪些项目先出现？哪些活动同时发生？
当应用于整个项目时，活动网络图可以帮助团队和项目负责人了解项目期间每个点需要哪些资源。它还可以为项目经理提供更好地估计项目时间表的能力。
当应用于流程时，该图可以帮助团队识别流程中的所有活动。它还可以帮助团队了解哪些活动是上游活动和下游活动以及哪些活动一起发生。一旦团队了解了这些事情，他们就可以开始讨论当前序列是否是最有效的序列，并了解一项活动的更改可能如何影响另一项活动的结果。
活动网络图是通过以下步骤创建的：
1. 列出流程中涉及的所有活动。团队可以从自己的知识或团队以前使用的工具（例如 SIPOC 或流程图）中汲取经验。 2. 将每项活动写在单独的卡片或便利贴上。 3. 从左到右排列活动。 同时进行的活动被放置在图上一个水平空间的垂直线上。 4. 写下每项活动的预计时间。

rows: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。
zh.text: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。
5. 画出每一步的关键路径线。在注明垂直（同时步骤）的地方，该线会经过耗时最长的步骤。 6. 将关键路径上列出的时间相加，以了解流程的总体时间估计。在下面的示例中，关键路径的总时间（以分钟为单位）为：5 + 2 + 12 + 9 + 10 + 5，即 43。

en.text: An affinity diagram is a tool that lets the team list and group a large number of ideas quickly and systematically. Affinity diagrams work well when you are first approaching a process or idea because they help you sort through all of the associated ideas to identify some of the main points the team will need to consider throughout the DMAIC project. Affinity diagrams are also a good tool to use if you have a larger-than-normal brainstorming group, which means they can be used in department meetings or in DMAIC meetings that include subject matter experts that aren’t part of the core team. The goal of an affinity diagram is to help the group understand the relationships between inputs, outputs, products, services, workers, customers, processes, and ideas – or any subgroup of those things.

To conduct an affinity diagram, you’ll need a large workspace, such as a flat table, wall, or floor. You can also use a white board, and if you are working with a group across multiple locations, you can conduct the exercise via web conferencing software, though, as with most brainstorming tools, this would not be optimal.
An affinity diagram session is conducted by following the steps outlined below.

1. Provide everyone in the session with small notecards, sticky notes, or pieces of paper. 2. Begin brainstorming ideas about the process or problem. Ideas at this stage are very loose – encourage the team to write down anything that comes to mind. The ideas should be short – one or two words or very simple sentences, otherwise the team might be recording two ideas

on one note card. Encourage participants to record inputs, outputs, challenges, obstacles, customers, people, processes, actions, etc. If you are attempting to address a specific question, that question should drive the type of things recorded on the notes. 3. Brainstorming can occur individually for a few minutes or as a group. Individual brainstorming is likely to present a number of ideas that aren’t influenced by others’ input, which can be valuable. 4. After brainstorming, have everyone spread the cards on a floor or table, or stick the notes to a wall or white board. 5. Work as a team to identify ideas that are related. Place those ideas next to each other. Consider working as a team without talking during this step to shift ideas around the diagram for a few minutes until most ideas are lumped into larger groups. 6. If one idea could belong to more than one group, make multiple cards with that idea on it so it can be put with multiple groups. 7. It’s okay to have groups of one when an idea doesn’t seem to fit with anything else. 8. Once all the notes are grouped, hold a discussion time with the team to answer questions:
a. Are there patterns in the ideas?

b. Does the diagram spark any new ideas or clarify ideas or relationships? c. Do ideas fall into natural sequences for processes or timelines? d. Do ideas fall into natural groups of causes? 9. The questions asked and answered will depend on what process or overall question the team is addressing with the affinity diagram. 10. Title each group of ideas by pulling out what seems to be the main idea of that grouping. 11. Record the diagram for use later. You can have someone copy it onto a piece of paper, convert it to a computer diagram, or simply snap a digital picture for storing with other team documents.

zh.text: 亲和图是一种工具，可以让团队快速、系统地列出和分组大量想法。当您第一次接触某个流程或想法时，亲和图效果很好，因为它们可以帮助您对所有相关想法进行排序，以确定团队在整个 DMAIC 项目中需要考虑的一些要点。如果您有一个比正常规模更大的头脑风暴小组，亲和图也是一个很好的工具，这意味着它们可以在部门会议或 DMAIC 会议中使用，其中包括不属于核心团队的主题专家。亲和图的目标是帮助团队理解输入、输出、产品、服务、工人、客户、流程和想法之间的关系，或者这些事物的任何子组之间的关系。
要绘制亲和图，您需要一个大的工作空间，例如平坦的桌子、墙壁或地板。您还可以使用白板，如果您与多个地点的团队合作，则可以通过网络会议软件进行练习，但与大多数头脑风暴工具一样，这并不是最佳选择。
亲和图会话是按照下面概述的步骤进行的。
1. 为会议中的每个人提供小记事卡、便利贴或纸片。 2. 开始集思广益关于流程或问题的想法。这个阶段的想法非常松散——鼓励团队写下想到的任何东西。这些想法应该很短——一两个词或非常简单的句子，否则团队可能会记录两个想法

在一张记事卡上。鼓励参与者记录输入、输出、挑战、障碍、客户、人员、流程、行动等。如果您试图解决一个特定问题，该问题应该驱动笔记上记录的内容类型。 3. 头脑风暴可以单独进行几分钟，也可以集体进行。个人头脑风暴可能会提出许多不受他人意见影响的想法，这些想法可能很有价值。 4. 集思广益后，让每个人将卡片铺在地板或桌子上，或将笔记贴在墙上或白板上。 5. 团队合作找出相关的想法。将这些想法放在一起。考虑在这一步中以团队形式工作，不进行交谈，围绕图表转移想法几分钟，直到大多数想法集中到更大的组中。 6. 如果一个想法可以属于多个组，请制作多张带有该想法的卡片，以便可以将其放入多个组中。 7. 当一个想法似乎与其他想法不相符时，可以由一个人组成小组。 8. 将所有笔记分组后，与团队进行讨论以回答问题：
a.想法中有模式吗？
b.该图是否激发了任何新想法或澄清了想法或关系？ c.想法是否符合流程或时间表的自然顺序？ d.想法是否属于自然原因组？ 9. 提出和回答的问题将取决于团队使用亲和图解决的流程或总体问题。 10. 找出每组想法的标题，找出该组想法的主要想法。 11.记录该图以供以后使用。您可以让人将其复制到一张纸上，将其转换为计算机图表，或者简单地拍摄数码照片以与其他团队文档一起存储。

en.text: An interrelationship diagram is similar to a fishbone diagram in purpose. The goal of an interrelationship brainstorm is to understand what causes and effects are primary with regard to a problem or process. The outcome of the brainstorming session is a visual depiction that makes it easy to see which items are likely causing most of the effects and which effects are tied to which causes within a process.

As a Six Sigma expert you can deploy this exercise when teams don’t fully understand the relationships between causes and effects within a process, can’t agree on which causal factors might be primary within the process, or are just beginning to understand the process and need more overall knowledge before moving into a fishbone diagram process or measuring processes.
Conduct an interrelationship diagram session by following these steps:

1. Define the problem on a white board, piece of paper, or sticky note. 2. On other sticky notes, write brainstormed ideas about the problem. Specifically, what might have caused the problem? What might be causing things that cause the problems? What other issues might the problem cause? 3. Place the notes all around the problem on the white board.

4. As a team, consider each idea on each note. Decide whether that idea is somehow related to any other idea on the board. 5. If the idea is related in such a way to a second idea that it could cause, or be an input or factor, for another idea, draw an arrow going from the first idea to the one it might be an input, factor, or cause for. 6. If the idea is related in such a way to a second idea that it might be an effect or output from the second idea, draw an arrow going from the second idea to the first idea. 7. If necessary, draw multiple arrows to and from each idea. 8. At the end of the exercise, count the number of arrows coming in to the idea. Write those numbers in one color on each sticky note. 9. Count the number of arrows going out from each idea. Write those numbers in another color on each sticky note. 10. The notes with the highest total count – when you add those two numbers – are likely the key factors in the process or problem. You might also be able to identify key inputs (causes) and outputs (effects) based on the number of arrows coming in or out of various notes.

zh.text: 相互关系图的目的类似于鱼骨图。相互关系头脑风暴的目标是了解问题或过程的主要原因和影响。头脑风暴会议的结果是一种视觉描述，可以很容易地看出哪些项目可能导致大部分影响，以及哪些影响与流程中的哪些原因相关。

rows: 术语说明 Fishbone / Ishikawa（鱼骨图）：按类别梳理潜在原因的根因分析工具。
zh.text: 术语说明 Fishbone / Ishikawa（鱼骨图）：按类别梳理潜在原因的根因分析工具。
作为六西格玛专家，当团队没有完全理解流程中因果关系、无法就流程中哪些因果因素可能是主要因素达成一致、或者刚刚开始理解流程并在进入鱼骨图流程或测量流程之前需要更多整体知识时，您可以部署此练习。
按照以下步骤进行相互关系图会话：
1. 在白板、纸或便利贴上定义问题。 2. 在其他便签上，写下关于该问题的集思广益的想法。具体来说，是什么原因导致了这个问题？造成问题的原因可能是什么？该问题还可能导致哪些其他问题？ 3. 将问题周围的注释放在白板上。

4. 作为一个团队，考虑每个笔记上的每个想法。确定该想法是否与板上的任何其他想法有某种关系。 5. 如果这个想法与第二个想法相关，并且它可能导致另一个想法，或者成为另一个想法的输入或因素，则绘制一个从第一个想法到它可能是输入、因素或原因的箭头。 6. 如果该想法与第二个想法的关联方式可能是第二个想法的效果或输出，则绘制一个从第二个想法到第一个想法的箭头。 7. 如有必要，在每个想法之间绘制多个箭头。 8. 练习结束时，数一下进入该想法的箭头数量。将这些数字用一种颜色写在每张便签上。 9. 数一下从每个想法射出的箭头数量。用另一种颜色将这些数字写在每张便签上。 10. 总计数最高的注释（当您将这两个数字相加时）可能是流程或问题中的关键因素。您还可以根据进出各种音符的箭头数量来识别关键输入（原因）和输出（结果）。

en.text: A lot of Six Sigma education has to do with statistical analysis, but the methodology doesn’t work if Six Sigma leaders are too caught up in the numbers and data to realize that people play an important role in every process, every project, and every improvement initiative. One way Six Sigma teams can understand how people and other factors might support or challenge a process, project, or change is to conduct a force field analysis.

A force field analysis lets teams understand how factors surrounding a process might support (or drive) change or challenge (or restrain) change. In addition to people, factors might include culture, materials and resources requirements, customers, needs, machines, rules, and regulations. Sometimes, Six Sigma teams can work to change some of these factors to create additional support for an improvement. In other cases, the team cannot change restraining factors, but must be aware of those factors as they move forward with improvement initiatives.
Create a force field analysis by following these steps:

1. Start with the definition of the problem. This can be either the overall problem statement or a more detailed and smaller problem within the scope of the entire project. 2. Write the problem on the top of a white board or large piece of paper. 3. Draw a bold line down the center of the page. This line represents the status quo – the current state of the process. 4. Define the solution or desired state. If the team is just starting out, the desired state might be the overall project objective statement. If the team is working to solve a specific problem within the project scope, it might have a solution in mind. 5. Write the solution or objective statement at the bottom of the white board or page. 6. Brainstorm driving factors – factors that would move the status quo “forward”. 7. Write driving factors on the left side of the status quo line. 8. Brainstorm restraining factors – factors that would keep the status quo as is. 9. Write restraining factors on the right side of the status quo line.

10. Draw an arrow under each driving factor toward the status quo line; the length of each arrow represents the perceived strength of the driving factor. For example, the sponsor who holds financial decisions is on board with the solution. This would be a strong driving factor and might get a long arrow. 11. Draw an arrow under each restraining factor back toward the status quo line. The length of each arrow represents the perceived strength of the challenge. If the sponsor wasn’t on board with the solution yet, this would be a fairly strong restraining factor.
Once the diagram is completed, the team can see quickly:
Whether there are more driving factors or restraining factors
Whether driving factors or restraining factors have more “weight” (based on the number of longer arrows on either side)
Which factors are strongest
The team can then discuss whether any of the restraining factors can be weakened or converted to driving factors, which can be helpful in paving the way for smoother solution implementation.

zh.text: 六西格玛教育的很多内容都与统计分析有关，但如果六西格玛领导者过于沉迷于数字和数据，而没有意识到人在每个流程、每个项目和每个改进举措中发挥着重要作用，那么这种方法就不起作用。六西格玛团队了解人员和其他因素如何支持或挑战流程、项目或变革的一种方法是进行力场分析。
力场分析使团队能够了解流程周围的因素如何支持（或驱动）变革或挑战（或抑制）变革。除了人员之外，因素还可能包括文化、材料和资源要求、客户、需求、机器、规则和法规。有时，六西格玛团队可以努力改变其中一些因素，为改进创造额外的支持。 在其他情况下，团队无法改变限制因素，但在推进改进计划时必须意识到这些因素。
按照以下步骤创建力场分析：

1. 从问题的定义开始。这可以是总体问题陈述，也可以是整个项目范围内更详细、更小的问题。 2. 将问题写在白板或大纸上。 3. 在页面中央画一条粗线。这条线代表现状——流程的当前状态。 4. 定义解决方案或所需状态。如果团队刚刚起步，所需的状态可能是总体项目目标陈述。如果团队正在努力解决项目范围内的特定问题，那么它可能会想到一个解决方案。 5. 将解决方案或目标陈述写在白板或页面的底部。 6.集思广益的驱动因素——推动现状“前进”的因素。 7. 将驱动因素写在现状行的左侧。 8.集思广益，讨论限制因素——维持现状的因素。 9. 将限制因素写在现状线的右侧。

rows: 术语说明 Scope（项目范围）：说明改进项目包括什么、不包括什么，避免目标扩张。
zh.text: 术语说明 Scope（项目范围）：说明改进项目包括什么、不包括什么，避免目标扩张。
10. 在每个驱动因素下方画一个指向现状线的箭头；每个箭头的长度代表驱动因素的感知强度。例如，负责财务决策的发起人就支持该解决方案。这将是一个强大的驱动因素，并且可能会带来长箭。 11. 在每个限制因素下画一个箭头回到现状线。每个箭头的长度代表挑战的感知强度。如果发起人尚未同意该解决方案，这将是一个相当强大的限制因素。
图表完成后，团队可以快速看到：
是否存在较多驱动因素或制约因素
驱动因素或限制因素是否具有更大的“权重”（基于两侧较长箭头的数量）
哪些因素最强
然后，团队可以讨论是否可以削弱任何限制因素或将其转化为驱动因素，这有助于为更顺利的解决方案实施铺平道路。

en.text: A responsibility chart helps Six Sigma teams identify which people, teams, or departments have various types of connections or responsibilities to a process. The reason for understanding such relationships is for Six Sigma teams to understand who actually acts on or within the process, who needs to be informed about the process, who is ultimately accountable for the process, and who might offer valuable information about the process. Often, these various people can be identified with a charting process known as RACI, which stands for:
Responsible – the person or team who actually does the work (an employee who is responsible for collecting money at the cash register)
Accountable – the person in charge of the work (the supervisor of that shift)
Consult – any person involved in steps prior to the activity that should be kept in the loop (the sales person on the floor, the bookkeeper who creates starting drawers each morning)
Inform – any person involved in steps after the activity that should be kept in the loop (the bookkeeper, who also balances the drawers at the end of shifts)
Create a RACI matrix for your project or process by following these steps:

1. Create a list of all activities and decisions that are part of the process. Teams can get this information from previous brainstorming sessions or tools such as process maps. 2. List those items down the right side of a page or white board. 3. Create a list of all functional roles associated with a process. List those as the row headers across the top of the page or white board.
a. Activities and decisions might include items such as:
i. Measure lumber. ii. Cut lumber iii. Review lumber.
iv. Decide if lumber is the right length. b. Functional roles might include:

i. Lumber measurer ii. Lumber cutter iii. Auditor iv. Lumber packer 4. For each functional role and activity, decide if the role is responsible or accountable for each activity listed or if the role should be consulted or informed. In the example below, you can see that the cutter is responsible for measuring and cutting, the auditor is responsible for reviewing and deciding whether the lumber is correct, and the supervisor is accountable for all of it. The packer needs to be informed about whether the lumber is correct so he or she knows whether to pack it.
Cutter
Auditor Packer Supervisor Measure lumber R A Cut lumber R A Review lumber R A Decide if lumber is correct R I A

zh.text: 责任图帮助六西格玛团队识别哪些人员、团队或部门与流程有各种类型的联系或职责。理解这种关系的原因是让六西格玛团队了解谁在流程中或流程内实际行动，谁需要了解流程，谁最终对流程负责，以及谁可能提供有关流程的有价值的信息。通常，可以通过称为 RACI 的图表流程来识别这些不同的人，RACI 代表：
负责人 - 实际完成工作的个人或团队（负责在收银机收款的员工）
责任人——工作负责人（当班主管）
咨询 – 参与活动之前应保持循环的步骤的任何人员（现场销售人员、每天早上创建起始抽屉的簿记员）
通知 – 参与活动后应保持循环的步骤的任何人员（簿记员，他还在轮班结束时平衡抽屉）
按照以下步骤为您的项目或流程创建 RACI 矩阵：
1. 创建流程中所有活动和决策的列表。团队可以从之前的头脑风暴会议或流程图等工具中获取这些信息。 2. 在页面或白板的右侧列出这些项目。 3. 创建与流程关联的所有功能角色的列表。将它们列为页面或白板顶部的行标题。
a.活动和决策可能包括以下项目：
岛测量木材。二.砍伐木材 iii.审查木材。
四.确定木材的长度是否合适。 b.职能角色可能包括：
岛木材测量仪 ii.木材切割机 iii.审计师四。木材包装工 4. 对于每个职能角色和活动，确定该角色是否对列出的每项活动负责或负责，或者是否应咨询或通知该角色。在下面的示例中，您可以看到切割师负责测量和切割，审核员负责检查并确定木材是否正确，而主管则对这一切负责。包装工需要了解木材是否正确，以便他或她知道是否包装。
切刀
审核员 包装员 主管 测量木材 R A 切割木材 R A 审查木材 R A 确定木材是否正确 R I A

en.text: A Six Sigma leader has to develop a good understanding of group dynamics. Not every project team will work the same, and sometimes Six Sigma leaders face interpersonal challenges within the group environment. Some of these challenges can derail brainstorming efforts because of personality clashes. If you have one or more quiet or shy individuals, for example, you might not be getting all of their perspective. Even if everyone on the team is fairly vocal, if there is a natural leader, bully, or more outspoken member, they might be getting more ideas in than others. On way you can reduce the impact of personality on brainstorming sessions is to use the nominal group technique to encourage input from everyone.

1. Provide everyone with pen and paper. 2. Being with a question, topic, or concern that is relevant to the project or the phase you are in. 3. Take 5 to 10 minutes and invite everyone to brainstorm their own ideas. Make sure you note that at this stage, there are no bad or wrong ideas. 4. Once everyone is finished writing, go around the table, having each person state an idea from their list. Each person should only state one idea at a time before letting others have a turn. 5. Write the ideas down on a white board or flipchart, but don’t discuss the ideas at this time. 6. As ideas are shared, individuals can pass if every idea on their list has been shared by themselves or someone else. 7. Once all ideas are listed, lead a discussion based on the ideas or use the ideas to fuel a secondary activity, such as an affinity diagram or fishbone diagram.

Nominal group technique is also a good tool to use if the team is stuck or is dealing with a particularly difficult concept. The technique helps break thoughts out of ruts in two ways. First, it removes some of the fear individuals can have at sharing half-formed verbal ideas. Team members who are writing ideas down can think through those ideas a bit more and might feel more comfortable listing them later. Second, the act of writing itself is proven to break down creative and thought barriers, and can help the team members brainstorm more ideas.

zh.text: 六西格玛的领导者必须对团队动态有深入的了解。并非每个项目团队的工作方式都相同，有时六西格玛领导者会在团队环境中面临人际关系挑战。其中一些挑战可能会因性格冲突而使头脑风暴的努力脱轨。例如，如果你有一个或多个安静或害羞的人，你可能无法了解他们的全部观点。即使团队中的每个人都相当直言不讳，如果有一个天生的领导者、恶霸或更直言不讳的成员，他们可能会比其他人获得更多的想法。减少个性对头脑风暴会议影响的一种方法是使用名义小组技术来鼓励每个人的意见。

1. 为每个人提供笔和纸。 2. 提出与项目或您所处阶段相关的问题、主题或担忧。 3. 花 5 到 10 分钟时间，邀请每个人集思广益，讨论自己的想法。请务必注意，在这个阶段，没有坏的或错误的想法。 4. 每个人都写完后，绕桌子走一圈，让每个人从他们的清单中陈述一个想法。每个人一次只能陈述一个想法，然后再让其他人发言。 5. 将想法写在白板或活动挂图上，但此时不要讨论这些想法。 6. 当想法被共享时，如果列表中的每个想法都被他们自己或其他人共享过，那么个人就可以通过。 7. 列出所有想法后，根据这些想法进行讨论或使用这些想法来推动次要活动，例如亲和图或鱼骨图。
如果团队陷入困境或正在处理一个特别困难的概念，名义小组技术也是一个很好的工具。该技术可以通过两种方式帮助我们打破思维定势。首先，它消除了个人在分享半成形的口头想法时可能产生的一些恐惧。写下想法的团队成员可以进一步思考这些想法，并且稍后列出它们可能会更舒服。其次，事实证明，写作本身可以打破创意和思维障碍，并可以帮助团队成员集思广益。

en.text: Check sheets as a tool have been touched on multiple times in this book, but it’s worth mentioning them again in a more formal way. Check sheets are simple tools that can be extremely powerful for managing teams, people, processes, and data. The reason check sheets are so powerful is that they require an action, and when you require a physical and recordable action, personal responsibility for the action involved is usually higher.

A check sheet is any templated form used to record information. The information you record can be a specific piece of data, but it might also be a notation that a routine check, analysis, or certain other task was performed. The purpose of a check sheet, beyond formalizing and mandating an action, is to streamline the process of reporting the action.

Consider the check sheets often seen in public restroom facilities in restaurants or stores. These check sheets are usually on the door or wall near the door. Each hour, a staff member initials and dates the check sheet to indicate that the restroom was checked. By requiring the staff member to initial and date the check sheet, companies make it more likely staff will check the restroom at appropriate times. The initial and date indicates that staff ensured the facilities were stocked, clean, and in working order.

This premise can be used in a variety of processes to ensure data collection or certain tasks are performed at the right time by the right person. Check sheets can also be used to manage tasks within a project environment – a Six Sigma leader might provide check sheets to each subject matter expert during a project meeting so that each person knows what needs to be done before the next meeting and has a physical requirement for recording process.
As a Six Sigma expert, it’s important to understand when the simplest solution fits your need, and the checklist is often that solution.

zh.text: 本书多次提到检查表作为一种工具，但值得以更正式的方式再次提及。检查表是简单的工具，对于管理团队、人员、流程和数据来说非常强大。检查表之所以如此强大，是因为它们需要采取行动，而当您需要采取实际且可记录的行动时，个人对所涉及行动的责任通常会更高。
检查表是用于记录信息的任何模板表格。您记录的信息可以是特定的数据，但也可能是执行例行检查、分析或某些其他任务的表示法。检查表的目的除了使行动正式化和强制执行之外，还在于简化报告行动的过程。
考虑一下餐馆或商店的公共卫生间设施中常见的检查表。这些检查表通常位于门上或门附近的墙上。每小时，一名工作人员都会在检查表上签上姓名缩写并注明日期，以表明卫生间已被检查过。通过要求员工在检查表上签名并注明日期，公司可以让员工更有可能在适当的时间检查卫生间。姓名首字母和日期表明工作人员确保设施库存充足、干净且运转正常。
这一前提可用于各种流程，以确保数据收集或某些任务由正确的人在正确的时间执行。检查表还可以用于管理项目环境中的任务——六西格玛领导者可能会在项目会议期间向每个主题专家提供检查表，以便每个人都知道在下一次会议之前需要做什么，并对记录过程有实际要求。
作为六西格玛专家，重要的是要了解最简单的解决方案何时适合您的需求，而清单通常就是该解决方案。

en.text: SWOT analysis is a common business brainstorming technique that helps teams understand high-level strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats (challenges) for a project, improvement, or process. SWOT analyses can be conducted anytime during the DMAIC phases, but are especially helpful during define phases or anytime storming has occurred and the team needs something fast and easy to help them get back on track.

Some people break up SWOT analysis into a visual depiction, drawing a large grid of four squares, one each to represent Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats. This can be helpful if you have a project group that is especially visual, but is not necessary. The point of a SWOT analysis is really to answer some fundamental questions through brainstorming.
1. Strengths: What about the process works? What is done better, faster, cheaper, more unique?

2. Weaknesses: What doesn’t work? What are the disadvantages, problems, losses, errors, or customer dissatisfaction points? 3. Opportunities: What can be fixed? What are some easy-to-fix issues? Where are improvements needed? 4. Threats: What obstacles exist to improvement? What risks will the project team face? What resource challenges might there be?

zh.text: SWOT 分析是一种常见的商业头脑风暴技术，可帮助团队了解项目、改进或流程的高级优势、劣势、机会和威胁（挑战）。 SWOT 分析可以在 DMAIC 阶段的任何时间进行，但在定义阶段或发生风暴并且团队需要快速、简单的方法来帮助他们回到正轨时尤其有用。
有些人将 SWOT 分析分解为视觉描述，绘制一个由四个正方形组成的大网格，每个正方形代表优势、劣势、机会和威胁。如果您有一个特别视觉化的项目组，这会很有帮助，但这不是必需的。 SWOT 分析的要点实际上是通过头脑风暴来回答一些基本问题。
1. 优点：流程效果如何？什么做得更好、更快、更便宜、更独特？
2. 弱点：什么不起作用？有哪些缺点、问题、损失、错误或客户不满意点？ 3. 机会：什么可以解决？有哪些容易解决的问题？哪些地方需要改进？ 4. 威胁：改进存在哪些障碍？项目团队将面临哪些风险？可能存在哪些资源挑战？

en.text: Starburst brainstorming is a method for generating a series of questions about a problem, process, or idea. Those questions can later be used in defining processes, problems, products, or other ideas. On the surface, the starburst tool is similar to the 5 Whys, but instead of answering each question and then asking another question, the team simply comes up with as many various questions as possible.
Conduct a starburst brainstorming session by following these steps:

1. Draw a six-pointed star on a white board or large piece of paper. 2. On each of the points, write one of the following: Who, What, Where, When, Why, How. 3. At the center of the star, write one or a few words describing the central idea. 4. For each point of the star, brainstorm questions about the center that start with the word at that point. For example, a team might be working to create more customer satisfaction regarding a food-service product. For the purpose of this example, imagine a team working to solve a problem of customer satisfaction with an individually-wrapped snack cake that is typically sold in convenience stores and newsstands.
a. The center of the star might simply state “ABC Snack Cake.” b. Questions at the “Who?” tip of the star might include:
i. Who is the primary customer for the snack cake?
ii. Who is most likely to eat the snack cake? iii. Who will buy the snack cake? (not always the same person who eats it) iv. Who will sell the snack cake?
v. Who will market the snack cake?
vi. Who will make the snack cake? c. “What?” questions might include:
i. What flavor or flavors should the snack cake be?

ii. What packaging is best for the snack cake? iii. What ingredients are important to the snack cake? d. Go through all the points of the star, asking questions that match the word at that point. Generate as many questions as you can within a set period of time. It’s a good idea to set a time limit for question generation for each point of the star – up to five minutes is usually good – to keep questions from become too detailed or out of scope for the problem, process, or idea you are dealing with.

The purpose of the starburst session is not to answer the questions you come up with, and that can be a challenge for Six Sigma leaders. Team members will naturally want to move immediately from one question to an answer, which can derail the process and result in a discussion that is not productive or ranges out of scope. Direct team members back to asking questions for the duration of the brainstorming session. Those questions can later be compiled and used when conducting other
exercises, such as SIPOCs or process maps. The questions help ensure details aren’t left out and that all possible queries are considered.

zh.text: 星爆式头脑风暴是一种针对某个问题、流程或想法提出一系列问题的方法。这些问题稍后可用于定义流程、问题、产品或其他想法。从表面上看，星爆工具类似于“5个为什么”，但团队并没有回答每个问题然后提出另一个问题，而是简单地提出尽可能多的各种问题。
按照以下步骤进行星爆式头脑风暴会议：
1. 在白板或大纸上画一个六角星。 2. 在每一点上写下以下内容之一：人物、事件、地点、时间、原因、方式。 3. 在星星的中心，写一个或几个描述中心思想的单词。 4. 对于星形的每个点，集体讨论以该点的单词开头的中心问题。例如，一个团队可能正在努力提高客户对食品服务产品的满意度。出于此示例的目的，想象一个团队正在努力解决客户对通常在便利店和报摊出售的单独包装的零食蛋糕的满意度问题。
a.星星的中心可能只是简单地写着“ABC Snack Cake”。 b.关于“谁？”的问题星尖可能包括：
岛谁是零食蛋糕的主要客户？
二.谁最有可能吃零食蛋糕？三.谁会买零食蛋糕？ （吃的人不一定是同一个人） iv．谁来卖点心蛋糕？
v. 谁来销售零食蛋糕？
六.谁来做点心蛋糕？ c. “什么？”问题可能包括：
岛点心蛋糕应该是什么口味？
二.什么包装最适合零食蛋糕？三.零食蛋糕的哪些成分很重要？ d.浏览星星的所有点，提出与该点的单词匹配的问题。在规定的时间内提出尽可能多的问题。最好为星形的每个点设置问题生成的时间限制（通常最多五分钟），以防止问题变得过于详细或超出您正在处理的问题、流程或想法的范围。
星爆会议的目的不是回答你提出的问题，这对六西格玛领导人来说可能是一个挑战。团队成员自然会希望立即从一个问题转向一个答案，这可能会破坏流程并导致讨论没有成效或超出范围。指导团队成员在头脑风暴会议期间继续提问。这些问题可以在以后进行整理并在进行其他操作时使用
练习，例如 SIPOC 或流程图。这些问题有助于确保不遗漏细节并考虑所有可能的查询。

en.text: One of the biggest challenges with brainstorming as a Six Sigma team is that, after a few meetings or as the team gets deeper into the DMAIC process, the team can become stuck on certain beliefs and ideas. Once certain ideas are proposed, it can be hard to “get away” from them. Teams might find themselves circling back around to the same ideas repeatedly. Sometimes, this means the team is on the right track and the iteration of the same concepts throughout the DMAIC process and brainstorming is confirmation that those are the right things to be thinking about. Other times, however, it can be a sign that the team is stuck.

It can be difficult at times to tell the difference, and Six Sigma experts do have to rely on analysis and data to help make a decision about whether the team is going in the right direction or not. If a Six Sigma expert believes that the team is not going in the right direction, he or she can try figuring brainstorming to try to get team members to think more creatively or from a different perspective about the issue.

Figuring brainstorming, or role-play, can be combined with many of the other tools in this chapter to generate new thoughts. Choose one of the simpler brainstorming exercises or simply ask a question and brainstorm responses. However, ask participants to brainstorm as someone other than themselves. They might brainstorm as the CEO of the company. They might brainstorm as a person working on the product line. The might brainstorm as Johnny Depp, the President of the United States, or Cookie Monster.

Here is where Six Sigma leadership is important. Is the group likely to respond better to a silly situation that cracks some of the boundaries they’ve built, or are they more likely to respond to a serious role- playing situation? This helps you decide whether to go with Cookie Monster or CEO.

One challenge with this type of team activity is balancing the stress release and creativity of the silly activity with a true need to be productive. If things are devolving into pure fun without productivity, then the Six Sigma team leader should wrap up the activity and move on with another. The time is usually not wasted, though – breaking away from the seriousness of the project for a few minutes is often itself enough to help teams think more creatively.

zh.text: 作为六西格玛团队进行头脑风暴的最大挑战之一是，在几次会议之后或随着团队深入 DMAIC 流程，团队可能会陷入某些信念和想法。一旦提出某些想法，就很难“摆脱”它们。团队可能会发现自己反复回到相同的想法。有时，这意味着团队正走在正确的轨道上，并且在整个 DMAIC 流程和头脑风暴中重复相同的概念可以确认这些是需要考虑的正确的事情。然而，其他时候，这可能表明团队陷入困境。
有时很难区分，六西格玛的专家确实必须依靠分析和数据来帮助决定团队是否在朝着正确的方向前进。如果六西格玛专家认为团队没有朝着正确的方向前进，他或她可以尝试进行头脑风暴，试图让团队成员更有创意地思考或从不同的角度思考问题。
头脑风暴或角色扮演可以与本章中的许多其他工具结合起来，以产生新的想法。选择一种更简单的头脑风暴练习，或者简单地提出问题并头脑风暴答案。但是，请参与者以其他人的身份进行头脑风暴。他们可能会作为公司的首席执行官进行集思广益。他们可能会作为产品线工作人员进行集思广益。可能会以美国总统约翰尼·德普或饼干怪物的身份进行头脑风暴。
这就是六西格玛领导力的重要性。团队是否可能对打破他们所建立的一些界限的愚蠢情况做出更好的反应，或者他们是否更有可能对严肃的角色扮演情况做出反应？这可以帮助您决定是选择 Cookie Monster 还是 CEO。
这种类型的团队活动面临的一个挑战是平衡愚蠢活动的压力释放和创造力与生产力的真正需要。如果事情变成纯粹的乐趣而没有生产力，那么六西格玛团队领导应该结束活动并继续进行另一个活动。不过，时间通常不会被浪费——从项目的严肃性中抽离几分钟，通常就足以帮助团队进行更有创意的思考。

en.text: Brainwriting is somewhat like the nominal group technique. In fact, nominal group technique is actually a form of brainwriting.
