Reasons you might incorporate this type of brainstorming in your meetings include increasing input from quieter or more reserved team members, reducing how only one or two vocal members impact the outcome of a meeting, and generating ideas that other brainstorming methods are not generating. Remember, the physical act of writing itself contributes to thought processes and can help the brain make connections between ideas that it is less likely to make during verbal brainstorming.
There are numerous types of brainwriting exercises that you can use in a team environment. Nominal group technique is one, and this section provides some other options.
The Pool Brainwriting Method

The pool method can help generate different ideas because participants build off of each other’s ideas without the pressure of a fast verbal brainstorming sessions. Each participant gets a piece of paper and a pen. You can print the pages with a question or idea at the top or state the prompt for the session once everyone is ready. Each person writes two to three ideas on their paper and then places the paper back in the center of the table. Each person takes another paper from the pool (one that already has ideas written on it from someone else) and adds two to three more ideas. Papers are written on, replaced, and repulled repeatedly. There aren’t timed rounds – each participant moves at his or her own pace and pulls from the center pool as pages become available.

At the end of the exercise, which lasts a predetermined time, the team records all ideas on a single page or white board and categorizes and sorts them appropriately. The length of time the session might last depends on the number of people involved and how complex the problem is. A good time limit for this exercise is typically between 10 and 30 minutes, and the exercise works best with four to eight participants.
The Card Brainwriting Method

Often, the goal of brainwriting is to build upon each other’s ideas, which is accomplished by returning and pulling pages from the pool in the above method. The card method uses a similar approach but records each idea on a separate card for more flexibility during the analysis or categorization portion of the exercise.

The card method works well with groups of four to eight people. Begin by providing each participant with a stack of index cards and a pen. Pose a question or problem – it sometimes helps to write this question or problem on a whiteboard or large piece of paper so participants can reference it throughout the exercise.

Participants start by brainstorming their own ideas by writing each idea on one of the cards. After an idea is written down, the card containing that idea is passed to the left. When a participant has written as many ideas as he or she can think of quickly on cards, they begin picking up and reading the cards passed from the person on their right.

If a participant can add anything to the idea on any card they read, they write the new or extended idea on a new card and attach it to the existing card. Those cards are then passed to the left. If the participant can’t add anything new, then the cards are simply passed to the left.

When a participant receives his or her own cards back, then they keep them. The brainwriting session ends when all participants have received all of their original cards back, hopefully now with attachments of other ideas. The moderator then picks up the cards and the cards can be used in further brainstorming and idea organization. You could use the cards to create an affinity diagram, for example.

The card brainwriting method is also a good way to start a group effort to create a process map. Instead of writing ideas for solutions to a problem, the team writes down individual parts of a process – including activities, inputs, and outputs. Those pieces can then form the bare bones of a process map, which can be added on to by the team.

zh.text: 头脑风暴有点像名义小组技术。事实上，名义小组技术实际上是一种书面形式。您可能在会议中采用这种类型的头脑风暴的原因包括：增加安静或保守的团队成员的投入，减少只有一两个发声成员对会议结果的影响，以及产生其他头脑风暴方法无法产生的想法。请记住，写作本身的身体行为有助于思维过程，并可以帮助大脑在想法之间建立联系，而在口头头脑风暴中不太可能建立这种联系。
您可以在团队环境中使用多种类型的头脑风暴练习。名义组技术是其中之一，本节提供了一些其他选项。
池写脑力法

集体方法可以帮助产生不同的想法，因为参与者可以借鉴彼此的想法，而无需进行快速口头头脑风暴会议的压力。每个参与者都会得到一张纸和一支笔。 您可以打印顶部带有问题或想法的页面，或者在每个人都准备好后说出会议提示。每个人在纸上写下两到三个想法，然后将纸放回桌子中央。每个人从池中取出另一张纸（上面已经写有其他人的想法）并添加两到三个想法。纸张被反复书写、替换和拉回。没有定时回合——每个参与者按照自己的节奏移动，并在页面可用时从中心池中提取。
在持续预定时间的练习结束时，团队将所有想法记录在一页或白板上，并对它们进行适当的分类和排序。会议持续的时间长短取决于涉及的人数以及问题的复杂程度。此练习的最佳时间限制通常为 10 到 30 分钟，并且该练习最适合 4 到 8 名参与者。
卡片脑写法
通常，头脑风暴的目标是建立在彼此的想法之上，这是通过在上述方法中从池中返回和提取页面来实现的。卡片法使用类似的方法，但将每个想法记录在单独的卡片上，以便在练习的分析或分类部分中具有更大的灵活性。
卡片法适用于四到八人的团体。首先为每个参与者提供一叠索引卡和一支笔。提出问题 - 有时将这个问题写在白板或大纸上会有所帮助，以便参与者可以在整个练习中参考它。
参与者首先将每个想法写在一张卡片上，集思广益地讨论自己的想法。写下一个想法后，包含该想法的卡片就会传递到左侧。当参与者在卡片上写下他或她能快速想到的尽可能多的想法时，他们开始拿起并阅读右边的人递过来的卡片。
如果参与者可以在他们读到的任何卡片上的想法中添加任何内容，他们就会在新卡片上写下新的或扩展的想法并将其附加到现有的卡片上。然后这些卡被传递到左边。如果参与者无法添加任何新内容，那么卡片就会简单地传递到左侧。
当参与者收到自己的卡片时，他们会保留它们。 当所有参与者收到所有原始卡片后，头脑风暴会议结束，希望现在还附有其他想法的附件。然后主持人拿起卡片，卡片可以用于进一步的头脑风暴和想法组织。例如，您可以使用这些卡片来创建亲和图。
卡片写脑法也是开始团队努力创建流程图的好方法。团队不是写下问题解决方案的想法，而是写下流程的各个部分——包括活动、输入和输出。然后，这些部分可以形成流程图的基本框架，团队可以将其添加到其中。

en.text: This chapter, and most of the information throughout this book, concentrates on activities and methods to be used in a group environment. This is because facing and solving problems as a team is a fundamental part of the Six Sigma methodology. Brainstorming as a team is critical because it presents multiple perspectives and lets individual team members build thoughts on top of ideas generated by others. However, it’s not always possible or even a good idea to brainstorm about every single issue as a group.

Sometimes, the Six Sigma leader must consider an issue or idea before presenting it to the group. Other times, the issue about which you want to brainstorm isn't something that is appropriate to share with the entire team. Remember, if you are in a Black Belt or project leadership role, you might have access to information that is sensitive or confidential. While Six Sigma projects and process improvement work best in transparent environments, it's simply not always possible for every member of a Six Sigma team to know all of the information about a process. As a Six Sigma leader, you should work to create a team that you feel is capable of dealing appropriately with sensitive information, but you should also be prepared to facilitate a team in unique situations when some information sharing is limited. This can be especially true in industries such as healthcare or finance, where the data itself is sensitive. Other reasons exist for brainstorming on your own as a project leader or Six Sigma expert. Your team might be floundering due to storming or scope creep, two challenges covered in earlier units. You might want to come into a team meeting prepared with some ideas or understanding of the problem -- although you should always be careful not to lead others too closely with your own ideas because you might reduce the efficacy of the team. As a Six Sigma expert, you might also face your own problems or information needs outside of a team environment. Whatever the reason, you can use abbreviated forms of many of the brainstorming tools in this chapter to brainstorm alone. Tools such as starburst brainstorming, SWOT analysis, and force field analysis can be conducted as an individual. For example, if your team seems to be stuck in a certain DMAIC phase, you can use the force field analysis to help you identify what problems or people are holding you in place. As a Six Sigma leader, you can then use that information to address the issues so the team can move forward with a project. Brainstorming is a powerful tool whether you use it as a team or as an individual. It takes practice to be able to best implement brainstorming methods within various group dynamics, but when you follow the basic guidelines in this chapter, you almost always have at least some positive result from a brainstorming session.

zh.text: 本章以及本书中的大部分信息都集中于在小组环境中使用的活动和方法。这是因为作为一个团队面对和解决问题是六西格玛方法论的基本组成部分。作为一个团队，头脑风暴至关重要，因为它提出了多种观点，并让各个团队成员可以在其他人产生的想法的基础上构建想法。然而，作为一个团队对每个问题进行集思广益并不总是可能的，甚至不是一个好主意。

有时，六西格玛领导者必须先考虑一个问题或想法，然后再向小组提出。其他时候，您想要集思广益的问题并不适合与整个团队分享。请记住，如果您担任黑带或项目领导角色，您可能会访问敏感或机密信息。虽然六西格玛项目和流程改进在透明的环境中效果最好，但六西格玛团队的每个成员并不总是能够了解流程的所有信息。作为六西格玛的领导者，您应该努力创建一个您认为能够适当处理敏感信息的团队，但您也应该准备好在某些信息共享受到限制的特殊情况下为团队提供便利。在医疗保健或金融等数据本身很敏感的行业尤其如此。作为项目负责人或六西格玛专家，还存在其他原因需要您自行集思广益。您的团队可能会因风暴或范围蔓延而陷入困境，这两个挑战在早期单元中已涉及。 您可能希望在参加团队会议时准备好一些想法或对问题的理解——尽管您应该始终小心，不要让其他人与您自己的想法过于接近，因为这可能会降低团队的效率。作为六西格玛专家，您也可能会面临团队环境之外的自身问题或信息需求。无论出于什么原因，您都可以使用本章中许多头脑风暴工具的缩写形式来单独进行头脑风暴。星爆头脑风暴、SWOT 分析和力场分析等工具可以单独进行。例如，如果您的团队似乎陷入了某个 DMAIC 阶段，您可以使用力场分析来帮助您确定哪些问题或人员阻碍了您的发展。作为六西格玛的领导者，您可以使用该信息来解决问题，以便团队能够推进项目。无论您作为团队还是个人使用，头脑风暴都是一个强大的工具。需要练习才能在各种群体动态中最好地实施头脑风暴方法，但是当您遵循本章中的基本准则时，您几乎总能从头脑风暴会议中获得至少一些积极的结果。

source.extraction: python-docx body-order extraction; source-TOC sectionized bilingual content

en.text: Process maps are a vital part of many Six Sigma projects. Process maps are valuable tools in all stages of a DMAIC project. While good process maps can take a long time to create, they can be reused throughout the project and after the project for a variety of purposes.

rows: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。
zh.text: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。
流程图是许多六西格玛项目的重要组成部分。流程图在 DMAIC 项目的所有阶段都是有价值的工具。虽然良好的流程图可能需要很长时间才能创建，但它们可以在整个项目中以及项目结束后出于各种目的重复使用。

rows: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
zh.text: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
为什么要创建流程图

en.text: Process maps are not just simple flow charts, although teams might choose to create a flow chart as the process map for a specific project or phase. Process maps are visual depictions of a process and usually include all of the activities and decisions within the process. They can show various levels of process, and we'll look at the difference between high-level organizational process maps and detailed process maps in this chapter. Teams create process maps for a variety of reasons. First, a process map is a good place to start when teams are trying to understand the overall process. If a visual depiction of the process doesn't already exist, the team can work with subject matter experts to create such a depiction. The act of creating the map is actually valuable in itself because it lets the team ask many questions about the process and explore the process deeply in order to convert knowledge gained into a working diagram. During the Measure phase, process maps can help teams understand what inputs or outputs should be measured, and when and where during a process data can or should be acquired. Process maps are also helpful during the Analyze phase, because visual depictions of the flow of a process often make it easier to see where rework is occurring or where bottlenecks are possible. Process maps also let the team visualize a future state for a process. A Six Sigma team might begin with a current-state process map during the Define phase of a project and create a desired-state map in the Analyze or Improve phase. Side-by-side comparisons of the current- and future-state maps let teams see exactly what changes need to be made, ensure that all current-state functionality is addressed in the updated process, and double check that all challenges are solved by the new process. Process maps can also help teams see whether they might be creating new challenges, such as rework loops or bottlenecks within an updated process. Finally, process maps in their various forms are valuable documentation for a Six Sigma team and for an organization as a whole. Six Sigma teams can use all or part of process maps they create in tollgate and other presentations to ensure decision makers, sponsors, and auxiliary staff understand the process before moving forward with any aspect of the project or with making decisions about the project or process. Organizations can use process maps in training documents or as a guide for non-Six Sigma staff who need to understand new processes or changes to existing processes.

zh.text: 流程图不仅仅是简单的流程图，尽管团队可能选择创建流程图作为特定项目或阶段的流程图。流程图是流程的直观描述，通常包括流程中的所有活动和决策。它们可以显示不同级别的流程，我们将在本章中了解高级组织流程图和详细流程图之间的区别。团队出于各种原因创建流程图。首先，当团队试图了解整个流程时，流程图是一个很好的起点。如果流程的视觉描述尚不存在，团队可以与主题专家合作创建这样的描述。创建地图的行为本身实际上很有价值，因为它让团队可以提出有关流程的许多问题并深入探索该流程，以便将获得的知识转化为工作图表。在测量阶段，流程图可以帮助团队了解应该测量哪些输入或输出，以及在过程中何时何地可以或应该获取数据。流程图在分析阶段也很有用，因为流程流程的可视化描述通常可以让您更容易地看到哪里发生返工或哪里可能出现瓶颈。流程图还让团队能够直观地看到流程的未来状态。六西格玛团队可能会在项目的定义阶段从当前状态流程图开始，并在分析或改进阶段创建所需状态图。通过对当前状态和未来状态图进行并排比较，团队可以准确了解需要进行哪些更改，确保在更新的流程中解决所有当前状态的功能，并仔细检查新流程是否解决了所有挑战。流程图还可以帮助团队了解他们是否可能会带来新的挑战，例如更新流程中的返工循环或瓶颈。最后，各种形式的流程图对于六西格玛团队和整个组织来说都是有价值的文档。 六西格玛团队可以使用他们在收费站和其他演示文稿中创建的全部或部分流程图，以确保决策者、发起人和辅助人员在推进项目的任何方面或做出有关项目或流程的决策之前了解流程。组织可以在培训文件中使用流程图，或作为需要了解新流程或现有流程变更的非六西格玛员工的指南。

en.text: This chapter will cover two basic types of process maps. The first is the flow chart, which presents a view of the process that typically flows down the page. Flow charts usually give the overview of a process or detail a specific part of the process. When multiple flow charts are used for detailed pieces of a process,
they can reference each other to provide an entire description of a process.

The second type of process map covered in this chapter is the swimlane process map, which usually provides a left-to-right and top-to-bottom visual of the process. A swimlane process map breaks up the process into different sectors, often by who is responsible for the work, when work occurs, or what type of work is being performed. At a glance, teams can not only see the flow of the work through the process, but can see who or what is responsible for each step.

zh.text: 本章将介绍两种基本类型的流程图。第一个是流程图，它显示了通常沿着页面流动的流程的视图。流程图通常给出流程的概述或流程的特定部分的详细信息。当多个流程图用于流程的详细部分时，
它们可以互相引用以提供流程的完整描述。
本章介绍的第二种类型的流程图是泳道流程图，它通常提供从左到右、从上到下的流程可视化。泳道流程图将流程分解为不同的部分，通常按工作负责人、工作发生时间或正在执行的工作类型来划分。一目了然，团队不仅可以看到整个流程的工作流程，还可以看到谁或什么负责每个步骤。

en.text: Many people attempt to create process maps using various shapes, connecting them with arrows to show the flow of work. That is the basic idea of a flow chart or process map, but each type of shape actually says something specific about the information presented inside or near it. Anyone who is familiar with process map symbology should be able to look at a process map and discern numerous details just from the shapes and the way they are presented. In this section, we’ll cover most of the common flow chart/process map shapes and what they are used for.
Process Shape

The process shape is the most common shape in most process maps or flow charts. It is a rectangle. Depending on how you plan to use the process map and what your company’s design specifications are, you can use default colors of your Word, Excel, or Visio program, update shape colors to match branding or publication requirements for your enterprise, or create a simple black-and-white rectangle. This is true for all of the shapes in this chapter.

A process shape is used to denote an action step within the process. Something is happening, and usually it is a person or people doing the action. The description of the action is usually typed as text within the shape, which is also true for most of the shapes presented in this section.
Alternate Process Shape
The alternate process shape is used to indicate that an action is an alternate to something that is normal within the process. It is a rectangle with curved corners.
Predefined Process Shape

A predefined process shape is used when there is a process within a process and that subprocess is already defined in another document. The shape lets readers know that more than one step is occurring at this point in the overall process, and that those steps are formerly documented somewhere else in the organization. The documentation might be via standard operating procedures, but could also be in the format of another process map.

If a subprocess is known and a Six Sigma team decides not to fully document those steps – but the process isn’t formerly documented elsewhere or the team doesn’t know if it is – then this shape should not be used.
Terminator Shape

When viewing a map for a complex process, it can be difficult to tell where the process begins and ends. This is especially true when a printed process map spans multiple pages and might have more than one possible end point. The terminator shape is used to show the beginning and endings of a process. At the beginning, the terminator shape usually includes an action that triggers the rest of the process.
Preparation Shape

The preparation shape, which is a hexagon, indicates that a step in the process is in preparation for something else. An example might be cleaning a piece of equipment before using it or setting the oven temperature before preparing a recipe.

The manual operation shape indicates a part of the process that is performed by a person and is not automated. In processes that are mostly human-driven, you don’t necessarily have to differentiate between manual operation steps and regular process steps. The manual operation step shape is actually
more important to use when depicting mostly automated or computerized processes because you would want the sudden need for human interaction within the process to stand out.
Delay Shape

The delay shape lets you indicate that a waiting time occurs in the process. Delay shapes can be valuable to Six Sigma teams because they often indicate a possible point where muda is occurring. When teams see delay shapes on process maps, they should ask questions about why the delay is occurring – is it, for example, because one step in the process is batching outputs, so the next step is awaiting a batch? Is it because a bottleneck is occurring upstream in the process? These are valuable insights for a Six Sigma team.
Decision Shape

The decision shape indicates that a decision is being made in the process. Typically, the decision indicated by the diamond shape is a yes/no or go/no go variety. One example of this type of decision branch in a process is seen when further approval is needed for certain types of actions. For example, a team that processes customer refunds might have some leeway to process lower dollar refunds without approvals. If the refund is under $50, the staff request is processed without further action; if the refund is over $50, a supervisor might need to be involved.
Connector Shape
A connector shape usually indicates a jump from one part of a process to another. Sometimes, this shape is used to indicate that an audit or inspection occurs at a certain part of the process.
Document Shape

The document shape indicates that a step in a process creates a document. Using the refund example above, at some point in that process, a check is printed. A check is a document, and that step in the process could use this shape.
Multi-Document Shape

The multi-document shape indicates a step in the process that creates multiple documents. Using the same refund example, it’s possible that the accounting department batches the checks, printing all refunds at one time each day or week. That step in the process could use this shape.
Display Shape

The display shape is used to show that a step in a process involves the display of some information to a person. For example, a person using a company communication portal to send email might have to log into the portal first. During that process, the software might display a user agreement; if the process for using the software were mapped, this shape could be used to indicate that step.
Data Shape
The data shape is used to show that inputs are going into a process or that outputs are coming from a process. It is also called and In/Out (or I/O) shape.
Manual Input Shape
The manual input shape is used to show a step that involves manual entry of information by a person, usually using a computer.
Off-Page Connector Shape

The off-page connector shape is used to show that part of the process is depicted on another page. You can also use off-page connectors to link multiple processes that are documented on various pages.
Or Shape

The or shape is different from the basic diamond decision shape depicted earlier in this list. The or shape also indicates a branch of the process, usually in more than two possible directions. Typically, the process map provides some data for how to decide which branch the flow should take at the or shape. For example, consider the refund example used above.

In the previous scenario, there were two options for each refund request: either the request was under $50 and could be processed or over $50 and had to be worked by a supervisor. In a new version of the process, the work can take one of three paths. First, if the refund request is $20 or under, it is approved automatically when entered by a staff member and is routed to check generation. If the refund request is $20 to $50, it must be reviewed by a second staff member, who must agree with the refund decision
before it is routed to the check process. If the refund is above $50, it must be approved by a supervisor before a check is cut.
On a process map, the three-prong decision would come out of the or shape. The instructions for which path to choose could be as simple as “Refund </= $20,” “Refund $20 to $50,” and “Refund > $50.”
Merge Shape

If processes can branch off based on decision shapes and or shapes, they might also come back together. When multiple branches of work flow within a process come back together, you can indicate this with a merge shape.
Extract Shape

If processes split into two or more parallel flows without a decision or criteria, then you can use an extract shape to indicate this fact. This isn’t one of the more common symbols seen on process maps, and sometimes the triangle indicates something else. When the triangle is presented with an M inside of it, it can show that a measurement must occur at a certain point in the workflow.
Collate Data Shape

The collate data shape is used when data or material in a process has to be organized using a standard format. Cutting fabric into certain lengths before sewing or ordering the admissions forms before considering them are both examples of when this symbol might be used.
Sort Data Shape

The sort data shape indicates that information or materials must be arranged in a certain order. Sorting materials by color before moving to the next step in a manufacturing process might warrant this symbol, for example.
All of the shapes within your flow chart or process map are connected with lines and arrows. The arrows are important, because they guide the reader through the map in the proper sequence.
Where Do These Shapes Come From?

All of the shapes listed in this chapter are available in Microsoft programs, including Word, Excel, and Visio. For those without specific process-mapping software, Excel is probably one of the easiest programs to use for creating process maps and flow charts. Simply turn off the view gridlines function to give yourself a clean slate with which to work.
You can insert all of the flow chart shape into Excel using the Insert > Shapes option.
Include a Key with Your Process Map

While someone familiar with process mapping tools will recognize most of the above symbols quickly, not everyone in a business environment will. Most people in business environments are familiar with the basic process shape, decision and connector shapes, and terminator shape. Because of this, you can create the bulk of most process maps using these shapes. If you think another shape is warranted, then it’s a good idea to add a process map key to your diagram to ensure anyone who looks at it can follow it.

It’s also a good idea to add a process map key because not everyone uses all of the above symbols for exactly the same reasons. The connector shape can mean several different things, as can several of the other shapes.

You probably noted as you were reading through the list of common shapes that some activities might be appropriate for two or even three process shapes. Outside of some basic rules, there isn’t a right and wrong way to add details on a process map. Ultimately, the goal for creating a process map should be clarity. If adding a different type of shape is going to make the process easier to understand on your visual, then do it. If you can simply use the basic process shape along with text to spell out the process, then remember that in Six Sigma, less really is more.

zh.text: 许多人尝试使用各种形状创建流程图，并用箭头将它们连接起来以显示工作流程。这是流程图或流程图的基本思想，但每种类型的形状实际上都说明了其内部或附近呈现的信息的具体内容。任何熟悉流程图符号系统的人都应该能够查看流程图并仅从形状及其呈现方式中辨别出大量细节。在本节中，我们将介绍大多数常见的流程图/流程图形状及其用途。
工艺形状
流程形状是大多数流程图或流程图中最常见的形状。它是一个矩形。根据您计划使用流程图的方式以及公司的设计规范，您可以使用 Word、Excel 或 Visio 程序的默认颜色，更新形状颜色以匹配企业的品牌或发布要求，或者创建简单的黑白矩形。对于本章中的所有形状都是如此。
流程形状用于表示流程中的操作步骤。某事正在发生，通常是一个或多个人在采取行动。操作的描述通常在形状中以文本形式键入，这对于本节中介绍的大多数形状也是如此。
替代工艺形状
替代流程形状用于指示某个操作是流程中正常操作的替代操作。它是一个带有弧形角的矩形。
预定义的工艺形状
当流程中存在流程并且该子流程已在另一个文档中定义时，将使用预定义的流程形状。该形状让读者知道在整个流程的这一点上发生了多个步骤，并且这些步骤以前已记录在组织的其他地方。文档可以通过标准操作程序，但也可以采用其他流程图的格式。
如果某个子流程已知，并且六西格玛团队决定不完整记录这些步骤（但该流程以前没有在其他地方记录过，或者团队不知道是否记录过），则不应使用此形状。
终结者形状
查看复杂流程的地图时，可能很难判断流程的开始和结束位置。当打印的流程图跨越多页并且可能具有多个可能的终点时尤其如此。终止符形状用于显示过程的开始和结束。一开始，终止符形状通常包括触发其余过程的动作。
制备形状
准备形状是六边形，表明该过程中的一个步骤是为其他事情做准备。一个例子可能是在使用设备之前对其进行清洁，或者在准备食谱之前设置烤箱温度。
手动操作形状表示由人执行且非自动化的过程的一部分。在主要由人驱动的流程中，您不一定需要区分手动操作步骤和常规流程步骤。手动操作步骤形状实际上是
在描述大多数自动化或计算机化流程时使用更重要，因为您希望突显流程中突然需要的人机交互。

rows: 术语说明 Interaction（交互作用）：一个因素的效果会随另一个因素水平不同而变化。
zh.text: 术语说明 Interaction（交互作用）：一个因素的效果会随另一个因素水平不同而变化。
延迟形状
延迟形状可让您指示过程中发生等待时间。延迟形状对于六西格玛团队来说很有价值，因为它们通常表明可能发生 Muda 的点。当团队在流程图上看到延迟形状时，他们应该询问为什么会发生延迟 - 例如，是否因为流程中的一个步骤是批处理输出，所以下一步是等待一批？是因为流程上游出现瓶颈吗？这些对于六西格玛团队来说都是宝贵的见解。
决策形态
决策形状表明正在做出决策。通常，菱形形状指示的决策是是/否或通过/不通过的变化。当某些类型的操作需要进一步批准时，可以看到流程中此类决策分支的一个例子。例如，处理客户退款的团队可能有一定的余地，可以在未经批准的情况下处理金额较低的退款。如果退款低于 50 美元，工作人员的请求将得到处理，无需采取进一步行动；如果退款超过 50 美元，则可能需要主管介入。
连接器形状
连接器形状通常表示从流程的一个部分到另一部分的跳转。有时，此形状用于指示在流程的某个部分进行审核或检查。
文件形状
文档形状指示流程中的步骤创建文档。使用上面的退款示例，在该过程的某个时刻，会打印一张支票。支票是一份文件，流程中的该步骤可以使用此形状。
多文档形状
多文档形状表示创建多个文档的过程中的一个步骤。使用相同的退款示例，会计部门可能会批量处理支票，每天或每周一次打印所有退款。该过程中的该步骤可以使用此形状。
显示形状
显示形状用于表明流程中的某个步骤涉及向人员显示某些信息。例如，使用公司通信门户发送电子邮件的人可能必须首先登录该门户。在此过程中，软件可能会显示用户协议；如果绘制了软件的使用过程，则可以使用该形状来指示该步骤。
数据形状
数据形状用于显示输入正在进入流程或输出来自流程。它也称为 In/Out（或 I/O）形状。
手动输入形状
手动输入形状用于显示涉及人通常使用计算机手动输入信息的步骤。
离页连接器形状
页面外连接器形状用于显示该过程的一部分在另一页面上描述。您还可以使用页外连接器来链接各个页面上记录的多个流程。
或形状
或 形状与此列表前面描述的基本菱形决策形状不同。或 形状还表示过程的分支，通常在两个以上可能的方向上。通常，流程图提供一些数据，用于如何决定流程应采用 或 形状的哪个分支。例如，考虑上面使用的退款示例。
在之前的场景中，每个退款请求有两种选择：请求金额低于 50 美元且可以处理，或者请求金额超过 50 美元且必须由主管处理。在新版本的流程中，工作可以采取三种路径之一。首先，如果退款申请金额为 20 美元或以下，则由工作人员输入后会自动获得批准，并路由至支票生成。如果退款申请金额为 20 至 50 美元，则必须由第二名工作人员审核，该工作人员必须同意退款决定
在将其发送至检查流程之前。如果退款超过 50 美元，则必须在开具支票之前获得主管批准。
在流程图上，三管齐下的决策将来自 或 形状。选择哪条路径的说明可以很简单，例如“退款 </= 20 美元”、“退款 20 美元到 50 美元”和“退款 > 50 美元”。
合并形状
如果流程可以根据决策形状和/或形状进行分支，那么它们也可能会重新聚集在一起。当流程中的多个工作流分支重新聚集在一起时，您可以使用合并形状来指示这一点。
提取形状
如果流程在没有决策或标准的情况下分成两个或多个并行流，那么您可以使用提取形状来指示这一事实。这不是流程图上最常见的符号之一，有时三角形还表示其他含义。 当三角形内部带有 M 时，可以表明必须在工作流程中的某个点进行测量。
整理数据形状
当必须使用标准格式组织流程中的数据或材料时，将使用整理数据形状。在缝纫之前将织物切割成一定的长度或在考虑招生表格之前先订购招生表格都是可能使用此符号的示例。
对数据形状进行排序
排序数据形状表示信息或材料必须按照一定的顺序排列。例如，在进入制造过程的下一步之前按颜色对材料进行分类可能需要使用此符号。
流程图或流程图中的所有形状都用线条和箭头连接。箭头很重要，因为它们引导读者以正确的顺序浏览地图。
这些形状从何而来？
本章列出的所有形状均可在 Microsoft 程序中使用，包括 Word、Excel 和 Visio。对于那些没有特定流程图软件的人来说，Excel 可能是用于创建流程图和流程图的最简单的程序之一。只需关闭视图网格线功能即可让自己重新开始工作。
您可以使用“插入”>“形状”选项将所有流程图形状插入 Excel 中。
在流程图中包含一个密钥
虽然熟悉流程图工具的人会很快识别上述大多数符号，但并不是业务环境中的每个人都能识别。商业环境中的大多数人都熟悉基本流程形状、决策和连接器形状以及终结器形状。因此，您可以使用这些形状创建大部分流程图。如果您认为需要使用其他形状，那么最好在图表中添加流程图键，以确保看到它的任何人都可以遵循它。
添加流程图键也是一个好主意，因为并非每个人都出于完全相同的原因使用上述所有符号。连接器形状可以表示多种不同的含义，其他几种形状也是如此。
您可能在阅读常见形状列表时注意到，某些活动可能适合两个甚至三个流程形状。 除了一些基本规则之外，在流程图上添加详细信息没有正确和错误的方法。最终，创建流程图的目标应该是清晰。如果添加不同类型的形状将使该过程在视觉上更容易理解，那么就这样做。如果你可以简单地使用基本的流程形状和文字来拼出流程，那么请记住，在六西格玛中，少即是多。

en.text: A basic flow chart begins with a trigger activity, includes inputs and processes, decisions, and a final action. Flow charts usually begin at the top of the page and flow down the page. Secondarily, you would read a flow chart from left to right as you move down the page, if a secondary direction is required. In the above flow chart, you can see that a trigger activity occurs, which involves some type of data input. Using that input, a person makes a decision. That decision directs the flow of work either to the process
on the right or the process on the left. After that action is done, the overall process, as recorded on this flow chart, ends.

It’s important to note that the terminator that ends the process being mapped on the flow chart or process map rarely ends the work itself. The only time the final terminator signals the complete end of the work is when you are mapping the very last process before the end customer or you are mapping the entire enterprise process at a very high level. In most cases, however, the final terminator on your process map is likely to indicate the end of this particular process and the beginning of the next. For example, in a beverage bottling plant, a team might be mapping the process that prepares bottles to receive ingredients. The process map begins when a fresh, new bottle is introduced to the process and it ends when the bottle is cleaned, has a label, and is put on the line to receive the liquid it will house. After the process that was mapped, the bottle goes through other process to fill it, cap it, inspect it, and pack it.

zh.text: 基本流程图以触发活动开始，包括输入和流程、决策和最终操作。流程图通常从页面顶部开始并沿着页面向下流动。其次，如果需要辅助方向，您可以在向下移动页面时从左到右阅读流程图。在上面的流程图中，您可以看到发生了一个触发活动，其中涉及某种类型的数据输入。使用该输入，一个人做出决定。该决定将工作流程引导至流程
右侧或左侧的过程。该操作完成后，如该流程图所记录的整个过程就结束了。

值得注意的是，结束流程图或流程图上映射的流程的终止符很少会结束工作本身。最终终结者唯一表示工作完全结束的情况是，当您在绘制最终客户之前的最后一个流程时，或者在非常高的级别上绘制整个企业流程时。然而，在大多数情况下，流程图上的最终终止符可能表示此特定流程的结束和下一个流程的开始。例如，在饮料装瓶厂中，团队可能正在绘制准备瓶子以接收原料的流程。当一个新鲜的新瓶子被引入流程时，流程图开始，当瓶子被清洗、贴上标签并被放到生产线上以接收将要容纳的液体时，流程图结束。完成绘制的流程后，瓶子将经过其他流程来灌装、封盖、检查和包装。

en.text: One of the most widely used process map formats in the Six Sigma world is the swimlane diagram. The swimlane diagram is a flowchart, but it is one that is constrained somewhat by rows and columns, known as swimlanes. You can design your swimlane as you want to best depict the process you are working with. Two common options are:

1. Using columns to depict time or activity phases in the process and using rows to depict the person, department, or group responsible. 2. Using columns to depict the person, department, or group responsible and using rows to depict the time or activity phases in the process.
No matter which method you choose, someone should be able to read your swimlane diagram by starting at the top left and reading to the right and down.
Begin by Identifying the Swimlanes

Start your process map session by identifying the swimlanes associated with the process you plan to map. If you’ve already done a SIPOC as part of the Define phase of a project, then you can use the information from that activity to create your swimlanes. If not, you can use some of the brainstorming activities from the previous chapter to help generate ideas for your process map. Responsibility diagrams, for example, can help you understand how the activities within the process might be broken into organic categories.

If you don’t yet have a basic idea about possible swimlanes, first ask yourself or your team: what are all the steps in the process? Don’t worry yet about putting the steps in order or even getting all the detailed steps brainstormed. You can add to your map as you create it. Because you don’t want the team to start worrying too much about the order of steps or about how they are connected, use notecards to write steps on for this stage. Once the team thinks all major steps are written down, you can sort the steps into groups to get a better idea of possible swimlanes.

To understand how swimlanes might look for a real-world process, consider the process a brand uses to generate ideas for blog posts, write blog posts, and post blogs to a website. You can see, just from the previous sentence, the process is already somewhat broken down into major categories:
Ideas are created
Blog posts are written
Blog posts are posted
Those three sections might become the row designations for the swimlane process map.

Now, consider that different people or groups handle each of the subcategories within the process. The marketing team generates the ideas for content, a team of contracted writers writes the blog posts, and an internal quality team reviews and then posts the blogs.
The foundation of the swimlane diagram might look something like the figure below.
Create Step-by-Step List for Process Activities

Next, create a simple step-by-step list for all the activities within the process. If you are familiar with a process and are making a process map on your own, you can start putting the steps into shapes on the grid – you can always move them around as needed later. If you are not familiar with the process or are working with a group to create the process map, it helps to gather data first.
For the purpose of the blog generation example, here are some steps that are included in the process.

1. At the beginning of each month, the marketing team generates ideas for the next month’s content. 2. The marketing team generates 15 to 25 ideas, but chooses only 8 to 10 ideas to be written. 3. Once the 8 to 10 items are chosen, the marketing team chooses a title and keywords for each blog post. 4. The topic, along with any necessary special instructions, the titles, and the keywords are provided to the contract writing team. 5. Writers draft blog posts. 6. Writers check that blog posts fit guidelines. 7. Writers submit blogs posts. 8. Quality editors review blog posts. 9. Quality editors check that blog posts fit guidelines and request revisions if they do not. 10. Quality editors line edit blog posts. 11. Quality editors format blog posts.
12. Quality editors enter posts into blogging platform. 13. Quality editors schedule blog posts for publication in the next month.

You can break the above steps into various swimlanes and rows. Steps 1 through 4 belong under the marketing team and mostly in the row for idea creation. The very last step begins to cross the lines into the next phase of the process.

Steps 5 through 7 belong under the writing team and typically fall into the writing phase of the process. The last steps belong under the quality editor section, but they do involve a decision step that could send work back to the writers.
Consider how steps 1 through 4 might be placed on the swimlane diagram.

The terminal shape – the activity that starts the entire process – is that it is time to brainstorm the blog ideas for next month. The team brainstorms ideas and then chooses 8 to 10. For the next step, the activity is a predefined process. This means that somewhere, the company has a published SOP or

guidelines for researching and applying keywords to blog post ideas. Once that is done, someone chooses a title for each idea and the blogs are assigned to the writers. Note that the final activity – assigning the blogs to the writers – is in a rectangle that somewhat straddles the swimlane line. This is a helpful visual cue that the responsibility for work at this point in the process shifts from the marketing team to the writers.
Next, consider how steps 5 and 7 might look on the process map.

The three steps are presented under the contracted writers swimlane and in the row associated with writing blogs. The final step crosses the line slightly, indicating responsibility is being passed to the quality editors. Consider the remainder of the process map, which falls under the quality editor swimlane.

You can see that the quality editor responsibilities actually span writing and posting phases. More importantly, you can see that if the blog doesn’t meet guidelines, the editor sends it back to the writer. The team can see here that there is a possibility for rework in this process, and it can see exactly how that rework is handled. The entire process map is shown in the image below.

While this is a fairly basic example as process maps go, it includes all of the basic premises of creating a swimlane process map. You can apply these basics to create simple to very complex process maps of any process in Excel or with Word or other any programs that include process map shapes as an option.

zh.text: 六西格玛世界中最广泛使用的流程图格式之一是泳道图。泳道图是一种流程图，但它在一定程度上受到行和列的限制，称为泳道。您可以根据自己的需要设计泳道，以最好地描述您正在使用的流程。两个常见的选项是：
1. 使用列来描述流程中的时间或活动阶段，使用行来描述负责人、部门或小组。 2. 使用列来描述负责人、部门或小组，并使用行来描述流程中的时间或活动阶段。
无论您选择哪种方法，人们都应该能够通过从左上角开始向右和向下阅读来阅读您的泳道图。
首先确定泳道
通过识别与您计划映射的流程关联的泳道来开始您的流程图会话。如果您已经完成了 SIPOC 作为项目定义阶段的一部分，那么您可以使用该活动中的信息来创建您的泳道。如果没有，您可以使用上一章中的一些头脑风暴活动来帮助为流程图生成想法。例如，职责图可以帮助您了解如何将流程中的活动分解为有机类别。

rows: 术语说明 SIPOC：供应商、输入、流程、输出、客户；用于快速界定流程边界。
zh.text: 术语说明 SIPOC：供应商、输入、流程、输出、客户；用于快速界定流程边界。
如果您对可能的泳道还没有基本的了解，请首先问问自己或您的团队：该过程中的所有步骤是什么？不用担心如何将步骤按顺序排列，甚至不用头脑风暴来头脑风暴所有的详细步骤。您可以在创建地图时将其添加到地图中。因为您不希望团队开始过多担心步骤的顺序或步骤的连接方式，所以使用记事卡写下此阶段的步骤。一旦团队认为所有主要步骤都已写下来，您就可以将这些步骤分组，以更好地了解可能的泳道。
要了解泳道如何寻找现实世界的流程，请考虑品牌用于生成博客文章创意、撰写博客文章以及将博客发布到网站的流程。您可以看到，仅从上一句话来看，该过程已经分为几大类：
想法被创造出来
博客文章已写好
博客文章已发布
这三个部分可能成为泳道流程图的行指定。
现在，考虑不同的人或团体处理流程中的每个子类别。营销团队产生内容创意，签约作家团队撰写博客文章，内部质量团队审核并发布博客。
泳道图的基础可能如下图所示。
为流程活动创建分步列表
接下来，为流程中的所有活动创建一个简单的分步列表。如果您熟悉某个流程并且正在自己制作流程图，您可以开始将步骤放入网格上的形状中 - 您可以随时根据需要移动它们。如果您不熟悉该流程或正在与一个小组一起创建流程图，那么首先收集数据会有所帮助。
出于博客生成示例的目的，以下是该过程中包含的一些步骤。

1. 每个月初，营销团队都会为下个月的内容产生想法。 2. 营销团队产生 15 至 25 个创意，但只选择 8 至 10 个创意进行撰写。 3. 一旦选择了 8 到 10 个项目，营销团队就会为每篇博客文章选择标题和关键字。 4. 向合同撰写团队提供主题以及任何必要的特殊说明、标题和关键词。 5. 作者起草博客文章。 6. 作者检查博客文章是否符合指南。 7. 作者提交博客文章。 8. 优质编辑审查博客文章。 9. 质量编辑检查博客文章是否符合指南，如果不符合则要求修改。 10. 优质编辑在线编辑博客文章。 11. 优质编辑对博客文章进行格式化。
12. 优质编辑将帖子输入博客平台。 13. 质量编辑安排博客文章在下个月出版。
您可以将上述步骤分解为各种泳道和行。第 1 步到第 4 步属于营销团队，主要用于创意创造。最后一步开始跨越界限进入流程的下一阶段。
第 5 步到第 7 步属于写作团队，通常属于该过程的写作阶段。最后的步骤属于质量编辑部分，但它们确实涉及一个决策步骤，可以将工作发送回作者。
考虑如何将步骤 1 到 4 放置在泳道图上。
最终的形状——启动整个过程的活动——是时候集思广益下个月的博客想法了。团队集思广益，然后选择 8 到 10 个。对于下一步，活动是一个预定义的过程。 这意味着公司在某个地方发布了 SOP 或
研究关键字并将其应用于博客文章创意的指南。一旦完成，有人会为每个想法选择一个标题，并将博客分配给作者。请注意，最后的活动（将博客分配给作者）位于一个稍微横跨泳道线的矩形中。这是一个有用的视觉提示，表明流程中此时的工作责任从营销团队转移到了作家身上。
接下来，考虑步骤 5 和 7 在流程图上的外观。
这三个步骤显示在签约作家泳道下以及与撰写博客相关的行中。最后一步稍微越界了，表明责任正在转移给质量编辑。考虑流程图的其余部分，它属于质量编辑器泳道。
您可以看到质量编辑的职责实际上跨越了写作和发布阶段。更重要的是，您可以看到，如果博客不符合准则，编辑会将其发送回作者。团队可以在这里看到这个流程中存在返工的可能性，并且可以准确地看到返工是如何处理的。整个流程图如下图所示。
虽然这是一个相当基本的流程图示例，但它包含创建泳道流程图的所有基本前提。您可以应用这些基础知识，在 Excel 或 Word 或其他任何包含流程图形状选项的程序中创建任何流程的简单到非常复杂的流程图。

title.en: Tips for Creating Concise, Attractive Process Maps
title.zh: 创建简洁、有吸引力的流程图的技巧

en.text: Admittedly, the process maps shown in the above images are a bit rough. That was done on purpose to show you how the creation of a process map occurs as you are working through brainstorming and thinking about the process. If the sole purpose of the process map is to provide a Six Sigma team with visual guidance as it works through the DMAIC process, you can leave the process map in a rough-draft state.

If you plan to use the process map in presentations or training documents, however, consider cleaning it up a bit. First, make sure all the color combinations that you used will translate well to whatever medium you plan to use the map in. The above map features the white-on-blue text in each shape, which might not translate well to black-and-white hardcopy documents.

Next, take time to make sure shapes and arrows line up in an aesthetically pleasing and logical fashion. Review your map to ensure arrows are pointing in the right direction, try to keep arrows from crossing over each other too much, and ensure the start and end of each arrow is touching a process shape. This helps reduce confusion when you aren’t there it.

Finally, use concise and active language when possible to keep text in each shape short and understandable. Here are some edits that could be made to the example above to make language more active and concise.
“Team lists new ideas for next month’s blog” becomes “Generate blog ideas”
“Team chooses 8 to 10 ideas from those brainstormed” becomes “Choose 8 to 10 ideas”
“Blogs are assigned to writers” becomes “Assign posts to writers”
“Blog is written” becomes “Write blog”
You can see that the shorter versions almost always start with an action verb, which is ideal in process mapping and flow charting.

zh.text: 不可否认，上图中显示的流程图有点粗糙。这样做的目的是为了向您展示当您通过头脑风暴和思考流程时如何创建流程图。如果流程图的唯一目的是为六西格玛团队在 DMAIC 流程中提供视觉指导，您可以将流程图保留为草稿状态。
但是，如果您打算在演示文稿或培训文档中使用流程图，请考虑对其进行一些清理。首先，确保您使用的所有颜色组合都能很好地适应您计划使用地图的任何媒体。 上面的地图在每个形状中都具有蓝底白字文本，这可能无法很好地转换为黑白硬拷贝文档。
接下来，花点时间确保形状和箭头以美观且合乎逻辑的方式排列。检查地图以确保箭头指向正确的方向，尽量避免箭头彼此交叉过多，并确保每个箭头的起点和终点接触过程形状。当您不在场时，这有助于减少混乱。
最后，尽可能使用简洁、活跃的语言，使每种形状的文本简短易懂。以下是可以对上面的示例进行的一些编辑，以使语言更加活跃和简洁。
“团队列出下个月博客的新想法”变为“生成博客想法”
“团队从头脑风暴中选择 8 到 10 个想法”变成“选择 8 到 10 个想法”
“博客分配给作者”变为“将帖子分配给作者”
“写博客”变成“写博客”
您可以看到较短的版本几乎总是以动作动词开头，这在流程图和流程图中是理想的选择。

source.extraction: python-docx body-order extraction; source-TOC sectionized bilingual content

en.text: Value stream mapping is similar, but in some ways different, to process mapping. Value stream mapping is a tool perfected by lean manufacturing and works best in processes that are manufacturing, delivery, shipping, or factory oriented. However, value stream mapping can be useful in a variety of other industries if the Six Sigma expert understands the purpose of the exercise and how to translate the method into his or her process.
What is Value Stream Mapping?

Value stream mapping is a pictorial representation, including the addition of numerical data, of the value stream of product or ideas. When used properly, value stream mapping provides Six Sigma teams with the big picture of a process, lets teams understand what the process looks like today, helps teams create a vision for improving a process, and links the flow of materials, people, and information in one image. A value stream map also helps break down communication barriers because the symbology used tends to be similar or at least intuitive; as such, it makes it easier for teams and support staff to understand where waste – or muda – is occurring in a process.
Evaluating the Seven Flows

A value stream map is one of the few tools that truly captures all of the flow that occurs in a process. It’s possible to capture some information about all of the major seven flows on a value stream map, though most value stream maps concentrate most on raw material, parts, and products than on the other four types of flow. The seven types of flow are:
People
Raw material
Parts
Products
Equipment
Information
Engineering

zh.text: 价值流图与流程图相似，但在某些方面有所不同。价值流图是一种通过精益制造完善的工具，在制造、交付、运输或工厂导向的流程中效果最佳。然而，如果六西格玛专家了解练习的目的以及如何将该方法转化为他或她的流程，那么价值流图可以在其他各种行业中发挥作用。
什么是价值流图？
价值流图是产品或创意价值流的图形表示，包括添加数字数据。如果使用得当，价值流图可以为六西格玛团队提供流程的全局图景，让团队了解当前的流程是什么样子，帮助团队制定改进流程的愿景，并将物料流、人员流和信息流链接到一个图像中。价值流图还有助于打破沟通障碍，因为所使用的符号系统往往是相似的或至少是直观的；因此，它使团队和支持人员更容易了解流程中何处出现浪费（或 Muda）。
评估七种流程
价值流图是真正捕获流程中发生的所有流程的少数工具之一。尽管大多数价值流图主要集中在原材料、零件和产品上，而不是其他四种类型的流，但可以在价值流图上捕获有关所有主要七种流的一些信息。七种流类型是：
人
原料
零件
产品展示
设备
信息
工程
价值流图符号系统

en.text: Value stream maps use different symbols than most process maps do, and the symbols tend to be more specific. For example, if the value stream involves the delivery of goods via truck, the map includes an actual picture of a truck – usually with a text indication of how many times the shipments occur in a given period.

The image above indicates one truck shipment per week. A number of other common value stream map symbols are included below. You can download common symbols for Excel from www.lean.org/ common/display/?o=866 to create value stream maps in Excel. You can also use a value stream map software to create a map.

Note that value stream mapping doesn’t require the use of these exact symbols, especially if you are using the concept of value stream mapping in an industry where such symbols are less useful. You could use the value stream concept to map the workflow in an emergency room department, for example, where different symbols would make more sense. Consider these symbols as a starting point.

The manufacturing process box indicates a specific action in the production process. The name of the action goes in the top part of the box – in this case, “press.” In the bottom, you can put text notes or illustrations as desired. One common use of the bottom section of the process box is indicating how much time occurs during that particular step.
Outside sources are either suppliers or customers. Use this shape to indicate when inputs come from outside the organization or process or when outputs go to the end customer of the process.
Data boxes are important because they house the analytical meat of the value stream map. Common information recorded in data boxes include:
Cycle Time, or C/T. Cycle time is the time it takes for the process as a whole – or a step within the process – to complete one iteration.
Changeover Time, C/T. Changeover time is the time it takes for a change in shift, tools, or other resources in the process or a single step within the process.
Number of operators
Number of shifts
Time worked in a shift
First-pass yield quality
Batch sizes

Takt time, which is the rate at which products must be finished to meet the demand of the customer. Takt time for an individual step in the process might be seen as the time in which the step needs to be finished to keep from slowing down the process.

The inventory box indicates that inventory usually stacks up at a certain point in the process. It is usually coupled with text that says how much inventory is available in amount, time, or both. You might note that there are 100 pieces of inventory regularly at that point in the process. You might note that there are two-day’s worth of inventory at that point in the process; you could note both.

A push arrow indicates that something – either product or information – is pushed from one part of the process to another part of the process. The push arrow means the product or information is generated by that part of the process regardless of the need for it in the downstream parts of the process.
The opposite of a push arrow would be a pull indicator, which means that downstream processes pull inventory, parts, or information as needed from upstream locations.
The finished goods arrow indicates that the output of the process is being pushed or delivered to the customer – whether that customer is internal, external, or another process.

The FIFO arrow indicates that this part of the process features a first-in, first-out sequence, which is important in some processes. Consider processes that deal with food ingredients; if a recipe in a manufacturing plant calls for milk, then the operators should pull the oldest (still good) milk from storage first to keep from wasting product that goes bad while in inventory.

The supermarket is an area in the process where small amounts of inventory are stored. Downstream processes can stock as needed from the supermarket instead of stocking from the larger inventory supply, which might be more difficult to access. An upstream process feeds the supermarket, ensuring enough inventory is stocked. An example of a supermarket might be seen in a large office. One team might have a drawer where supplies, such as paper, pens, paperclips, and staples, are kept. The team can pull from that drawer instead of requisitioning from the larger stock room or from the actual office supply store every time supplies are needed.
The physical pull symbol indicates that product or materials have to be physically pulled from the previous step, station, or supermarket to the next step.

The move-by-forklift symbol is self-explanatory, though not always necessary in every process. You might, however, have a symbol for other types of movement, such as move by hand cart or conveyor belt. The only time you need to include specifications about how items are moved is when those details might become important to the process analysis.
This is also a self-explanatory symbol and also not necessary in every process. You might, instead, have train, plane, or even bike shipments listed on your value stream map.

The type of information indicated by the straight manual flow arrow is typically communicated via memos, face-to-face speaking, phone calls, and reports. The manual information arrow might be accompanied by notes regarding the type of information and the frequency with which it is delivered.

The crooked electronic info flow arrow indicates information that is electronic in nature, such as information communication via Internet, portals, EDI, and automated reporting systems. The type of data and the frequency with which it is delivered can also be included with this symbol.

The withdrawal Kanban shape indicates that there is a Kanban card or signal at that process step. The Kanban card instructs the operator at that particular step to withdraw inventory from a supermarket or other stock location.

The production Kanban shape indicates that a production Kanban card or signal is located at that part of the process. This card tells the operator to produce a certain number of parts to feed a process that is downstream.

The safety stock icon indicates that a “just-in-case” storage of inventory exists at this point in the process. Safety stock is not like a supermarket – it is not intended to be a permanent storage of inventory. Instead, it is a temporary storage of inventory that reduces the chance that downtime or other issues upstream will keep all the downstream processes from occurring. For example, in a fast food restaurant, someone stocks the cup and drink dispenser each morning. These stocks are not meant to be the permanent storage for those items, but a smaller number of items are kept at hand so employees can quickly supply drinks to consumers.

The operator shape indicates a human operator or employee within the process. You don’t have to include the operator shape in every diagram where people are – in some cases, the nature of the value stream map allows readers to assume people are part of the process. However, if automated processes are included amid people-powered processes, this symbol can be used for clarity where needed.

The schedule box is simply a text box that lets you note how work, orders, or other parts of the process are scheduled. You can couple the box along with arrows to show that orders are sent weekly, for example, or that a production schedule for staff is created each month.

zh.text: 价值流图使用与大多数流程图不同的符号，并且符号往往更具体。例如，如果价值流涉及通过卡车运送货物，则地图包含卡车的实际图片 - 通常带有文本指示在给定时期内发生了多少次运输。

rows: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。
zh.text: 术语说明 Process Map（流程图）：把流程步骤、流向和责任可视化，帮助识别断点和浪费。
上图显示每周一卡车发货。下面包括许多其他常见的价值流图符号。您可以从 www.lean.org/common/display/?o=866 下载 Excel 的常用符号，以在 Excel 中创建价值流图。 您还可以使用价值流图软件来创建地图。
请注意，价值流映射不需要使用这些精确的符号，特别是当您在此类符号不太有用的行业中使用价值流映射的概念时。例如，您可以使用价值流概念来绘制急诊室部门的工作流程，其中不同的符号更有意义。将这些符号视为起点。
制造过程框表示生产过程中的具体动作。操作的名称位于框的顶部 - 在本例中为“按”。在底部，您可以根据需要放置文字注释或插图。流程框底部的一个常见用途是指示在该特定步骤中发生了多少时间。
外部来源是供应商或客户。使用此形状来指示输入何时来自组织或流程外部，或者输出何时到达流程的最终客户。
数据框很重要，因为它们包含价值流图的分析内容。数据框中记录的常见信息包括：
周期时间，或 C/T。周期时间是整个流程（或流程中的一个步骤）完成一次迭代所需的时间。
转换时间，C/T。转换时间是指流程中班次、工具或其他资源或流程中单个步骤发生变化所需的时间。
操作人员数量
班次数量
时间轮班工作
一次合格率质量
批量大小
节拍时间，即为满足客户需求而必须完成产品的速度。流程中单个步骤的节拍时间可能被视为需要完成该步骤以避免减慢流程的时间。
库存框表示库存通常会在流程中的某个时刻堆积起来。它通常与说明可用库存数量、时间或两者兼而有之的文本结合在一起。您可能会注意到，流程中此时经常有 100 件库存。您可能会注意到，在此过程中，此时有两天的库存量；你可以同时注意两者。
推箭头表示某些东西（产品或信息）从流程的一个部分推到流程的另一部分。推箭头表示产品或信息由流程的该部分生成，而不管流程的下游部分是否需要它。
与推箭头相反的是拉指示器，这意味着下游流程根据需要从上游位置拉库存、零件或信息。
成品箭头表示流程的输出正在推送或交付给客户 - 无论该客户是内部、外部还是其他流程。
FIFO 箭头表示这部分流程具有先进先出的顺序，这在某些流程中很重要。考虑处理食品成分的流程；如果制造工厂的配方需要牛奶，那么操作员应该首先从存储中取出最旧的（仍然是好的）牛奶，以防止浪费在库存期间变质的产品。
超市是流程中存放少量库存的区域。下游流程可以根据需要从超市进行库存，而不是从较大的库存供应中进行库存，因为后者可能更难以获取。上游流程为超市提供原料，确保有足够的库存。超市的例子可以在大型办公室中看到。一个团队可能有一个抽屉，用来存放纸张、笔、回形针和订书钉等用品。每次需要用品时，团队可以从抽屉中取出，而不是从更大的储藏室或实际的办公用品商店申请。
物理拉动符号表示产品或材料必须从上一个步骤、站点或超市物理拉动到下一步。
叉车移动符号是不言自明的，但在每个过程中并不总是必需的。但是，您可能有其他类型移动的符号，例如用手推车或传送带移动。您唯一需要包含有关如何移动项目的规范的时候是这些细节可能对流程分析变得很重要的时候。
这也是一个不言自明的符号，也不是每个过程中都必需的。相反，您的价值流地图上可能会列出火车、飞机甚至自行车运输。
直接手动流程箭头指示的信息类型通常通过备忘录、面对面讲话、电话和报告进行传达。手动信息箭头可能附有有关信息类型及其传递频率的注释。
弯曲的电子信息流箭头表示本质上是电子的信息，例如通过互联网、门户、EDI和自动报告系统进行的信息通信。该符号还可以包含数据类型及其传送频率。
提款看板形状表明该流程步骤有看板卡或信号。看板卡指示操作员在该特定步骤从超市或其他库存地点提取库存。

rows: 术语说明 Kanban（看板）：用可视化信号控制工作流、库存或任务状态。
zh.text: 术语说明 Kanban（看板）：用可视化信号控制工作流、库存或任务状态。
生产看板形状表明生产看板卡或信号位于流程的该部分。该卡告诉操作员生产一定数量的零件来供给下游流程。
安全库存图标表示流程中此时存在“以防万一”的库存存储。安全库存不像超市——它的目的不是永久储存库存。相反，它是库存的临时存储，可以减少上游停机或其他问题导致所有下游流程发生的可能性。例如，在一家快餐店，有人每天早上都会储备杯子和饮水机。这些库存并不是为了永久存储这些物品，而是保留少量物品，以便员工可以快速向消费者提供饮料。
操作员形状表示流程中的操作员或员工。您不必在每个人员所在的图表中都包含运算符形状 - 在某些情况下，价值流图的性质允许读者假设人员是流程的一部分。但是，如果自动化流程包含在人员驱动的流程中，则可以在需要时使用此符号以保持清晰。
计划框只是一个文本框，可让您记录如何计划工作、订单或流程的其他部分。例如，您可以将该框与箭头结合起来，以显示每周发送订单，或者每月为员工创建一个生产计划。

en.text: Creating a value stream map is very different from creating a process map. One point of value stream mapping is that it forces you to really experience the process, which means that committee-creation of value stream maps isn’t going to provide the best outcome. The entire team can get involved in value stream mapping, if you as the Six Sigma expert think that is valuable, but the best results often come when everyone starts with their own individual value stream map. This is also a good process for the Six Sigma experts in a team, because it forces each one to view the process individually.

While you can use the icons above and numerous software programs to create a final copy of your value stream map, use paper and pencil for your first drafts. This lets you record information as you walk through the process, changing it quickly as you go.

When creating process maps, you often concentrate on the process, the employees, and the requirements. When creating value stream maps, you should always concentrate most on the customer and the product.

Physically walk the process whenever possible instead of sitting at a computer or working with a brainstorming session. Some experts recommend walking the process backwards; others say start at the beginning – find a method that works best for you. Don’t try to map every single part of the process, especially if you are mapping a large-scale production process. Instead, follow the major raw materials through to the outputs. If you put all the processes on a single map, it becomes too hard to read and you lose the value of the tool.

While you might eventually create a value stream map of a desired future state, start by mapping the way things are. Even if you see they aren’t working, record the actual process. Use real-time data for the value stream map whenever possible. For example, don’t use historical production and changeover times; when you walk the process, use a stopwatch to record your own observations.

zh.text: 创建价值流图与创建流程图有很大不同。价值流图的一点是它迫使你真正体验这个过程，这意味着委员会创建的价值流图不会提供最好的结果。整个团队都可以参与价值流图绘制，如果你作为六西格玛专家认为这是有价值的，但最好的结果往往是当每个人都从自己的个人价值流图开始时。对于团队中的六西格玛专家来说，这也是一个很好的流程，因为它迫使每个人都单独查看流程。
虽然您可以使用上面的图标和许多软件程序来创建价值流图的最终副本，但请使用纸和铅笔来绘制初稿。这使您可以在整个过程中记录信息，并在过程中快速更改它。
创建流程图时，您通常会关注流程、员工和需求。创建价值流图时，您应该始终最关注客户和产品。
尽可能亲自走动这个过程，而不是坐在电脑前或进行头脑风暴。一些专家建议倒着走这个过程；其他人说从头开始——找到最适合你的方法。不要尝试绘制流程的每个部分，尤其是在绘制大规模生产流程时。相反，要跟踪主要原材料直至产出。如果将所有流程放在一张地图上，就会变得难以阅读，并且会失去该工具的价值。
虽然您最终可能会创建所需未来状态的价值流图，但首先要绘制事物的现状。即使您发现它们不起作用，也要记录实际过程。尽可能使用实时数据来绘制价值流图。例如，不要使用历史生产和转换时间；当你走这个过程时，用秒表记录你自己的观察结果。

en.text: Begin value stream mapping by selecting the product family or process you are concerned with. In a manufacturing environment, you might select product type C and only concern yourself with that product. For example, a toy factory might make dolls, doll houses, and toy cars, but the Six Sigma team

might only be concerned with doll houses. In fact, the Six Sigma team might only be concerned with the doors that are made for the doll houses – you decide what scope of value stream map makes sense for your project.
Next, collect as much relevant data on the current state of the process as possible. Do this by walking the process and taking notes, reviewing actual data, and speaking with subject matter experts.
Create a Value Stream Map Drawing

To create an example of a value stream map while going through the following steps, we’ll consider a simple real-world example. In our example, a factory makes crayons. The Six Sigma team is concerned with the process that makes the 8-pack of primary colored crayons.
The value stream map will need to include information such as:
The suppliers of raw goods (wax, dye, and paper)
Wax melting
Dye addition
Pouring into molds to create crayon shapes
Inspection points
Wrapping crayons with appropriate paper
Packing crayons into boxes of eight
Shipping to final customer -- in this case, retail stores

1. Begin with the major process steps in the value stream. These are indicated by process boxes and are joined in a left-to-right line by the appropriate arrows – often by push arrows. For our example process, let’s assume a few basic process steps:

a. The wax is melted and dye is added b. Wax is poured into molds c. Molds are emptied d. Inspection occurs e. Shaped crayons are wrapped with matching colored paper f. Crayons are packed in boxes g. Shipping

Above, you can see a rough sketch of the process part of the value stream map. Kanban cards or signals tell employees when to pull wax and dye from the supermarket inventory. (The signal might be a light that the previous batch has been processed and the machines have returned to process start

parameters). The employees add the ingredients and start the first piece of the process, which is automated after the initial ingredient measurements. The wax is melted, the dye is mixed in, the colored wax is poured into molds, and the mold sets for 10 minutes before crayons are emptied to the inspection station.

You’ll note that the arrows connecting the first processes are different, because we want to show that they are automated and the process simply runs together unless someone intervenes in that batch. The rest of the processes have push arrows, because inspection, paper addition, and packing don’t operate on a batch process and work as long as inventory is flowing through.
2. Add the important aspects from outside of the process, including process controls, supplies, customers, and shipments.
3. Add data boxes for each part of the process.

4. Add inventory signals and any other information you think would be pertinent for the value stream map. For example, in reviewing the crayon process used in the example, the Six Sigma expert might note that inventory builds up before the packing process because it is slower than the inspection and paper-wrapping process. That could be indicated with an inventory signal and text.

You can also add controls for the process. In this case, the retailer places orders one month ahead for how many boxes it will need each week in the next month. The crayon-making company creates a monthly forecast, but orders once a week from suppliers. Suppliers deliver in multiple batches a week. Production control manages every part of the process with scheduling to keep up with the demands of the retailers.
Finally, add a timeline at the bottom that notes the time it takes for a single item to process through the enter process, breaking the time into value added and non-value added.

In the above example, the yellow portions are value added. They represent time that the product is actually being made or changed. The white portions are the setup, lead times, and inventory wait times – and this particular process is for a single color of crayons and doesn’t take into account any time spent preparing machines for changing colors.
You can add up the times to see that the total process time is 194 minutes or 3.23 hours.
The value added time is 19 minutes, so the team can assume there is definitely waste that can be removed from this process.

Remember, value stream maps aren’t supposed to be formal documents – they are working pictures of the process to help you and the rest of a Six Sigma team better understand the process and related challenges. So, you can add as much or as little information as you think is necessary. You can also come back and add information later if needed. The first draft of a value stream map, whether using pen and paper or a computer program, is probably going to be messy, and that’s okay. In fact, that’s a sign that you are doing it right. You can always make a clean, final copy for inclusion in presentations and training documents if desired.
What Do You Do with Value Stream Maps?

Value stream mapping is a helpful tool for all of the DMAIC phases. During Define, value stream mapping as an activity forces you to become involved with the process at a physical and daily level, which can boost your understanding of the process you are working to improve. It also provides the entire team with another visual that helps when discussing the process and the problem. A good value stream map lets the team visualize exactly where the process and problem might occur with respect to overall production.

Creating a value stream map means you’ve already conducted some measurement, creating a foundation for the Measure phase. The value stream map also points to measurement and data collection points that can be further used in Measure, though teams should never limit themselves to just the first types of information gathered in value stream mapping.

During Analyze, value stream maps are a tool that helps teams brainstorm and identify root causes or problem points in a process, and you can use the initial value stream map to identify activity or resources that don’t provide value – those that create muda in the process. Finally, during Improve, teams can begin creating a future state value stream map, building off the old map and using the information and solutions they have come up with through the DMAIC process. By putting those solutions on paper in the form of a value stream map, teams can better understand where issues might arise and alter solutions appropriately to reduce the number of issues experienced during implementation and beyond.

zh.text: 通过选择您关心的产品系列或流程来开始价值流图绘制。在制造环境中，您可以选择产品类型 C 并且只关心该产品。例如，玩具厂可能生产娃娃、娃娃屋、玩具车，但六西格玛团队
可能只关心玩具屋。 事实上，六西格玛团队可能只关心为娃娃屋制作的门——您可以决定价值流图的哪个范围对您的项目有意义。

rows: 术语说明 Scope（项目范围）：说明改进项目包括什么、不包括什么，避免目标扩张。
zh.text: 术语说明 Scope（项目范围）：说明改进项目包括什么、不包括什么，避免目标扩张。
接下来，收集尽可能多的有关流程当前状态的相关数据。通过走遍整个过程并做笔记、审查实际数据以及与主题专家交谈来做到这一点。
创建价值流图
为了在执行以下步骤时创建价值流图示例，我们将考虑一个简单的现实示例。在我们的示例中，一家工厂生产蜡笔。六西格玛团队负责8支装原色蜡笔的制作过程。
价值流图需要包括以下信息：
原材料供应商（蜡、染料和纸张）
蜡熔
染料添加
倒入模具中以形成蜡笔形状
检查点
用合适的纸包裹蜡笔
将蜡笔装入八盒中
运送给最终客户——在本例中为零售店
1. 从价值流中的主要流程步骤开始。这些由流程框指示，并通过适当的箭头（通常是推动箭头）连接在从左到右的线上。对于我们的示例流程，我们假设几个基本流程步骤：
a.蜡熔化并添加染料 b．将蜡倒入模具中 C.模具被清空 d．检查发生 e. f. 形状蜡笔用相配的彩色纸包裹。蜡笔装在盒子 g 中。运输
在上面，您可以看到价值流图的流程部分的粗略草图。看板卡或信号告诉员工何时从超市库存中取出蜡和染料。 （该信号可能是一个指示灯，表明前一批已处理完毕，并且机器已返回到处理开始状态
参数）。员工添加成分并开始流程的第一部分，该流程在初始成分测量后自动完成。蜡熔化，混合染料，将彩色蜡倒入模具中，模具固定 10 分钟，然后将蜡笔清空到检查站。
您会注意到，连接第一个流程的箭头是不同的，因为我们想表明它们是自动化的，并且流程只是一起运行，除非有人干预该批次。其余流程都有推动箭头，因为检查、纸张添加和包装不会在批处理流程中运行，并且只要库存在流动就可以工作。
2. 添加流程外部的重要方面，包括流程控制、供应、客户和发货。
3. 为流程的每个部分添加数据框。
4. 添加库存信号以及您认为与价值流图相关的任何其他信息。例如，在审查示例中使用的蜡笔流程时，六西格玛专家可能会注意到库存在包装流程之前就已积累，因为它比检查和包装流程慢。这可以通过库存信号和文本来指示。
您还可以为流程添加控件。在这种情况下，零售商提前一个月下订单，确定下个月每周需要多少个盒子。这家蜡笔制造公司每月都会进行预测，但每周都会向供应商订购一次。供应商每周分多批交货。生产控制通过调度来管理流程的每个部分，以满足零售商的需求。
最后，在底部添加一个时间线，记录单个项目通过输入过程进行处理所需的时间，将时间分为增值时间和非增值时间。
在上面的例子中，黄色部分是增值的。它们代表产品实际制造或更改的时间。白色部分是设置、交货时间和库存等待时间——这个特定的过程是针对单色蜡笔的，没有考虑到准备机器改变颜色所花费的任何时间。
您可以将时间相加，发现总处理时间为 194 分钟或 3.23 小时。
增值时间为 19 分钟，因此团队可以假设这个过程中肯定存在可以消除的浪费。
请记住，价值流图不应该是正式文件——它们是流程的工作图片，可以帮助您和六西格玛团队的其他成员更好地理解流程和相关挑战。 因此，您可以根据需要添加尽可能多或尽可能少的信息。如果需要，您也可以稍后返回并添加信息。价值流图的初稿，无论是使用笔和纸还是计算机程序，可能会很混乱，但这没关系。事实上，这表明你做得对。如果需要，您始终可以制作一份干净的最终副本，以便包含在演示文稿和培训文档中。
您如何使用价值流图？
价值流图是适用于所有 DMAIC 阶段的有用工具。在定义过程中，价值流图作为一项活动迫使您在实际和日常层面上参与该流程，这可以增进您对正在努力改进的流程的理解。它还为整个团队提供了另一种视觉效果，有助于讨论流程和问题。良好的价值流图可以让团队准确地直观地看到整个生产中可能发生流程和问题的位置。

rows: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
zh.text: 术语说明 DMAIC：定义、测量、分析、改进、控制；适用于改进已有流程。
创建价值流图意味着您已经进行了一些测量，为测量阶段奠定了基础。价值流图还指出了可以在测量中进一步使用的测量和数据收集点，尽管团队永远不应该将自己局限于价值流图中收集的第一类信息。

在分析过程中，价值流图是一种工具，可以帮助团队集思广益并识别流程中的根本原因或问题点，您可以使用初始价值流图来识别不提供价值的活动或资源 - 那些在流程中创造 Muda 的活动或资源。最后，在改进期间，团队可以开始创建未来状态价值流图，构建旧地图并使用他们通过 DMAIC 流程提出的信息和解决方案。通过将这些解决方案以价值流图的形式写在纸上，团队可以更好地了解可能出现问题的位置，并适当地更改解决方案，以减少实施期间及之后遇到的问题数量。

rows: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。
zh.text: 术语说明 Root Cause（根本原因）：真正驱动问题发生的原因；解决它才能防止问题反复出现。

title.en: Tips for Creating a Future-State Value Stream Map
title.zh: 创建未来状态价值流图的技巧

en.text: 1. Use the current-state value stream map and all other information gathered and analyzed to identify as many sources of waste in the process as possible. 2. Categorize waste into two types: those that are the result of remote locations, machinery, and product designs which are all necessary elements to the end product and those that are not the result of these things. 3. Concentration on the second type of waste and create a future state value stream map that removes them all. 4. Review the future state map to ensure processes can flow appropriately. Ask yourself questions such as:

a. What is the Takt time required at each station to meet overall demands, and is each step in the process capable of meeting that Takt time? If not, the team might need to address steps that are not capable. b. How will production be scheduled? Usually, there is a specific part of the process that controls the production schedule. Ultimately, the best and most optimal control is by the customer (The customer needs 100 per day, so the process should make 100 per day). However, there are times when a specific part of the process is only capable of X units per time period, and the team is unable to change that capability because of resource or other restraints. If the process is not controlled by this “pacemaker step,” then inventory is going to build up before it. c. Is batch processing needed, or can it be removed? d. What process changes and improvements are required to implement the future state value stream? Sometimes, machinery changes, staffing changes, or capital investments are required, and these aren’t always possible. Before moving forward with future-state plans, Six Sigma teams have to make sure resources will be approved and available.

As with anything in Lean and Six Sigma processes, the value stream map isn’t a requirement. In fact, one of the cardinal rules of value stream mapping is not to map just to map. Yes, a finished and polished map looks nice – and can be especially impressive in presentations or documents when other people in the organization are not fully familiar with the tool. However, if creating a value stream map isn’t going to help a Six Sigma team through any of the DMAIC phases or if the process involved simply doesn’t lend itself to mapping in this fashion, then skip this tool. A Six Sigma expert should always be aware that muda can creep into any process – and that includes the process of evaluating and improving something. If you’re creating a value stream map without reason, then you are creating muda of overproduction.

zh.text: 1. 使用当前状态价值流图以及收集和分析的所有其他信息来识别流程中尽可能多的浪费源。 2. 将废物分为两类： 那些是远程位置、机械和产品设计的结果，这些都是最终产品的必要元素，而那些则不是这些因素的结果。 3. 专注于第二种浪费，并创建未来状态价值流图，将其全部消除。 4. 检查未来状态图以确保流程能够正常进行。问自己一些问题，例如：

a.满足总体需求所需的每个工位的节拍时间是多少？流程中的每个步骤是否能够满足该节拍时间？如果没有，团队可能需要解决不具备能力的步骤。 b.生产将如何安排？通常，流程中有一个特定部分来控制生产计划。最终，最好、最优化的控制是由客户进行的（客户每天需要 100 个，因此流程应该每天生产 100 个）。然而，有时流程的特定部分每个时间段只能处理 X 个单位，并且由于资源或其他限制，团队无法更改该功能。如果这个过程不受这个“起搏器步骤”的控制，那么库存就会在它之前积累起来。 c.是否需要批处理，或者可以删除它吗？ d.实施未来状态价值流需要哪些流程改变和改进？有时，需要更换机器、更换人员或进行资本投资，但这些并不总是可行。在推进未来状态计划之前，六西格玛团队必须确保资源获得批准并可用。

与精益和六西格玛流程中的任何内容一样，价值流图不是必需的。事实上，价值流映射的基本规则之一不是仅仅为了映射而映射。是的，经过精心设计的地图看起来不错，并且当组织中的其他人不完全熟悉该工具时，在演示文稿或文档中尤其令人印象深刻。但是，如果创建价值流图无法帮助六西格玛团队完成任何 DMAIC 阶段，或者所涉及的流程根本不适合以这种方式进行映射，那么请跳过此工具。六西格玛专家应该始终意识到，muda 可以渗透到任何过程中，包括评估和改进某些事物的过程。如果你毫无理由地创建价值流图，那么你就是在制造生产过剩的泥潭。

dictionary.explanation: 一套以数据为基础的流程改进方法，也是一种统计质量目标；核心是减少变异、缺陷、返工和客户不满意。
统计学中常用来表示标准差。六西格玛语境中，它常用于表达流程波动水平和质量表现。
用于概括流程质量表现的等级化指标。西格玛水平越高，通常代表缺陷机会越少、流程输出越稳定。
一套有结构、有步骤的方法体系，不是单个技巧或工具。
把输入转化为输出的一系列活动。六西格玛改进通常围绕流程的测量、分析、改进和控制展开。
流程输出或执行方式中的差异。变异越大，缺陷、返工和客户体验不一致的风险通常越高。
未满足客户需求、规格要求或流程要求的输出。
一次可能发生缺陷的机会。DPMO 等指标会把缺陷数放到机会数的语境里衡量。
在较小或较受控范围内试用新想法、产品或系统，先发现问题再扩大推广。
Defects Per Million Opportunities，指每百万次机会中的缺陷数；数值越低，流程质量越高。

dictionary.translation: 定义、测量、分析、改进、控制
dictionary.explanation: 六西格玛改进既有流程时常用的五阶段方法：Define、Measure、Analyze、Improve、Control。
符合要求的输出比例。第 1 章使用公式：（机会数 - 缺陷数）/ 机会数 × 100。
客户需求、抱怨、期望和偏好的系统化输入，用于帮助团队确定真正重要的质量要求。
为了产生最终结果所需的所有活动、事项、人员和信息的序列，可用于识别浪费和改进机会。
持续寻找并优先处理流程中的改进机会，而不是把质量改进视为一次性项目。
流程波动处于可解释、可监控的状态。六西格玛改进后需要控制机制防止流程退回旧状态。
